topics.title: Electron Beam Welding - Guns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Welding - Guns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Electron Beam Welding (EBW) Gun

An **Electron Beam Welding Gun** (also called an **electron gun** or **electron beam generator**) is the core sub-assembly of an Electron Beam Welding machine responsible for the **generation, acceleration, focusing, and deflection of a high-velocity, high-energy-density beam of electrons** that is ultimately directed onto the workpiece to produce a weld.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the electron gun is defined as a **high-vacuum thermionic/feld-emission device** that converts electrical energy into a coherent, focused stream of accelerated electrons, with **kinetic energies typically ranging from 30 keV to 200 keV** and **power densities in the order of 10⁶ to 10⁹ W/mm²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> The electron gun is the *heart* of the EBW process. Any question on EBW processes, deep penetration welding, or high-vacuum welding will invariably trace back to the gun's design and operational physics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **high-pressure water jet cutter** used in industry to slice through steel plates. Now, replace the *water* with a *stream of electrons* travelling at nearly the **speed of light** (about 0.3c to 0.7c), and replace the *steel cutting jet* with a focused, narrow electron beam that melts and vaporises metal on contact.

The electron gun in EBW is analogous to the **nozzle and pump assembly of a water jet cutter**:
- The **cathode** = the pump (source of particles)
- The **anode** = the nozzle (accelerator)
- The **focusing coil** = the lens (concentrator)
- The **deflection coil** = the steering mirror (controller)

When you switch on the gun, it does not 'shoot' electrons like a bullet — it **boils them off a hot tungsten filament** (thermionic emission), then **electrostatically accelerates** them through a potential difference (typically **60 kV to 150 kV**), and finally **electromagnetically focuses** them onto a spot as small as **0.1 mm to 1.0 mm** in diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world anchor:** EBW guns are used in joining critical aerospace components such as **turbojet engine blades, nuclear fuel cladding, titanium rocket casings, and biomedical implants (e.g., titanium hip stems)** where conventional arc welding would distort or contaminate the joint.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics of an EBW Gun

- **Accelerating Voltage (V):** 30 kV – 200 kV (typically 60–150 kV)
- **Beam Current (I):** 10 mA – 1000 mA
- **Beam Power (P):** P = V × I → typically 0.3 kW to 100 kW
- **Power Density:** 10⁶ W/mm² – 10⁹ W/mm²
- **Vacuum Level in Gun:** 10⁻⁴ Pa to 10⁻⁶ Pa
- **Spot Diameter:** 0.1 mm – 1.0 mm
- **Electron Velocity:** up to **0.7 c** (where c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s)
- **Depth-to-Width Ratio of Weld:** up to **25:1** (signature of EBW)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Sub-Systems of an EB Gun

An EB gun is not a single component — it is an **integrated electro-optical assembly** comprising four functional sub-systems. Each must be analysed independently before the gun can be understood as a whole.

### 2.1.1 The Electron Emission Sub-System (The Cathode Assembly)

This sub-system is responsible for **liberating free electrons** from a solid surface. Three emission mechanisms exist:

**(a) Thermionic Emission (Most Common in EBW)**
- A **tungsten (W)** or **lanthanum hexaboride (LaB₆)** filament is heated to a temperature of **2400 °C – 2800 °C**.
- At these temperatures, the thermal energy of conduction electrons exceeds the **work function (φ)** of the cathode material.
- Electrons 'boil off' the surface — analogous to water molecules evaporating from a hot pan.
- Emission current is governed by the **Richardson–Dushman Law** (see formula sheet).

**(b) Field Emission**
- An extremely high electric field (> 10⁹ V/m) is applied to a sharp cathode tip.
- Electrons are literally 'pulled' out of the metal by electrostatic force — no heating required.
- Used in **pulsed EBW** for very high instantaneous beam currents.

**(c) Plasma (Hollow Cathode) Emission**
- A low-pressure gas discharge produces a plasma inside a hollow cathode.
- Electrons are extracted from the plasma boundary.
- Used in **non-vacuum / out-of-vacuum EBW** variants.

### 2.1.2 The Acceleration Sub-System (The Anode Assembly)

The emitted electrons are **accelerated by a high potential difference** between the cathode (negative) and the anode (positive, usually at ground potential). The kinetic energy gained is:

$$E_k = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

Where:
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (elementary charge)
- $V$ = accelerating voltage (volts)
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg (electron rest mass)
- $v$ = velocity of electron (m/s)

The electron **velocity** is therefore:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$

At **V = 150 kV**, the velocity reaches approximately **0.7 c**, requiring **relativistic correction** in high-voltage EBW (≥ 100 kV).

### 2.1.3 The Focusing Sub-System (Electromagnetic Lens)

A **magnetic lens** (a water-cooled copper coil carrying DC current) produces a strong axial magnetic field that focuses the diverging electron beam into a tight spot on the workpiece.

The focal length $f$ of a magnetic lens is approximately:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{e}{8m_e V} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B_z^2(z) \, dz$$

Where $B_z(z)$ is the axial magnetic flux density profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Higher accelerating voltage → longer focal length → spot is harder to focus tightly. Hence, **medium voltage EBW (60–80 kV)** offers the best focus stability for precision welds.

### 2.1.4 The Deflection Sub-System (Beam Steering Coils)

A pair of orthogonal **electromagnetic deflection coils** sweeps the focused beam across the workpiece in a programmed pattern (lines, circles, spirals). This allows welding of **non-linear seams** without moving the workpiece — a key advantage of EBW over laser welding for 3D contours.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is written as `\vert` in the table to preserve markdown table syntax. Never use a raw $\vert$ inside a table cell.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Standard Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beam Power | $P = V \cdot I$ | W (watts) | Total thermal power delivered to workpiece |
| 2 | Power Density | $PD = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}} = \dfrac{VI}{\pi r^2}$ | W/mm² | Determines penetration mode (conduction vs keyhole) |
| 3 | Electron Kinetic Energy | $E_k = eV$ | J (or eV) | Energy per electron from accelerating field |
| 4 | Non-relativistic Electron Velocity | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m_e}}$ | m/s | Valid for V < 60 kV |
| 5 | Relativistic Electron Velocity | $v = c\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{m_e c^2}{eV + m_e c^2}\right)^2}$ | m/s | Valid for V ≥ 60 kV |
| 6 | Richardson–Dushman (Thermionic) Current Density | $J = A_R T^2 \exp\left(-\dfrac{\phi}{k_B T}\right)$ | A/m² | Emission current from cathode |
| 7 | Child's Law (Space-Charge Limited Current) | $J = \dfrac{4\epsilon_0}{9}\sqrt{\dfrac{2e}{m_e}} \cdot \dfrac{V^{3/2}}{d^2}$ | A/m² | Maximum extractable current density |
| 8 | Perveance of Gun | $\mathcal{P} = \dfrac{I}{V^{3/2}}$ | A/V^{3/2} | Figure of merit of gun design |
| 9 | Heat Input per Unit Length | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v_{travel}}$ | J/mm | Where $\eta$ = efficiency (0.6–0.85 for EBW) |
| 10 | Spot Diameter (Aberration-Limited) | $d = \sqrt{d_0^2 + d_s^2 + d_c^2}$ | mm | $d_0$ = diffraction, $d_s$ = spherical aberration, $d_c$ = chromatic |
| 11 | Magnetic Lens Focal Length | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{e}{8 m_e V} \int B_z^2 \, dz$ | m⁻¹ | Focus strength of EM lens |
| 12 | Cathode Life (Empirical) | $t_{life} \approx \dfrac{m_{filament}}{R_{evap} \cdot A_{surface}}$ | hours | Tungsten: 50–200 hrs; LaB₆: 500–1500 hrs |

**Constants:**
- $A_R$ (Richardson constant) ≈ $1.2 \times 10^6$ A·m⁻²·K⁻²
- $\phi_{W}$ (Tungsten work function) ≈ **4.5 eV**
- $\phi_{LaB_6}$ (Lanthanum hexaboride work function) ≈ **2.7 eV**
- $k_B$ (Boltzmann constant) = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $\epsilon_0$ (permittivity of free space) = $8.85 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of EB Guns

- **Aerospace:** Welding of titanium, Inconel, and nimonic alloys for turbine and rocket components.
- **Nuclear:** Welding of zirconium-alloy fuel cladding tubes.
- **Automotive:** Joining of transmission gears and sensors.
- **Medical:** Hermetic sealing of titanium and stainless-steel implants.
- **Electronics:** Welding of vacuum-sealed packages and micro-sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **deep, narrow, near-parallel-sided weld profile** of EBW (depth-to-width ratio up to 25:1) arises directly from the extremely high power density produced by the focused electron beam — something no arc-based process can match.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Electron Velocity from Accelerating Voltage

**Starting Premise:** The work done by the electric field on a single electron equals the kinetic energy it gains.

**Step 1 — Work–Energy Equivalence Statement**

$$W_{electric} = \Delta E_k$$

The work done on the electron as it traverses a potential difference $V$ is:

$$W_{electric} = eV$$

The kinetic energy gained is:

$$\Delta E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 - 0 = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

**Step 2 — Equating the Two Sides**

$$eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $v$**

Multiply both sides by 2:

$$2eV = m_e v^2$$

Divide both sides by $m_e$:

$$\frac{2eV}{m_e} = v^2$$

Take the square root of both sides:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting Numerical Values for V = 150 kV (Non-relativistic approximation)**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 150{,}000}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 150{,}000 = 4.806 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

Divide by $m_e$:

$$\frac{4.806 \times 10^{-14}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}} = 5.276 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

Take the square root:

$$v \approx 2.297 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Express as a Fraction of the Speed of Light**

$$\frac{v}{c} = \frac{2.297 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 0.766$$

**Conclusion:** At 150 kV, electrons travel at approximately **0.77 c**. Since this exceeds 0.5c, **relativistic correction is mandatory** for accurate velocity prediction in high-voltage EBW. The non-relativistic formula gives an error of roughly 15–20% at this voltage.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Density at the Workpiece

**Starting Premise:** Power density is the beam power concentrated per unit area of the focused spot.

**Step 1 — Beam Power Expression**

The beam power is the product of accelerating voltage and beam current:

$$P_{beam} = V \cdot I$$

**Step 2 — Spot Area**

For a circular spot of radius $r$:

$$A_{spot} = \pi r^2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Power Density Definition**

$$PD = \frac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} = \frac{VI}{\pi r^2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

Given:
- $V = 100 \text{ kV} = 10^5 \text{ V}$
- $I = 100 \text{ mA} = 0.1 \text{ A}$
- $d = 0.5 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow r = 0.25 \text{ mm}$

Compute beam power:

$$P = V \cdot I = 10^5 \times 0.1 = 10{,}000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}$$

Compute spot area:

$$A = \pi \times (0.25)^2 = \pi \times 0.0625 = 0.1963 \text{ mm}^2$$

Compute power density:

$$PD = \frac{10{,}000}{0.1963} \approx 50{,}928 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 5.1 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Arc Welding**

Conventional GMAW (MIG) achieves roughly 5,000 W/mm². EBW is therefore an order of magnitude more concentrated, which is why EBW produces **keyhole-mode deep penetration** welds that arc welding physically cannot replicate.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Perveance — Figure of Merit of an EB Gun

**Starting Premise:** The space-charge limited current (Child's Law) is given by:

$$I = K \cdot V^{3/2}$$

where $K$ is a geometric constant that depends on cathode–anode geometry.

**Step 1 — Definition of Perveance**

Perveance is defined as the ratio of beam current to the 3/2 power of accelerating voltage:

$$\mathcal{P} = \frac{I}{V^{3/2}}$$

**Step 2 — Significance**

A **higher perveance** gun can deliver more current at a given voltage, or alternatively, more current for a given power. It is the single most important figure of merit for gun design efficiency.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example**

For $I = 250 \text{ mA} = 0.25 \text{ A}$ and $V = 60 \text{ kV} = 6 \times 10^4 \text{ V}$:

$$V^{3/2} = (6 \times 10^4)^{3/2} = 6^{3/2} \times 10^6 = 14.697 \times 10^6$$

$$\mathcal{P} = \frac{0.25}{14.697 \times 10^6} = 1.70 \times 10^{-8} \text{ A/V}^{3/2}$$

**Step 4 — Typical Range**

| Gun Type | Perveance (A/V^{3/2}) |
|---|---|
| Low-perveance (precision) | $10^{-9}$ – $10^{-8}$ |
| Medium-perveance (general EBW) | $10^{-8}$ – $10^{-7}$ |
| High-perveance (high-power EBW) | $10^{-7}$ – $10^{-6}$ |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Emission Mechanism Comparison

| Property | Thermionic (W) | Thermionic (LaB₆) | Field Emission | Plasma Cathode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Work function (eV) | **4.5** | **2.7** | N/A | N/A |
| Operating temp (°C) | 2400–2800 | 1500–1800 | 25 (room) | 800–1200 |
| Current density (A/cm²) | 1–5 | 20–100 | 10³–10⁵ | 1–10 |
| Cathode life (hrs) | 50–200 | 500–1500 | >5000 | >2000 |
| Vacuum required (Pa) | $10^{-4}$ | $10^{-4}$ | $10^{-7}$ | $10^{-2}$–$10^{0}$ |
| Beam quality | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | High | Very high | Moderate |
| Common use | General EBW | Precision EBW | Pulsed EBW / e-beam lithography | Non-vacuum EBW |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: EB Gun Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional sub-systems** of an EBW gun and their interconnections. This representation is preferred over a physical drawing because it conveys the engineering architecture more precisely for an academic question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[High Voltage Power Supply 60 to 150 kV] --> FIL[Filament Heater Supply 0 to 10 V AC]
    PWR --> ANODE[Anode at Ground Potential]
    FIL --> CATH[Cathode Tungsten or LaB6 Filament]
    CATH --> EMIT[Electron Emission Thermionic Process]
    EMIT --> ACCEL[Acceleration Region Cathode to Anode Gap]
    ACCEL --> FOC[Focusing Coil Electromagnetic Lens]
    FOC --> DEF[Deflection Coils X and Y Steering]
    DEF --> WPIE[Workpiece Target Spot 0.1 to 1.0 mm]
    WPIE --> KEYH[Keyhole Formation Deep Penetration]
    KEYH --> WELD[Solidified Weld Bead]
    VAC[Vacuum System 10 to the power minus 4 Pa] --> GUNCH[Gun Chamber High Vacuum]
    GUNCH --> CATH
    GUNCH --> ANODE
    GUNCH --> FOC
    GUNCH --> DEF
    WCH[Work Chamber Vacuum 10 to the power minus 1 Pa] --> WPIE
    CTRL[Programmable Controller CNC] --> DEF
    CTRL --> WCH
    CTRL --> FIL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Power and vacuum systems feed the gun in parallel.
- The **electrons flow** linearly from cathode → emission → acceleration → focus → deflection → workpiece → weld.
- The **controller** orchestrates deflection, filament heating, and chamber conditions.

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Electron Generation to Weld Solidification

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Ctrl as CNC Controller
    participant Fil as Filament Supply
    participant Cat as Cathode
    participant Acc as Accelerating Field
    participant Lens as Magnetic Lens
    participant Def as Deflection Coils
    participant Wp as Workpiece

    Ctrl->>Fil: Energise filament (T to 2500 deg C)
    Fil->>Cat: Heat cathode
    Cat->>Cat: Thermionic emission (Richardson Dushman)
    Cat->>Acc: Emit electrons into accelerating gap
    Acc->>Acc: Accelerate to kinetic energy eV (30 to 150 keV)
    Acc->>Lens: Deliver divergent beam to magnetic lens
    Lens->>Lens: Focus to spot diameter 0.1 to 1.0 mm
    Lens->>Def: Deliver focused beam to deflection coils
    Def->>Wp: Sweep beam along joint path
    Wp->>Wp: Local melting and vaporisation produces keyhole
    Wp->>Wp: Keyhole solidifies to form deep narrow weld
```

## 4.3 Schematic Explanation of Key Sub-Assemblies

For the purposes of the KTU 2024 examination, students must be able to **label the following sub-assemblies** on a schematic cross-section of an EB gun:

| Label | Sub-Assembly | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Cathode (filament) | Electron source |
| **B** | Wehnelt (grid cup) | Beam shaping & current control |
| **C** | Anode (grounded) | Acceleration; defines gun exit aperture |
| **D** | Magnetic focusing coil | Beam convergence to spot |
| **E** | Deflection coils (X, Y) | Programmable beam steering |
| **F** | Viewing optics / camera port | Real-time weld observation |
| **G** | Vacuum port | Connects to gun chamber pump |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wehnelt electrode** is frequently asked in KTU exams. It is a negatively-biased electrode surrounding the cathode that **suppresses electron emission from the sides of the filament** and **shapes the beam into a converging cone** as it leaves the cathode assembly. It acts as a beam-shaping 'skirt'.

---
