# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define "effective throat" of a fillet weld. Why is it important in joint design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **effective throat** of a fillet weld is defined as the **shortest distance from the root of the fillet to the weld face** (or, more precisely, the perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse of the triangular cross-section).

**Importance in joint design:**

1. It represents the **minimum cross-sectional area** of the weld and is therefore the **critical plane** along which shear failure occurs under load.
2. All **strength calculations** (e.g., allowable load on a lap joint) are based on the effective throat dimension, not the leg size.
3. For an equal-leg fillet weld with leg size $s$, the effective throat is mathematically given by:
$$t = 0.707 \cdot s$$
4. It serves as the **design parameter** to be specified on the weld symbol and verified during inspection.

Hence, effective throat is the single most important dimension governing the **load-bearing capacity** of a fillet weld.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between a square butt joint and a single-V butt joint. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Square Butt Joint | Single-V Butt Joint |
|:--------|:------------------|:--------------------|
| **Edge Preparation** | None — plates meet with vertical edges | Each plate is bevelled to form a V (typically 60° included angle) |
| **Plate Thickness Range** | Up to **3 mm** (thin sheets) | **3 to 25 mm** (medium plates) |
| **Penetration** | Partial (limited) | **Full penetration** achievable |
| **Weld Metal Volume** | Minimal | Significant (proportional to plate thickness) |
| **Cost & Skill** | Lowest — no machining, no special skills | Higher — beveling required, multi-pass welding |
| **Application Example** | Tin sheet roofing, HVAC ducting | Pressure vessel shells, boiler drums, structural girders |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **five basic types of welded joints** as classified by AWS. For each, state **one typical industrial application**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **T-joint** is formed between a 10 mm thick flange plate and a 12 mm thick web plate using **fillet welds on both sides**. The joint is subjected to a **tensile load of 120 kN** acting along the flange. The allowable shear stress in the weld metal is **108 MPa**. Determine the **required leg size** of the fillet weld if the weld length on each side is **150 mm**. Use the standard effective-throat formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**AWS classifies welded joints into five basic geometric types:**

1. **Butt Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** Two members lie in the same plane and are joined along their edges.
   - **Sketch description:** Two parallel lines (plates) meeting end-to-end with a weld bead at the seam.
   - **Application:** Longitudinal seams of **pressure vessels** and **pipelines**.

2. **Lap Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** Two members overlap each other and are joined by fillet welds at the edges of the overlap.
   - **Sketch description:** Two parallel lines (plates) with one shifted sideways to overlap the other, welds at top and bottom edges.
   - **Application:** **Sheet metal** fabrications and **bracing members** in truss structures.

3. **T-Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** One member is placed perpendicular to another, forming a "T" shape.
   - **Sketch description:** A vertical plate welded to the top surface of a horizontal plate.
   - **Application:** **Web-to-flange joints** in **I-beams** and **girders**.

4. **Corner Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** Two members meet at an angle (typically 90°) at their edges, forming an "L" shape.
   - **Sketch description:** Two plates meeting at a corner, welded along the outer (or inner) edge.
   - **Application:** **Sheet metal enclosures, box sections, and frames**.

5. **Edge Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** Two parallel (or nearly parallel) members with their edges fused together.
   - **Sketch description:** Two plates side by side, weld bead along the seam where they meet.
   - **Application:** **Sheet metal flooring** and **light-gauge panels**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Applied tensile load: $F = 120$ kN = $1{,}20{,}000$ N
- Allowable shear stress: $\tau_{allow} = 108$ N/mm²
- Weld length on each side: $L = 150$ mm
- Number of fillet welds: 2 (one on each side of the web)
- Effective throat formula: $t = 0.707 \cdot s$

**Step 1: Express the total load-carrying capacity of the welded T-joint.**

The T-joint has **two fillet welds** (one on each side of the web), and each resists shear along its effective throat. The total throat area is:

$$A_{t,total} = 2 \cdot (0.707 \cdot s \cdot L)$$

$$A_{t,total} = 1.414 \cdot s \cdot L$$

**Step 2: Equate the allowable capacity to the applied load.**

$$F = \tau_{allow} \cdot A_{t,total}$$

$$1{,}20{,}000 = 108 \cdot 1.414 \cdot s \cdot 150$$

**Step 3: Solve for $s$.**

$$1{,}20{,}000 = 108 \cdot 212.1 \cdot s$$

$$1{,}20{,}000 = 22{,}906.8 \cdot s$$

$$s = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{22{,}906.8}$$

$$s = 5.24 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Apply a practical factor and adopt a standard size.**

The calculated leg size of 5.24 mm is below the minimum practical fillet leg for 10 mm plate. As a general design rule, **minimum leg size = plate thickness** (capped by AWS D1.1 limits). For a 10 mm plate, the minimum recommended leg is **6 mm**, and we round **up** for safety.

$$\boxed{s_{adopted} = 6 \text{ mm (or 8 mm for comfortable margin)}}$$

**Verification of adopted size with $s = 8$ mm:**

$$A_t = 2 \times 0.707 \times 8 \times 150 = 1696.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\tau_{induced} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1696.8} = 70.7 \text{ MPa} < 108 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Safe)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Describe the **various edge preparations** used in butt welding of plates. State the **thickness range** and **relative weld metal volume** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **single-V butt joint** is to be made between two **20 mm thick** mild steel plates using **GMAW**. The **groove angle is 60°** and the **root gap is 2 mm**. The **weld length is 1.5 m**. Calculate the **approximate volume of weld metal** required (neglecting reinforcement). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Edge Preparation | Geometry Description | Plate Thickness Range | Relative Weld Metal Volume | Remarks |
|:-----------------|:---------------------|:----------------------|:---------------------------|:--------|
| **Square Butt** | No bevel, vertical edges with a small root gap (0–2 mm) | $\leq$ 3 mm | **Very Low** | Lowest cost; cannot achieve full penetration in thick plates |
| **Single-V** | Each plate bevelled at half the included angle (typically 30° from vertical, making 60° total) | 3–25 mm | **Medium** | Good for one-sided access; full penetration achievable |
| **Double-V** | V-groove on both sides of the joint | 15–50 mm (and above) | **Medium** (split between two sides) | Balanced heat input → less distortion; requires flipping |
| **Single-U** | Concave curved profile, root face + land, included angle 8°–15° | 25–100 mm | **Low** (less than V) | Machining required; preferred for thick high-strength steels |
| **Double-U** | U-groove on both sides | > 40 mm | **Low** | Maximum balance and minimum distortion for very thick plates |
| **Single-Bevel** | One plate bevelled, the other square | 6–25 mm (T-joints) | **Low–Medium** | Used in T-joints, corner joints, one-sided access |
| **J-Groove** | One plate J-shaped, other square | 15–50 mm (T-joints) | **Low** | Reduced weld metal in single-sided access situations |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Plate thickness: $t_p = 20$ mm
- Groove angle (included): $\theta = 60°$
- Root gap: $b = 2$ mm
- Weld length: $L = 1.5$ m = $1500$ mm
- Reinforcement neglected.

**Step 1: Recall the volume formula for a single-V groove (without reinforcement):**

$$V = L \cdot t_p \left( b + t_p \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the half-angle:**

$$\frac{\theta}{2} = \frac{60°}{2} = 30°$$

**Step 3: Look up the tangent value:**

$$\tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.5774$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the formula:**

$$V = 1500 \cdot 20 \cdot \left( 2 + 20 \cdot 0.5774 \right)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the inner bracket:**

$$2 + 20 \times 0.5774 = 2 + 11.547 = 13.547 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Multiply:**

$$V = 1500 \times 20 \times 13.547$$

$$V = 30{,}000 \times 13.547$$

$$V = 4{,}06{,}410 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 7: Convert to cubic centimetres (cm³) for readability:**

$$V = 4{,}06{,}410 \text{ mm}^3 \times \frac{1 \text{ cm}^3}{1000 \text{ mm}^3}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 406.4 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 0.406 \text{ litres of weld metal}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **practical quantity** for cost estimation. At a typical GMAW deposition rate of 3 kg/hour and a steel density of 7.85 g/cm³, this represents about 1.5 minutes of actual arc time (excluding setup, inter-pass cleaning, etc.).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **WHERE STUDENTS COMMONLY LOSE MARKS IN JOINT DESIGN QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.707 constant** in fillet weld strength problems. This single omission can cost **3 to 5 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 2. **Confusing leg size and throat.** A common trap: the question gives "leg size 10 mm" and the student directly uses 10 mm as the throat. The throat is 0.707 × 10 = **7.07 mm**. Always re-derive it.
>
> 3. **Unit errors in numerical problems.** Loads in **kN** must be converted to **N** (multiply by 1000) before substituting into N/mm² formulas. Forgetting this gives an answer **1000× too small** and full marks are lost.
>
> 4. **Not including both fillet welds** in a lap joint with double-sided welding. The total length is $2L$, not $L$.
>
> 5. **Drawing mistakes:** Omitting the **root face** or **root gap** in groove sketches, or showing the **weld symbol on the wrong side** of the reference line. Examiners mark the welding symbol interpretation strictly per AWS A2.4.
>
> 6. **No mention of distortion control** in design justifications — for thick plates, examiners expect a comment on **double-sided preparation** to reduce angular distortion.
>
> 7. **In weld length sizing, ignoring the end-return.** A proper answer must add **$2s$** to the calculated length to allow for crater-crack safety. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 8. **In groove angle conversions,** mixing up degrees and radians. For $V = L \cdot t_p (b + t_p \tan(\theta/2))$, the angle is in **degrees** (tangent takes degrees in calculator mode — verify your calculator is in DEG, not RAD).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JOINT DESIGN — QUICK REVISION BULLETS**
>
> - The **five basic AWS joint types** are: **B**utt, **L**ap, **T**, **C**orner, **E**dge. Mnemonic: **"BLTCE"** (like a sandwich).
> - **Effective throat** of an equal-leg fillet weld: $t = 0.707 \cdot s$. Memorize this number — it is **non-negotiable**.
> - **Effective throat** of an unequal-leg fillet weld: $t = \dfrac{s_1 s_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2}}$.
> - **Shear capacity** of a fillet weld: $F = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}$.
> - **Plate thickness → Edge preparation rule of thumb:**
>   - $\leq 3$ mm → Square butt
>   - 3–25 mm → Single V or single bevel
>   - > 25 mm → Double V, U, or J groove
> - **Weld symbol on drawing:** below reference line = arrow side, above = other side, on line = field weld.
> - **Weld metal volume** for a V-groove butt joint: $V = L \cdot t_p (b + t_p \tan(\theta/2))$.
> - **Heat input** is *not* a direct joint design parameter, but the **groove geometry** controls how the heat distributes — narrower grooves concentrate heat, wider grooves spread it.
> - **Tangent values to memorize for exams:** $\tan 30° = 0.577$, $\tan 45° = 1.000$, $\tan 60° = 1.732$.
> - **Always add end-returns** of length $2s$ in fillet weld length calculations.
> - **Always convert kN to N** in numerical problems (multiply by 1000).
> - **Weld symbol standards:** AWS A2.4 (American), ISO 2553 (European/International). Both are valid in KTU answers.
> - **Full penetration (CJP)** welds develop **100%** of base metal strength; **partial penetration (PJP)** typically 70–80%; **fillet welds** roughly 70%.
> - **Square butt joints** do **NOT** give full penetration in plates above 3 mm — a classic KTU trap question.
> - **Lap joints** introduce **eccentric loading** and **bending moment** in addition to shear; advanced design must account for this combined stress.
> - **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** and **PQR (Procedure Qualification Record)** are the **output documents** of a properly designed joint — remember to mention them in descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metal Joining** is defined as the process of permanently or temporarily uniting two or more metallic components to form a single, functionally integrated structure. **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques** refer to modern, sophisticated joining processes that go beyond conventional arc welding, employing non-conventional energy sources (electron beam, laser, plasma, friction, ultrasonic vibration, etc.) to produce joints with superior metallurgical, mechanical, dimensional, and environmental performance characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 – Applications):**
> "Applications of advanced metal joining techniques encompass the systematic study of where, why, and how a specific joining process is selected and deployed in real industrial environments, based on the material, geometry, performance requirement, and economic constraints of the end product."

In the context of **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**, this topic directly maps to:
- **CO1:** *Understand the fundamental and advanced principles of metal joining processes.*
- **CO2:** *Identify and apply appropriate joining techniques for various industrial applications.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a tailor who has a full **toolkit** of stitching tools — needle and thread, stapler, fabric glue, snap buttons, and even ultrasonic fabric bonding. The tailor does not use the same tool for a silk saree and a denim jacket. Likewise, an engineer has a *toolkit* of joining methods: **arc welding, gas welding, resistance welding, laser welding, electron beam welding, friction stir welding, brazing, soldering, adhesive bonding**, and more. The selection depends on:
- What materials are being joined?
- How strong must the joint be?
- Will the joint be exposed to heat, corrosion, or pressure?
- Is the part delicate (like a sensor) or massive (like a ship's hull)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Applications" Matter in KTU Exams:**
> Examiners frequently test whether a student can **match a process to its application**. Memorizing the process definition is not enough — you must know *where it is used*, *why it is preferred*, and *what industries rely on it*.

## 1.3 Categories of Applications

The applications of advanced metal joining techniques can be broadly classified into the following engineering domains:

| S.No. | Engineering Domain | Typical Materials Joined | Critical Performance Demand |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Aerospace | Titanium, Inconel, Aluminium alloys | High strength-to-weight ratio, fatigue resistance |
| 2 | Automotive | Mild steel, AHSS, Aluminium, Magnesium | Mass production, crashworthiness |
| 3 | Marine & Shipbuilding | High-strength steel, Aluminium alloys | Corrosion resistance, large thickness |
| 4 | Nuclear Power | Austenitic SS, Zirconium alloys | Leak-tightness, radiation resistance |
| 5 | Petrochemical & Chemical | Carbon steel, SS, Ni-alloys | Corrosion resistance, pressure integrity |
| 6 | Electronics & Microelectronics | Copper, Kovar, Au-Sn alloys | Micro-scale precision, low heat input |
| 7 | Medical Devices | Titanium, SS 316L, Co-Cr alloys | Biocompatibility, hermetic sealing |
| 8 | Power Generation | Creep-resistant steels, Superalloys | High-temperature endurance |
| 9 | Pipeline & Pressure Vessels | API-grade steels | High pressure, fracture toughness |
| 10 | Construction & Structural | Structural steel, Rebar | Load-bearing capacity, ductility |

**Key Performance Metrics to Remember (Bold Emphasis):**
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):** Region whose microstructure is altered by welding heat.
- **Depth of Penetration (DOP):** Distance the joint extends into the base metal.
- **Distortion:** Unwanted change in shape caused by thermal cycling.
- **Joint Efficiency (η):** Ratio of joint strength to base metal strength, expressed as a percentage.
- **Dilution:** Percentage of base metal melted into the weld pool.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process–Application Matching Logic

The selection of a metal joining process for a given application is governed by four interlinked engineering parameters:

### A. Material Compatibility
- **Reactive metals** (Titanium, Zirconium, Magnesium) → require inert/vacuum shielding → **Electron Beam Welding (EBW), Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)**.
- **Dissimilar metals** (Steel–Aluminium, Copper–Aluminium) → require controlled heat input → **Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Diffusion Bonding, Explosive Welding, Brazing**.
- **Refractory metals** (Tungsten, Molybdenum) → require vacuum → **EBW**.

### B. Joint Geometry & Thickness
- **Thin sheets (< 3 mm)** → **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW), Laser Welding, Ultrasonic Welding**.
- **Medium thickness (3–25 mm)** → **GTAW, GMAW, Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**.
- **Heavy sections (> 25 mm)** → **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW), Electroslag Welding (ESW), EBW, Narrow Gap Welding**.

### C. Production Volume
- **Mass production** (automotive bodies) → **RSW, GMAW, FSW (linear)**, robotic LBW.
- **Low volume / custom** (aerospace, nuclear) → **GTAW, PAW, EBW, LBW**.

### D. Quality & Performance Demand
- **Aerospace-grade fatigue life** → **EBW, LBW, FSW, PAW**.
- **Leak-tight hermetic seals** → **EBW, LBW, Brazing, Diffusion Bonding**.
- **Corrosive service** → **GTAW with low HAZ, FSW, Corrosion-resistant filler selection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight (Why this matters):**
> Modern industries rarely use a *single* joining process for a product. A passenger aircraft may use **riveting + adhesive bonding + FSW + laser welding + brazing** in different sections. This is called **hybrid joining**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **essential equations, metrics, and process parameters** that govern the application suitability of advanced joining processes.

| Quantity / Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Heat Input (HI) | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Energy supplied per unit weld length |
| Penetration Depth (LBW) | $D \approx \dfrac{2 P}{v \cdot \rho \cdot (c_p \cdot \Delta T + L)}$ | mm | Depth of fusion for given power $P$ and speed $v$ |
| Power Density (PD) | $PD = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | W/mm² | Heat concentration at the joint |
| Joint Efficiency (η_j) | $\eta_j = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ | % | Strength ratio of welded joint to parent metal |
| Dilution (D) | $D = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100$ | % | Fraction of base metal in the weld pool |
| Cooling Rate (CR) | $CR \approx \dfrac{2\pi k (T_c - T_0)^2}{(HI)^2}$ | °C/s | Affects HAZ microstructure |
| FSW Tool Rotational Speed | $N$ (rpm) | rev/min | Process parameter for FSW |
| FSW Traverse Speed | $S$ (mm/min) | mm/min | Linear welding speed in FSW |
| Brazing Clearance | $0.03$ to $0.15$ mm | mm | Optimum gap for capillary action |
| Soldering Temperature | $T \lt 450 \degree C$ | °C | Upper thermal limit |
| Brazing Temperature | $450 \degree C \le T \lt 950 \degree C$ | °C | Working thermal range |
| Vacuum Level (EBW) | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa | Pa | Chamber pressure for EBW |

Where:
- $\eta$ = Process efficiency (0.6–0.85 for arc processes; ~0.7 for GMAW; ~0.9 for EBW).
- $V$ = Arc Voltage (V).
- $I$ = Welding Current (A).
- $v$ = Travel Speed (mm/min).
- $P$ = Beam Power (W).
- $A$ = Beam Spot Area (mm²).
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of material.
- $T_c$ = Critical temperature.
- $T_0$ = Pre-heat / ambient temperature.
- $L$ = Latent heat of fusion.
- $\rho$ = Density.
- $c_p$ = Specific heat.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Pitfall Reminder:**
> Never use the **vertical pipe symbol `|`** in markdown tables. KTU evaluator scripts and markdown renderers often break. Use **`\vert`** or **`\mid`** instead.

## 2.3 Process-to-Industry Mapping (Why and How)

The following is the **KTU-recommended process selection chart** that examiners love to test:

> **Memorize this triadic mapping:**
> *(Process) → (Industry) → (Reason for Selection)*

| Joining Process | Primary Industry | Reason for Selection |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **GTAW (TIG)** | Aerospace, Nuclear, Chemical | Clean, slag-free, precise control, no spatter |
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | Automotive, Shipbuilding, Structural | High deposition rate, semi-automatic, versatile |
| **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** | Aerospace, Tube manufacturing | Higher energy density than GTAW, keyhole mode |
| **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** | Aerospace, Nuclear, Sensors | Deep penetration, vacuum purity, narrow HAZ |
| **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** | Automotive, Electronics, Medical | High speed, low distortion, automation-friendly |
| **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Aerospace, Ship panels, Rail | Solid-state, no melting, superior fatigue |
| **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** | Automotive bodies (cars) | Fast, suitable for thin sheets, robotic |
| **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** | Electronics, Foils, Battery tabs | Cold process, joins dissimilar, low heat |
| **Brazing** | HVAC, Carbide tools, Heat exchangers | Joins dissimilar metals, low distortion |
| **Soldering** | Electronics PCB, Plumbing | Low temperature, electrical conductivity |
| **Diffusion Bonding** | Aerospace turbine blades, Nuclear | No bulk melting, joins powder/sheet |
| **Explosion Welding** | Bimetallic plates (Steel–Ti, Steel–Cu) | Joins incompatible metals as clad plates |
| **Adhesive Bonding** | Aerospace panels, Automotive | Stress distribution, vibration damping |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Applications

The applications of advanced metal joining techniques underpin nearly **every modern engineering marvel**:

- **Boeing 787 Dreamliner** uses **FSW** for fuselage panels (50% fewer fasteners, 30% weight reduction).
- **Space Shuttle Main Engines** used **EBW** for Inconel thrust chamber assemblies.
- **Tesla EV battery packs** use **laser welding** for tab-to-cell connection (high-speed, low-resistance joints).
- **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels** use **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** and **Electroslag Welding (ESW)** for thick-section joining.
- **Smartphones** use **ultrasonic welding** for sealing aluminium casings.
- **Cardiac pacemakers** use **laser welding** for hermetic titanium enclosures (biocompatible, leak-proof).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The KTU 2024 Scheme values "derivation-quality" answers, not just final numbers. Even for descriptive application-based questions, students should show *comparative analytical steps*. Below, every step of selection logic and numerical computation is **explicitly** written out.

## 3.1 Analytical Step: Process Selection Using Heat Input Criterion

**Problem:** A 6 mm thick AA 6061-T6 aluminium alloy plate is to be joined for an aerospace application. Two options are proposed: **GTAW** and **FSW**. Justify the selection by computing the heat input per unit length, given that:
- For GTAW: $V = 22$ V, $I = 200$ A, $v = 250$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.6$.
- For FSW: Heat input is empirically given by $HI_{FSW} = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu N F_t}{v \times 1000}$, where $\mu = 0.4$, $N = 800$ rpm, $F_t = 4500$ N, $v = 80$ mm/min.

### Step 1 — Compute Heat Input for GTAW

The standard heat input formula for arc welding is:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
HI_{GTAW} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 22 \times 200}{250 \times 1000}
$$

Multiplying the numerator:

$$
0.6 \times 22 = 13.2
$$

$$
13.2 \times 200 = 2640
$$

Now dividing by the denominator:

$$
HI_{GTAW} = \dfrac{2640}{250{,}000}
$$

$$
HI_{GTAW} = 0.01056 \text{ kJ/mm} = 10.56 \text{ J/mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Heat Input for FSW

The empirical FSW heat input equation is:

$$
HI_{FSW} = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu N F_t}{v \times 1000}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
HI_{FSW} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 0.4 \times 800 \times 4500}{80 \times 1000}
$$

First, compute the numerator piece by piece:

$$
2 \pi = 6.2832
$$

$$
6.2832 \times 0.4 = 2.5133
$$

$$
2.5133 \times 800 = 2010.6
$$

$$
2010.6 \times 4500 = 9{,}047{,}775
$$

Now the denominator:

$$
80 \times 1000 = 80{,}000
$$

Therefore:

$$
HI_{FSW} = \dfrac{9{,}047{,}775}{80{,}000} \approx 113.1 \text{ J/mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Comparative Analysis

| Criterion | GTAW | FSW | Verdict |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Heat input per unit length | 10.56 J/mm | 113.1 J/mm | FSW higher |
| Nature of process | Fusion (melting) | Solid-state | FSW better (no melt defects) |
| HAZ in AA 6061 | Wide, strength loss | Minimal, no melting | FSW better |
| Fatigue life | Moderate | Excellent | FSW better |
| Suitability for aerospace AA 6061-T6 | Acceptable | **Preferred** | **Select FSW** |

### Step 4 — Engineering Conclusion

Despite the higher "nominal" heat input, FSW distributes this energy through **mechanical stirring and frictional plastic deformation**, not through arc melting. For aerospace-grade **AA 6061-T6**, the alloy is **heat-treatable** and loses strength in the HAZ. Hence, **FSW is the preferred process**, and the heat-input value alone is not the sole metric — the **mechanism of heat delivery** is equally critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When comparing processes, **always** mention (a) heat input, (b) microstructure effect, (c) mechanical property outcome, and (d) economic/automation factor. This earns full marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Step: Joint Efficiency Calculation for an EBW Joint

**Problem:** A Ti-6Al-4V titanium alloy plate is electron-beam welded. The ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of the base metal is $\sigma_{base} = 950$ MPa, and the joint UTS is $\sigma_{joint} = 902$ MPa. Determine the joint efficiency and comment on the application's suitability for an aerospace fuselage.

### Step 1 — Apply the Joint Efficiency Formula

$$
\eta_j = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

$$
\eta_j = \dfrac{902}{950} \times 100
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Ratio

$$
\dfrac{902}{950} = 0.9495
$$

$$
\eta_j = 0.9495 \times 100 = 94.95 \%
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

A joint efficiency of **94.95%** is **exceptional** (industrial benchmark is > 90%). This confirms that:
- EBW preserves the parent metal's strength almost completely.
- The narrow HAZ in vacuum-EBW prevents metallurgical degradation of Ti-6Al-4V.
- The joint is **fully qualified** for aerospace primary structures (e.g., fuselage stringers, engine pylons).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Exams:**
> A joint efficiency above 90% indicates a *high-integrity* process — qualify your answer with the term **"aerospace-grade"** in exam scripts.

## 3.3 Analytical Step: Dilution in GMAW for Automotive Application

**Problem:** During a robotic GMAW operation on a mild-steel automotive chassis, the cross-section of the base metal melted is $A_{base} = 9$ mm², and the filler metal deposited is $A_{filler} = 6$ mm². Calculate the dilution.

### Step 1 — Apply the Dilution Formula

$$
D = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

$$
D = \dfrac{9}{9 + 6} \times 100 = \dfrac{9}{15} \times 100
$$

### Step 3 — Compute

$$
\dfrac{9}{15} = 0.6
$$

$$
D = 60 \%
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

A dilution of **60%** is **moderately high** (typical range 30–60% for GMAW). For the automotive chassis, this is acceptable because:
- The dilution contributes to **deep fusion**, ensuring fatigue endurance under dynamic loading.
- However, if the application were a **corrosion-resistant overlay**, dilution should be kept **below 30%** to retain the alloying content of the filler.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Dilution is *not always* to be minimized. It is *application-dependent*. State the context explicitly in your answer.

## 3.4 Comparative Selection Matrix for Aerospace Materials

| Material | Recommended Process | Why? | Typical Component |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| AA 2024-T3 | FSW, LBW | Avoid HAZ softening | Fuselage skin |
| AA 7075-T6 | FSW | High strength retention | Wing stringers |
| Ti-6Al-4V | EBW, PAW, LBW | Vacuum purity, narrow HAZ | Engine pylons, landing gear |
| Inconel 718 | EBW, PAW, GTAW | Crack-resistant, superalloy | Turbine casings |
| Carbon Steel (A36) | GMAW, SAW | Cost-effective, high deposition | Structural frames |
| SS 304 / 316 | GTAW, PAW | Corrosion resistance | Pressure vessels |
| Zirconium alloy | EBW | Neutron transparency, vacuum | Nuclear fuel cladding |
| Copper (pure) | EBW, USW, Brazing | High thermal conductivity | Busbars, heat exchangers |
| Tungsten | EBW | High melting point, vacuum | X-ray targets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Industry-Process Selection Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **decision flow** an engineer follows when matching a metal joining technique to an industrial application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application Requirement] --> B{Is the part in Aerospace or Nuclear domain?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is the material reactive or refractory?}
    B -- No --> D{Is the production high volume?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Select EBW or PAW in vacuum or inert shield]
    C -- No --> F[Select GTAW or Laser Welding]
    D -- Yes --> G{Is the joint thin sheet below 3 mm?}
    D -- No --> H[Select GMAW or SAW]
    G -- Yes --> I[Select RSW or Laser Welding]
    G -- No --> J[Select FSW or Robotic GMAW]
    E --> K[Output: Validated Process for Critical Application]
    F --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    H --> K
    K --> L[End: Document Welding Procedure Specification]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Process-to-Application Mapping Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Aerospace] --> B1[FSW]
    A1 --> B2[EBW]
    A1 --> B3[LBW]
    A2[Automotive] --> B4[RSW]
    A2 --> B5[GMAW]
    A2 --> B6[LBW]
    A3[Marine] --> B7[SAW]
    A3 --> B8[FSW]
    A4[Nuclear] --> B9[EBW]
    A4 --> B10[SAW]
    A4 --> B11[GTAW]
    A5[Electronics] --> B12[USW]
    A5 --> B13[LBW]
    A5 --> B14[Soldering]
    A6[Medical] --> B15[LBW]
    A6 --> B16[Diffusion Bonding]
    A7[Bimetallic Clad] --> B17[Explosion Welding]
    A8[Heat Exchangers] --> B18[Brazing]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Architecture of Advanced Joining Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Advanced Metal Joining Techniques]
    R0 --> R1[Fusion Welding Processes]
    R0 --> R2[Solid State Welding Processes]
    R0 --> R3[Brazing and Soldering]
    R0 --> R4[Adhesive Bonding]
    R0 --> R5[Hybrid Joining]
    R1 --> R11[GTAW]
    R1 --> R12[GMAW]
    R1 --> R13[PAW]
    R1 --> R14[EBW]
    R1 --> R15[LBW]
    R2 --> R21[FSW]
    R2 --> R22[USW]
    R2 --> R23[Diffusion Bonding]
    R2 --> R24[Explosion Welding]
    R2 --> R25[Forge Welding]
    R3 --> R31[Vacuum Brazing]
    R3 --> R32[Induction Brazing]
    R3 --> R33[Torch Brazing]
    R3 --> R34[Furnace Brazing]
    R4 --> R41[Epoxy Bonding]
    R4 --> R42[Anaerobic Adhesives]
    R5 --> R51[Laser Arc Hybrid]
    R5 --> R52[FSW Riveting Hybrid]
    R5 --> R53[Weld Bonding]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Application Intensity Matrix (Visual Concept)

The following is a **textual representation** of the Application-Intensity Matrix, illustrating which industries have the highest adoption of each process. (Pictorial chart not shown in this section; the textual block below is the KTU-compliant substitute.)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern (As per University Norms):**
> - Part A: 3 marks each (short answer, 1 question to be answered if choice is given).
> - Part B: 14 marks each (full descriptive, internal choice is mandatory).
> - Bloom's Levels: Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Evaluate (L5), Create (L6).
> - Each question must be tagged with a **simulated KTU past-year label** to mirror actual question-paper style.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**"List any three industrial applications of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) and state the metallurgical reason for its selection in each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Aerospace fuselage panels (e.g., Boeing 787):** FSW is selected because it is a *solid-state* process — no melting — thereby avoiding porosity and HAZ softening in **AA 2024-T3** and **AA 7075-T6** aluminium alloys. *(1 mark)*
2. **Marine deck panels and ship hulls:** FSW joins long, thick aluminium panels in a single pass with high fatigue strength and minimal distortion, critical for ship structural integrity. *(1 mark)*
3. **Railway high-speed train body shells:** FSW eliminates the strength loss that occurs in arc welding of age-hardenable aluminium alloys, preserving crashworthiness. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**"Why is Electron Beam Welding (EBW) preferred over Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW) for joining titanium alloys in aerospace applications?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Vacuum environment:** EBW is performed in a vacuum chamber, preventing contamination of reactive titanium by oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen — a critical requirement for aerospace-grade joints. *(1 mark)*
2. **Narrow HAZ and high depth-to-width ratio:** EBW's extremely high power density (10⁶–10⁷ W/mm²) produces deep, narrow welds with minimal HAZ, preserving the fatigue strength of Ti-6Al-4V. *(1 mark)*
3. **Low distortion and high joint efficiency:** Typical joint efficiency exceeds 90%, which is mandatory for primary airframe structures. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) "Discuss in detail the applications of advanced metal joining techniques in the aerospace industry, covering at least five distinct processes. For each process, justify the metallurgical or performance-based reason for its selection." (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The aerospace industry demands joints with **high strength-to-weight ratio, fatigue endurance, corrosion resistance, and zero-defect reliability**. Five key processes and their applications are:

1. **Friction Stir Welding (FSW) — Fuselage and Wing Panels:** *(1.5 marks)*
   FSW is solid-state, avoiding the HAZ softening typical of fusion welding in age-hardenable aluminium alloys (AA 2024, AA 7075). The Boeing 787 fuselage uses FSW for skin-stringer panels, reducing rivet count by 80% and weight by ~15%.

2. **Electron Beam Welding (EBW) — Engine Pylons and Landing Gear Assemblies:** *(1.5 marks)*
   EBW provides deep penetration with a narrow, parallel-sided fusion zone in vacuum, eliminating atmospheric contamination of titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V). It is used in the Ariane rocket engine thrust chamber and Boeing 777 landing gear forgings.

3. **Laser Beam Welding (LBW) — Turbine Blades and Honeycomb Structures:** *(1 mark)*
   LBW offers high speed and minimal thermal distortion, suitable for joining thin-walled Inconel turbine blade assemblies and titanium honeycomb sandwich panels in jet engine nacelles.

4. **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) — Aero-engine Casings:** *(1 mark)*
   PAW's keyhole mode delivers deeper penetration than GTAW, used for circumferential welds in Inconel 718 engine casings where defect-free, full-penetration welds are mandatory.

5. **Diffusion Bonding — Turbine Blade Cooling Passages:** *(1 mark)*
   Diffusion bonding joins internal cooling channels in single-crystal nickel-superalloy turbine blades without recrystallization, preserving the high-temperature strength of the parent material.

6. **Adhesive Bonding — Composite-Alu Hybrid Panels:** *(1 mark)*
   Used in modern wing skins to bond CFRP composites to aluminium substructures, distributing stress and damping vibration.

**(b) "A 12 mm thick Inconel 718 plate is to be welded for a rocket-engine combustion chamber. Compare the suitability of GTAW, EBW, and LBW. Recommend the best process and justify your answer with reference to penetration, HAZ, vacuum requirement, and joint efficiency." (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | GTAW | EBW | LBW |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Maximum penetration (single pass, 12 mm) | ~6 mm (multi-pass needed) | Full penetration (one pass) | Full penetration (one pass) |
| HAZ width | Wide (3–5 mm) | Very narrow (< 0.5 mm) | Narrow (0.5–1 mm) |
| Vacuum required | No | Yes (mandatory) | No (but shielding gas preferred) |
| Joint efficiency (typical) | 80–85% | 90–95% | 88–92% |
| Distortion | Moderate | Very low | Low |
| Sensitivity to Inconel's hot cracking | High (if parameters not tuned) | Low (rapid solidification) | Moderate |
| Cost and cycle time | Low cost, slow | High equipment cost, fast | Moderate cost, very fast |
| Suitability for Inconel 718 | Acceptable for non-critical | **Best for critical** | Good for thin sections |

**Conclusion:** For the rocket-engine combustion chamber — a *highly critical, leak-tight, fatigue-loaded* component — **EBW is the recommended process** because: *(3 marks)*
- It offers **full penetration in a single pass**, eliminating multi-pass defects.
- The **vacuum environment** prevents oxidation of nickel and chromium, preserving corrosion resistance.
- The **narrow HAZ** retains the precipitation-hardened strength of Inconel 718.
- Joint efficiency of **>90%** is essential for a pressure-loaded combustion chamber.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) "Explain the application of advanced metal joining techniques in the automotive industry, with emphasis on mass-production compatibility. Discuss at least four processes." (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The automotive industry demands **high-speed, repeatable, low-cost joints** in steel and aluminium body structures. Four principal processes are:

1. **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) — Body-in-White (BIW):** *(2 marks)*
   RSW is the **dominant** automotive joining process, with 4000–5000 spots in a typical car. Modern robots perform up to 60 spots/minute. Used for joining thin (0.6–3 mm) cold-rolled steel, HSLA, and AHSS sheets.

2. **Laser Beam Welding (LBW) — Tailored Blanks and Roof Dicing:** *(1.5 marks)*
   LBW welds tailored blanks (different-thickness steel sheets) into single panels for doors and floor pans, enabling weight reduction. Also used for roof-dice cuts and door hemming.

3. **Friction Stir Welding (FSW) — Battery Trays and EV Underbody:** *(1.5 marks)*
   With the rise of electric vehicles, FSW is increasingly used for **aluminium battery enclosures and motor housings**. The Mazda RX-8 rear door panel and Ford F-150 drive-axle housing are early examples.

4. **Weld Bonding (Adhesive + Spot Weld Hybrid):** *(1 mark)*
   Weld bonding combines RSW spots with structural adhesive, distributing load and improving crashworthiness while reducing spot count by 30–40%.

5. **MIG/MAG (GMAW) — Chassis and Suspension Components:** *(1 mark)*
   Used for thicker sections (3–8 mm) of chassis frames and sub-assemblies, with robotic CO₂/MIG systems producing consistent weld quality.

**(b) "With the rise of electric vehicles, the joining of copper-to-aluminium and aluminium-to-aluminium battery tab connections has become critical. Discuss the processes used, the challenges involved, and the metallurgical reasons for their selection." (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Processes used (4 marks):**
1. **Ultrasonic Welding (USW) — Copper-to-Aluminium tabs:** USW is a *cold* solid-state process, avoiding the brittle **Cu–Al intermetallic compounds** (CuAl₂, Cu₉Al₄) that form in fusion welding. Weld time is < 0.5 s per tab, ideal for mass production of cylindrical 18650/21700 cells.
2. **Laser Beam Welding (LBW) — Prismatic/Pouch Cell Tabs:** LBW joins thin aluminium and nickel tabs with very low heat input, preventing damage to the lithium cell separator. The rapid, automated, single-sided access suits high-volume lines.
3. **Resistance Spot Micro-Welding (μ-RSW) — Tab-to-Busbar Connections:** Used for low-resistance electrical interconnections; copper-to-copper and aluminium-to-aluminium joints in busbars.
4. **Friction Stir Welding (FSW) — Module-to-Module Pack Joining:** For large flat battery enclosures, FSW joins aluminium top and bottom covers with no porosity, ensuring leak-tightness and crash safety.

**Challenges and metallurgical reasons (3 marks):**
- **Dissimilar-metal intermetallics:** Cu–Al joints are prone to brittle IMC formation. USW and FSW, being solid-state, *suppress* melting and thereby *minimize* IMC growth.
- **Thermal sensitivity of Li-ion cells:** Any heat above ~80°C damages the electrolyte and separator. LBW and USW deliver highly localized heat, protecting the cell.
- **Electrical conductivity demand:** Joint resistance must be < 0.1 mΩ to prevent I²R heating. USW produces joints with conductivity close to that of the parent metals.
- **Cycle time:** Battery pack welding must be completed in seconds. USW and LBW are the only industrial methods meeting this throughput.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in "Applications" Questions:**
> 1. **Mere listing without justification:** Writing "FSW is used in aerospace" without explaining *why* earns only 1 of 3 marks. Always add the *metallurgical or performance reason*.
> 2. **Confusing process names:** Students interchange *brazing* and *soldering*, or *FSW* and *Friction Welding*. Memorize the temperature threshold: **Soldering < 450°C < Brazing < 950°C < Welding**.
> 3. **Ignoring economic and production-volume factors:** An answer that recommends EBW for an *automotive door panel* (mass production) is wrong on *cost grounds* — RSW or LBW is correct. Always factor in **production volume and unit cost**.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the dissimilar-metal issue:** For Cu–Al, Ti–Steel, or Mg–Al joints, always state that *fusion welding leads to brittle intermetallics* and *solid-state processes are preferred*.
> 5. **Skipping numerical support in 14-mark answers:** Include at least one **numerical example** (heat input, joint efficiency, dilution) — this converts a 9-mark answer to a 14-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 – Applications:**

- **Definition:** Advanced metal joining techniques are non-conventional processes using controlled energy sources (beam, friction, ultrasonic, etc.) for superior metallurgical joints.
- **Four Selection Criteria:** *Material compatibility, Joint thickness, Production volume, Quality demand.*
- **Aerospace → FSW, EBW, LBW, PAW, Diffusion Bonding.**
- **Automotive → RSW, GMAW, LBW, Weld Bonding, FSW (EVs).**
- **Marine / Shipbuilding → SAW, ESW, FSW.**
- **Nuclear → EBW, GTAW, SAW, Diffusion Bonding.**
- **Electronics → USW, LBW, Soldering, Diffusion Bonding.**
- **Medical → LBW, Diffusion Bonding, Micro-EBW.**
- **Bimetallic Clad → Explosion Welding, Roll Bonding, Diffusion Bonding.**
- **Cu–Al Dissimilar Joints → USW, FSW, Brazing** (avoid fusion → brittle IMCs).
- **Heat Input Formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \times 1000}$ (kJ/mm).
- **Joint Efficiency Formula:** $\eta_j = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ (%).
- **Dilution Formula:** $D = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100$ (%).
- **Process–Industry Triad (Memorize):** *(Process) → (Industry) → (Reason)*.
- **Solid-state vs Fusion:** Solid-state (FSW, USW, Diffusion, Explosion) → no melting → no HAZ, no porosity, no solidification cracks.
- **Hybrid Joining:** Modern industries increasingly use *process combinations* (e.g., weld bonding, laser-arc hybrid, FSW-riveting).
- **Quality Benchmark:** Joint efficiency > 90% qualifies as *aerospace-grade*.
- **Thickness Mapping:** Thin (< 3 mm) → RSW, USW, LBW; Medium (3–25 mm) → GTAW, GMAW, PAW; Heavy (> 25 mm) → SAW, ESW, EBW, Narrow-Gap.
- **Remember the “Impossible Combinations”:** *Magnesium + Steel* (no fusion welding); *Copper + Aluminium* (no arc welding); *Tungsten + anything* (vacuum only); *Glass + Metal* (use adhesive or brazing with metallization).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes – Applications.**
> Continue with Module 2 (Principles of Arc Welding) using the same KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.
