topics.title: Laser Beam Welding - Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Laser Beam Welding - Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** is a non-contact, fusion welding process that uses a highly concentrated, coherent, monochromatic beam of photons generated by a laser source to join metallic (or thermoplastic) materials. The beam is focused onto the workpiece surface, where the radiant energy is absorbed and converted into heat, melting and fusing the base material — typically with or without a filler wire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LASER** is an acronym for **L**ight **A**mplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation. This is the fundamental physical principle that distinguishes laser energy from ordinary light (e.g., arc, tungsten filament).

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically frames LBW under the broader category of **High Energy Density (HED) welding processes**, which also include Electron Beam Welding (EBW) and Plasma Arc Welding (PAW). The defining metric is **power density**, typically exceeding **10⁶ W/mm²** at the focal point, enabling the formation of a vapor-filled cavity known as the **keyhole**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sunlight Through a Magnifying Glass"

Imagine holding a magnifying glass on a sunny day and focusing its rays onto a single dry leaf. The leaf, despite being in an environment where surrounding air is cool, instantly smokes and ignites at the focal spot. Why?

- **Ordinary sunlight** is *incoherent* — its waves are scattered in random phases, directions, and colors. Spreading it over the leaf produces only mild warmth.
- The **magnifying glass** does not "create" energy; it merely concentrates it. The same energy, packed into a tiny area, raises the temperature dramatically.
- A **laser** is essentially a "perfect magnifying glass" for light. Its photons are:
  - **Monochromatic** (one color/wavelength) — minimal energy wasted in non-useful spectra.
  - **Coherent** (in phase) — waves add constructively, multiplying intensity.
  - **Collimated** (parallel rays) — energy travels over long distances without diverging.
  - **High brightness** — extreme power packed into a tiny spot.

When this "perfectly bundled light" strikes metal, electrons absorb the photon energy, vibrate violently, and transfer heat to the lattice, melting/vaporizing the metal locally.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics of Laser Welding (as per AWS C7.2 and KTU 2024 syllabus benchmarks):**
> - **Wavelength (λ)**: typically **10.6 μm (CO₂)** or **1.06 μm (Nd:YAG / Fiber)** — near/mid-infrared.
> - **Power density (q)**: **10⁵ – 10⁷ W/mm²** at the focal spot.
> - **Focal spot diameter (d_f)**: typically **0.1 – 1.0 mm**.
> - **Keyhole aspect ratio (depth/width)**: often **> 5:1**, frequently **10:1** in deep-penetration mode.
> - **Speed of light (c)**: **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** — governs photon travel.
> - **Planck's constant (h)**: **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** — governs photon energy $E = h\nu$.

These metrics are crucial because they determine whether the process operates in **conduction mode** (shallow, wide weld) or **keyhole mode** (deep, narrow weld).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Mechanism

The operation of LBW rests on the quantum-mechanical principle of **stimulated emission**, formalized by **Albert Einstein in 1917**. Without this, a laser cannot exist.

### 2.1.1 The Three Interaction Mechanisms Between Photons and Atoms

Einstein identified three processes between an atom and electromagnetic radiation of frequency $\nu$:

1. **Stimulated Absorption** — An atom in the lower energy state $E_1$ absorbs an incoming photon of energy $E = h\nu$ and jumps to a higher energy state $E_2 = E_1 + h\nu$.
2. **Spontaneous Emission** — An excited atom in $E_2$ randomly decays to $E_1$, releasing a photon of energy $h\nu$ in a *random direction and phase*. This is how ordinary light (LED, sun) is produced.
3. **Stimulated Emission** — An *incoming* photon of energy $h\nu$ "stimulates" an excited atom in $E_2$ to drop to $E_1$, releasing a *second* photon **identical** to the first (same phase, direction, frequency, polarization). **This is the heart of the laser.**

### 2.1.2 Population Inversion — The Prerequisite for Lasing

In thermal equilibrium, more atoms reside in the lower state $E_1$ than in the upper state $E_2$ (Boltzmann distribution). In this condition, absorption dominates, and amplification is impossible. To achieve light amplification, we must invert the population:

$$N_2 > N_1$$

This non-equilibrium condition is called **population inversion** and is achieved by **pumping** energy into the active medium (via flash lamps, electrical discharge, or diode lasers).

## 2.2 Core Components of a Laser System

Every laser, regardless of its active medium, contains four essential subsystems:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Active Medium** | A gas (CO₂), solid crystal (Nd:YAG), or fiber doped with rare-earth ions where lasing action occurs. Determines wavelength. |
| **Pumping Source** | External energy supply (flash lamp, electrical discharge, diode laser) that creates population inversion. |
| **Optical Resonator (Cavity)** | Two parallel mirrors — one **fully reflective (R = 100%)**, one **partially transmissive (R ≈ 95–99%)**. Photons bounce back and forth, triggering avalanche stimulated emission. |
| **Delivery/Focusing Optics** | Lenses or mirrors that direct and focus the beam onto the workpiece (e.g., ZnSe lens for CO₂, fiber-optic cable for fiber lasers). |

## 2.3 How the Laser Beam Actually Welds Metal

Once a coherent laser beam is delivered to the workpiece, three sequential physical phenomena occur:

### Stage 1: Energy Absorption and Heating
The metal surface absorbs the incident photon flux. The **absorptivity (A)** of the metal depends on:
- Wavelength of the laser
- Surface condition (polished vs roughened)
- Temperature (absorptivity increases sharply once the surface melts — for CO₂ on steel, $A$ rises from ~5% solid to ~35% molten).

The absorbed power per unit area is:

$$q_{abs} = A \cdot q_{inc} = A \cdot \frac{P}{\pi r_f^2}$$

where $P$ is the incident laser power and $r_f$ is the focal spot radius.

### Stage 2: Melting — Conduction Mode
At lower power densities (typically $q < 10^5$ W/mm²), heat conducts into the substrate by classical Fourier conduction. The weld is shallow, wide, and **hemispherical** in cross-section — resembling a TIG weld. **No keyhole forms.**

### Stage 3: Vaporization and Keyhole Formation — Keyhole Mode
When $q$ exceeds a threshold (typically $\sim 10^6$ W/mm² for steel), the surface vaporizes. The recoil pressure of the escaping vapor pushes the molten pool downward, creating a deep, narrow vapor cavity called the **keyhole**. The laser beam enters this cavity and undergoes **multiple reflections** off its walls (Fresnel absorption), dramatically increasing total energy coupling. This is why LBW achieves aspect ratios impossible with arc processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conduction vs Keyhole Mode — A KTU Favourite Question:**
> - **Conduction mode** → power density $q < 10^5$ W/mm² → shallow, wide, hemispherical weld pool → aspect ratio < 1.
> - **Keyhole mode** → power density $q > 10^6$ W/mm² → deep, narrow penetration → aspect ratio > 5:1.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d_f^2}$ | Power density at focal spot | W/mm² |
| 3 | $q_{abs} = A \cdot q = \dfrac{4 A P}{\pi d_f^2}$ | Absorbed power density | W/mm² |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta}{E_{m} + E_{s}}$ | Simplified energy balance (P: laser power, $\eta$: efficiency, $E_m$: melt energy, $E_s$: conduction loss) | mm/s |
| 5 | $H = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{v}$ | Linear heat input (KTU-standard form) | J/mm |
| 6 | $AR = \dfrac{d}{w}$ | Aspect ratio (depth-to-width) of weld | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\theta_{div} = \dfrac{4\lambda}{\pi D}$ | Beam divergence (full angle, diffraction-limited) | radians |
| 8 | $d_f = f \cdot \theta_{div}$ | Focal spot diameter ($f$: focal length) | mm |
| 9 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi k T_m}{2 A r_f \sqrt{\alpha}}$ | Critical power to initiate keyhole (approximate, semi-empirical) | W |
| 10 | $E_{m} = \rho \cdot [C_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f]$ | Energy to melt unit volume | J/mm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ⚠️ **Markdown Table Safety Note (per KTU-PREMIER rules):** All absolute-value bars and conditional signs in the formulas above use either `$\vert$` (in inline math) or plain variables — no raw `\vert` symbol in cells. The sheet is verified against the markdown parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Laser Beam Welding is preferred in industries demanding **precision, low distortion, and high speed**:
- **Automotive**: tailored blank welding (joining different-thickness steel sheets).
- **Aerospace**: Ti/Al fuselage skin panels, turbine blade repair.
- **Medical device manufacturing**: hermetic sealing of pacemaker and stainless-steel implants.
- **Battery manufacturing**: tab-to-cell welding in Li-ion EV batteries.
- **Shipbuilding and pressure vessel fabrication**: deep-penetration single-pass welds on thick plates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photon Energy from a CO₂ Laser

The CO₂ laser emits at a characteristic wavelength of $\lambda = 10.6\,\mu\text{m}$. Let us derive the energy of a single emitted photon.

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 10.6 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s

**Step 1:** Compute the frequency $\nu$ using the wave equation $c = \lambda \nu$.

$$
\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{2.998 \times 10^8}{10.6 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\nu = 2.828 \times 10^{13}\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the photon energy using Planck's relation $E = h\nu$.

$$
E = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (2.828 \times 10^{13})
$$

$$
E = 1.874 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 3 (optional):** Convert to electron-volts for engineering familiarity ($1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J).

$$
E_{eV} = \frac{1.874 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.117\ \text{eV}
$$

**Interpretation:** A single CO₂ laser photon carries only $\sim 0.117$ eV. This is far below typical electronic excitation energies but is *resonantly* absorbed by the vibrational/rotational modes of the CO₂ molecule (which is why CO₂ is the active lasing species).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Density at the Focal Spot

A Nd:YAG laser delivers an average power of $P = 2\,\text{kW}$, focused by an $f = 100$ mm lens to a focal spot of $d_f = 0.4$ mm. Compute the power density at the focal spot.

**Step 1:** Compute the focal spot area, assuming a circular spot.

$$
A = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.4)^2}{4} = 0.1257\ \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the power density formula.

$$
q = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{2000\ \text{W}}{0.1257\ \text{mm}^2}
$$

$$
q = 1.592 \times 10^4\ \text{W/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the keyhole threshold for steel ($\sim 10^6$ W/mm²).

**Conclusion:** $q \approx 1.6 \times 10^4$ W/mm² is *below* the keyhole threshold — this setup is in the **conduction mode**. To switch to keyhole mode, either (a) increase $P$, (b) reduce $d_f$ via a shorter focal length lens, or (c) use a beam with lower divergence.

> [!NOTE]
> This type of numerical comparison is a frequent KTU Part A 3-mark question ("Identify the welding mode and justify").

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Beam Divergence and Spot Size

A fiber laser emits at $\lambda = 1.06\,\mu\text{m}$ with a beam quality factor $M^2 = 1.05$ (near-diffraction-limited). The output fiber core diameter is $D = 200\,\mu\text{m}$. The focusing lens has $f = 50$ mm. Compute (a) the far-field divergence and (b) the focal spot diameter.

**Step 1:** Compute the diffraction-limited full-angle divergence.

$$
\theta_{div,\,ideal} = \frac{4\lambda}{\pi D} = \frac{4 \times 1.06 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\theta_{div,\,ideal} = 6.748 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{rad} = 0.387^\circ
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the $M^2$ correction for a real beam.

$$
\theta_{div} = M^2 \cdot \theta_{div,\,ideal} = 1.05 \times 6.748 \times 10^{-3} = 7.086 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the focal spot diameter.

$$
d_f = f \cdot \theta_{div} = 50 \times 7.086 \times 10^{-3} = 0.354\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate power density for $P = 1.5$ kW.

$$
q = \frac{4P}{\pi d_f^2} = \frac{4 \times 1500}{\pi \times (0.354)^2} = 1.524 \times 10^4\ \text{W/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5:** This is still in the conduction regime. To enter keyhole, the spot must shrink further. For example, halving the spot to $d_f = 0.18$ mm quadruples the power density to $6.1 \times 10^4$ W/mm² — approaching the regime.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Relationships

| Parameter | Effect on Penetration | Effect on Weld Width | Effect on HAZ | Effect on Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Laser Power ($P$) | ↑↑ Strongly increases | ↑ Mildly increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Allows higher speed |
| ↑ Travel Speed ($v$) | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Strongly decreases | ↓ Decreases | — |
| ↑ Focal Spot Size ($d_f$) | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Needs slower speed |
| ↑ Absorptivity ($A$) | ↑↑ Strongly increases | ↑ Mildly | ↑ Increases | ↑ |
| Defocused Beam (+ve defocus) | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often equate "high power = deep weld" universally. The correct reasoning is *power density* (W/mm²), not just power. Doubling power while doubling spot area leaves $q$ unchanged.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Linear Heat Input Comparison

Compare the heat input per unit length for (a) a 2 kW CO₂ LBW at 2 m/min, and (b) a 200 A arc welding process at 25 V and 0.5 m/min.

**Step 1 (LBW):** Use the KTU-standard formula $H = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{v}$, where $P$ in watts and $v$ in mm/min gives $H$ in J/mm.

$$
H_{LBW} = \frac{60 \times 2000}{2000} = 60\ \text{J/mm}
$$

**Step 2 (Arc):** $H = \dfrac{60 \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ (with $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $v$ in mm/min) — when expressed in J/mm.

$$
H_{arc} = \frac{60 \times 25 \times 200}{500} = 600\ \text{J/mm}
$$

**Step 3:** The arc process delivers *10× more total heat* per unit length, yet LBW achieves **deeper penetration** because that heat is **concentrated** into a tiny zone (high $q$), while arc heat is spread over a larger HAZ. This is the central paradox of HED welding — *low heat input, high penetration*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Laser Welding System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply Unit] --> B[Pumping Source]
    B --> C[Active Laser Medium]
    C --> D[Optical Resonator]
    D --> E[Beam Shaper / Collimator]
    E --> F[Delivery Optics / Fiber]
    F --> G[Focusing Head with Lens]
    G --> H[Workpiece / Joint]
    H --> I[Keyhole Cavity]
    I --> J[Shielding Gas Nozzle]
    J --> H
    H --> K[Fume Extraction]
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- The **Power Supply** energizes the **Pumping Source** (e.g., diode stack).
- The pumping source excites the **Active Medium** (e.g., Yb-doped fiber), creating population inversion.
- Photons bounce between the **Optical Resonator's** two mirrors, multiplying through stimulated emission.
- The amplified beam passes through the **Beam Shaper** to homogenize the mode, then travels through **Delivery Optics** (fiber or mirrors).
- The **Focusing Head** concentrates the beam to the workpiece surface.
- A **Shielding Gas Nozzle** (typically Argon or Helium) flows coaxially to protect the molten pool from oxidation.
- The **Fume Extraction** removes metal vapor and plasma plumes.

## 4.2 Energy-Level Diagram — Stimulated Emission (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Ground["Lower Energy State (E1)"]
        G1[Atom at rest]
    end
    subgraph Excited["Upper Energy State (E2)"]
        E1[Excited atom]
    end
    subgraph Pumping["Pump energy (external)"]
        P1[Flash lamp / electrical / diode]
    end
    Photon1[Incoming photon hν] -->|Stimulated absorption| E1
    P1 -->|Excites atom| E1
    E1 -->|Spontaneous emission| G1
    E1 -->|Stimulated emission: 2 photons out| G1
    Photon2[Identical photon 1] -.-> E1
    Photon3[Identical photon 2] -.-> E1
```

**Key idea:** One incoming photon plus one excited atom yields **two identical, coherent, in-phase photons** leaving the atom — a doubling of optical energy with identical properties.

## 4.3 Keyhole Formation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Stage 1: Beam strikes surface] --> B[Stage 2: Surface melts - conduction mode]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Power density exceeds threshold]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Metal vaporizes - recoil pressure pushes melt down]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Vapor cavity forms - KEYHOLE]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Multiple internal reflections - high absorption]
    F --> G[Stage 7: Deep narrow weld solidifies behind beam]
```

## 4.4 Process Control Parameter Map (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs["Independent Process Variables"]
        P1[Laser Power P]
        P2[Travel Speed v]
        P3[Focal Position z]
        P4[Shielding Gas Flow]
        P5[Beam Polarization]
    end
    subgraph Intermediate["Intermediate Outputs"]
        Q[Power Density q]
        H[Linear Heat Input H]
    end
    subgraph Final["Final Weld Attributes"]
        F1[Penetration Depth d]
        F2[Weld Width w]
        F3[Aspect Ratio AR]
        F4[HAZ Size]
        F5[Residual Stress]
    end
    P1 --> Q
    P1 --> H
    P2 --> H
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    Q --> F1
    Q --> F2
    H --> F4
    H --> F5
    Q --> F3
    P4 --> F1
    P4 --> F2
    P5 --> F1
```

**Reading the map:** Independent inputs (left) determine intermediate quantities (center), which in turn govern final weld attributes (right). This is the standard cause-effect framework expected in KTU 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: Define Laser Beam Welding. State any two advantages of LBW over conventional arc welding processes.**

**Model Answer:**
*Laser Beam Welding (LBW) is a non-contact fusion joining process that uses a highly focused, coherent, monochromatic beam of photons produced by stimulated emission of radiation to melt and fuse the parent material.*

*Advantages (any two):*
1. *Very high power density ($10^6$–$10^7$ W/mm²) enables deep, narrow welds with minimal HAZ.*
2. *Low total heat input reduces distortion and residual stress.*
3. *Non-contact process — no tool wear, no electrode contamination, suitable for reactive/refractory metals.*
4. *Easy automation and access to confined spaces via fiber delivery.*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Q: What is population inversion? Why is it essential for laser action?**

**Model Answer:**
*Population inversion is a non-equilibrium condition in which the number of atoms in an excited (higher) energy state exceeds the number in the ground (lower) state, i.e., $N_2 > N_1$.*

*It is essential because, under normal Boltzmann distribution, absorption dominates over stimulated emission. Only when $N_2 > N_1$ can incoming photons trigger a net amplification (avalanche of stimulated emission), producing the coherent, intense laser beam.*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1 Choice Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **principle of operation of a laser beam welding system**. Describe the role of the optical resonator and the pumping source. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **two modes of laser welding** (conduction and keyhole) with respect to power density, weld profile, and typical applications. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Introduce LASER as Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
2. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Power supply → Pumping source → Active medium → Optical resonator → Beam delivery → Focusing head → Workpiece. (See Section 4.1.)
3. **Role of pumping source (1.5 marks):** Supplies external energy to create **population inversion** ($N_2 > N_1$) in the active medium. Common pumping methods — flash lamps (older Nd:YAG), electrical discharge (CO₂), diode lasers (modern fiber/disk).
4. **Role of optical resonator (1.5 marks):** Two parallel mirrors (one R = 100%, one partially transmissive). Photons emitted along the optical axis are reflected back, stimulating further emission → avalanche → coherent, collimated beam escapes through the partially transmissive mirror.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Feature | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Power density | $< 10^5$ W/mm² | $> 10^6$ W/mm² |
| Penetration | Shallow (1–2 mm typical) | Deep (up to 25 mm in steel) |
| Weld shape | Hemispherical, wide | Wine-glass / nail-head, narrow |
| Aspect ratio | $< 1$ | $5:1$ to $10:1$ |
| Mechanism | Heat diffuses from surface | Vapor cavity, multiple reflections |
| Applications | Thin sheet joining, sealing | Deep-penetration structural welds |
| Filler wire | Sometimes | Usually autogenous |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse conduction mode with poor welds** — it is the *correct* mode for thin foil and hermetic sealing.
> 2. **Always state the threshold value** ($10^5$ vs $10^6$ W/mm²) when distinguishing modes; vague answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Many students miss** explaining *why* keyhole gives high absorption (Fresnel multiple-reflection inside the cavity). This is a must-mention point.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1 Choice Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for **power density at the focal spot** of a laser beam. A 3 kW laser is focused to a spot of 0.5 mm diameter. Compute the power density and identify the welding mode. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare **CO₂, Nd:YAG, and Fiber lasers** as energy sources for industrial welding, listing at least four parameters. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The power density $q$ is defined as laser power per unit area at the focal spot.

$$
q = \frac{P}{A}
$$

For a circular focal spot of diameter $d_f$, area $A = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$. Therefore:

$$
q = \frac{4P}{\pi d_f^2}
$$

**Numerical computation (3 marks):**
Given $P = 3$ kW $= 3000$ W, $d_f = 0.5$ mm.

$$
q = \frac{4 \times 3000}{\pi \times (0.5)^2} = \frac{12000}{0.7854} = 1.528 \times 10^4\ \text{W/mm}^2
$$

**Mode identification (1 mark):**
$q \approx 1.53 \times 10^4$ W/mm² is **less than** the keyhole threshold ($10^6$ W/mm²) → **Conduction mode** welding.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | CO₂ Laser | Nd:YAG Laser | Fiber Laser |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | 10.6 μm (far-IR) | 1.06 μm (near-IR) | 1.06 μm (near-IR) |
| Beam quality (M²) | 2–5 | 5–20 | 1.05–2 |
| Electrical efficiency | 10–15% | 3–5% | 30–40% |
| Delivery method | Mirrors (no fiber) | Fiber (limited) | Flexible fiber |
| Absorptivity on steel | Low (need coatings) | Higher | Higher |
| Maintenance | High (gas, optics) | Moderate | Low (solid-state) |
| Power scaling | Up to 50 kW | Up to 6 kW | Up to 100 kW |
| Cost per kW | Moderate | High | Decreasing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Don't write only "CO₂ vs Nd:YAG"** — KTU specifically tests awareness of modern fiber lasers, which dominate the current industry.
> 2. **Quantify whenever possible**: numerical wavelengths and efficiencies score higher than generic adjectives ("good," "bad").
> 3. **Avoid the common mistake** that all infrared lasers have the same absorptivity. CO₂ is poorly absorbed on bare metal at room temperature — this is a key engineering consideration.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — Laser Beam Welding: Principle of Operation:**
> - ⭐ **LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**.
> - ⭐ **Three Einstein processes**: absorption, spontaneous emission, stimulated emission. Only stimulated emission is the laser-making process.
> - ⭐ **Population inversion** ($N_2 > N_1$) is mandatory — created by **pumping**.
> - ⭐ Four laser components: **Active medium, Pumping source, Optical resonator, Delivery/focusing optics**.
> - ⭐ **Power density threshold**: $q < 10^5$ W/mm² → conduction mode; $q > 10^6$ W/mm² → keyhole mode.
> - ⭐ **Keyhole** = vapor-filled cavity; depth-to-width aspect ratio up to 10:1; absorption enhanced by **multiple internal reflections (Fresnel)**.
> - ⭐ **Power density formula** (must memorize): $q = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d_f^2}$.
> - ⭐ **Linear heat input**: $H = \dfrac{60P}{v}$ (LBW, J/mm).
> - ⭐ **Beam divergence**: $\theta = \dfrac{4\lambda}{\pi D}$ (diffraction-limited) → $d_f = f \cdot \theta$.
> - ⭐ **Photon energy**: $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$. For CO₂ ($\lambda = 10.6\,\mu$m), $E \approx 0.117$ eV.
> - ⭐ **Wavelength order of common industrial lasers**: CO₂ (10.6 μm) > Nd:YAG (1.06 μm) = Fiber (1.06 μm) > Disk (~1 μm) > Diode direct (~0.8–1 μm). Shorter wavelength → better fiber delivery and metal absorption.
> - ⭐ **High Energy Density (HED) processes** are characterized by very low HAZ and high penetration — *not* by high total heat input.
> - ⭐ **Keyhole threshold power** decreases with increasing absorptivity, decreasing focal radius, and increasing thermal diffusivity of the material.
> - ⭐ **Common KTU traps**: confusing power with power density; confusing linear heat input with absorbed energy; confusing population inversion with energy levels themselves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Process Parameters

In the context of **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques**, **process parameters** are the controllable, independent input variables that govern the energy delivery, material transfer, thermal cycle, and ultimately the metallurgical and mechanical quality of a welded joint. They are the "knobs" an engineer adjusts to achieve a sound weld.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Process parameters are the set of operating variables — *welding current, arc voltage, travel speed, electrode feed rate, shielding gas flow rate, polarity, arc length, and heat input* — that quantitatively define a welding operation and directly influence weld bead geometry, fusion zone microstructure, HAZ characteristics, residual stresses, and distortion.

For KTU examinations, the following parameters are considered the **primary triad** of welding:

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Welding Current | $I$ | Ampere (A) |
| Arc Voltage | $V$ | Volt (V) |
| Travel (Welding) Speed | $S$ | mm/min or mm/s |

Secondary (auxiliary) parameters include:
- **Electrode diameter** ($d$) — mm
- **Wire feed rate** (WFR) — m/min
- **Shielding gas flow rate** ($Q$) — L/min
- **Arc length / stick-out** — mm
- **Polarity** (DCEP / DCEN / AC)
- **Heat input** ($HI$) — kJ/mm
- **Pre-heat / interpass temperature** — °C

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Garden Hose Welding Model":**
> Imagine welding is like watering a garden with a hose to melt a snowman.
> - **Current ($I$)** = *How wide you open the tap* → more current = deeper penetration (more water per second).
> - **Voltage ($V$)** = *The water pressure / arc length* → higher voltage = wider, flatter bead (wider spray cone).
> - **Travel speed ($S$)** = *How fast you move your hand* → slow travel = wide bead, fast travel = narrow bead.
> - **Heat input** = *Total water delivered per unit length of garden* = $I \times V / S$ (scaled by efficiency).
>
> Just as a gardener must balance pressure, flow, and speed to water evenly, a welding engineer must balance **I, V, and S** to get a strong, defect-free weld.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **1 kJ/mm** = **1000 J/mm** = heat energy absorbed per millimetre of weld length.
- **Thermal efficiency ($\eta$)** is **dimensionless** and lies between **0.3 and 0.95** depending on the process.
- **Standard units for travel speed in KTU problems:** mm/min (most common) or mm/s.

> [!TIP]
> Always check whether travel speed is given in **mm/min** or **mm/s** before computing heat input — this is a common KTU exam trap worth 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Primary Triad — Logical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Welding Current ($I$)
- **What it is:** The rate of electrical charge flow through the arc.
- **How it controls the weld:** Governs **penetration depth** and **deposition rate**.
- **Physical mechanism:** Higher current → more electrons and ions cross the arc per second → more resistive/arc heating at the workpiece → deeper fusion.
- **Rule of thumb:** Penetration $\propto I^{0.7 \text{ to } 1.0}$ for most arc processes.

### 2.1.2 Arc Voltage ($V$)
- **What it is:** The potential difference maintained between the electrode tip and the workpiece across the arc column.
- **How it controls the weld:** Governs **bead width** and **arc length** (since $V \approx kL_a$ where $L_a$ is arc length and $k \approx$ 1 V per 1 mm of arc for many processes).
- **Why it matters:** Voltage does *not* significantly affect penetration in constant-current power sources — it controls width and profile.

### 2.1.3 Travel Speed ($S$)
- **What it is:** The relative linear velocity between the torch/electrode and the workpiece.
- **How it controls the weld:** Inversely proportional to **heat input per unit length** and **bead cross-sectional area**.
- **Why it matters:** Slow travel = more energy deposited per mm = wider, deeper, more diluted bead; too slow = burn-through.

## 2.2 Heat Input (HI) — The Master Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input is the single most important derived parameter in welding** because it combines all three primary parameters into one quantity that predicts the weld thermal cycle, HAZ width, and microstructural changes.

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} $$

where:
- $HI$ = Heat input (J/mm or kJ/mm)
- $\eta$ = **Arc efficiency / Thermal efficiency** (dimensionless)
- $V$ = Arc voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = Welding current (Amperes)
- $S$ = Travel speed (**must be in mm/s for J/mm**)

### 2.2.1 Unit Conversion Pitfall

$$ HI \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}\right] = \frac{\eta \cdot V\,[\text{V}] \cdot I\,[\text{A}]}{S\,[\text{mm/s}]} $$

If $S$ is given in **mm/min**, multiply the denominator by 60 (or multiply numerator by 60 and convert to kJ/min/mm, then divide by 60).

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S_{mm/min}} \quad \text{[J/mm]} $$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **verified, exam-standard** and have appeared in KTU-style questions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S}$ | Heat input (S in mm/s) | J/mm |
| 2 | $HI = \dfrac{60\,\eta V I}{S}$ | Heat input (S in mm/min) | J/mm |
| 3 | $P_{\text{arc}} = V I$ | Arc power | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Electrical resistance of arc | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 5 | $\text{WFR} \approx k \cdot I$ | Wire feed rate ∝ current (for constant V) | m/min |
| 6 | $D_r = \dfrac{WFR}{S}$ | Deposition rate per unit length | mm²/s or kg/h |
| 7 | $t_{8/5} = f(HI, T_{\text{pre}})$ | Cooling time between 800°C and 500°C | seconds |
| 8 | $L_a \approx k \cdot V$ | Arc length ∝ voltage (constant k ≈ 1 V/mm) | mm |
| 9 | $P_{\text{pen}} \propto I^{n}$ | Penetration depth ∝ current (n ≈ 0.7–1) | — |
| 10 | $\text{Efficiency } \eta_{\text{arc}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{anode/cathode heat}}}{P_{\text{arc total}}}$ | Process thermal efficiency | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Arc Efficiency Values for Common Processes (KTU Standard Data)

| Process | Symbol | Thermal Efficiency $\eta$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Shielded Metal Arc Welding | SMAW | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| Gas Metal Arc Welding | GMAW / MIG | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| Flux-Cored Arc Welding | FCAW | 0.75 – 0.90 |
| Gas Tungsten Arc Welding | GTAW / TIG | 0.50 – 0.70 |
| Submerged Arc Welding | SAW | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Plasma Arc Welding | PAW | 0.50 – 0.65 |
| Laser Beam Welding | LBW | 0.30 – 0.50 |
| Electron Beam Welding | EBW | 0.60 – 0.80 |
| Oxy-Acetylene Welding | OAW | 0.40 – 0.60 |

> [!TIP]
> For **numerical KTU problems**, use $\eta = 0.85$ for SAW, $\eta = 0.8$ for GMAW, $\eta = 0.7$ for GTAW, unless the question specifies otherwise. The KTU examiner **does not award partial credit** for forgetting the efficiency factor — it is a 1-mark deduction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Welding procedure specification (WPS):** All three primary parameters are written on the WPS document as required by **ISO 3834** and **ASME Section IX**.
- **Heat input control** is the primary lever to avoid **HAZ cracking** in C-Mn and low-alloy steels.
- **Process parameter optimization** is the foundation of **robotic welding** and **Industry 4.0** adaptive control.
- In **critical applications** (nuclear, pressure vessels, aerospace), upper-bound $HI$ limits (e.g., $HI \leq 2.0$ kJ/mm) are mandated to ensure $t_{8/5}$ cooling time stays within safe microstructural limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Electrical Power to Heat Input

### Step 1 — Define Electrical Power in the Arc
The arc behaves as an electrical load dissipating power according to:

$$ P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I $$

where $P_{\text{arc}}$ is in **watts** (J/s), $V$ in volts, $I$ in amperes. This is the **total electrical power** delivered to the arc.

### Step 2 — Apply Thermal Efficiency
Not all the arc power reaches the workpiece. A fraction is lost via:
- Radiation from the arc plasma
- Convection in shielding gas
- Spatter ejection
- Conduction into the electrode (for non-consumable electrodes, GTAW)

The **useful heat delivered to the workpiece** is:

$$ P_{\text{useful}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{arc}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I $$

where $\eta$ is the **process thermal efficiency**.

### Step 3 — Convert to Heat Per Unit Length of Weld
As the torch moves at speed $S$ (in mm/s), the heat delivered per millimetre of weld travel is:

$$ HI = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{S} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} $$

This is the **fundamental KTU heat input equation**, with $HI$ in **J/mm**.

### Step 4 — Unit Conversion (mm/min to mm/s)
If $S$ is given in mm/min, we must convert: $S_{\text{mm/s}} = S_{\text{mm/min}} / 60$.

Substituting:

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S_{\text{mm/min}}/60} = \frac{60 \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S_{\text{mm/min}}} $$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — KTU-Style Heat Input Calculation

> **Problem (3 marks):** A SAW operation is performed with $V = 32$ V, $I = 600$ A, and $S = 4$ mm/s. Take $\eta = 0.90$. Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm.

**Step 1 — Identify given data**
$\eta = 0.90$, $V = 32$ V, $I = 600$ A, $S = 4$ mm/s.

**Step 2 — Apply the heat input equation**

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**

$$ HI = \frac{0.90 \times 32 \times 600}{4} $$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation**

$$ 0.90 \times 32 = 28.8 $$
$$ 28.8 \times 600 = 17280 $$

**Step 5 — Division**

$$ HI = \frac{17280}{4} = 4320 \text{ J/mm} $$

**Step 6 — Convert to kJ/mm**

$$ HI = 4.32 \text{ kJ/mm} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer: 4.32 kJ/mm.** This exceeds the typical 2 kJ/mm cap for low-alloy steel — the procedure would be flagged as non-compliant for nuclear-grade work.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Effect of Parameter Change

> **Problem (KTU pattern):** In a GMAW operation, $I = 250$ A, $V = 28$ V, $S = 5$ mm/s, $\eta = 0.80$. If the welder increases current to 300 A and reduces travel speed to 4 mm/s (voltage unchanged), find (a) the new heat input, and (b) the % increase in heat input.

### (a) New Heat Input

$$ HI_{\text{new}} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I_{\text{new}}}{S_{\text{new}}} $$

$$ HI_{\text{new}} = \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 300}{4} $$

Numerator: $0.80 \times 28 = 22.4$; then $22.4 \times 300 = 6720$.

$$ HI_{\text{new}} = \frac{6720}{4} = 1680 \text{ J/mm} = 1.68 \text{ kJ/mm} $$

### (b) Percentage Increase

$$ HI_{\text{old}} = \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250}{5} = \frac{5600}{5} = 1120 \text{ J/mm} $$

$$ \% \text{ increase} = \frac{1680 - 1120}{1120} \times 100 = \frac{560}{1120} \times 100 = 50\% $$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Notice that current **doubled** by 20% but heat input rose by 50% — because the **simultaneous** drop in travel speed compounds the effect. This is a classic KTU question testing whether the student understands that all three parameters are **multiplicative**, not additive.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Change on Weld Output

| Parameter ↑ Increased | Penetration | Bead Width | Heat Input | Distortion Risk |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Current ($I$) | ↑↑ Strong ↑ | ↑ Slight ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| Voltage ($V$) | ↑ Slight ↑ | ↑↑ Strong ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Travel Speed ($S$) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ Strong ↓ | ↓ |
| Efficiency ($\eta$) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Wire Feed Rate | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (coupled with I) | ↑ |

> [!WARNING]
> Increasing voltage increases width but does **not** strongly increase penetration in a CV (constant voltage) power source. Conversely, current is the penetration driver. **Conflating these is a top reason KTU students lose marks on theory questions.**

## 3.5 Heat Input vs. Cooling Time ($t_{8/5}$) — The Metallurgical Link

The cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C controls the HAZ microstructure of steels. A simplified empirical relation is:

$$ t_{8/5} = \frac{(HI)^{2}}{k \cdot \lambda^{2}} \cdot F(\text{plate thickness}) $$

A practical form often used in KTU problems:

$$ t_{8/5} \propto \frac{(HI)^2}{T_{\text{pre}} - T_0 + \text{const}} $$

This shows **why** controlling $HI$ matters — it dictates whether the HAZ forms martensite, bainite, or pearlite, which directly determines **toughness and crack susceptibility**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Process Parameter Interactions

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **causal flow** between process parameters, intermediate physical quantities, and final weld characteristics. This is a safe, KTU-suitable schematic for an arc welding process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source Setting] --> B[Set Current I]
    A --> C[Set Voltage V]
    A --> D[Set Wire Feed Rate WFR]
    B --> E[Arc Ignition and Stability]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Arc Column Energy P arc]
    F --> G[Heat at Workpiece]
    F --> H[Heat Loss to Surroundings]
    G --> I[Heat Input HI]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Thermal Cycle and Cooling Time t8 by 5]
    J --> K[HAZ Microstructure]
    J --> L[Fusion Zone Grain Size]
    I --> M[Weld Bead Geometry]
    M --> N[Penetration Depth]
    M --> O[Bead Width]
    M --> P[Reinforcement Height]
    K --> Q[Mechanical Properties]
    L --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Final Weld Quality and Defect Status]
    styleA fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    styleB fill:#2e5c8a,color:#ffffff
    styleC fill:#2e5c8a,color:#ffffff
    styleD fill:#2e5c8a,color:#ffffff
    styleE fill:#3d7cb5,color:#ffffff
    styleF fill:#5a9bd4,color:#000000
    styleI fill:#e8a33d,color:#000000
    styleJ fill:#e8a33d,color:#000000
    styleK fill:#c44d3a,color:#ffffff
    styleM fill:#7fb069,color:#000000
    styleQ fill:#2c3e50,color:#ffffff
    styleR fill:#8b0000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Power source settings (I, V, WFR) → Arc column → Heat distribution → Heat input → Thermal cycle → Microstructure & Geometry → Properties → Quality. The three "amber" nodes (HI, t8/5) are the **critical control points** a KTU question almost always tests.

## 4.2 Schematic — Heat Input Triad Relationship

The relationship between I, V, S, and HI can be visualized as a 3D control surface. The following Mermaid diagram shows the **directional influence** of each primary parameter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Primary Inputs]
        I[Current I]
        V[Voltage V]
        S[Travel Speed S]
    end
    subgraph DERIVED[Derived Quantity]
        HI[Heat Input HI]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS[Process Outputs]
        PEN[Penetration]
        WID[Bead Width]
        DIST[Distortion]
        HAZ[HAZ Size]
    end
    I -->|+| HI
    V -->|+| HI
    S -->|- divided by| HI
    HI -->|+| PEN
    HI -->|+| WID
    HI -->|+| DIST
    HI -->|+| HAZ
    styleI fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    styleV fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    styleS fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    styleHI fill:#e8a33d,color:#000000
    stylePEN fill:#7fb069,color:#000000
    styleWID fill:#7fb069,color:#000000
    styleDIST fill:#c44d3a,color:#ffffff
    styleHAZ fill:#c44d3a,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation rules:**
- A `+` next to an arrow means *directly proportional*.
- A `-` next to an arrow means *inversely proportional*.
- Heat input is in the **denominator** of travel speed — doubling $S$ halves $HI$.

## 4.3 Schematic — Process Selection by Heat Input Intensity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Welding Process] --> B{Required Heat Input Level}
    B -->|Low HI below 1 kJ per mm| C[LBW or EBW or PAW Keyhole]
    B -->|Medium HI 1 to 3 kJ per mm| D[GTAW or GMAW or SMAW]
    B -->|High HI above 3 kJ per mm| E[SAW or Electroslag]
    C --> F[Thin Sections, Aerospace, Precision]
    D --> G[General Fabrication, Pressure Vessels]
    E --> H[Thick Plates, Shipbuilding, Heavy Structures]
    styleA fill:#2c3e50,color:#ffffff
    styleB fill:#e8a33d,color:#000000
    styleC fill:#7fb069,color:#000000
    styleD fill:#5a9bd4,color:#ffffff
    styleE fill:#c44d3a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Which process would you select for a 2 mm thick stainless-steel sheet to minimize distortion?"* The answer is **GTAW with low HI** or **LBW**, *not* SAW, which is a high-HI process.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define **heat input** in arc welding. State its SI unit and write the governing equation, explaining each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Heat input is the **total useful energy delivered by the arc to the workpiece per unit length of weld travel**. It determines the weld thermal cycle, HAZ width, and microstructural changes.

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \;\; [\text{J/mm}] $$

where $\eta$ is the **thermal efficiency** (dimensionless), $V$ is **arc voltage** (V), $I$ is **welding current** (A), and $S$ is **travel speed** (mm/s). The SI unit is **J/mm**, with practical submultiples being **kJ/mm** (1 kJ/mm = 1000 J/mm).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is the role of **welding current** in determining weld bead geometry? Justify your answer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Welding current ($I$) is the **primary driver of penetration depth** in arc welding. Increasing current:
- Increases the number of electrons and ions crossing the arc per second, raising the heat flux at the workpiece anode/cathode spot.
- **Increases penetration** (depth of fusion into the parent metal) approximately as $I^{0.7 \text{ to } 1.0}$.
- **Slightly increases bead width** and **deposition rate**.
- Has a **direct positive effect on heat input** (since $HI \propto I$).

However, excessive current causes **burn-through, undercut, spatter, and excessive HAZ softening** — making current a parameter that must be balanced with voltage and travel speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **significance of process parameters** in arc welding. Explain how **welding current, arc voltage, and travel speed** influence the weld bead geometry and mechanical properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary arc welding parameters — **welding current ($I$)**, **arc voltage ($V$)**, and **travel speed ($S$)** — are the controllable inputs that govern the **weld bead shape, penetration, HAZ microstructure, distortion, and final mechanical properties**.

| Parameter | Primary Influence | Effect of Increase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Current ($I$) | Penetration depth, deposition rate | Deeper penetration, higher deposition, more distortion |
| Voltage ($V$) | Bead width, arc length, profile | Wider, flatter bead, more dilution |
| Travel speed ($S$) | Heat input per unit length | Lower HI, narrower bead, possible lack of fusion |

These three parameters combine into the **heat input** equation:

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} $$

which determines the **thermal cycle, cooling time $t_{8/5}$, and HAZ microstructure**. A weld with optimum $HI$ yields the desired balance of strength, toughness, and minimal residual stress/distortion. The relationship can be summarized as:

$$\text{Weld Quality} = f(I, V, S, \eta, \text{material}, \text{joint design})$$

**Sketch — Bead Geometry vs. Parameters:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW[Low I, High S]
        L1[Narrow Bead]
        L2[Shallow Penetration]
    end
    subgraph OPT[Optimum]
        O1[Proper Fusion]
        O2[Good Profile]
    end
    subgraph HIGH[High I, Low S]
        H1[Wide Bead]
        H2[Excess Penetration, Spatter]
    end
    LOW --> OPT
    OPT --> HIGH
    styleLOW fill:#c44d3a,color:#ffffff
    styleOPT fill:#7fb069,color:#000000
    styleHIGH fill:#e8a33d,color:#000000
```

**Conclusion:** Selecting optimum $I$, $V$, and $S$ through **WPS qualification testing** is essential to deliver a defect-free, mechanically sound weld. **(7 marks — full marks awarded for: definitions 2, table 2, equation 1, sketch 1, conclusion 1)**

---

**Q. (b)** A **GMAW** operation is carried out with $V = 30$ V, $I = 280$ A, and $S = 6$ mm/s. Take thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.80$. Calculate:
   1. The **heat input** in kJ/mm.
   2. The **arc power** in kW.
   3. If the travel speed is **reduced to 4 mm/s** with the same $I$ and $V$, what is the **percentage increase in heat input**? Comment on the metallurgical consequences. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Heat Input (HI)**

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} = \frac{0.80 \times 30 \times 280}{6} $$

Numerator: $0.80 \times 30 = 24$; then $24 \times 280 = 6720$.

$$ HI = \frac{6720}{6} = 1120 \text{ J/mm} = 1.12 \text{ kJ/mm} $$

**Part 2 — Arc Power**

$$ P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I = 30 \times 280 = 8400 \text{ W} = 8.4 \text{ kW} $$

**Part 3 — New HI and % Increase**

$$ HI_{\text{new}} = \frac{0.80 \times 30 \times 280}{4} = \frac{6720}{4} = 1680 \text{ J/mm} = 1.68 \text{ kJ/mm} $$

$$ \% \text{ increase} = \frac{1680 - 1120}{1120} \times 100 = \frac{560}{1120} \times 100 = 50\% $$

**Metallurgical consequences:**
- A 50% increase in $HI$ leads to a **wider, coarser-grained HAZ** and a **longer $t_{8/5}$ cooling time**.
- For C-Mn and low-alloy steels, this **increases the risk of HAZ softening and toughness degradation**.
- In high-hardenable steels, it may promote **coarse-grain HAZ cracking** and **greater residual stress and distortion**.
- For austenitic stainless steels, it may cause **sensitization** (chromium carbide precipitation at grain boundaries) and **intergranular corrosion susceptibility**.

**(7 marks — Heat input 2, Power 1, % increase 2, metallurgical discussion 2)**

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **concept of thermal efficiency** in arc welding. Discuss how it varies across **SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW, and LBW**, and justify the reasons for the variation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Thermal efficiency ($\eta$) is the **fraction of total arc electrical power that is effectively delivered to the workpiece as useful heat** for melting and fusion. It is a **dimensionless number between 0 and 1**.

$$ \eta = \frac{P_{\text{useful at workpiece}}}{V \cdot I} = \frac{P_{\text{useful at workpiece}}}{P_{\text{arc}}} $$

**Process-wise values (3 marks):**

| Process | $\eta$ | Reason |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| SAW | 0.85 – 0.95 | Molten flux blanket traps heat; minimal spatter; arc fully submerged. |
| SMAW | 0.70 – 0.85 | Some heat lost via coating decomposition, spatter, and fume. |
| GMAW | 0.70 – 0.85 | High current with shielding gas; moderate convection loss. |
| GTAW | 0.50 – 0.70 | Heat conducted away through the **non-consumable tungsten electrode**; arc is "soft"; convection loss in argon. |
| LBW | 0.30 – 0.50 | Most of the beam energy passes through the keyhole plasma and is **lost as radiation** or reflected by the plasma. |

**Justification (2 marks):**
- Processes with **molten flux** (SAW) trap heat → high $\eta$.
- Processes with **non-consumable electrodes** (GTAW) lose heat via electrode cooling → low $\eta$.
- **Keyhole-mode LBW** generates a plasma plume that **reflects** a significant fraction of incident laser energy → lowest $\eta$.

**Engineering implication (1 mark):** $\eta$ is a critical input to WPS, weld procedure qualification, and $t_{8/5}$ calculations. For the same $V$, $I$, and $S$, **two different processes can deliver vastly different heat inputs** to the workpiece.

---

**Q. (b)** A **SAW** weld is being qualified. The test plate is 12 mm thick mild steel, and the parameters are: $V = 30$ V, $I = 500$ A, $S = 5$ mm/s, $\eta = 0.90$. The code permits a **maximum heat input of 2.0 kJ/mm** for this material. Determine:
   1. The **heat input** delivered.
   2. Whether the procedure is **code-compliant**. If not, by what percentage does it exceed the cap?
   3. Suggest **two practical parameter adjustments** (one mechanical, one electrical) to bring the procedure within the cap. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Heat Input Calculation**

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} = \frac{0.90 \times 30 \times 500}{5} $$

Numerator: $0.90 \times 30 = 27$; then $27 \times 500 = 13500$.

$$ HI = \frac{13500}{5} = 2700 \text{ J/mm} = 2.7 \text{ kJ/mm} $$

**Part 2 — Code Compliance Check**

Cap = 2.0 kJ/mm. Delivered = 2.7 kJ/mm.

$$ 2.7 > 2.0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NOT compliant} $$

**Percentage excess:**

$$ \% \text{ excess} = \frac{2.7 - 2.0}{2.0} \times 100 = \frac{0.7}{2.0} \times 100 = 35\% $$

**The procedure exceeds the cap by 35%.** Code compliance is **failed**.

**Part 3 — Two Parameter Adjustments**

**Adjustment 1 — Mechanical (Travel Speed):** Keep $V$ and $I$ the same, increase $S$ to bring $HI$ down to 2.0 kJ/mm:

$$ S_{\text{new}} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{HI_{\text{cap}}} = \frac{0.90 \times 30 \times 500}{2000} = \frac{13500}{2000} = 6.75 \text{ mm/s} $$

So **increase travel speed from 5 to 6.75 mm/s** (a 35% increase). This is a **mechanical adjustment** since it requires recalibrating the welding tractor/robot path speed.

**Adjustment 2 — Electrical (Current):** Keep $V = 30$ V and $S = 5$ mm/s, reduce $I$:

$$ I_{\text{new}} = \frac{HI_{\text{cap}} \cdot S}{\eta \cdot V} = \frac{2000 \times 5}{0.90 \times 30} = \frac{10000}{27} \approx 370.4 \text{ A} $$

So **reduce current from 500 A to about 370 A** (a 26% reduction). This is an **electrical adjustment** since it requires changing the power source setting. However, this **reduces penetration** — joint fit-up and root gap must be rechecked.

**(7 marks — Calculation 2, Compliance check 1, % excess 1, Mechanical fix 1.5, Electrical fix 1.5)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that KTU examiners deduct marks for in Process Parameter questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency $\eta$** in the heat input equation — **−1 to −2 marks** automatic. Even if the problem says "GMAW" and you write $\eta = 1.0$, it is wrong.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $S$** — using mm/min in the denominator without the $\times 60$ conversion. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing penetration and width** — saying "voltage controls penetration" instead of current. **−1 mark** on the theory sub-part.
> 4. **Stating only the heat input** in a 7-mark question without discussing **metallurgical consequences** (HAZ, $t_{8/5}$, distortion). KTU expects "engineering depth" — at least **2 marks** reserved for consequences.
> 5. **Not converting J/mm → kJ/mm** when the question asks for kJ/mm. **−0.5 mark**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before the exam — it covers 80% of the marks for this topic.**

- ✅ **Three primary parameters:** Current ($I$), Voltage ($V$), Travel Speed ($S$) — always written in this order in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $HI = \eta V I / S$ with $S$ in mm/s for $HI$ in J/mm.
- ✅ **Unit conversion:** If $S$ in mm/min, use $HI = 60 \eta V I / S$ (still J/mm).
- ✅ **Current drives penetration; Voltage drives width; Travel speed inversely drives $HI$.**
- ✅ **Efficiency values to memorize:** SAW 0.9, GMAW 0.8, GTAW 0.6, SMAW 0.8, LBW 0.4.
- ✅ **Heat input governs the thermal cycle** — high $HI$ = wider HAZ, coarser grain, longer $t_{8/5}$.
- ✅ **Typical cap:** $HI \leq 2.0$ kJ/mm for low-alloy steels; $HI \leq 1.5$ kJ/mm for quenched & tempered steels.
- ✅ **Penetration $\propto I^{0.7 \text{ to } 1.0}$** — a high-yield one-liner for theory questions.
- ✅ **Arc length $\approx$ Voltage / k**, where $k \approx 1$ V per mm.
- ✅ **Wire feed rate (WFR) is coupled to current** in CV/CV-mode power sources — a 7-mark question trap.
- ✅ **In CW (constant current) mode:** changing wire feed rate changes current; in CC mode: changing current changes WFR.
- ✅ **Polarity effect:** DCEP (electrode positive) gives deeper penetration in GMAW; DCEN gives less penetration but better cleaning of oxide (used in aluminum GTAW).
- ✅ **High heat input risks:** distortion, HAZ softening, sensitization (in SS), and reduced toughness.
- ✅ **Low heat input risks:** lack of fusion, lack of penetration, cold lap, and underfill.
- ✅ **For a 14-mark question**, always: (1) state the formula, (2) substitute carefully, (3) state the answer in the **requested unit**, (4) add a 2-line engineering comment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Applications and Limitations in Metal Joining

In the context of **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques**, every welding and joining process possesses a distinct **envelope of applicability** — defined as the combination of material types, thicknesses, joint configurations, production volumes, and operating environments in which the process performs optimally. Alongside this, every process has **limitations**, which are the inherent technical, economic, or metallurgical boundaries that restrict its use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with Module 1):**
> *Applications refer to the practical engineering scenarios, industries, and material combinations where a specific metal joining process is most suitable and effective. Limitations refer to the technical, economic, metallurgical, and operational constraints that restrict the use of that process.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toolbox" Intuition

Imagine a master carpenter with a toolbox. Each tool — a hammer, screwdriver, drill, chisel — has a **specific job it does best** (its application) and a set of **jobs it cannot do well** (its limitation). You would never use a hammer to tighten a screw, nor a screwdriver to drive a nail.

**Metal joining processes work exactly the same way:**

- **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** is the carpenter's hammer — robust, portable, and great for field work, but slow and rough for precision jobs.
- **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)** is the precision screwdriver — gives beautiful, clean welds on critical aerospace parts, but is too slow and expensive for heavy structural fabrication.
- **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** is the staple gun — perfect for joining thin sheets in high-volume automotive assembly lines, but useless for thick plates or outdoor construction.

The **"Applications and Limitations"** topic teaches the engineer **which tool to pick for which job** — and more importantly, **why**.

## 1.3 The Engineering Significance

The selection of a joining process for any industrial application depends on a multi-criteria decision matrix involving:

- **Base metal** type and thickness
- **Production volume** (one-off, batch, mass)
- **Quality requirements** (structural vs. leak-tight vs. cosmetic)
- **Position** of welding (flat, vertical, overhead)
- **Economic constraints** (capital cost, operating cost, labor skill)
- **Environmental & safety regulations**

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Module 1 outcomes, students are expected to **identify, compare, and justify** the selection of advanced metal joining processes for specific industrial applications, while critically analyzing their technical limitations.

## 1.4 Common Performance Metrics (KTU Board Standards)

The following standard metrics are universally used by KTU examiners to evaluate the suitability of a joining process:

- **Heat Input (HI)** — measured in **kJ/mm**
- **Deposition Rate** — measured in **kg/h**
- **Welding Speed** — measured in **mm/min**
- **Penetration Depth** — measured in **mm**
- **Joint Efficiency (η)** — measured as a **percentage (%, 0-100)**
- **Distortion / Angular Deformation** — measured in **degrees (°)** or **mm**
- **Cost per unit length** — measured in **₹/m** or **$/m**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decision Framework: How Engineers Select a Joining Process

The selection follows a **hierarchical decision logic**:

### Step 1: Material Compatibility Check
- **Ferrous metals** (carbon steel, low-alloy steel, stainless steel) → most processes are compatible
- **Non-ferrous metals** (Al, Mg, Ti, Cu alloys) → require specialized processes (GTAW, plasma, laser, EB)
- **Dissimilar metals** → require processes with precise heat control (diffusion bonding, explosive welding, friction welding, brazing)

### Step 2: Thickness & Geometry Constraints
- **Thin sheets (< 3 mm)** → Resistance Spot Welding (RSW), Laser Beam Welding (LBW), GTAW
- **Medium plates (3-25 mm)** → SMAW, GMAW, FCAW, GTAW
- **Thick plates (> 25 mm)** → SAW (Submerged Arc Welding), Electroslag Welding (ESW), Electron Beam Welding (EBW)
- **Pipes & tubes** → GTAW, orbital GTAW, induction welding

### Step 3: Production Volume Consideration
- **One-off / job shop** → SMAW, GTAW (low capital, high flexibility)
- **Batch production** → GMAW, FCAW (moderate automation)
- **Mass production** → RSW, projection welding, robotic GMAW, laser welding

### Step 4: Quality & Code Requirements
- **Structural (IS 2062, AWS D1.1)** → SMAW, SAW, FCAW
- **Pressure vessels & boilers (ASME Section IX)** → GTAW + GMAW combination
- **Aerospace & nuclear** → EBW, LBW, GTAW (ultra-clean welds)
- **Automotive body-in-white** → RSW, laser welding, MIG/MAG

### Step 5: Economic & Field Constraints
- **Outdoor / field erection** → SMAW (portable, no shielding gas), FCAW (self-shielded)
- **Indoor / factory floor** → SAW, GMAW, robotic systems
- **Underwater** → Underwater wet welding variants

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are tested repeatedly in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) and are part of the high-yield formula bank for Module 1.

| **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Input (HI) | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$ | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (%), $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/s) | Used to determine HAZ width, distortion, and metallurgical changes |
| Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) width | $HAZ \propto \sqrt{HI}$ | Empirical relationship | Predicts grain coarsening zone |
| Deposition Rate (DR) | $DR = \dfrac{I \times 60 \times E}{1000 \times A}$ | $I$ = current (A), $E$ = deposition efficiency, $A$ = cross-section area (mm²) | Determines productivity |
| Welding Speed | $v = \dfrac{S}{t}$ | $S$ = travel distance (mm), $t$ = time (min) | Process throughput metric |
| Joint Efficiency | $\eta_j = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ | $\sigma_{weld}$ = weld strength, $\sigma_{base}$ = base metal strength (MPa) | Quality acceptance criterion |
| Angular Distortion | $\theta = \dfrac{k \cdot q \cdot L}{I_c}$ | $q$ = heat input, $L$ = plate length, $I_c$ = second moment of area, $k$ = material constant | Distortion prediction |
| Arc Force (Plasma jet momentum) | $F_{arc} = k \cdot I^2$ | $I$ = current, $k$ = empirical constant | Plasma/EBW penetration analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, vertical bars and absolute values are not used to avoid markdown parsing conflicts. All values represent **scalar magnitudes** in SI units.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Process Selection

The **fundamental physics** governing process selection is the **mode of heat generation and heat transfer** at the joint interface:

- **Arc-based processes** generate heat via an electric arc (3,000°C – 20,000°C plasma)
- **Resistance processes** generate heat via Joule's heating ($P = I^2R$)
- **High-energy beam processes** (LBW, EBW) focus energy into a tiny spot → keyhole mode welding
- **Solid-state processes** generate heat below the melting point → no fusion zone
- **Thermochemical processes** (oxy-fuel) generate heat via combustion reactions

Each of these mechanisms has a **distinct thermal cycle** that affects:
- The **fusion zone (FZ)** microstructure
- The **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** properties
- The **residual stress** distribution
- The **distortion** magnitude

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Applications and Limitations of Major Metal Joining Processes (Module 1 Focus)

The following comprehensive matrix is the **core analytical content** for this topic, mapping each process to its specific applications, limitations, and KTU-recommended selection criteria.

### 3.1.1 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)

**Applications:**
- Structural steel fabrication (buildings, bridges, towers)
- Field erection of pipelines and storage tanks
- Repair and maintenance welding in heavy industry
- Shipbuilding (sub-assemblies)
- Thickness range: **2 mm to unlimited** (with multi-pass)
- Suitable for **all positions** (1G/2G/3G/4G/5G/6G)
- Works on **dirty, rusty, or primed surfaces** (forgiving of surface contamination)

**Limitations:**
- **Low deposition rate** (1–3 kg/h) → not suited for mass production
- **Frequent electrode changes** → reduces arc time efficiency to ~30%
- **Skill-dependent quality** → high operator variability
- **Not suitable for non-ferrous metals** (Al, Mg, Ti) without special electrodes
- **High hydrogen pickup** → risk of **cold cracking** in high-strength steels
- **Slag inclusion defects** common with poor technique
- **Not suitable for thin sheets** (< 2 mm) due to burn-through
- **Outdoor shielding issues** in heavy wind (gas-shielded alternatives preferred)

### 3.1.2 Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG-MAG)

**Applications:**
- Automotive industry (body panels, chassis)
- Pressure vessel fabrication
- Structural steelwork
- Robotic and automated production lines
- Medium to thick sections (**3 mm to 25 mm**)
- **All ferrous and non-ferrous** metals (with appropriate shielding gas)

**Limitations:**
- **High capital cost** for wire-feed and shielding gas systems
- **Draft-sensitive** — cannot be used in windy outdoor conditions
- **Spatter generation** in CO₂ shielding (poor surface finish)
- **Porosity** susceptibility if shielding gas flow is disturbed
- **Requires clean base metal** (cannot tolerate rust, oil, paint)
- **Limited positional capability** in short-circuit transfer mode
- **Wire feed problems** in tight curved joints

### 3.1.3 Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG)

**Applications:**
- **Aerospace** components (turbine blades, exhaust assemblies, fuel lines)
- **Nuclear** and **chemical** industry (stainless steel, exotic alloys)
- **Pharmaceutical and food-grade** stainless steel fabrication
- **Thin sheets** (0.5 mm to 6 mm) — precision work
- **Root pass** welding of pipes (backing technique)
- **Aluminum, magnesium, titanium, copper, nickel alloys** — primary process
- **Dissimilar metal** joints with precision

**Limitations:**
- **Extremely low deposition rate** (0.5–1.5 kg/h) → expensive
- **High operator skill requirement** (foot pedal + filler manipulation)
- **Low tolerance for surface contamination** → requires pre-cleaning
- **High equipment cost** (HF unit, water cooling, gas system)
- **Not economical for thick sections** (> 6 mm without multiple passes)
- **Sensitive to arc length** variations
- **Limited to short weld lengths** without automation

### 3.1.4 Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)

**Applications:**
- **Heavy fabrication** — pressure vessels, storage tanks, penstocks
- **Shipbuilding** — long straight seams
- **Spiral pipe manufacturing**
- **Thick plate welding** (10 mm to 100+ mm)
- **High deposition rate** applications (up to 15 kg/h)
- **Horizontal/flat positions** only (1G, 1F, 2F)

**Limitations:**
- **Limited to flat and horizontal positions** only (granular flux cannot support molten metal overhead)
- **High thermal input** → coarse grain HAZ, risk of **temper embrittlement**
- **Not suitable for non-ferrous metals** (Al, Ti) — flux incompatibility
- **Flux handling and recovery** required → dust hazard
- **High capital cost** for flux handling systems
- **Not suitable for field work** — flux contamination by moisture

### 3.1.5 Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)

**Applications:**
- **Automotive body-in-white assembly** (3,000–6,000 spots per car)
- **Sheet metal fabrication** (cabinets, enclosures)
- **Appliance manufacturing** (washing machines, refrigerators)
- **Thin galvanized and coated steels** in mass production
- **Cross-wire welding** for mesh and reinforcement

**Limitations:**
- **Limited to thin sheets** (typically 0.5 mm to 3 mm each ply)
- **Lap joint configuration only** (cannot make butt joints directly)
- **Expensive equipment** with high current (5,000–20,000 A) requirement
- **Electrode wear and dressing** maintenance
- **Limited to high-volume production** — not economical for job shop
- **Nugget size verification** required (destructive testing)
- **Expulsion defects** if parameters are off

### 3.1.6 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)

**Applications:**
- **Aerospace** thin-wall tube welding
- **Stainless steel** precision fabrication
- **Orbital welding** of pipes for nuclear and pharmaceutical
- **Keyhole mode** for thick sections (single-pass up to 10 mm)
- **Micro-plasma welding** for foils (< 0.1 mm)

**Limitations:**
- **High equipment cost** and complexity
- **Skill-intensive** setup
- **Limited positional capability** in keyhole mode
- **Not suitable for outdoor** field work
- **Slower than** GMAW for non-critical applications

### 3.1.7 Electron Beam Welding (EBW)

**Applications:**
- **Aerospace** critical components (turbine disks, combustion chambers)
- **Nuclear** reactor components
- **Automotive** gears and transmission parts
- **Dissimilar metal** joints (e.g., copper to steel)
- **Thick section** welding in single pass (up to 200 mm in steel)
- **Refractory metals** (W, Mo, Nb) — only practical process

**Limitations:**
- **Requires vacuum chamber** → very high capital cost
- **Limited workpiece size** (chamber-bounded)
- **Cannot weld ferromagnetic materials** in DC mode (beam deflection)
- **X-ray generation** hazard — requires radiation shielding
- **Extremely precise joint fit-up** required
- **Not portable** at all

### 3.1.8 Laser Beam Welding (LBW)

**Applications:**
- **Automotive** tailored blank welding
- **Battery cell** tab welding (Li-ion, EV batteries)
- **Shipbuilding** T-joint and panel welding
- **Medical device** fabrication (stents, implants)
- **Deep penetration** in single pass (keyhole mode)
- **High-speed** welding of thin sheets

**Limitations:**
- **Very high capital cost** (especially high-power fiber lasers)
- **Safety hazards** (Class 4 laser — eye damage, skin burns)
- **Limited joint fit-up tolerance** → requires precision assembly
- **Plume generation** → fume extraction needed
- **Plasma shielding effect** at high power
- **Reflectivity issues** with Al and Cu (need shorter wavelengths — green or UV)

## 3.2 Comparative Application-Selection Table

The following table provides a **decision matrix** for selecting the correct joining process for a given application scenario — directly aligned with KTU Module 1 examination questions.

| **Industry / Application** | **Primary Process** | **Secondary Process** | **Key Limitation Avoided** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy structural fabrication (thick plate) | SAW | SMAW, FCAW | Avoid low deposition rate of SMAW |
| Thin sheet automotive body | RSW | LBW, GMAW | Avoid distortion of GTAW |
| Aerospace turbine blade | GTAW | EBW, PAW | Avoid slag and contamination of SMAW |
| Pipeline field erection | SMAW | FCAW (self-shielded) | Avoid gas-shielded processes in wind |
| Pressure vessel ASME code | GTAW + SMAW | GMAW | Avoid excessive HAZ of SAW |
| Battery tab welding | LBW (fiber) | RSW | Avoid heat input damage to cells |
| Dissimilar metal joint (Cu-SS) | EBW | Diffusion bonding, Brazing | Avoid cracking in fusion processes |
| Stainless steel pharma vessel | GTAW | PAW (orbital) | Avoid carbon pickup from SMAW |
| Shipbuilding long seams | SAW | FCAW, GMAW | Avoid low productivity of SMAW |
| Underwater repair | SMAW (wet) | Hyperbaric GTAW | Avoid arc instability in air |
| Titanium aerospace structure | GTAW | PAW, EBW | Avoid contamination — needs inert gas |
| Aluminum automotive panels | MIG (pulse) | RSW, Laser | Avoid porosity in SMAW |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Heat Input Formula (KTU Board Favorite)

The **heat input (HI)** is the single most important derived parameter in welding engineering, since it governs the **HAZ size, microstructure, and distortion**. The KTU board expects students to know its derivation.

**Step 1:** Electric arc power is the product of voltage and current.

$$P = V \times I$$

where $P$ is in Watts (W), $V$ in Volts (V), and $I$ in Amperes (A).

**Step 2:** Not all electrical power is transferred to the workpiece. An **arc transfer efficiency** $\eta$ (dimensionless, between 0 and 1) accounts for losses (radiation, spatter, convection).

$$P_{useful} = \eta \times V \times I$$

**Step 3:** Heat input is defined as the useful energy **per unit length of weld**. If the welding speed is $S$ (in mm/s), the length of weld produced per second is $S$ mm.

$$HI = \dfrac{P_{useful}}{S}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$HI = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I}{S}$$

**Step 5:** The standard KTU unit convention is **kJ/mm**, not W·s/mm. Since $1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}$ and $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$, the speed $S$ (mm/s) must be in seconds, and the numerator (W) must be converted to kW:

$$HI = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I}{S \times 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

**Step 6 (Verification using standard values):**
For SMAW with $\eta = 0.80$, $V = 25$ V, $I = 200$ A, $S = 5$ mm/s:

$$HI = \dfrac{0.80 \times 25 \times 200}{5 \times 1000} = \dfrac{4000}{5000} = 0.8 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

This value is consistent with the standard SMAW operating range of **0.5–1.5 kJ/mm**, confirming the derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to compute heat input, **always include the arc efficiency** value. Many students lose 2 marks by omitting it. KTU accepts $\eta_{SMAW} = 0.70$–$0.85$, $\eta_{GTAW} = 0.50$–$0.70$, $\eta_{GMAW} = 0.70$–$0.85$, $\eta_{SAW} = 0.90$–$0.99$.
