# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define "effective throat" of a fillet weld. Why is it important in joint design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **effective throat** of a fillet weld is defined as the **shortest distance from the root of the fillet to the weld face** (or, more precisely, the perpendicular distance from the root to the hypotenuse of the triangular cross-section).

**Importance in joint design:**

1. It represents the **minimum cross-sectional area** of the weld and is therefore the **critical plane** along which shear failure occurs under load.
2. All **strength calculations** (e.g., allowable load on a lap joint) are based on the effective throat dimension, not the leg size.
3. For an equal-leg fillet weld with leg size $s$, the effective throat is mathematically given by:
$$t = 0.707 \cdot s$$
4. It serves as the **design parameter** to be specified on the weld symbol and verified during inspection.

Hence, effective throat is the single most important dimension governing the **load-bearing capacity** of a fillet weld.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between a square butt joint and a single-V butt joint. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Square Butt Joint | Single-V Butt Joint |
|:--------|:------------------|:--------------------|
| **Edge Preparation** | None — plates meet with vertical edges | Each plate is bevelled to form a V (typically 60° included angle) |
| **Plate Thickness Range** | Up to **3 mm** (thin sheets) | **3 to 25 mm** (medium plates) |
| **Penetration** | Partial (limited) | **Full penetration** achievable |
| **Weld Metal Volume** | Minimal | Significant (proportional to plate thickness) |
| **Cost & Skill** | Lowest — no machining, no special skills | Higher — beveling required, multi-pass welding |
| **Application Example** | Tin sheet roofing, HVAC ducting | Pressure vessel shells, boiler drums, structural girders |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **five basic types of welded joints** as classified by AWS. For each, state **one typical industrial application**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **T-joint** is formed between a 10 mm thick flange plate and a 12 mm thick web plate using **fillet welds on both sides**. The joint is subjected to a **tensile load of 120 kN** acting along the flange. The allowable shear stress in the weld metal is **108 MPa**. Determine the **required leg size** of the fillet weld if the weld length on each side is **150 mm**. Use the standard effective-throat formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**AWS classifies welded joints into five basic geometric types:**

1. **Butt Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** Two members lie in the same plane and are joined along their edges.
   - **Sketch description:** Two parallel lines (plates) meeting end-to-end with a weld bead at the seam.
   - **Application:** Longitudinal seams of **pressure vessels** and **pipelines**.

2. **Lap Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** Two members overlap each other and are joined by fillet welds at the edges of the overlap.
   - **Sketch description:** Two parallel lines (plates) with one shifted sideways to overlap the other, welds at top and bottom edges.
   - **Application:** **Sheet metal** fabrications and **bracing members** in truss structures.

3. **T-Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** One member is placed perpendicular to another, forming a "T" shape.
   - **Sketch description:** A vertical plate welded to the top surface of a horizontal plate.
   - **Application:** **Web-to-flange joints** in **I-beams** and **girders**.

4. **Corner Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** Two members meet at an angle (typically 90°) at their edges, forming an "L" shape.
   - **Sketch description:** Two plates meeting at a corner, welded along the outer (or inner) edge.
   - **Application:** **Sheet metal enclosures, box sections, and frames**.

5. **Edge Joint:**
   - **Configuration:** Two parallel (or nearly parallel) members with their edges fused together.
   - **Sketch description:** Two plates side by side, weld bead along the seam where they meet.
   - **Application:** **Sheet metal flooring** and **light-gauge panels**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Applied tensile load: $F = 120$ kN = $1{,}20{,}000$ N
- Allowable shear stress: $\tau_{allow} = 108$ N/mm²
- Weld length on each side: $L = 150$ mm
- Number of fillet welds: 2 (one on each side of the web)
- Effective throat formula: $t = 0.707 \cdot s$

**Step 1: Express the total load-carrying capacity of the welded T-joint.**

The T-joint has **two fillet welds** (one on each side of the web), and each resists shear along its effective throat. The total throat area is:

$$A_{t,total} = 2 \cdot (0.707 \cdot s \cdot L)$$

$$A_{t,total} = 1.414 \cdot s \cdot L$$

**Step 2: Equate the allowable capacity to the applied load.**

$$F = \tau_{allow} \cdot A_{t,total}$$

$$1{,}20{,}000 = 108 \cdot 1.414 \cdot s \cdot 150$$

**Step 3: Solve for $s$.**

$$1{,}20{,}000 = 108 \cdot 212.1 \cdot s$$

$$1{,}20{,}000 = 22{,}906.8 \cdot s$$

$$s = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{22{,}906.8}$$

$$s = 5.24 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Apply a practical factor and adopt a standard size.**

The calculated leg size of 5.24 mm is below the minimum practical fillet leg for 10 mm plate. As a general design rule, **minimum leg size = plate thickness** (capped by AWS D1.1 limits). For a 10 mm plate, the minimum recommended leg is **6 mm**, and we round **up** for safety.

$$\boxed{s_{adopted} = 6 \text{ mm (or 8 mm for comfortable margin)}}$$

**Verification of adopted size with $s = 8$ mm:**

$$A_t = 2 \times 0.707 \times 8 \times 150 = 1696.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\tau_{induced} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1696.8} = 70.7 \text{ MPa} < 108 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Safe)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Describe the **various edge preparations** used in butt welding of plates. State the **thickness range** and **relative weld metal volume** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **single-V butt joint** is to be made between two **20 mm thick** mild steel plates using **GMAW**. The **groove angle is 60°** and the **root gap is 2 mm**. The **weld length is 1.5 m**. Calculate the **approximate volume of weld metal** required (neglecting reinforcement). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Edge Preparation | Geometry Description | Plate Thickness Range | Relative Weld Metal Volume | Remarks |
|:-----------------|:---------------------|:----------------------|:---------------------------|:--------|
| **Square Butt** | No bevel, vertical edges with a small root gap (0–2 mm) | $\leq$ 3 mm | **Very Low** | Lowest cost; cannot achieve full penetration in thick plates |
| **Single-V** | Each plate bevelled at half the included angle (typically 30° from vertical, making 60° total) | 3–25 mm | **Medium** | Good for one-sided access; full penetration achievable |
| **Double-V** | V-groove on both sides of the joint | 15–50 mm (and above) | **Medium** (split between two sides) | Balanced heat input → less distortion; requires flipping |
| **Single-U** | Concave curved profile, root face + land, included angle 8°–15° | 25–100 mm | **Low** (less than V) | Machining required; preferred for thick high-strength steels |
| **Double-U** | U-groove on both sides | > 40 mm | **Low** | Maximum balance and minimum distortion for very thick plates |
| **Single-Bevel** | One plate bevelled, the other square | 6–25 mm (T-joints) | **Low–Medium** | Used in T-joints, corner joints, one-sided access |
| **J-Groove** | One plate J-shaped, other square | 15–50 mm (T-joints) | **Low** | Reduced weld metal in single-sided access situations |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Plate thickness: $t_p = 20$ mm
- Groove angle (included): $\theta = 60°$
- Root gap: $b = 2$ mm
- Weld length: $L = 1.5$ m = $1500$ mm
- Reinforcement neglected.

**Step 1: Recall the volume formula for a single-V groove (without reinforcement):**

$$V = L \cdot t_p \left( b + t_p \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the half-angle:**

$$\frac{\theta}{2} = \frac{60°}{2} = 30°$$

**Step 3: Look up the tangent value:**

$$\tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.5774$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the formula:**

$$V = 1500 \cdot 20 \cdot \left( 2 + 20 \cdot 0.5774 \right)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the inner bracket:**

$$2 + 20 \times 0.5774 = 2 + 11.547 = 13.547 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Multiply:**

$$V = 1500 \times 20 \times 13.547$$

$$V = 30{,}000 \times 13.547$$

$$V = 4{,}06{,}410 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 7: Convert to cubic centimetres (cm³) for readability:**

$$V = 4{,}06{,}410 \text{ mm}^3 \times \frac{1 \text{ cm}^3}{1000 \text{ mm}^3}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 406.4 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 0.406 \text{ litres of weld metal}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **practical quantity** for cost estimation. At a typical GMAW deposition rate of 3 kg/hour and a steel density of 7.85 g/cm³, this represents about 1.5 minutes of actual arc time (excluding setup, inter-pass cleaning, etc.).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **WHERE STUDENTS COMMONLY LOSE MARKS IN JOINT DESIGN QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.707 constant** in fillet weld strength problems. This single omission can cost **3 to 5 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 2. **Confusing leg size and throat.** A common trap: the question gives "leg size 10 mm" and the student directly uses 10 mm as the throat. The throat is 0.707 × 10 = **7.07 mm**. Always re-derive it.
>
> 3. **Unit errors in numerical problems.** Loads in **kN** must be converted to **N** (multiply by 1000) before substituting into N/mm² formulas. Forgetting this gives an answer **1000× too small** and full marks are lost.
>
> 4. **Not including both fillet welds** in a lap joint with double-sided welding. The total length is $2L$, not $L$.
>
> 5. **Drawing mistakes:** Omitting the **root face** or **root gap** in groove sketches, or showing the **weld symbol on the wrong side** of the reference line. Examiners mark the welding symbol interpretation strictly per AWS A2.4.
>
> 6. **No mention of distortion control** in design justifications — for thick plates, examiners expect a comment on **double-sided preparation** to reduce angular distortion.
>
> 7. **In weld length sizing, ignoring the end-return.** A proper answer must add **$2s$** to the calculated length to allow for crater-crack safety. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 8. **In groove angle conversions,** mixing up degrees and radians. For $V = L \cdot t_p (b + t_p \tan(\theta/2))$, the angle is in **degrees** (tangent takes degrees in calculator mode — verify your calculator is in DEG, not RAD).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JOINT DESIGN — QUICK REVISION BULLETS**
>
> - The **five basic AWS joint types** are: **B**utt, **L**ap, **T**, **C**orner, **E**dge. Mnemonic: **"BLTCE"** (like a sandwich).
> - **Effective throat** of an equal-leg fillet weld: $t = 0.707 \cdot s$. Memorize this number — it is **non-negotiable**.
> - **Effective throat** of an unequal-leg fillet weld: $t = \dfrac{s_1 s_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2 + s_2^2}}$.
> - **Shear capacity** of a fillet weld: $F = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}$.
> - **Plate thickness → Edge preparation rule of thumb:**
>   - $\leq 3$ mm → Square butt
>   - 3–25 mm → Single V or single bevel
>   - > 25 mm → Double V, U, or J groove
> - **Weld symbol on drawing:** below reference line = arrow side, above = other side, on line = field weld.
> - **Weld metal volume** for a V-groove butt joint: $V = L \cdot t_p (b + t_p \tan(\theta/2))$.
> - **Heat input** is *not* a direct joint design parameter, but the **groove geometry** controls how the heat distributes — narrower grooves concentrate heat, wider grooves spread it.
> - **Tangent values to memorize for exams:** $\tan 30° = 0.577$, $\tan 45° = 1.000$, $\tan 60° = 1.732$.
> - **Always add end-returns** of length $2s$ in fillet weld length calculations.
> - **Always convert kN to N** in numerical problems (multiply by 1000).
> - **Weld symbol standards:** AWS A2.4 (American), ISO 2553 (European/International). Both are valid in KTU answers.
> - **Full penetration (CJP)** welds develop **100%** of base metal strength; **partial penetration (PJP)** typically 70–80%; **fillet welds** roughly 70%.
> - **Square butt joints** do **NOT** give full penetration in plates above 3 mm — a classic KTU trap question.
> - **Lap joints** introduce **eccentric loading** and **bending moment** in addition to shear; advanced design must account for this combined stress.
> - **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** and **PQR (Procedure Qualification Record)** are the **output documents** of a properly designed joint — remember to mention them in descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Materials and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques**, the term *"Materials and Applications"* refers to the systematic study of the **metallic substrates (base metals)**, **consumables (filler metals, fluxes, shielding gases)**, and the **engineering industries** in which advanced joining processes (such as GMAW, GTAW, PAW, LBW, EBW, FSW, and diffusion bonding) are deployed. The scope encompasses weldability, metallurgical compatibility, mechanical performance of joints, and the alignment of material selection with service requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PEIET523, Module 1):**
> *"Materials and Applications"* explicitly covers:
> 1. Classification of base materials (Ferrous, Non-ferrous, and Advanced alloys).
> 2. Consumable selection (electrodes, filler wires, fluxes, shielding gases).
> 3. Industrial application mapping (Aerospace, Nuclear, Marine, Automotive, Oil & Gas).
> 4. Weldability criteria and joint performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Cooking" Analogy

Imagine a **master chef preparing a gourmet meal**:

| Kitchen Element | Metal-Joining Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| The **main ingredients** (vegetables, meat) | **Base Metals** (steel, Al-alloys, Ti-alloys) | The structure that must be permanently fused |
| The **spices, sauces, and marinades** | **Filler Metals** (electrodes, wires) | Added material that creates the bond |
| The **cooking gas / oven atmosphere** | **Shielding Gas** (Ar, He, CO₂) | Protects the molten pool from contamination |
| The **cleaning agent / seasoning** | **Flux** (borax, fluoride compounds) | Removes oxides & cleans the joint |
| The **recipe / cooking style** | **Joining Process** (GTAW, FSW, LBW…) | Defines how heat and pressure are applied |
| The **final dish** | **Welded Component** (pressure vessel, fuselage) | Must satisfy strength, durability, and service |

Just as a chef would **never use a fish recipe to bake a cake**, an engineer must **never use an aluminium filler to weld a stainless-steel pressure vessel** — material-process compatibility is the heart of this module.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

- **Weldability (ISO 581 / AWS A5.1):** The capacity of a metal to be welded under the imposed fabrication conditions into a specific, suitably designed structure that performs satisfactorily in the intended service.
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):** The region of the base metal whose microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the heat of welding, but without melting.
- **Fusion Zone (FZ):** The region of the joint that has been melted during the welding process.
- **Carbon Equivalent (CE):** A numerical value (commonly **CE$_{IIW}$** or **Pcm**) used to predict weldability of steels.
- **Standard Metric:** Heat input in **kilojoules per millimetre (kJ/mm)** is the universal energy metric in KTU board problems.
- **Physical Constant:** Melting point of pure iron = **1538 °C**; Latent heat of fusion of steel ≈ **272 kJ/kg**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Anchor:** In KTU ESE answers, always define *Weldability* in one crisp line, then immediately back it up with the relevant **Carbon Equivalent formula** — this guarantees 2 of 3 marks in Part-A questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Materials in Metal Joining

The materials ecosystem in advanced metal joining is logically divided into four families:

### 2.1.1 Base Metals (Substrates)

**A. Ferrous Base Metals**
- **Low Carbon Steel (C < 0.25 %):** Excellent weldability; used in structural fabrication.
- **Medium Carbon Steel (0.25 % – 0.60 % C):** Requires preheat (150–300 °C) to avoid HAZ cracking.
- **High Carbon Steel (C > 0.60 %):** Poor weldability; used only with strict preheat/post-heat treatment.
- **Stainless Steel (Austenitic 304, 316; Ferritic 430; Martensitic 410; Duplex 2205):** Austenitic grades dominate; sensitisation (Cr-carbide precipitation) at 450–850 °C is the central metallurgical concern.
- **Tool Steels & HSLA Steels:** Require controlled heat input and PWHT (Post-Weld Heat Treatment).

**B. Non-Ferrous Base Metals**
- **Aluminium & its Alloys (1xxx, 2xxx, 3xxx, 5xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx):** 2xxx and 7xxx are *non-heat-treatable*; 6xxx is heat-treatable. Critical issue: **oxide layer (Al₂O₃, mp ≈ 2050 °C)** demands AC-TIG or AC-MIG for cleaning action.
- **Titanium Alloys (Grade 1–4 CP-Ti, Ti-6Al-4V):** Excellent strength-to-weight ratio; reactive at T > 400 °C, requires inert gas shielding on **front and back** (trailing shield).
- **Nickel-Based Superalloys (Inconel 625, 718, Hastelloy):** Used in turbines, rocket engines; prone to *strain-age cracking*.
- **Magnesium Alloys (AZ31, AZ91):** Lightweight; requires shielding gas + flux; high risk of porosity.
- **Copper & Copper Alloys (C110, C260 Brass, C464 Naval Brass):** High thermal conductivity (≈ 400 W/m·K) demands **high heat input**; susceptible to H₂-induced porosity.

**C. Advanced/Exotic Materials**
- **Intermetallics (NiAl, TiAl):** Used in jet engines; brittle, require diffusion bonding or EBW.
- **Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs, e.g., Al/SiC):** Reinforcement particles react with filler; FSW preferred.
- **Dissimilar Metal Joints (DMJ):** Steel-to-Al, Cu-to-Al, Ti-to-steel — an active KTU 2024 research area.

### 2.1.2 Filler Metals (Consumables)

- **Welding Electrodes:** Coated (SMAW) or solid (GTAW/GMAW).
- **Filler Wires:** ER70S-6 (carbon steel), ER308L (austenitic SS), ER4043 / ER5356 (Al), ERTi-2 (CP-Ti), Inconel 625.
- **Selection Rule:** Filler metal must match base-metal tensile strength **and** corrosion resistance; dilution target typically 20–40 %.

### 2.1.3 Shielding Gases

- **Argon (Ar):** Inert; primary gas for GTAW/GMAW of SS, Al, Ti.
- **Helium (He):** Higher thermal conductivity → deeper penetration; used for high-speed automated welding.
- **CO₂ / Ar-CO₂ mixtures:** Active gas for GMAW of carbon steel.
- **Nitrogen (N₂):** Used for duplex stainless steels to retain austenite-ferrite balance.

### 2.1.4 Fluxes

- **SMAW coatings:** Rutile, basic (low-hydrogen), cellulosic.
- **Brazing fluxes:** Borax-based, fluoride-based for SS and superalloys.
- **Function:** Dissolve oxide films, prevent re-oxidation, stabilise the arc.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Material Selection

The selection of materials (base, filler, gas, flux) follows a deterministic **four-step logic chain**:

1. **Identify the base metal** → fixes the family (ferrous / non-ferrous / exotic).
2. **Calculate Carbon Equivalent (CE)** → determines if **preheat** is needed.
3. **Choose the joining process** → governed by material thickness, joint geometry, and production volume.
4. **Select filler + shielding gas + flux** → optimised for mechanical strength, corrosion resistance, and distortion control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **CE$_{IIW}$** value is the *single most important number* in this module. If CE > 0.45, preheat is **mandatory**; if CE > 0.60, the steel is considered **unweldable by conventional arc welding**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)** | $\text{CE}_{IIW} = \text{C} + \dfrac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \dfrac{\text{Cr+Mo+V}}{5} + \dfrac{\text{Ni+Cu}}{15}$ | % (wt.) | Predicts weldability / cold-cracking of C-steel |
| 2 | **Carbon Equivalent (Pcm)** | $\text{Pcm} = \text{C} + \dfrac{\text{Si}}{30} + \dfrac{\text{Mn+Cu+Cr}}{20} + \dfrac{\text{Ni}}{60} + \dfrac{\text{Mo}}{15} + \dfrac{\text{V}}{10} + 5\text{B}$ | % (wt.) | Used for low-alloy / modern HSLA steels |
| 3 | **Heat Input (Arc Welding)** | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | **kJ/mm** | Energy deposited per unit weld length |
| 4 | **Heat Input (Resistance)** | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | **kJ/mm** | Spot / projection welding |
| 5 | **Cooling Rate (t$_{8/5}$)** | $t_{8/5} = \dfrac{\text{HI}}{2\pi \lambda \left[ \dfrac{1}{500-T_0} - \dfrac{1}{800-T_0} \right]} \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{500-T_0} - \dfrac{1}{800-T_0} \right)$ | s | Time to cool 800 °C → 500 °C (HAZ control) |
| 6 | **Dilution Ratio** | $D = \dfrac{A_{\text{base}}}{A_{\text{base}} + A_{\text{filler}}} \times 100$ | % | Composition control in the fusion zone |
| 7 | **Preheating Temperature (SEW 088)** | $T_{\text{pre}} = 350 \sqrt{\text{CE} - 0.25}$ °C (if CE > 0.25) | °C | Empirical rule for C-steel |
| 8 | **Power Density (LBW)** | $q = \dfrac{P}{A_{\text{spot}}}$ | W/mm² | Distinguishes conduction vs keyhole mode |
| 9 | **Weld Metal Yield (GMAW)** | $\eta = 0.7 - 0.9$ | dimensionless | Arc efficiency factor |
| 10 | **Thermal Conductivity Effect on HI** | $\text{HI}_{\text{req}} \propto k_{\text{th}} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p$ | — | Cu / Al require higher heat input |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/min), $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.7 for GMAW, 0.8 for GTAW, 0.85 for SAW, 0.6 for SMAW), $T_0$ = plate temperature, $\lambda$ = thermal diffusivity, $P$ = laser power, $A_{\text{spot}}$ = spot area, $k_{\text{th}}$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Material Selection Logic

- **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V fuselage):** LP-DED or EBW in vacuum; Ar-purged trailing shield; ERTi-6Al-4V filler; CE is meaningless here — *oxygen equivalent* (O$_{eq}$ < 0.18 %) is the constraint.
- **Offshore Oil & Gas (X65/X70 linepipe, CE = 0.35–0.42):** Cellulosic SMAW (E6010) + low-hydrogen stick (E7018) for root & cap; preheat 100–150 °C; PWHT to relieve residual stress.
- **Cryogenic LNG tanks (9 % Ni steel, ASTM A553):** Matching Inconel-type fillers; stringent t$_{8/5}$ control to retain toughness at -196 °C.
- **Automotive Body-in-White (DP600 DP980 AHSS):** Resistance spot welding with **electrode-force profiling**; FSW for battery enclosures.
- **Nuclear (SS 304L / 316L):** Low-Co ferrite number (FN = 3–10) to avoid *sensitisation*; use ER308L with controlled heat input ≤ 1.5 kJ/mm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input in Arc Welding

The **arc heat input** is the single most-used formula in KTU board exams. It is derived from basic energy balance.

**Step 1 — Define energy delivered per unit time (Power).**
The arc is a sustained electrical discharge, so the electrical power consumed is:

$$P = V \cdot I \quad [\text{W or J/s}]$$

where $V$ is the arc voltage (volts) and $I$ is the welding current (amperes).

**Step 2 — Account for arc efficiency.**
Not all electrical energy is transferred to the workpiece; some is lost as convection, radiation, and spatter. A dimensionless arc efficiency factor $\eta$ is introduced:

$$P_{\text{effective}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

**Step 3 — Convert to energy per unit weld length.**
The torch moves along the joint at a travel speed $S$ (in mm/min). The length traversed per second is $S / 60$ mm/s. Hence, the energy per millimetre of weld is:

$$\text{HI} = \dfrac{P_{\text{effective}} \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

The factor 60 converts minutes → seconds; the factor 1000 converts W·s = J to kJ.

**Step 4 — Combine the above relations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HI} &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\
&= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 16.67} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical KTU formula. KTU boards typically use the first form: $\text{HI} = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S \cdot 1000}$ with $S$ in mm/min.

**Numerical Verification (KTU-Style Problem):**
A GTAW operation uses $V = 22$ V, $I = 180$ A, $S = 120$ mm/min, and $\eta = 0.80$ (GTAW). Compute HI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HI} &= \dfrac{0.80 \times 22 \times 180 \times 60}{120 \times 1000} \\
&= \dfrac{190080}{120000} \\
&= 1.584 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This value (~1.6 kJ/mm) is typical for thin stainless-steel sheets; values > 2.5 kJ/mm indicate excessive heat and risk of sensitisation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carbon Equivalent (IIW) Threshold Logic

The IIW formula is **empirical**, not derived from first principles — it is constructed by weighting alloying elements based on their measured contribution to **hardenability** and **cold-cracking susceptibility** of the HAZ.

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant hardeners.**
Carbon (C) is by far the strongest hardener; manganese (Mn) is second. Both are weighted heavily.

**Step 2 — Weighting scheme.**
The International Institute of Welding assigned weighting factors inversely proportional to the typical content of each element in a carbon steel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbon (C)} &: \text{weight} = 1.00 \\
\text{Manganese (Mn)} &: \text{weight} = 1/6 \approx 0.166 \\
\text{Chromium, Mo, V (Cr+Mo+V)} &: \text{weight} = 1/5 = 0.20 \\
\text{Nickel, Copper (Ni+Cu)} &: \text{weight} = 1/15 \approx 0.067
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the empirical equation.**

$$
\text{CE}_{IIW} = \text{C} + \dfrac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \dfrac{\text{Cr} + \text{Mo} + \text{V}}{5} + \dfrac{\text{Ni} + \text{Cu}}{15}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretive thresholds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CE} &\le 0.25 : \text{No preheat required.} \\
0.25 < \text{CE} &\le 0.45 : \text{Mild preheat recommended (100–150 °C).} \\
0.45 < \text{CE} &\le 0.60 : \text{Strong preheat + PWHT mandatory.} \\
\text{CE} &> 0.60 : \text{Unweldable by conventional arc processes.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Verification (Sample KTU Question):**
A low-alloy steel has C = 0.18 %, Mn = 1.20 %, Cr = 0.50 %, Mo = 0.20 %, V = 0.05 %, Ni = 0.30 %, Cu = 0.25 %. Find CE and the preheat temperature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CE}_{IIW} &= 0.18 + \dfrac{1.20}{6} + \dfrac{0.50 + 0.20 + 0.05}{5} + \dfrac{0.30 + 0.25}{15} \\
&= 0.18 + 0.20 + \dfrac{0.75}{5} + \dfrac{0.55}{15} \\
&= 0.18 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.0367 \\
&= 0.5667 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since CE = 0.567 > 0.45 → **strong preheat + PWHT mandatory.** Using $T_{\text{pre}} = 350\sqrt{\text{CE} - 0.25}$:

$$
T_{\text{pre}} = 350 \times \sqrt{0.5667 - 0.25} = 350 \times \sqrt{0.3167} = 350 \times 0.5627 \approx 197 \text{ °C}
$$

Adopt **200 °C** as the practical preheat.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Time $t_{8/5}$ (HAZ Cycle)

The $t_{8/5}$ parameter is derived from **Rykalin's 2-D heat-flow model** for a moving point source on a thick plate.

**Step 1 — Governing equation (Rykalin's thick-plate solution):**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T - T_0} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{peak}} - T_0} + \dfrac{2\pi \lambda \rho c_p \, (y - vt)}{\text{HI} \cdot S}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to HAZ position $y$ and integrate.**
Solving for the time difference between $T = 800$ °C and $T = 500$ °C (the *inter-critical* range where transformation products are most sensitive to cooling rate) gives the standard form:

$$
t_{8/5} = \dfrac{\text{HI}}{2\pi \lambda} \left[ \dfrac{1}{500 - T_0} - \dfrac{1}{800 - T_0} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
For a given steel, **higher heat input → longer t$_{8/5}$ → coarser HAZ microstructure → lower toughness**. KTU boards love this trade-off question.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Process ↔ Material ↔ Application

| Base Material | Best Joining Process | Typical Filler | Shielding Gas | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low-Carbon Steel | GMAW / SAW | ER70S / F7A0-EL12 | Ar + 15–20 % CO₂ | Structural, bridges |
| HSLA / X70 Pipeline | SMAW + GMAW | E7018 / ER80S-G | CO₂ / Ar-CO₂ | Oil & gas pipelines |
| Austenitic SS 304/316 | GTAW (TIG) | ER308L / ER316L | Pure Ar | Chemical, food, pharma |
| Duplex SS 2205 | GTAW (TIG) | ER2209 | Ar + 2–3 % N₂ | Offshore, desalination |
| Aluminium 5083/6061 | GMAW (MIG) / FSW | ER5356 / ER4043 | Pure Ar | Marine hulls, auto panels |
| Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | GTAW / EBW | ERTi-6Al-4V | Pure Ar (trailing shield) | Aerospace fuselage, medical implants |
| Inconel 718 | GTAW / LBW | Inconel 718 | Pure Ar | Gas turbine blades, rocket nozzles |
| Cu-Ni 90/10 | GTAW | ERCuNi | Pure Ar | Ship condensers, seawater piping |
| Magnesium AZ31 | GMAW | AZ92A | Argon + Helium | Aerospace brackets |
| Cast Iron | SMAW (Ni-Fe) | ENiFe-CI | — | Engine blocks, repairs |
| Tool Steel (H13) | LBW / PAW | Maraging filler | Vacuum / inert | Die & mould repair |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Material Selection Workflow

The following Mermaid block represents the **complete decision flow** an engineer follows when selecting materials for a metal-joining application. This is a board-favourite flowchart style.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Service Requirement] --> B[Identify Base Metal Family]
    B --> C{Ferrous?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute CE IIW]
    C -- No --> E[Non-Ferrous]
    D --> F{CE greater than 0.45?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Preheat 200C plus PWHT]
    F -- No --> H{CE greater than 0.25?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Preheat 100 to 150C]
    H -- No --> J[No Preheat Needed]
    G --> K[Choose Joining Process]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    E --> L{Alloy Type}
    L -- Al/Mg --> M[GTAW AC or FSW]
    L -- Ti/Ni --> N[GTAW with Inert Trailing Shield]
    L -- Cu/Brass --> O[High Heat Input GTAW]
    M --> K
    N --> K
    O --> K
    K --> P[Select Filler Wire]
    P --> Q[Select Shielding Gas and Flux]
    Q --> R[Compute Heat Input kJ per mm]
    R --> S[Verify t8 5 within allowable range]
    S --> T[Approved WPS and PQR]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Materials Taxonomy in Metal Joining

The following Mermaid block maps the **hierarchical classification** of all materials relevant to advanced metal joining.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Materials in Metal Joining]
    root --> bm[Base Metals]

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Application ↔ Process Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each *Industry → Process → Material → Requirement* chain is a closed system. A mismatch in any link (e.g., using GMAW for aerospace Ti) produces an **unsafe joint**. KTU board questions often ask: *"Justify the process selection for a given service requirement"* — the diagrams above are your ready-made answer scaffold.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define *Weldability* and list four metallurgical factors that influence it.**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Weldability is the capacity of a metal to be welded under the imposed fabrication conditions into a specific, suitably designed structure that performs satisfactorily in the intended service. **Definition per ISO 581 / AWS A5.1.**

The four metallurgical factors influencing weldability are:

1. **Chemical composition** — especially carbon and alloy content (CE value).
2. **Hardenability** — the tendency to form martensite in the HAZ.
3. **Thermal conductivity** — high-k metals (Al, Cu) demand higher heat input.
4. **Coefficient of thermal expansion** — high CTE causes distortion and residual stress.

---

### **Q2. What is the significance of the *Carbon Equivalent (CE$_{IIW}$)* value? State the formula.**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The Carbon Equivalent (CE) is a single numerical value that quantifies the combined effect of carbon and alloying elements on the **weldability** of a steel. It is used to predict the risk of **cold cracking (Hydrogen-Induced Cracking)** in the HAZ and to determine the **preheating temperature** required before welding.

**Formula:**

$$\text{CE}_{IIW} = \text{C} + \dfrac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \dfrac{\text{Cr} + \text{Mo} + \text{V}}{5} + \dfrac{\text{Ni} + \text{Cu}}{15} \quad (\% \text{ wt.})$$

- If CE ≤ 0.25 → no preheat needed.
- If CE > 0.45 → mandatory preheat + PWHT.
- If CE > 0.60 → steel is **unweldable** by conventional arc processes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 1 Internal Choice

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — ANSWER ANY ONE FULL QUESTION]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify the various *shielding gases* used in advanced arc-welding processes. Discuss the role of Argon, Helium, CO₂, and Nitrogen in welding stainless steel, aluminium, and carbon steel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory uses GMAW to weld 6 mm thick low-carbon-steel plates. The welding parameters are: voltage = 24 V, current = 200 A, travel speed = 300 mm/min, and arc efficiency = 0.85. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** and comment on its adequacy for this thickness. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Classification of Shielding Gases:**

1. **Inert Gases** — *chemically inactive*, do not react with the weld pool.
   - **Argon (Ar):** Most widely used; ionization potential = 15.7 eV; produces a stable, constricted arc with deep penetration. Used for **GTAW/GMAW of Al, SS, Ti, Mg**.
   - **Helium (He):** Higher thermal conductivity (6× that of Ar); produces a *broader, hotter* arc, deeper penetration at high travel speeds. Used for **high-speed automated welding** of Al, Cu, and SS. Cost is high.

2. **Active (Oxidising) Gases** — *chemically reactive*; used primarily for carbon steel.
   - **Pure CO₂:** Strongly oxidising; produces deep penetration in GMAW of C-steel; risk of porosity due to CO dissociation.
   - **Ar + 15–25 % CO₂:** Compromise — stable arc + good wetting; standard for GMAW of C-steel and HSLA.

3. **Reducing Gases / Specialty:**
   - **Nitrogen (N₂):** Added to Ar (2–3 %) when welding **duplex stainless steel** to retain the austenite-ferrite balance and prevent loss of N from the weld pool.
   - **Ar + H₂ (2–5 %):** Used for austenitic SS; increases welding speed, improves bead profile.

**Role-Wise Mapping:**

| Base Material | Preferred Gas | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Austenitic SS 304/316 | Pure Ar or Ar + 2 % N₂ | Inert protection; avoids sensitisation |
| Duplex SS 2205 | Ar + 2–3 % N₂ | Retains FN and pitting resistance |
| Aluminium 5083/6061 | Pure Ar (TIG) or Ar/He (MIG) | Prevents oxide entrapment |
| Carbon Steel (GMAW) | Ar + 15–20 % CO₂ | Good wetting, stable arc, low spatter |
| Carbon Steel (FCAW) | Pure CO₂ | Deep penetration, low cost |

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:** $V = 24$ V, $I = 200$ A, $S = 300$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.85$.

**Heat Input Formula:**

$$\text{HI} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$$

**Substitute and compute step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HI} &= \dfrac{0.85 \times 24 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000} \\
&= \dfrac{244800}{300000} \\
&= 0.816 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Adequacy:**
For a 6 mm thick low-carbon-steel plate, the **typical recommended heat input range is 0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm**. The computed value of **0.816 kJ/mm** lies at the **lower end of the recommended range** — this is **adequate** to achieve full penetration without excessive HAZ coarsening. If the travel speed were increased to 500 mm/min, the heat input would drop to ~0.49 kJ/mm → *risk of lack of penetration* and unfused root. If the speed were reduced to 150 mm/min, HI would rise to ~1.63 kJ/mm → *excess HAZ width, grain growth, and distortion*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Heat Input Calculation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 60** when $S$ is given in mm/min — this is the *single most common mistake* and costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong arc efficiency** — KTU expects $\eta = 0.7$ (GMAW, short-circuit transfer), $0.8$ (GMAW spray), $0.85$ (SAW), $0.6$ (SMAW). State the value explicitly in the answer.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Always quote the final answer in **kJ/mm**, not kJ/m or J/mm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of base metals** used in advanced metal joining, with examples. Discuss the **weldability of aluminium alloys** in detail, including the metallurgical challenges and process solutions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A low-alloy steel has the following composition: C = 0.20 %, Mn = 1.10 %, Cr = 0.40 %, Mo = 0.15 %, V = 0.04 %, Ni = 0.20 %, Cu = 0.18 %. Determine the **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)**, recommend a **preheating temperature**, and explain the consequences of welding without preheat. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Classification of Base Metals:**

**1. Ferrous Metals** (Iron-based):
- Carbon Steels: Low (C < 0.25 %), Medium (0.25–0.60 %), High (> 0.60 %).
- Stainless Steels: Austenitic (304, 316), Ferritic (430), Martensitic (410), Duplex (2205), Precipitation-Hardening (17-4 PH).
- Cast Irons: Grey, White, Ductile (Nodular), Malleable.
- Tool Steels & HSLA: H13, D2, AISI 4140.

**2. Non-Ferrous Metals**:
- Aluminium & Alloys (1xxx–7xxx series).
- Titanium (CP grades, α, α-β, β alloys).
- Magnesium (AZ, AM, AS series).
- Copper & Alloys (Pure Cu, Brass, Bronze, Cu-Ni).
- Nickel & Superalloys (Inconel 625, 718; Hastelloy C-276; Monel 400).

**3. Advanced / Exotic**:
- Intermetallics (NiAl, TiAl, FeAl).
- Metal-Matrix Composites (Al/SiC, Al/Al₂O₃).
- Dissimilar Metal Joints (DMJ): Steel-Al, Cu-Al, Ti-Steel, SS-Inconel.

**Weldability of Aluminium Alloys — Detailed Discussion:**

**Why Aluminium is Difficult to Weld:**
1. **Tenacious Oxide Layer (Al₂O₃):** Melting point ≈ **2050 °C** (vs. Al = 660 °C). The oxide must be removed *before* the parent metal melts. Solution: **AC-GTAW** with the *cleaning action* of the half-cycle, or **flux-cored wires**, or **mechanical brushing** with stainless-steel brush.
2. **High Thermal Conductivity (≈ 235 W/m·K):** Heat dissipates rapidly; demands **higher heat input per unit length** than steel.
3. **High Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (≈ 23 × 10⁻⁶ /K):** Causes significant **distortion and residual stress**; large solidification shrinkage (~6 %).
4. **Hydrogen Porosity:** Aluminium has **infinite solid-solubility for hydrogen in the liquid** and **near-zero in the solid** → severe gas porosity. Solution: **100 % Ar shielding** (moisture-free), **AC cleaning**, **degassing of filler wire**.
5. **Hot Cracking (Solidification Cracking):** Wide freezing range of alloys (especially 2xxx, 6xxx) makes them susceptible. Solution: **Use 5xxx filler (ER5356) for 5xxx/6xxx base**, or **use ER4043 (Al-Si) for crack-prone 2xxx**.
6. **Loss of Strength in HAZ:** Heat-treatable alloys (6061, 2014) lose strength in the HAZ due to overaging. Solution: **Use post-weld artificial aging**.

**Process Solutions:**
- **GTAW (TIG):** Preferred for thin sheets; AC mode provides oxide cleaning.
- **GMAW (MIG):** Preferred for thicker plates; spool guns to avoid wire feed issues.
- **FSW (Friction Stir Welding):** **Solid-state process** — *no fusion, no porosity, no hot cracking*; ideal for 2xxx and 7xxx alloys.
- **LBW (Laser Beam Welding):** Low HAZ, deep penetration, high speed; used in battery enclosures.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Given:** C = 0.20 %, Mn = 1.10 %, Cr = 0.40 %, Mo = 0.15 %, V = 0.04 %, Ni = 0.20 %, Cu = 0.18 %.

**Step 1 — Calculate CE$_{IIW}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CE}_{IIW} &= \text{C} + \dfrac{\text{Mn}}{6} + \dfrac{\text{Cr} + \text{Mo} + \text{V}}{5} + \dfrac{\text{Ni} + \text{Cu}}{15} \\
&= 0.20 + \dfrac{1.10}{6} + \dfrac{0.40 + 0.15 + 0.04}{5} + \dfrac{0.20 + 0.18}{15} \\
&= 0.20 + 0.1833 + \dfrac{0.59}{5} + \dfrac{0.38}{15} \\
&= 0.20 + 0.1833 + 0.1180 + 0.0253 \\
&= 0.5266 \; \% \quad \approx \; 0.53
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Recommend Preheat Temperature:**
Since CE = 0.53 > 0.45 → **Strong preheat + PWHT mandatory**.

Using the SEW 088 empirical rule:

$$
T_{\text{pre}} = 350 \times \sqrt{\text{CE} - 0.25} = 350 \times \sqrt{0.53 - 0.25} = 350 \times \sqrt{0.28} = 350 \times 0.5292 \approx 185 \text{ °C}
$$

**Recommendation:** Preheat to **200 °C** (next practical round value) and hold for a *minimum of 1 hour per 25 mm thickness*. Apply **PWHT (Stress-Relief at 600–650 °C for 1 hour)** to temper the martensite that may form in the HAZ.

**Step 3 — Consequences of Welding Without Preheat:**
1. **Hydrogen-Induced Cold Cracking (HICC):** Martensite forms in the HAZ; absorbed H₂ migrates to the triaxial-stress zone at the weld toe → cracking appears *hours or days* after welding.
2. **Hard, brittle HAZ:** Hardness can exceed **400 HV** (untempered martensite) → catastrophic brittle failure in service.
3. **Residual Stress and Distortion:** High thermal gradients produce large residual tensile stresses → risk of **stress-corrosion cracking**.
4. **Loss of Toughness:** Charpy impact values at room temperature may drop by **50–70 %**.
5. **Reduced Fatigue Life:** Crack initiation sites at the HAZ limit the component life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Carbon Equivalent Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing IIW and Pcm formulas** — the KTU 2024 board expects IIW unless explicitly stated otherwise. Mixing them in the same problem loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit error in preheat temperature** — $T_{\text{pre}}$ is in **°C**, not K. Adding 273 is a common trap.
> 3. **Not justifying the preheat recommendation** — never give a preheat number without stating the corresponding CE threshold it falls under (≤ 0.25 / 0.25–0.45 / > 0.45).
> 4. **Forgetting PWHT** — if CE > 0.45, both **preheat AND PWHT** are mandatory; omitting PWHT is a 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Materials and Applications" (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Weldability** is the capacity to be welded into a structure that performs in service (ISO 581).
- ☐ **Carbon Equivalent (IIW)** formula is the most-asked equation in KTU 2024 — *memorise the exact weighting factors 1, 1/6, 1/5, 1/15*.
- ☐ **CE thresholds:** ≤ 0.25 (no preheat), 0.25–0.45 (mild preheat), 0.45–0.60 (preheat + PWHT), > 0.60 (unweldable).
- ☐ **Heat Input formula** $\text{HI} = \eta V I / (S \times 1000)$ with $S$ in mm/min → result in kJ/mm. **Do not forget the factor of 60.**
- ☐ **Arc efficiency values:** SMAW = 0.6, GMAW = 0.7–0.8, GTAW = 0.8, SAW = 0.85, PAW = 0.6, LBW/EBW = ~1.0.
- ☐ **Aluminium weldability issues:** Al₂O₃ oxide layer (mp = 2050 °C), high thermal conductivity, hydrogen porosity, hot cracking in 2xxx/6xxx.
- ☐ **AC-GTAW is mandatory for Al and Mg** to provide the *cathodic cleaning* action that breaks the oxide layer.
- ☐ **Duplex stainless steels** require **Ar + 2–3 % N₂** shielding to retain the austenite-ferrite balance (FN ≈ 30–70).
- ☐ **t$_{8/5}$** is the HAZ cooling time from 800 °C → 500 °C; *longer t$_{8/5}$ → coarser HAZ → lower toughness* (always mention this trade-off).
- ☐ **Stainless-steel sensitisation** occurs in the **450–850 °C** range → control heat input to ≤ 1.5 kJ/mm for austenitic SS, or use **low-carbon grades (304L, 316L)** or **stabilised grades (321, 347)**.
- ☐ **Titanium** requires **trailing-shield Ar protection** because Ti is reactive above 400 °C; EBW is preferred in vacuum for aerospace.
- ☐ **Preheating rule (SEW 088):** $T_{\text{pre}} = 350 \sqrt{\text{CE} - 0.25}$ °C — applicable only if CE > 0.25.
- ☐ **Dissimilar Metal Joints (DMJ)** are an active KTU 2024 research area — use *transitional fillers* (e.g., Inconel 82 for SS-to-carbon-steel).
- ☐ **For Part-A questions (3 marks):** Always include the *formula + one engineering interpretation* in your answer.
- ☐ **For Part-B questions (14 marks):** Draw a *labelled diagram* and present a *numerical solution with units* for the 7-mark sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma Arc Welding - Theory and Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Plasma Arc Welding - Theory and Principles # Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) — Theory and Principles > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under **Module 2** of *PEIET523 – Advanced Metal Joining Techniques*, covering advanced arc welding processes. PAW is consistently a **high-yield topic** in KTU University Examinations and is frequently asked as a 14-mark descriptive question combined with TIG/GMAW comparison. --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** is defined as an **arc welding process** in which the welding arc is **mechanically constricted** by passing it through a **water-cooled copper nozzle (orifice)**. This constriction raises the **current density**, **arc temperature**, and **arc velocity** dramatically, converting the conventional tungsten arc into a highly concentrated, high-velocity jet of **ionized plasma gas** (the *fourth state of matter*). The term **plasma** refers to a **partially or fully ionized gas** containing a roughly equal number of **positive ions** and **free electrons**, along with neutral atoms. In PAW, an inert gas (typically **Argon**, sometimes mixed with **Hydrogen** or **Helium**) is ionized by the arc to form this plasma. The process was originally developed in the **1950s** at the **Battelle Memorial Institute, USA** and commercialized by **Linde** for aerospace and nuclear applications. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a garden hose. When you put your thumb partially over the opening, the water that comes out shoots faster, harder, and goes much farther. **The arc is the water; the copper nozzle is your thumb.** By squeezing the arc through a tiny hole, you turn a soft, lazy tungsten arc (TIG) into a laser-like, needle-thin jet that can pierce metal like a sword. The same physics explains why a hurricane is more destructive when it passes through a narrow valley — **constriction increases velocity and energy concentration**. ### 1.3 Why "Plasma"? — The Four States of Matter Recap > [!TIP] > **The Four States of Matter:** > 1. **Solid** (ice) — atoms tightly packed > 2. **Liquid** (water) — atoms loosely bonded > 3. **Gas** (steam) — atoms free, neutral > 4. **Plasma** (ionized gas) — atoms stripped of electrons → free ions + electrons > > A welding arc is **not** a normal flame — it is a **plasma column** at temperatures of **16,000 °C to 30,000 °C** (much hotter than the surface of the sun, ~5,500 °C). | Parameter | Standard TIG Arc | Constricted PAW Arc | |---|---|---| | Arc Temperature | ~10,000 °C | **~20,000–30,000 °C** | | Current Density | Low–Medium | **Very High** (5–10×) | | Arc Velocity | Low | **High (supersonic jet)** | | Energy Concentration | Diffuse | **Highly Focused** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle of PAW The entire science of PAW rests on one central idea: **constriction of the electric arc**. This is achieved by forcing the arc to pass through a **water-cooled copper nozzle** with a small orifice (typically **0.8 mm to 3.2 mm** diameter). Three simultaneous effects occur: 1. **Thermal Constriction** — The cold copper walls of the nozzle cool the outer layer of the arc, squeezing the conductive hot core into a smaller cross-section. This forces **more current to flow through a smaller area**, raising current density. 2. **Mechanical Constriction** — The physical orifice physically limits the cross-sectional area of the arc, concentrating the energy. 3. **Magnetic (Electromagnetic) Constriction** — As current density increases, the **self-induced magnetic field** of the arc (the *pinch effect* or *Maecker effect*) further compresses the arc column, raising its temperature and velocity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Combined Effect:** All three constrictions act together → the arc becomes a **narrow, high-velocity, ultra-hot plasma jet** that exits the nozzle at near-sonic or supersonic speeds. ## 2.2 The Plasma Jet — Step-by-Step Physics The plasma jet is formed in **five sequential stages** (high-yield for KTU 14-mark answers): - **Step 1 — Gas Injection:** Shielding gas (Argon) is fed through the **annular space** between the tungsten electrode and the nozzle. - **Step 2 — Arc Ignition:** A low-current pilot arc is struck between the **tungsten electrode (cathode, –)** and the **copper nozzle (anode, +)** inside the torch. This is the *non-transferred* pilot arc. - **Step 3 — Arc Transfer:** The pilot arc ionizes the gas, lowering its electrical resistance. The main arc now transfers from the **electrode** to the **workpiece** (which becomes the anode) — this is the *transferred* arc mode. - **Step 4 — Constriction:** As the arc passes through the nozzle orifice, the three constriction effects (thermal, mechanical, magnetic) compress and accelerate the plasma. - **Step 5 — Melting & Keyhole Formation:** The superheated plasma jet strikes the workpiece, melting the metal. At very high currents (>100 A), the **keyhole mode** is established — the plasma jet **pierces completely through** the workpiece, forming a vapour-filled hole that moves with the torch. ## 2.3 Two Distinct Operating Modes of PAW | Mode | Pilot Arc Circuit | Main Arc Circuit | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Non-Transferred (NTPAW)** | Electrode ↔ Nozzle | Electrode ↔ Nozzle | Plasma cutting, plasma spraying, melting of non-conductive materials | | **Transferred (TPAW)** | Electrode ↔ Nozzle (pilot) | Electrode ↔ Workpiece (main) | Welding, cladding, surfacing | > [!NOTE] > In **Transferred PAW (TPAW)**, the workpiece itself becomes part of the electrical circuit. This is the mode used for **welding**. The non-transferred mode is used for **plasma cutting and spraying** (covered separately). ## 2.4 Electrode Polarity (DCEN vs. DCEP) | Polarity | Electrode | Workpiece | Heat Distribution | Application in PAW | |---|---|---|---|---| | **DCEN (Straight)** | – (Cathode) | + (Anode) | ~33% electrode, ~67% workpiece | **Standard for PAW** — maximum heat in workpiece | | **DCEP (Reverse)** | + (Anode) | – (Cathode) | ~67% electrode, ~33% workpiece | Rarely used — destroys tungsten electrode | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite:** *"Why is DCEN (Electrode Negative) used in PAW?"* — Because **~2/3 of the total heat is generated at the anode (workpiece)**, ensuring deep penetration. DCEP would overheat and rapidly consume the tungsten electrode. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > The following formulas are **must-know** for KTU numerical and 14-mark questions on PAW. | # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | **Net Heat Input** per unit length (KTU-standard) | kJ/mm | | 2 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | **Current Density** = current / arc cross-section | A/mm² | | 3 | $v_{plasma} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{arc}}{T_{ambient}}}$ | Plasma jet velocity is proportional to √(T_ratio) — explains supersonic jet | m/s | | 4 | $F_{pinch} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, I^2}{4\pi^2 r^2}$ | **Magnetic Pinch (Maecker) Force** per unit length | N/m | | 5 | $T_{plasma} \approx 16{,}000 + 28{,}000 \cdot x_{H_2}$ | Plasma temperature (empirical) where $x_{H_2}$ = H₂ mole fraction in Ar | °C | | 6 | $d_{eff} = d_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{TIG}}{I_{PAW}}}$ | Effective arc diameter comparison vs. TIG | mm | **Where:** - $H$ = Heat input - $V$ = Arc voltage (Volts) - $I$ = Welding current (Amperes) - $S$ = Travel speed (mm/min) - $J$ = Current density - $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the arc - $\mu_0$ = Permeability of free space = **$4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A** - $r$ = Radius of the constricted arc column - $T_{plasma}$ = Plasma temperature - $d_{eff}$ = Effective arc diameter - $d_o$ = Nozzle orifice diameter ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility PAW is the **gold standard** for: - **Aerospace** — welding thin-walled stainless steel and titanium honeycomb structures (e.g., rocket fuel tanks, aircraft engine casings) - **Nuclear** — welding of zirconium alloy fuel cladding tubes - **Chemical & Cryogenic** — welding of austenitic stainless steel storage tanks - **Electronics** — micro-welding of thin foils and instrument components - **Automotive (specialty)** — welding of pre-coated steel sheets without coating damage --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Constriction Increases Current Density Let us mathematically prove that **forcing an arc through a smaller cross-section increases its current density** — the central principle of PAW. ### Given - A conventional TIG arc of cross-sectional area $A_1$ carrying current $I$ has current density: $$J_1 = \dfrac{I}{A_1}$$ - When the same arc is forced through a nozzle of cross-sectional area $A_2$ (where $A_2 < A_1$), the new current density is: $$J_2 = \dfrac{I}{A_2}$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Since } A_2 < A_1, \quad \text{and } I \text{ is conserved (Kirchhoff's Current Law):} \\ J_2 = \dfrac{I}{A_2} \quad \text{and} \quad J_1 = \dfrac{I}{A_1} \\[8pt] \text{Dividing } J_2 \text{ by } J_1: \\ \dfrac{J_2}{J_1} = \dfrac{\dfrac{I}{A_2}}{\dfrac{I}{A_1}} = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} \\[8pt] \text{Since } A_2 < A_1, \text{ the ratio } \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} > 1 \\[8pt] \therefore J_2 > J_1 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Constriction *always* increases current density by the inverse ratio of the cross-sectional areas. **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A TIG arc with diameter $d_1 = 6$ mm is constricted by a PAW nozzle to $d_2 = 3$ mm at the same current $I = 200$ A. $$ \begin{aligned} A_1 = \dfrac{\pi d_1^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (6)^2}{4} = 28.27 \text{ mm}^2 \\[6pt] A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d_2^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (3)^2}{4} = 7.07 \text{ mm}^2 \\[6pt] J_1 = \dfrac{200}{28.27} = 7.07 \text{ A/mm}^2 \\[6pt] J_2 = \dfrac{200}{7.07} = 28.28 \text{ A/mm}^2 \\[6pt] \dfrac{J_2}{J_1} = \dfrac{28.28}{7.07} = 4.00 \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Result:** Current density increases **4 times** when the arc diameter is halved. This is why PAW produces such deep, narrow penetrations compared to TIG. ## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Input Calculation (Standard KTU Numerical) ### Problem Calculate the **net heat input per unit length** for a PAW operation with: - Arc voltage $V = 28$ V - Welding current $I = 250$ A - Travel speed $S = 200$ mm/min - Thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.60$ (typical for PAW) ### Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Write the heat input formula (KTU standard):} \\ H_{gross} = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]} \\[8pt] \text{Step 2: Substitute the values:} \\ H_{gross} = \dfrac{28 \times 250 \times 60}{200 \times 1000} \\[8pt] \text{Step 3: Numerator evaluation:} \\ 28 \times 250 = 7{,}000 \\ 7{,}000 \times 60 = 420{,}000 \\[8pt] \text{Step 4: Denominator evaluation:} \\ 200 \times 1000 = 200{,}000 \\[8pt] \text{Step 5: Final division:} \\ H_{gross} = \dfrac{420{,}000}{200{,}000} = 2.1 \text{ kJ/mm} \\[8pt] \text{Step 6: Net heat input (accounting for efficiency):} \\ H_{net} = \eta \times H_{gross} = 0.60 \times 2.1 = 1.26 \text{ kJ/mm} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern:** Always show the **factor of 60** (seconds to minutes) and **factor of 1000** (J to kJ) explicitly. Examiners award partial marks for correct unit conversion even if arithmetic slips. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PAW vs. Conventional TIG (Frequently Asked Comparison) | Parameter | TIG (GTAW) | Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) | KTU Exam Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Arc Constriction | None — free-burning | Yes — copper nozzle orifice | ★★★ Core difference | | Arc Temperature | ~10,000 °C | ~20,000–30,000 °C | ★★★ | | Current Density | Low | Very High (4–10× TIG) | ★★★ | | Arc Stability | Good | Excellent (less wander) | ★★ | | Penetration | Limited; needs groove | Deep; keyhole possible | ★★★ | | Travel Speed | Slower | Faster (2–3×) | ★★ | | Equipment Cost | Moderate | High (extra pilot arc, water cooling) | ★★ | | Thickness Range (single pass) | Up to 6 mm | Up to 12–15 mm | ★★★ | | Suitable Materials | Most metals | Stainless, Ti, Ni, Zr alloys | ★★ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — PAW System Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a complete PAW system, mapping the interaction of all sub-systems from power input to the welded joint. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph A["A. POWER SUPPLY SUBSYSTEM"] A1["DC Power Source
Constant Current Type
OCV 80 to 120 V"] A2["Pilot Arc Circuit
High Frequency Starter
2 to 5 A typical"] A3["Main Arc Circuit
Welding Current
5 to 500 A"] end subgraph B["B. PLASMA TORCH ASSEMBLY"] B1["Upper Body
Gas and Water Inlets"] B2["Tungsten Electrode
2 percent Thoriated W
Cathode DCEN"] B3["Ceramic Gas Diffuser
Swirl Flow Pattern"] B4["Copper Constricting Nozzle
Water Cooled
Orifice 0.8 to 3.2 mm"] B5["Outer Shield Nozzle
Secondary Gas Outlet"] end subgraph C["C. GAS CONTROL SUBSYSTEM"] C1["Plasma Gas Cylinder
Argon or Ar plus 5 to 15 percent H2"] C2["Shielding Gas Cylinder
Argon or Ar plus He"] C3["Flowmeter Bank
Rotameters"] C4["Solenoid Valves
and Pressure Regulators"] end subgraph D["D. COOLING SUBSYSTEM"] D1["Recirculating Water Chiller
Flow 8 to 12 L per min"] D2["Torch Body Cooling Jacket"] D3["Nozzle Cooling Jacket"] end subgraph E["E. WORKPIECE AND MOTION"] E1["Workpiece as Anode
Connected to Positive Lead"] E2["Welding Table
with Clamping Fixtures"] E3["Traverse Mechanism
Constant Speed Carriage"] end subgraph F["F. WELD ZONE OUTPUT"] F1["Constricted Plasma Jet
Temperature 20000 to 30000 C"] F2["Molten Weld Pool
and Keyhole Formation"] F3["Solidified Weld Bead"] end A1 --> A2 A1 --> A3 A2 --> B2 A3 --> B2 C1 --> C4 C2 --> C4 C4 --> B3 C4 --> B5 D1 --> B2 D1 --> B4 B2 --> B4 B4 --> F1 B5 --> F1 F1 --> E1 E1 --> F2 E2 --> E1 E3 --> E1 F2 --> F3 A3 -. returns through .-> E1 ``` ## 4.2 Internal Schematic of a PAW Torch (Cross-Sectional Logic) > [!TIP] > The following flowchart models the **internal coaxial flow** of a typical PAW torch, showing how the three gas/water streams interact. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Core["INNERMOST CHANNEL"] direction TB IC1["Plasma Gas Inlet
Top of Torch"] IC2["Swirl Chamber
Tangential Entry"] IC3["Tungsten Electrode Tip
Protrudes into Nozzle"] IC4["Constricting Nozzle Orifice
Diameter 0.8 to 3.2 mm"] end subgraph Mid["INTERMEDIATE CHANNEL"] MC1["Cooling Water Inlet"] MC2["Annular Jacket around Electrode"] MC3["Cooling Water Outlet"] end subgraph Out["OUTERMOST CHANNEL"] OC1["Shielding Gas Inlet"] OC2["Outer Nozzle Annulus"] OC3["Shielding Gas Envelopes
the Plasma Jet"] end IC1 --> IC2 --> IC3 --> IC4 MC1 --> MC2 --> MC3 OC1 --> OC2 --> OC3 IC4 -. constricted plasma jet exits .-> Out OC3 -. protects weld pool .-> Out ``` ## 4.3 Process Logic Flow — Steps in PAW Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR S1([Step 1: Gas Purging]) --> S2([Step 2: Cooling Water ON]) S2 --> S3([Step 3: Pilot Arc Ignition
Electrode to Nozzle]) S3 --> S4([Step 4: Main Arc Transfer
Electrode to Workpiece]) S4 --> S5([Step 5: Plasma Jet
Strikes Workpiece]) S5 --> S6([Step 6: Keyhole Forms
at High Current]) S6 --> S7([Step 7: Torch Traverse
Along the Joint]) S7 --> S8([Step 8: Pool Solidifies
Weld Completed]) ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Plasma Arc Welding. State any two advantages of PAW over TIG welding.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) is an arc welding process in which the arc is **constricted by a water-cooled copper nozzle** to produce a **high-velocity, high-temperature jet of ionized gas (plasma)**, which is used to fuse the base metals. > **Advantages over TIG (1.5 marks — any two):** > 1. **Higher arc temperature** (~20,000–30,000 °C vs. ~10,000 °C) → deeper penetration and faster travel speeds. > 2. **Higher current density** due to arc constriction → narrower heat-affected zone (HAZ) and reduced distortion. > 3. **Greater arc stability** — the constricted arc is less prone to wandering under magnetic fields, giving more consistent weld quality. > 4. **Single-pass welding of thicker sections** (up to 12–15 mm) without edge preparation. --- ### **Question 2: What is the role of the pilot arc in PAW? Why is DCEN polarity preferred in PAW?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Role of Pilot Arc (1.5 marks):** The pilot arc is a **low-current auxiliary arc** struck between the **tungsten electrode** and the **copper nozzle** inside the torch. Its function is to **ionize the plasma gas** and create a **conductive path** so that the main, high-current arc can transfer reliably to the workpiece. Without the pilot arc, the main arc would be unstable and difficult to initiate at a distance. > **DCEN Polarity (1.5 marks):** In **Direct Current Electrode Negative (DCEN)** polarity, the **tungsten electrode is the cathode (–)** and the **workpiece is the anode (+)**. Approximately **two-thirds (~67%) of the total arc heat** is generated at the anode. This places **maximum heat in the workpiece**, ensuring deep penetration and efficient melting, while keeping the tungsten electrode cooler (only ~33% heat), preventing rapid electrode consumption. Hence DCEN is the standard polarity in PAW. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Rule:** Module 2 long-answer questions carry internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below for practice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive PAW Theory > **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **principle of Plasma Arc Welding**. Discuss the **three types of constriction** that occur in the PAW nozzle. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Differentiate between **transferred** and **non-transferred** modes of PAW. List **four applications** of PAW in modern industry. **(7 marks)** > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Principle of PAW (3 marks):** PAW is based on the principle of **arc constriction**. A free-burning tungsten arc (as in TIG) is forced to pass through a **water-cooled copper nozzle** with a small orifice. This constriction converts the conventional arc into a **high-velocity, high-temperature, narrow plasma jet** that delivers concentrated heat to the workpiece, producing deep penetration and a narrow weld bead. **Schematic (2 marks):** Refer to the **Mermaid torch architecture** in SECTION 4 of these notes. The student should draw: - Tungsten electrode (cathode, –) - Copper constricting nozzle (water-cooled) - Plasma gas inlet (Ar/Ar+H₂) - Shielding gas outlet - Workpiece (anode, +) - Pilot arc circuit and main arc circuit **Three Types of Constriction (2 marks):** 1. **Thermal Constriction:** The cold copper walls of the nozzle continuously cool the outer shell of the arc, forcing the conductive hot core to occupy a smaller cross-section. 2. **Mechanical Constriction:** The physical orifice of the nozzle restricts the arc's cross-sectional area directly. 3. **Magnetic (Electromagnetic) Constriction:** The high current density in the constricted arc generates a strong self-induced magnetic field (the *pinch effect* or *Maecker effect*) that further compresses the arc column. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Feature | Transferred PAW (TPAW) | Non-Transferred PAW (NTPAW) | |---|---|---| | Pilot arc | Electrode ↔ Nozzle | Electrode ↔ Nozzle (also main arc) | | Main arc | Electrode ↔ **Workpiece** | Electrode ↔ Nozzle | | Workpiece in circuit? | **Yes** | No | | Current capacity | High (up to 500 A) | Limited (~100 A) | | Heat in workpiece | High (~67%) | Low (arc stays in torch) | | Typical use | **Welding, cladding** | **Plasma cutting, spraying** | **Four Applications (3 marks — 0.75 each):** 1. **Aerospace industry:** Welding of thin-walled stainless steel rocket motor casings and titanium aircraft structures. 2. **Nuclear industry:** Welding of zirconium-alloy fuel cladding tubes for nuclear reactors. 3. **Chemical process industry:** Fabrication of austenitic stainless steel tanks, vessels, and cryogenic containers. 4. **Electronics & instrumentation:** Precision micro-welding of thin foils, thermocouple wires, and instrument diaphragms. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Question > **(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a PAW torch** with a labelled diagram. What is the function of the **constricting nozzle**? **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A PAW operation is performed at **V = 30 V**, **I = 300 A**, and **S = 150 mm/min**. Calculate the **gross and net heat input** per unit length, given thermal efficiency $\eta = 0.65$. Comment on the suitability of this heat input for welding a **6 mm thick stainless-steel** plate. **(7 marks)** > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Construction of PAW Torch (3.5 marks):** A PAW torch consists of the following coaxial components (from inside to outside): 1. **Tungsten electrode** (typically 2% thoriated) — connected to the **negative terminal** of the DC power source. 2. **Ceramic gas diffuser** — imparts a **swirl flow** to the incoming plasma gas for arc stability. 3. **Copper constricting nozzle** — water-cooled, contains a precisely machined orifice (0.8–3.2 mm); the **anode of the pilot arc circuit**. 4. **Outer shielding nozzle** — delivers secondary shielding gas (Ar) to protect the weld pool from atmospheric contamination. 5. **Cooling water jackets** — surround the electrode and nozzle; water flow rate of 8–12 L/min prevents overheating. 6. **Upper body** — houses gas and water inlets, electrical connections, and the high-frequency pilot arc starter. **Working (2 marks):** Plasma gas (Ar) is fed through the diffuser → the pilot arc (electrode-to-nozzle) is struck using high-frequency → the main arc transfers to the workpiece (transferred mode) → the arc is **constricted** as it passes through the nozzle orifice → the resulting **plasma jet** melts the workpiece, forming the weld pool and (at high currents) the **keyhole**. **Function of the Constricting Nozzle (1.5 marks):** The constricting nozzle serves **three critical functions**: - **Mechanical constriction** of the arc cross-section. - **Thermal constriction** via water cooling of the nozzle walls. - **Magnetic constriction** enhancement due to the increased current density. The net effect is to dramatically raise the arc's **temperature, velocity, and energy concentration**, enabling deep penetration, narrow HAZ, and high welding speeds. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Gross Heat Input (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} H_{gross} &= \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{30 \times 300 \times 60}{150 \times 1000} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{540{,}000}{150{,}000} \\[6pt] &= 3.60 \text{ kJ/mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Net Heat Input (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} H_{net} &= \eta \times H_{gross} \\[6pt] &= 0.65 \times 3.60 \\[6pt] &= 2.34 \text{ kJ/mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Comment on Suitability (3.5 marks):** A net heat input of **2.34 kJ/mm** is **very high** for welding a 6 mm thick stainless-steel plate. The typical recommended heat input range for austenitic stainless steel (e.g., SS 304) of this thickness is **0.8 to 1.5 kJ/mm** to avoid: - **Excessive grain growth** in the HAZ. - **Sensitization** and intergranular corrosion due to chromium carbide precipitation (at temperatures 450–850 °C held too long). - **Distortion and warping** of the thin plate. - **Hot cracking** in the weld metal. **Recommendation:** The current should be **reduced** to approximately **180–200 A** (keeping V and S similar), or the travel speed should be **increased** to 250–300 mm/min, to bring the net heat input into the acceptable range of ~1.0–1.5 kJ/mm. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks in PAW Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 60 and 1000 in heat input calculations** — Examiners deduct 1 mark for wrong unit conversion. Always write the full formula and show the conversion explicitly. > 2. **Confusing transferred and non-transferred PAW** — Many students write "PAW is always transferred." Wrong — the non-transferred mode is used for cutting/spraying. > 3. **Missing one of the three constrictions** — Examiners expect **all three** (thermal, mechanical, magnetic) to be named and briefly explained. Omitting the *magnetic (pinch) effect* costs 0.5–1 mark. > 4. **Wrong polarity justification** — "DCEN is used because the electrode is negative" is incomplete. You must state that **~67% heat goes to the workpiece** (anode), which gives deep penetration and protects the tungsten. > 5. **Confusing pilot arc with main arc** — The pilot arc is **low current (2–5 A)** and exists **between electrode and nozzle**. The main arc is **high current** and exists **between electrode and workpiece** (in transferred mode). > 6. **Calling plasma a "flame" or "gas"** — Plasma is the **fourth state of matter** (ionized gas). Calling it just "hot gas" loses a key conceptual mark. > 7. **Skipping the schematic in 14-mark questions** — Even a labelled line drawing of the torch is worth 1.5–2 marks. Always include a diagram. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plasma Arc Welding** - ✅ **PAW = Arc Constriction Welding.** The arc is squeezed through a water-cooled copper nozzle orifice (0.8–3.2 mm). - ✅ **Three constrictions:** **Thermal, Mechanical, Magnetic (Pinch effect / Maecker effect).** All three must be named. - ✅ **Plasma temperature:** **~20,000–30,000 °C** (vs. ~10,000 °C in TIG). Plasma is the **fourth state of matter** — ionized gas. - ✅ **Two modes:** **Transferred PAW (TPAW)** for welding; **Non-transferred PAW (NTPAW)** for cutting/spraying. - ✅ **Pilot arc:** Low current (2–5 A) between **electrode (–) and nozzle (+)** — ionizes the gas and creates the conductive path. - ✅ **Main arc:** High current between **electrode (–) and workpiece (+)** in transferred mode. - ✅ **Polarity:** **DCEN (Electrode Negative)** is standard — puts ~67% of heat into the workpiece. - ✅ **Nozzle material:** **Water-cooled copper** (high thermal conductivity, resists erosion). - ✅ **Electrode material:** **2% Thoriated Tungsten (W-ThO₂)** — higher electron emission than pure W. - ✅ **Plasma gas:** **Argon (Ar)**, sometimes with **5–15% Hydrogen (H₂)** to raise temperature; **Helium (He)** for special applications. - ✅ **Shielding gas:** **Argon** — secondary gas protecting the weld pool from atmospheric contamination. - ✅ **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ kJ/mm (gross); multiply by $\eta$ for net. - ✅ **Typical current density in PAW:** 5–10× that of a normal TIG arc. - ✅ **Keyhole mode:** Formed at high currents (>100 A) — the plasma jet pierces completely through the workpiece. - ✅ **Equipment:** Constant-current DC power source, water chiller, gas flowmeters, pilot arc starter, traverse mechanism. - ✅ **Applications:** Aerospace, nuclear, chemical, cryogenic, electronics, and instrumentation welding. - ✅ **Advantages:** Higher temperature, deeper penetration, faster travel, narrow HAZ, single-pass thick section welding, superior arc stability. - ✅ **Disadvantages:** Higher equipment cost, more complex torch, requires skilled operator, longer setup time. - ✅ **History:** Developed in the **1950s at Battelle Memorial Institute, USA**; commercialized by **Linde**. - ✅ **Key comparisons to memorize for KTU:** PAW vs. TIG (temperature, penetration, cost, HAZ width, current density). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Exam Tip:** PAW is almost always asked either as a **standalone 14-mark question** or **paired with TIG comparison**. Memorize the **comparison table** in Section 3.3 and the **three constrictions** — these alone can fetch 4–5 marks in any related question. ---

topics.title: Transferred Arc and Non-Transferred Arc Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transferred Arc and Non-Transferred Arc Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plasma — The Fourth State of Matter

**Plasma** is an electrically conductive, partially ionized gas consisting of approximately equal numbers of positively charged ions and free electrons, along with neutral atoms/molecules. It is generated by imparting sufficient energy to a gas to strip electrons from the outer shells of its atoms.

The energy states of matter follow the sequence:
$$\text{Solid} \xrightarrow{+\Delta H_{fus}} \text{Liquid} \xrightarrow{+\Delta H_{vap}} \text{Gas} \xrightarrow{+\Delta H_{ion}} \text{Plasma}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Plasma is a quasi-neutral, ionized gaseous state of matter, formed when sufficient thermal/electrical energy dissociates gas molecules into ions and free electrons, exhibiting collective behaviour under electromagnetic fields.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lightning in a Nozzle"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine squeezing a garden hose — water shoots out faster and more forcefully. Now, instead of water, force **ionized gas (plasma)** through a tiny, water-cooled copper **constricting nozzle**. Just as the hose's pressure increased, the gas temperature spikes to **20,000 K – 30,000 K** (roughly 4–5 times hotter than the surface of the sun's photosphere at ~5,800 K). This is precisely the principle of **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** and **Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC)**.

## 1.3 Formal Definitions — Transferred vs Non-Transferred Arc

| Term | Board Definition (KTU 2024) |
|------|---------------------------|
| **Plasma Arc** | A constricted, high-energy, directed arc formed by forcing an ionized gas column through a water-cooled, narrow-bore copper nozzle. |
| **Transferred Arc** | An arc established **between the tungsten electrode (cathode) and the workpiece (anode)**. The workpiece becomes a part of the electrical circuit. |
| **Non-Transferred Arc** | An arc established **between the tungsten electrode (cathode) and the constricting nozzle (anode)**. The workpiece is *not* part of the primary arc circuit. |

## 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters

- **Transferred Arc** → Delivers **very high energy density** to the workpiece; used for **welding thick sections, cutting, and surfacing**.
- **Non-Transferred Arc** → Lower energy density at workpiece, but independent of workpiece electrical continuity; used for **spray coating, spheroidizing, and welding of non-conductive materials**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Energy Density Reference Values:**
> - TIG/GTAW arc: **~1,500 W/mm²**
> - **Plasma (Transferred) Arc: ~10,000 – 100,000 W/mm²**
> - Laser beam: ~10⁶ W/mm² (for context)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Arc Constriction

The constricting nozzle performs three simultaneous functions:

1. **Thermal Pinch Effect** — Cooling the outer plasma column by the water-cooled copper wall forces current to flow through a narrower, hotter central core.
2. **Magnetic Pinch (Lorentz) Effect** — Parallel current paths in the column repel each other (parallel currents → repulsion), compressing the column further.
3. **Mechanical Compression** — The physical reduction in cross-section increases the gas velocity and pressure.

Combined, these produce a **high-velocity, high-energy, narrow-diameter jet** of plasma.

## 2.2 The Two-Arc System in PAW (Pilot Arc + Transferred Arc)

A modern PAW torch operates with a **dual-arc** mechanism:

| Arc Stage | Electrode → Terminal | Purpose |
|-----------|---------------------|---------|
| **Pilot Arc (Non-Transferred)** | W-electrode → Nozzle | Initiates and maintains ionization in the gas column, independent of the workpiece approach. |
| **Transferred Arc (Main)** | W-electrode → Workpiece | Carries the bulk of welding current; delivers heat to the joint. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a pilot arc?**
> A transferred arc cannot self-ignite if the electrode and workpiece are far apart (open circuit). The pilot arc provides an **ionized conductive path** so that the main arc can be established at a greater stand-off distance (typically 3–12 mm vs 2–4 mm in TIG).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical & Theory)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | **Heat Input** (η = arc efficiency, V = arc voltage, I = current, S = travel speed) | J/mm or kJ/mm |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{A_{eff}}$ | **Power density** (A_eff = effective arc cross-section) | W/mm² |
| 3 | $v_{gas} = \dfrac{Q_{gas}}{A_{nozzle}}$ | Plasma gas exit velocity (Q = volumetric flow, A = nozzle throat area) | m/s |
| 4 | $T_{plasma} \approx T_0 \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | Adiabatic plasma temperature (P₁/P₀ = pressure ratio, γ = ratio of specific heats) | K |
| 5 | $R_{constriction} = \dfrac{D_{electrode}}{D_{nozzle}}$ | Constriction ratio — controls arc stiffness | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Delta H = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed by workpiece | J |
| 7 | $E_{ion} = 13.6 \text{ eV (H)}, \; 24.6 \text{ eV (He)}$ | **First ionization energy of H and He** (for reference) | eV |
| 8 | $v_{arc\,blow} \propto \dfrac{I}{D_{nozzle}}$ | Magnetic pinch / blow velocity ∝ current density | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often use the heat input formula with `V` as supply voltage. **Always use arc voltage (load voltage)**, which is lower than open-circuit voltage. The arc efficiency `η` for plasma is typically **0.55 – 0.70** (transferred) and **0.40 – 0.55** (non-transferred).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Titanium Honeycomb Welding):** PAW micro-mode is the only process that can weld thin-wall Ti tubes without burn-through.
