# 📙 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🔷 5.1 📝 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Double Side Arc Welding (DSAW). Distinguish clearly between *Tandem Arc* and *Twin Arc* configurations with neat sketches.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** Double Side Arc Welding is an advanced arc welding process employing **two simultaneously operating electric arcs** to deliver higher heat input and deposition rate in a single pass, enabling full-penetration welding of thick plates without multi-pass sequences.

**Tandem Arc (1 mark):** Two electrodes are mounted **in-line along the weld direction** on the **same side** of the joint, each powered by a **separate power source**. The leading arc (DCEN) preheats; the trailing arc (DCEP) penetrates.

**Twin Arc (1 mark):** Two electrodes are mounted **side-by-side on the same torch**, connected to a **single power source in parallel**, sharing a common arc column. Primarily used in **submerged arc welding (SAW)** for spiral pipe production.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six advantages of Double Side Arc Welding over single-arc GMAW processes.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):

1. **Higher deposition rate** (up to 25 kg/hr vs 5–6 kg/hr for single GMAW).
2. **Deeper penetration** in a single pass — full penetration in plates up to 40 mm.
3. **Higher travel speed** (1.5–2.0 m/min achievable).
4. **Reduced number of passes** — single-pass welds replace 3–4 multi-pass welds.
5. **Lower distortion** due to balanced symmetric heat input.
6. **Narrower HAZ and finer grain structure** — improved mechanical properties.
7. (Bonus) Reduced filler metal wastage and lower interpass temperature requirements.

---

## 🔷 5.2 📝 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **🔸 Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **working principle, equipment setup, and process parameters** of a **Tandem GMAW (TIME process)** system with a suitable schematic. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A tandem GMAW system welds a **25 mm thick low-carbon steel plate** at a travel speed of **600 mm/min** with the following arc parameters:

- Leading arc: $V_1 = 28$ V, $I_1 = 320$ A, $\eta_1 = 0.78$
- Trailing arc: $V_2 = 32$ V, $I_2 = 420$ A, $\eta_2 = 0.82$

**Calculate:** **(i)** Total heat input in kJ/mm, **(ii)** Cooling time $t_{8/5}$ assuming $T_0 = 25°C$ and $\lambda = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

| Sub-Topic | Marks | Key Content |
|---|---|---|
| Principle (2) | 2 | Two arcs on same side; leading preheats, trailing penetrates; independent power sources |
| Equipment (2.5) | 2.5 | Two PSUs, two wire feeders, two GMAW torches, common carriage, shielding gas units, control console |
| Parameters (1.5) | 1.5 | Polarity (DCEN leading, DCEP trailing), wire dia, gas mix (Ar + 15–20% CO₂), arc gap, stickout |
| Schematic (1) | 1 | Neat labelled diagram showing both arcs, wire feed, gas flow, workpiece |

**Detailed content:**

- **Principle:** The TIME (Tandem Inverted Metal-gas Electric) process uses two GMAW arcs in tandem. The **leading arc operates in DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** — produces a **soft, wide, preheating arc** with low penetration. The **trailing arc operates in DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** — produces a **narrow, deep, penetrating arc** with cathodic cleaning. The two arcs work in **synergy** to deliver high deposition with deep penetration.
- **Equipment:** Two independently controlled inverter power sources; two wire feeders (one per torch); two GMAW torches mounted on a common motorized carriage; dual shielding gas supply lines; water-cooled torch cables; optional seam-tracking and arc-sensing modules.
- **Process Parameters:** Wire diameters typically 1.0–1.6 mm; arc lengths 3–6 mm; contact-tip-to-work distances 15–25 mm; shielding gas 80% Ar + 20% CO₂ at 15–25 L/min; inter-arc spacing 8–15 mm.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**(i) Total Heat Input (3.5 marks):**

Leading arc:
$$
HI_1 = \frac{0.78 \times 28 \times 320 \times 60}{600 \times 1000} = \frac{419{,}328}{600{,}000} = 0.6989 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

Trailing arc:
$$
HI_2 = \frac{0.82 \times 32 \times 420 \times 60}{600 \times 1000} = \frac{661{,}248}{600{,}000} = 1.1021 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

Total:
$$
HI_{total} = 0.6989 + 1.1021 = \boxed{1.801 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

**(ii) Cooling time $t_{8/5}$ (3.5 marks):**

Convert heat input to J/mm: $HI = 1.801 \times 1000 = 1801$ J/mm.

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{HI}{2\pi \lambda} \left[ \frac{1}{500 - T_0} - \frac{1}{800 - T_0} \right]
$$

Substituting $T_0 = 25°C$, $\lambda = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (note: convert HI per metre → divide by 1000 for SI consistency; using J/mm directly with mm units in denominator):

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{1801}{2\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-3}} \left[ \frac{1}{475} - \frac{1}{775} \right]
$$

$$
= \frac{1801}{0.05027} \times \left[ 2.105 \times 10^{-3} - 1.290 \times 10^{-3} \right]
$$

$$
= 35{,}828 \times 8.15 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{8/5} \approx 29.2 \text{ seconds}}
$$

This is within the safe cooling range (5–60 s) for low-carbon steel, ensuring no HAZ cracking.

---

### **🔸 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **Twin-Arc Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** process. Compare its **advantages and limitations** with conventional single-arc SAW. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Two parallel arc columns of length $L = 10$ mm carry currents $I_1 = 380$ A and $I_2 = 420$ A, separated by $d = 15$ mm. If the **permeability of the arc plasma is $\mu = 1.2 \mu_0$** (due to ionised species), calculate the **attractive force between the arcs**. Discuss how this **force enhances weld penetration** in DSAW. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**Process (3 marks):** Twin-arc SAW employs **two parallel electrodes** (wire or strip) connected to a **single power source in parallel**, submerged under a common **flux bed**. Both arcs share the same weld pool and flux layer. Typical setups use twin 3.2 mm or 4.0 mm wires with inter-arc spacing 6–10 mm.

**Schematic (1 mark):** Diagram should show — power source, two wires entering flux, twin arc beneath flux, weld pool, workpiece, flux hopper, recovery system.

**Advantages vs single SAW (2 marks):**
1. Higher deposition rate (up to 2× single-wire SAW).
2. Better bead shape, shallower reinforcement.
3. Higher travel speed possible.
4. Smoother surface finish.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Complex flux delivery (must cover both arcs uniformly).
2. Risk of arc interaction / arc blow.
3. Higher power-source capacity required.
4. Limited to thick plates with high current.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Force calculation (4 marks):**

$$
F = \frac{\mu \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot d}
$$

With $\mu = 1.2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 4.8\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

$$
F = \frac{(4.8\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 380 \times 420 \times 0.010}{2\pi \times 0.015}
$$

The $\pi$ cancels:

$$
F = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-7} \times 380 \times 420 \times 0.010}{2 \times 0.015}
$$

Numerator:
$$
4.8 \times 10^{-7} \times 380 = 1.824 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
1.824 \times 10^{-4} \times 420 = 7.6608 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
7.6608 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.010 = 7.6608 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Denominator: $2 \times 0.015 = 0.030$

$$
F = \frac{7.6608 \times 10^{-4}}{0.030} = 2.5536 \times 10^{-2} \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 25.5 \text{ mN}}
$$

**Penetration enhancement discussion (3 marks):**

1. The attractive force pulls both arc columns **closer together**, concentrating electromagnetic energy into a **smaller weld pool area**.
2. The combined **axial magnetic pinch force** on the molten pool increases by **20–40%** beyond the simple sum of individual arc forces (synergistic effect).
3. This extra downward force **depresses the pool surface**, **increases heat transfer to the root**, and produces a **deeper, narrower fusion zone** — increasing the **P/W ratio** from ~0.5 (single arc) to > 1.0 (DSAW).
4. Result: **full penetration in thicker plates** (up to 40 mm) in a **single pass**, eliminating multi-pass welds and the associated residual stress and distortion.

---

## 🔷 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalise — avoid these to retain full marks:**
>
> 1. **Heat input unit error:** Forgetting the **×60 / ×1000 conversion** factors — costs **2 marks** in numericals. Always state $HI$ in **kJ/mm** with $S$ in **mm/min**.
> 2. **Confusing tandem vs twin:** These are **NOT synonyms** in KTU Module 2. Tandem = two torches, two power sources, in-line. Twin = two wires, one power source, side-by-side. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Missing efficiency factor $\eta$:** Always include $\eta$ in heat-input calculations; for SAW use **0.85–0.95**, for GMAW use **0.65–0.85**.
> 4. **Polarity confusion:** In tandem GMAW, the **leading arc is DCEN** (preheats) and the **trailing arc is DCEP** (penetrates). Reversing these gives poor penetration. This is a **favourite 2-mark question**.
> 5. **No diagram in 14-mark answers:** A neat labelled schematic is **mandatory** for full marks; absent diagram loses **1–2 marks** automatically.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state *constant travel speed, steady-state heat flow, no heat loss to surroundings* in derivations.

---

## 🔷 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 One-Page Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **DSAW = Two arcs, simultaneously, for high productivity** (definition — never forget this).
- [ ] **Three physical mechanisms:** Additive heat, combined electromagnetic force, reorganised Marangoni flow.
- [ ] **Tandem ≠ Twin:** Tandem uses two PSUs, twin uses one PSU.
- [ ] **Polarity rule:** Leading DCEN, Trailing DCEP (TIME process).
- [ ] **Heat input formula (must memorise):** $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm.
- [ ] **Total HI for DSAW:** $HI_{total} = HI_1 + HI_2$ (additive).
- [ ] **Deposition rate formula:** $D = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot \rho$ kg/hr (multiply by 3600 if $v_f$ in m/s).
- [ ] **Arc-to-arc force:** $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2 L}{2\pi d}$ — attractive, increases penetration.
- [ ] **Penetration ratio P/W** > 1.0 achievable in DSAW (vs 0.4–0.7 for single arc).
- [ ] **Productivity gain:** 100–200% over single-arc GMAW.
- [ ] **Cooling time $t_{8/5}$:** critical for HAZ microstructure — use the **inverse-temperature formula**.
- [ ] **Top applications:** Shipbuilding, spiral pipe, pressure vessels, offshore, storage tanks.
- [ ] **Common shielding gas:** 80% Ar + 20% CO₂ (GMAW); fused flux (SAW).
- [ ] **Plate thickness range:** 15–40 mm single pass; up to 100 mm with multi-arc configurations.
- [ ] **Industry names to remember:** TIME process (ESAB), Twin-Arc (Lincoln Electric), Tandem SAW (Kemppi).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU mantra:** *"Two arcs, double deposition, deeper penetration, single pass — that's DSAW."*

---

### ✅ End of Notes — Module 2: Double Side Arc Welding
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) | Outcome-Based Education | Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L1–L4*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Magnetic Arc Welding # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Magnetic Arc Welding (MAW)** is a class of advanced fusion welding processes in which an externally applied or self-induced **electromagnetic (Lorentz) field** is used to **deflect, rotate, oscillate, or stabilize the welding arc** to achieve controlled heat distribution, deeper penetration, faster travel speeds, or symmetric joining of closed-sectional components. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context (Module 2 of *Advanced Metal Joining Techniques*), the term predominantly covers two sub-processes: 1. **Magnetically Impelled Arc Butt (MIAB) Welding** – a non-conventional butt-welding process in which a DC arc, struck across a small gap between two ferromagnetic tubular/solid workpieces, is forced to rotate circumferentially by a radial magnetic field; the molten faces are then forged together axially. 2. **Electromagnetic Arc Welding / Magnetically Controlled Arc Welding (EMAW / MCAW)** – a process in which a transverse or axial magnetic field is superimposed on a conventional TIG/GMAW arc to deflect the plasma jet, refine grain structure, or improve bead geometry. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student is expected to differentiate MIAB welding from conventional arc welding, describe its principle, equipment, process parameters, advantages, limitations, and applications. The electromagnetic force equation (**Lorentz Force, F = BIL**) is a **frequently tested derivation** in ESE. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Magnetic Spoon" Analogy Imagine you are heating a small pool of water on a flat plate with a candle flame. Now imagine an invisible "magnetic spoon" that you can use to **stir, push, or spin the flame sideways** without touching it. The flame still produces heat, but now the heat is **distributed evenly** across a larger area, or **pushed deep** into a narrow groove. That invisible "magnetic spoon" is the **Lorentz force** in Magnetic Arc Welding. - A **welding arc** is a stream of moving charged particles (electrons + ions). - A **magnetic field** exerts a sideways force on any moving charge. - Therefore, a magnet placed near the arc can **push the arc sideways, spin it around a pipe, or make it dance along the joint**. In **MIAB welding**, this magnetic push is used to make the arc **race around the circumference of a pipe** (like a child running around a maypole), melting the entire rim uniformly in a few seconds. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** An electric arc is **NOT** magnetic by itself — it is only **deflectable** because it is a stream of **charged particles in motion**. A stationary charge is unaffected; a moving charge is pushed sideways by the magnetic field. This is the essence of the Lorentz force. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Magnetic Arc Welding Processes | Sub-Process | Arc Movement Achieved | Primary Application | Typical Material | |---|---|---|---| | **MIAB Welding** | Rapid circumferential rotation | Butt welding of pipes, tubes, rods, rings | Carbon steel, low-alloy steel | | **Electromagnetic Arc Welding (EMAW)** | Lateral deflection / oscillation | Improved penetration, grain refinement | Aluminium, stainless steel | | **Magnetically Stabilized GMAW** | Arc stiffness control | High-speed welding, deep penetration | Structural steel | | **Magnetic Flux Oscillation Welding** | Periodic arc deflection | Underwater / thick-plate welding | Shipbuilding steel | --- ## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold-Defined Constants) - **Magnetic Flux Density (B)** — measured in **Tesla (T)** or **Weber/m² (Wb/m²)**. Typical welding values: **0.05 T to 0.5 T**. - **Lorentz Force (F)** — measured in **Newtons (N)**. - **Arc Current (I)** — **100 A to 1500 A (DC)** for MIAB; **50 A to 300 A** for EMAW. - **Arc Rotation Frequency in MIAB** — typically **50 Hz to 200 Hz** (i.e., arc completes 3000 to 12000 revolutions/minute). - **Open Circuit Voltage** — **12 V to 25 V DC** for MIAB. - **Forging Force (Axial Upset Force)** — **5 kN to 50 kN** depending on cross-section. - **Ambient Medium** — Usually **air** (no shielding gas required for MIAB on steel). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle The working of every Magnetic Arc Welding process rests on **three pillars**: ### Pillar 1: The Arc as a Current-Carrying Conductor An electric arc is essentially a **plasma column** carrying current **I** from the cathode (negative electrode) to the anode (positive workpiece). Because current is the organized flow of electrons, the arc **behaves like a flexible current-carrying wire** of length **L** and is therefore susceptible to electromagnetic forces. ### Pillar 2: The Lorentz Force (The "Why") When a current-carrying conductor lies inside an external magnetic field, a mechanical force acts on it. This is the **Laplace Force**, a special case of the **Lorentz Force**: $$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$$ where: - $F$ = force on the arc (N) - $B$ = external magnetic flux density (T) - $I$ = arc current (A) - $L$ = effective length of the arc column (m) - $\theta$ = angle between current direction and magnetic field direction For maximum deflection (perpendicular arrangement), $\theta = 90°$ and $\sin\theta = 1$, giving the **maximum force**: $$F_{max} = B \cdot I \cdot L$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Hot Tip:** Examiners often quote the **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** to test the *direction* of arc deflection. The question may ask: "In which direction will the arc be deflected?" — Use **F = B × I × L** with right-hand rule, or the **motor rule (F = IL × B)**. ### Pillar 3: Arc Motion and Heat Redistribution Once the Lorentz force moves the arc, the **heat input is spatially redistributed** along the path traced by the arc. For a rotating arc (MIAB), the heat is spread uniformly around the circumference. For a deflected arc (EMAW), the heat is pushed into the root of the joint. --- ## 2.2 Detailed Working of Magnetically Impelled Arc Butt (MIAB) Welding MIAB welding is a **batch butt-welding process** consisting of **four sequential stages**: ### Stage 1 — Clamping & Alignment The two workpieces (typically tubes/pipes) are clamped in a special machine with their facing ends separated by a small **arc gap (g)**, typically **1 mm to 3 mm**. The faces must be **flat, clean, and parallel**. ### Stage 2 — Arc Initiation A **DC power source** strikes an arc across the small gap. Due to the close spacing, the arc is short and produces intense localized heat. The arc current is ramped up (typically to **400–1000 A**) within milliseconds. ### Stage 3 — Magnetic Impulsion (Arc Rotation) A **radial magnetic field** is generated by: - Either an **external electromagnet (field coil)** wound around the workpiece, OR - The **interaction of arc current with the ferromagnetic workpiece** (a self-induced effect in some designs). This radial B-field is perpendicular to the axial arc current, so the Lorentz force $F = BIL$ acts **tangentially**, causing the arc to **rotate rapidly around the circumference** of the joint. The arc becomes a "ring of fire." ### Stage 4 — Forging (Upsetting) Once both facing surfaces reach forging temperature (typically **1200 °C to 1350 °C** for carbon steel), the **axial upset force** is applied instantly. Molten metal and oxides are squeezed out as **flash**, and a **solid-state forged bond** is created with a refined, equiaxed grain structure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Note:** MIAB is *not* a true fusion weld in the final stage — the bond is formed primarily by **forging (solid-state bonding)** at the forging temperature, with the arc serving only as a *heating mechanism*. This is why MIAB welds have excellent mechanical properties. --- ## 2.3 Heat Generation in the Arc Column The arc acts as a resistive heating element. By Joule's Law: $$H = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t \quad (\text{Joules})$$ where: - $H$ = heat generated in the arc (J) - $I$ = arc current (A) - $R$ = effective resistance of the arc plasma (Ω) - $t$ = duration of the arc (s) The **electrical power** dissipated in the arc is: $$P = V \cdot I = I^2 R \quad (\text{Watts})$$ For a rotating arc in MIAB welding, the **time-averaged heat flux** at any point on the circumference is: $$q_{avg} = \frac{P}{2\pi r} = \frac{V \cdot I}{2\pi r} \quad (\text{W/m})$$ where $r$ is the radius of the arc path (≈ workpiece radius). --- ## 2.4 The Velocity of the Rotating Arc (Key KTU Derivation) The rotating arc has a **mass $m$** of plasma (very small, on the order of **micrograms**). The Lorentz force accelerates it **tangentially**: $$a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{BIL}{m}$$ The tangential velocity of the arc after a time $t$: $$v = a \cdot t = \frac{BIL \cdot t}{m}$$ The **rotation frequency (revolutions per second)** for an arc of path radius $r$: $$f = \frac{v}{2\pi r} = \frac{BIL \cdot t}{2\pi r m}$$ For steady-state rotation in a uniform field, equating the **Lorentz driving force** to the **centripetal force requirement**: $$BIL = m \omega^2 r = m (2\pi f)^2 r$$ Solving for the rotation frequency: $$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{BIL}{mr}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is a **favorite KTU 14-mark derivation**. Commit the assumption that the arc behaves as a **point mass $m$ on a circular path of radius $r$** to memory. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = BIL\sin\theta$ | Lorentz/Laplace force on arc | N | | 2 | $F_{max} = BIL$ | Maximum force (θ = 90°) | N | | 3 | $H = I^2 R t$ | Joule heat in arc | J | | 4 | $P = VI$ | Electrical power dissipated | W | | 5 | $q_{avg} = \dfrac{VI}{2\pi r}$ | Circumferential heat flux (MIAB) | W/m | | 6 | $v = \dfrac{BIL \cdot t}{m}$ | Tangential arc velocity (impulse form) | m/s | | 7 | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{BIL}{mr}}$ | MIAB arc rotation frequency | Hz | | 8 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular velocity of arc | rad/s | | 9 | $E_{arc} = \dfrac{kT}{e}$ | Mean electron energy in plasma | eV | | 10 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 NI_{coil}}{l_{coil}}$ | Solenoid field (for external coil) | T | **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions:** - Arc plasma treated as a **single flexible current-carrying wire** (one-dimensional). - Magnetic field is **uniform and perpendicular** to the arc axis. - Arc mass $m$ is **constant** (no significant ablation during rotation). - No shielding gas interaction (for MIAB in air on steel). --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application of MAW | |---|---| | **Automotive** | MIAB welding of drive shafts, axle tubes, ring gears | | **Oil & Gas** | Pipe-to-pipe and pipe-to-flange welding in field joints | | **Shipbuilding** | Magnetic arc oscillation for thick-plate root passes | | **Aerospace** | EMAW for titanium and nickel superalloys (defect-free welds) | | **Power Plants** | Tube-to-tube-sheet welding in boilers and heat exchangers | | **Railways** | Continuous welded rail joints using magnetic arc stabilization | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lorentz Force on the Welding Arc **Statement to Prove:** A current-carrying arc, when placed in a transverse magnetic field of flux density $B$, experiences a sideways mechanical force $F = BIL\sin\theta$. **Step 1 — Consider an elemental charge carrier.** A single electron of charge $e$ moving with velocity $v$ inside a magnetic field $B$ experiences a force given by the **vector form of the Lorentz Law**: $$\vec{F}_1 = e \cdot (\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$ **Step 2 — Convert velocity to current.** If $n$ is the **number density of charge carriers** (carriers per m³), $A$ is the **cross-sectional area** of the arc column, then the current $I$ is: $$I = n \cdot e \cdot A \cdot v$$ **Step 3 — Force on a conductor of length $L$.** The total charge passing through the arc length $L$ in time $dt$ is $dQ = I \cdot dt$. Each carrier travels a distance $L$ in time $dt = L/v$. The number of carriers in length $L$ is $n \cdot A \cdot L$. The total force on the arc segment is: $$F = (n \cdot A \cdot L) \cdot e \cdot v \cdot B \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Step 4 — Simplify using $I = n e A v$.** $$F = (n \cdot e \cdot A \cdot v) \cdot L \cdot B \cdot \sin\theta$$ $$\boxed{F = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta}$$ This is the **fundamental Laplace force** acting on the welding arc. > **Conversion Logic:** In Step 3, we used the relationship $dt = L/v$ (time for one carrier to traverse length $L$) and multiplied by the total number of carriers in that segment. The $v$ in the numerator cancels one $v$ from the denominator of the differential, giving the clean product $I \cdot L$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rotation Frequency of the Arc in MIAB Welding **Given:** A DC arc of length $L$ carries current $I$ in a radial magnetic field $B$ between two coaxial workpieces of radius $r$. The arc plasma has effective mass $m$. **Find:** The steady-state rotation frequency $f$. **Step 1 — Write the tangential driving force.** $$F_{tangential} = B \cdot I \cdot L$$ **Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law in the tangential direction.** If the arc accelerates from rest, the velocity after time $t$ is: $$v(t) = \frac{F_{tangential}}{m} \cdot t = \frac{BIL}{m} \cdot t$$ **Step 3 — For a circular path of radius $r$, the centripetal acceleration is:** $$a_c = \frac{v^2}{r} = \omega^2 r$$ **Step 4 — Steady-state condition:** The Lorentz force must continuously supply the centripetal force: $$BIL = \frac{m \cdot v^2}{r}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $v = \omega r$:** $$BIL = \frac{m (\omega r)^2}{r} = m \omega^2 r$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $\omega$:** $$\omega^2 = \frac{BIL}{mr}$$ $$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{BIL}{mr}}$$ **Step 7 — Convert to frequency $f = \omega / (2\pi)$:** $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{BIL}{mr}}}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** The arc is treated as a **point mass on a string of length $r$** being whirled in a circle. The "string tension" is provided by the magnetic Lorentz force, not by a physical string. The angular velocity depends on the square root of $BIL/mr$, meaning **doubling the current increases the rotation frequency by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$**, not 2. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Input Rate per Unit Length of Circumference in MIAB **Given:** Arc voltage $V$, arc current $I$, workpiece outer radius $R_o$, inner radius $R_i$. **Find:** Heat input per unit circumferential length $q$ (W/m). **Step 1 — Total electrical power delivered to the arc.** $$P = V \cdot I \quad (\text{W})$$ **Step 2 — Effective heating radius (mean radius).** $$R = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2} \quad (\text{m})$$ **Step 3 — Circumferential length of the arc path.** $$C = 2\pi R \quad (\text{m})$$ **Step 4 — Assuming 100% of arc power reaches the workpiece (idealized):** $$q = \frac{P}{C} = \frac{V \cdot I}{2\pi R}$$ $$\boxed{q = \frac{V \cdot I}{2\pi R} \quad (\text{W/m})}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** This is a **power-per-unit-length** metric. In MIAB welding, the arc continuously sweeps the entire circumference, so the heat is uniformly distributed. This is why MIAB welds have **symmetric, defect-free microstructures** without the lopsided HAZ seen in stationary arc welds. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Solenoid Field Strength (for External Coil Design) **Given:** A coil of $N$ turns, length $l_{coil}$, carrying current $I_{coil}$, wound around the workpiece. **Find:** Magnetic flux density $B$ at the workpiece axis. **Step 1 — Apply Ampère's Law to an ideal solenoid:** $$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \cdot I_{enclosed}$$ **Step 2 — Inside the solenoid, $B$ is uniform and parallel to the axis:** $$B \cdot l_{coil} = \mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_{coil}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $B$:** $$\boxed{B = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_{coil}}{l_{coil}}}$$ where $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}$ is the permeability of free space. > **Conversion Logic:** This is a **design equation** for the field coil. For a coil of 500 turns, length 0.1 m, carrying 5 A: > > $$B = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 5}{0.1} \approx 3.14 \times 10^{-2} \text{ T} = 31.4 \text{ mT}$$ > > This is typical for industrial MIAB machines. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs Weld Quality | Parameter | Effect on Arc Speed | Effect on Heat Input | Effect on Weld Quality | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | ↑ Arc Current (I) | ↑↑ (square-root) | ↑↑ (linear) | Risk of burn-through if too high | 400–1000 A | | ↑ Magnetic Field (B) | ↑↑ (square-root) | No direct effect | Better arc stability, finer grain | 0.05–0.3 T | | ↑ Arc Gap (L) | ↑ (linear) | ↑ (longer arc = more resistance) | Wider HAZ, more flash | 1–3 mm | | ↑ Forging Force | No effect on arc | No effect | Better consolidation, less porosity | 5–50 kN | | ↑ Rotation Frequency | ↑ | No direct effect | Finer, more uniform grain | 50–200 Hz | | ↑ Arc Time | No effect on speed | ↑ (more total heat) | Deeper melting, more flash | 0.5–5 s | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MIAB Welding Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD PWR[DC Power Source
400 to 1000 A, 12 to 25 V] COIL[Field Excitation Coil
N turns, l coil length] CLMP1[Pneumatic / Hydraulic Clamp A
Holds Workpiece 1] CLMP2[Pneumatic / Hydraulic Clamp B
Holds Workpiece 2] WP1[Workpiece 1
Ferromagnetic Tube] WP2[Workpiece 2
Ferromagnetic Tube] GAP[Arc Gap
1 to 3 mm] ARC[Rotating Arc
f 50 to 200 Hz] BFLD[Radial Magnetic Field B
0.05 to 0.3 T] UFORGE[Axial Upset Forging Cylinder
5 to 50 kN] FLASH[Squeezed Flash and Oxide
Ejected Radially] WELD[Forged Solid-State Weld
Equiaxed Fine Grain] PWR --> GAP COIL --> BFLD CLMP1 --> WP1 CLMP2 --> WP2 WP1 --- GAP GAP --- WP2 BFLD --> ARC GAP --> ARC ARC --> UFORGE UFORGE --> FLASH UFORGE --> WELD ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Process Stages of MIAB Welding ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Stage 1
Clamp and Align
Gap 1 to 3 mm] --> S2[Stage 2
Strike DC Arc
I ramps to set value] S2 --> S3[Stage 3
Energize Field Coil
Arc rotates circumferentially] S3 --> S4[Stage 4
Heat to Forging Temp
1200 to 1350 C] S4 --> S5[Stage 5
Apply Upset Force
5 to 50 kN axial] S5 --> S6[Stage 6
Hold and Cool
Solid state bond forms] S6 --> S7[Stage 7
Retract Clamps
Eject Flash] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Electromagnetic Arc Welding (EMAW) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD TG[TIG / GMAW Torch
Conventional Arc Source] --> ARCZ[Arc Plasma Column] PMAG[Permanent Magnet Assembly
or External Coil] BEXT[Transverse Magnetic Field B
0.05 to 0.2 T] ARCZ --> WPP[Workpiece Joint] PMAG --> BEXT BEXT --> ARCZ FOR[Differential Force F equals BIL
Pushes Arc Sideways into Root] ARCZ --> FOR FOR --> WPP WPP --> OUT[Refined Weld Bead
Deep Penetration, Narrow HAZ] ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Parameter Influence Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR I[Arc Current I] --> ARC1[Arc Force BIL] B[Magnetic Field B] --> ARC1 L[Arc Length L] --> ARC1 ARC1 --> OMEGA[Arc Rotation Omega] OMEGA --> FREQ[Frequency f] FREQ --> HEAT[Heat Distribution Uniformity] HEAT --> QUAL[Forged Weld Quality] V[Arc Voltage V] --> POW[Power equals VI] POW --> HEAT T[Arc Time t] --> POW POW --> FORGET[Forging Temperature 1200 to 1350 C] FORGET --> QUAL ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Magnetic Arc Welding and list its two main variants. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Magnetic Arc Welding (MAW)** is a category of fusion welding processes in which an **externally applied or self-induced magnetic field** is used to deflect, rotate, oscillate, or stabilize the welding arc, primarily to control heat distribution, improve penetration, or achieve symmetric joining. The **two main variants** are: 1. **Magnetically Impelled Arc Butt (MIAB) Welding** — the arc is forced to rotate circumferentially between two butting ferromagnetic workpieces; the heated ends are then forged together axially. 2. **Electromagnetic Arc Welding (EMAW)** — a transverse or axial magnetic field is superimposed on a conventional arc (TIG/GMAW) to deflect the plasma jet into the joint root for better penetration and grain refinement. > **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** Definition = 1 mark; Variant 1 with brief explanation = 1 mark; Variant 2 with brief explanation = 1 mark. --- ### Question 2: State Fleming's Left-Hand Rule and explain its use in MIAB welding. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule (Motor Rule):** "If the thumb, forefinger, and middle finger of the left hand are held mutually perpendicular, with the forefinger pointing along the **magnetic field (B)**, the middle finger along the **current (I)**, then the **thumb** points in the direction of the **force (F)** acting on the conductor." **Application in MIAB Welding:** - The **forefinger** is aligned with the **radial magnetic flux density $B$** generated by the field coil. - The **middle finger** is aligned with the **axial arc current $I$** (from cathode to anode). - The **thumb** automatically points in the **tangential direction** — this is the direction in which the arc is pushed and rotates. > **Mark Allocation (KTU Pattern):** Statement of rule = 1 mark; Diagram/direction of fingers = 1 mark; Application to MIAB = 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Internal choice must be offered. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of Magnetically Impelled Arc Butt (MIAB) welding**. Discuss the role of the Lorentz force in producing arc rotation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **steady-state rotation frequency of the arc** in MIAB welding in terms of magnetic flux density $B$, arc current $I$, arc length $L$, arc mass $m$, and workpiece radius $r$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Introduction (1 mark):** MIAB welding is a non-conventional butt-welding process in which a DC arc is struck between two clamped ferromagnetic workpieces separated by a small gap (1–3 mm). The arc is forced to rotate circumferentially by a radial magnetic field. 2. **Equipment Setup (2 marks):** - Two workpieces clamped in a MIAB machine with their faces parallel and a small arc gap. - A DC power source (400–1000 A, 12–25 V) connected across the workpieces. - A field excitation coil (solenoid) wound around the workpiece, generating a radial magnetic field $B$ (0.05–0.3 T). - An axial upset cylinder capable of applying 5–50 kN forging force. 3. **Working Stages (3 marks):** - **Arc Initiation:** DC arc struck across the gap; current ramped up rapidly. - **Arc Rotation:** Radial B-field perpendicular to axial arc current $I$ creates a tangential Lorentz force $F = BIL$, rotating the arc around the circumference at 50–200 Hz. - **Heating:** Both facing surfaces heat uniformly to forging temperature (1200–1350 °C for steel). - **Forging:** Axial upset force is applied; molten metal and oxide are expelled as flash; a solid-state forged bond forms with equiaxed fine grain. 4. **Role of Lorentz Force (1 mark):** The Lorentz force $F = BIL$ is the *only* physical mechanism that drives arc rotation. Without it, the arc would remain stationary at the point of lowest impedance, leading to localized melting and asymmetric welds. > **Schematic:** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** Arc current $I$, arc length $L$, radial magnetic field $B$, arc plasma mass $m$, workpiece radius $r$. **Step 1 (1 mark):** The Lorentz force acting tangentially on the arc: $$F = BIL$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Applying Newton's second law, tangential acceleration: $$a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{BIL}{m}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** For steady-state circular motion, the centripetal force requirement is: $$F_{centripetal} = m \omega^2 r$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Equating Lorentz force to centripetal force (the magnetic field "supplies" the centripetal force continuously): $$BIL = m \omega^2 r$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Solving for $\omega$: $$\omega^2 = \frac{BIL}{mr} \implies \omega = \sqrt{\frac{BIL}{mr}}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Converting to frequency $f = \omega / 2\pi$: $$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{BIL}{mr}}}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Final physical interpretation: The rotation frequency is proportional to the square root of $BIL$, and inversely proportional to the square root of $mr$. Doubling either $B$ or $I$ increases $f$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **(a)** Compare **MIAB welding** with **conventional flash butt welding** and **resistance butt welding** in terms of principle, heat source, weld quality, and applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A MIAB welding machine operates with an arc current of **800 A**, an arc gap of **2 mm**, a magnetic flux density of **0.2 T**, and the effective arc mass is **$5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg**. The workpiece outer diameter is **60 mm**. Calculate: (i) the Lorentz force on the arc, and (ii) the rotation frequency of the arc. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks | Criterion | MIAB Welding | Flash Butt Welding | Resistance Butt Welding | |---|---|---|---| | **Principle** | Arc rotation by magnetic field + axial forging | Continuous arcing + flash-off + forging | Direct Joule heating (no arc) + forging | | **Heat Source** | DC arc rotated magnetically | Repeated make-break arcs (AC) | Resistance heating ($I^2R$) | | **Weld Quality** | Excellent (fine grain, no HAZ defects) | Good (some oxide entrapment) | Moderate (limited to small sections) | | **Application** | Tubes, pipes, ring gears, drive shafts | Rails, chains, large bars | Small wires, rods | | **Energy Efficiency** | High (concentrated, fast) | Moderate (flash loss) | Low (long heating time) | | **Surface Prep** | Critical (faces must be flat & clean) | Moderate | Less critical | | **Alloy Suitability** | Best for low/medium alloy steel | Versatile | Limited | > **Mark Allocation:** 7 criteria × 1 mark each = 7 marks. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given Data:** - $I = 800$ A - $L = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $B = 0.2$ T - $m = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg - Outer diameter $D = 60$ mm, so radius $r = 30$ mm $= 0.03$ m - $\theta = 90°$ (perpendicular B and I), so $\sin\theta = 1$ --- **Part (i): Lorentz Force on the Arc (3 marks)** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the formula: $$F = BIL\sin\theta$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute values: $$F = 0.2 \times 800 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Final answer: $$F = 0.32 \text{ N}$$ --- **Part (ii): Rotation Frequency (4 marks)** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the formula: $$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{BIL}{mr}}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Calculate the numerator $BIL$: $$BIL = 0.2 \times 800 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.32$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Calculate the denominator $mr$: $$mr = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.03 = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the ratio and final answer: $$\frac{BIL}{mr} = \frac{0.32}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.133 \times 10^{6}$$ $$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{2.133 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1460.6}{2\pi} \approx \frac{1460.6}{6.283}$$ $$\boxed{f \approx 232.4 \text{ Hz} \approx 13946 \text{ RPM}}$$ > **Interpretation:** The arc completes ~232 revolutions per second, or ~13,946 RPM. This is the speed at which the arc races around the 60 mm diameter pipe circumference, ensuring uniform heating in under 1 second. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Loss of Marks)** > 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Students often use arc gap = 2 mm directly in SI formulas without converting to **meters**. This gives a Lorentz force that is **1000× too large** and rotation frequency that is **~31.6× too large**. Always convert mm to m first. > 2. **Missing $\sin\theta$:** When B and I are not perpendicular, the formula $F = BIL\sin\theta$ must be used. If the examiner's question states "field is at 30° to arc axis," students who write $F = BIL$ lose 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing Arc Length vs Arc Gap:** In MIAB, $L$ in the formula is the **arc length** (essentially the gap), NOT the workpiece length. Many students misread and use workpiece dimensions. > 4. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$:** The frequency $f$ is in Hz, but the formula $\omega = \sqrt{BIL/mr}$ gives **angular velocity in rad/s**. Dividing by $2\pi$ is mandatory. > 5. **Equating Lorentz force to centripetal force without justification:** In the derivation, the student must explicitly state "in steady state, the Lorentz force continuously supplies the centripetal force." Otherwise, the examiner deducts for "missing physics." > 6. **Confusing MIAB with MAG welding:** MIAB is **not** Metal Active Gas welding. They are entirely different processes. Writing "MAG welding uses magnetic field" will be marked wrong. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Magnetic Arc Welding** ### ✅ Core Concepts - [ ] **Magnetic Arc Welding** = use of magnetic field to control welding arc. - [ ] **Two main types:** MIAB (rotating arc, butt welding) and EMAW (deflected arc, penetration enhancement). - [ ] The arc is a **stream of charged particles** → deflectable by magnetic field. - [ ] The driving force is the **Lorentz (Laplace) force:** $F = BIL\sin\theta$. ### ✅ Key Formulas (must memorize) - [ ] Lorentz force: $F = BIL$ - [ ] Heat in arc: $H = I^2Rt$ - [ ] Arc rotation frequency: $f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{BIL}{mr}}$ - [ ] Circumferential heat flux: $q = \frac{VI}{2\pi r}$ - [ ] Solenoid field: $B = \frac{\mu_0 N I}{l}$ ### ✅ Process Knowledge - [ ] MIAB stages: **Clamp → Arc Strike → Magnetic Rotation → Forging → Cool**. - [ ] Arc gap in MIAB: **1–3 mm**. - [ ] Arc current in MIAB: **400–1000 A DC**. - [ ] Rotation frequency: **50–200 Hz** (3000–12000 RPM). - [ ] Forging temperature for steel: **1200–1350 °C**. - [ ] MIAB weld is **solid-state forged**, not classical fusion. ### ✅ Direction & Rule - [ ] **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** determines arc deflection direction. - [ ] **Forefinger = B, Middle finger = I, Thumb = F** (tangential). ### ✅ Practical Notes - [ ] **No shielding gas** needed for MIAB on steel. - [ ] **Surface prep is critical** (flat, parallel, clean faces). - [ ] **Materials:** primarily low/medium carbon and low-alloy steels. - [ ] **Applications:** drive shafts, axle tubes, pipe joints, ring gears. - [ ] **Advantages:** fast (cycle time ~1 s), uniform weld, no HAZ defects, no filler metal. - [ ] **Limitations:** limited to circular/butt joints; requires special machine; not for non-ferromagnetic materials without external coil. ### ✅ KTU Exam Triggers - [ ] "Derive rotation frequency" — always show centripetal force balance. - [ ] "State Fleming's Left-Hand Rule" — always draw or describe finger positions. - [ ] "Compare MIAB with other butt welding processes" — use the comparison table. - [ ] Numerical problem — **check unit conversion (mm → m)** first.

topics.title: Basic Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 Basic Principles of Advanced Metal Joining Processes

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET523)**, the term **"Basic Principles"** under Module 3 refers to the fundamental physical, thermal, and metallurgical governing mechanisms of **high-energy density joining processes** — primarily **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** and **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)**. These are classified as **non-conventional fusion welding processes** where the heat required for coalescence is generated by a focused beam of high kinetic or radiant energy, producing **power densities** in the order of **10⁶ – 10⁹ W/mm²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Highlight):**
> *"Advanced metal joining techniques use concentrated energy sources (electron beams, laser beams, plasma arcs) to achieve localized melting and joining with minimum Heat Affected Zone (HAZ), minimum distortion, and deep penetration in a single pass."*

## 1.2 Core Physical Phenomena Involved

The basic principles of these advanced processes rest on **four fundamental physical phenomena**:

1. **Electron Emission & Acceleration** — Thermionic emission, followed by electrostatic acceleration of electrons in a high-vacuum environment.
2. **Photon Emission & Coherence** — Stimulated emission of radiation producing a coherent, monochromatic laser beam.
3. **Energy-Matter Interaction** — Conversion of kinetic energy (electrons) or radiant energy (photons) into thermal energy upon striking the workpiece.
4. **Keyhole Formation Mechanism** — Vaporization of metal creates a capillary cavity, allowing deep penetration via multiple internal reflections.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **🍕 Real-World Analogy — "The Burning Glass vs. The Sun's Ray":**
> Imagine you are trying to melt a thick metal plate. A regular welding torch spreads its heat like a **wide bonfire** — it works, but it heats a large area, causes distortion, and takes time. Now imagine you take a **magnifying glass** and focus the sun's rays into a single tiny spot. The energy at that point is so intense that it can burn a hole right through the metal. **Electron Beam Welding is like using trillions of microscopic "bullets" fired at the metal**, while **Laser Beam Welding is like using that magnifying glass** but with a beam of synchronized light particles. Both create a tiny, intense energy point that cuts through metal almost like a hot needle through butter.

## 1.4 Classification of High-Energy Density Processes

| Process | Energy Source | Power Density (W/mm²) | Typical Vacuum | Atmosphere |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** | Kinetic energy of electrons | **10⁶ – 10⁹** | High Vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) | Required |
| **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** | Coherent photons | **10⁵ – 10⁷** | Atmospheric / Shielding gas | Optional |
| **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** | Ionized gas (plasma) | **10⁵ – 10⁶** | Atmospheric | Shielding gas |
| **Conventional Arc Welding** | Electric arc | **10³ – 10⁴** | Atmospheric | Flux / Shielding gas |

> [!NOTE]
> The **order of magnitude difference in power density** is the single most important reason these processes are called "advanced" — they can achieve **depth-to-width ratios of 10:1 to 50:1**, impossible in conventional arc welding.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants (Board-Exam Important)

- **Charge of an electron:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Wien's displacement constant:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Electron Beam Welding (EBW)

The operating principle of EBW is a **four-stage energy conversion process**:

### Stage 1 — Thermionic Emission of Electrons
When a tungsten filament is heated to approximately **2500 °C**, electrons gain sufficient thermal energy to overcome the **work function (φ)** of the cathode material and escape into the vacuum.

$$J = A_R T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{e\phi}{k_B T}\right)$$

Where:
- $J$ = Emission current density (A/m²)
- $A_R$ = Richardson's constant = **1.2 × 10⁶ A/m²·K²**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature of filament (K)
- $\phi$ = Work function of tungsten ≈ **4.5 eV**
- $e$ = Charge of electron = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**

### Stage 2 — Acceleration
The emitted electrons are accelerated by a high negative potential (**accelerating voltage $V$ = 30 to 200 kV**) applied to the cathode, with the workpiece acting as the anode (grounded).

**Kinetic energy acquired by each electron:**

$$E_k = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

**Velocity of the accelerated electron:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$

Where $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$.

### Stage 3 — Focusing
Electromagnetic lenses (magnetic coils) focus the diverging electron beam to a spot diameter of **0.1 mm to 1 mm** at the workpiece. Beam current $I_b$ typically ranges from **20 mA to 1000 mA**.

### Stage 4 — Heat Generation on Impact
When high-velocity electrons strike the metal, their kinetic energy is converted to **heat (≈ 70–80%)**, with the remainder lost as X-ray radiation, backscattered electrons, and secondary electrons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why High Vacuum?**
> Without vacuum, electrons (mass = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg) would **scatter** off air molecules. A vacuum of **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr** ensures a **mean free path** greater than the working distance, allowing the beam to travel unimpeded.

## 2.2 Principle of Laser Beam Welding (LBW)

A **LASER** (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) generates a coherent, monochromatic, collimated beam of high radiant energy.

### Step-by-Step Working:

1. **Pumping** — External energy (electrical or optical) excites atoms of the lasing medium (e.g., CO₂, Nd:YAG, Fiber) to a higher energy state.
2. **Population Inversion** — More atoms exist in the excited state than the ground state (a non-equilibrium condition).
3. **Stimulated Emission** — A trigger photon of energy $E = hf$ causes an excited atom to emit an identical photon (same phase, direction, frequency).
4. **Optical Resonator** — Two mirrors at the ends of the lasing cavity cause photons to bounce back and forth, amplifying the beam.
5. **Beam Delivery** — The amplified laser pulse/continuous beam is delivered through mirrors or fiber optic cables and focused to a small spot using focusing optics.

**Energy per photon:**

$$E_{photon} = hf = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $f$ = Frequency of light (Hz)
- $c$ = Speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength of emitted laser (m)

### Common Industrial Lasers for Welding:

| Laser Type | Wavelength ($\lambda$) | Power Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ Laser** | 10.6 µm | Up to 25 kW | Thick section steels, automotive |
| **Nd:YAG Laser** | 1.06 µm | Up to 6 kW | Precision welding, aerospace |
| **Fiber Laser** | 1.07 µm | Up to 20 kW | Stainless steel, aluminium |
| **Disc Laser** | 1.03 µm | Up to 16 kW | High-speed welding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Beam-Material Interaction (Beer-Lambert Law):**
> Laser intensity decreases exponentially with depth:
> $$I(z) = I_0 (1 - R) e^{-\alpha z}$$
> Where $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient (m⁻¹) and $R$ is the surface reflectivity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_k = eV$ | Kinetic energy of accelerated electron | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $v = \sqrt{2eV/m_e}$ | Electron velocity after acceleration | m/s |
| 3 | $P = VI_b$ | Total beam power | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $P_d = P/A$ | Power density at focal spot | W/mm² |
| 5 | $A = \pi d^2/4$ | Focal spot area (circular) | mm² |
| 6 | $E_{photon} = hc/\lambda$ | Energy of one laser photon | Joules (J) |
| 7 | $I(z) = I_0(1-R)e^{-\alpha z}$ | Laser intensity inside material | W/m² |
| 8 | $Q = Pt/v_w$ | Heat input per unit length | J/mm |
| 9 | $J = A_R T^2 e^{-e\phi/k_BT}$ | Thermionic emission (Richardson-Dushman) | A/m² |
| 10 | $\theta = 1.22 \lambda/D$ | Beam divergence angle (Airy disk) | radians |
| 11 | $E_{min} = mc^2$ | Relativistic energy threshold | Joules |
| 12 | $\lambda_{max} = b/T$ | Wien's displacement law | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠️ LaTeX Pitfall Avoided:** All absolute value bars in formulas use the $\vert$ delimiter syntax where needed to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** EBW is used to join **titanium alloys** for rocket nozzle skirts and jet engine blisks where minimum HAZ is critical.
- **Nuclear Industry:** LBW is used to seal **zirconium alloy fuel rods** in nuclear reactors due to its precise, contamination-free welds.
- **Automotive:** High-power fiber lasers perform **remote welding** of tailored blanks in car body assembly at speeds up to **10 m/min**.
- **Electronics:** Pulsed Nd:YAG lasers are used for **micro-welding** of battery tabs in lithium-ion battery packs.
- **Defence:** EBW is the preferred process for welding **armor-grade steels** and dissimilar metal combinations in submarines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Electron Velocity After Acceleration

**Problem Context:** Find the velocity of an electron accelerated through a potential difference of $V = 60\ \text{kV}$ used in an EBW machine.

### Given Data
- $V = 60\ \text{kV} = 60{,}000\ \text{V}$
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$

### Step 1 — Energy Conservation
The work done by the electric field on the electron equals its kinetic energy gained.

$$W_{electric} = eV = E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Velocity $v$

$$\frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV$$

$$v^2 = \frac{2eV}{m_e}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times 60{,}000}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{1.9224 \times 10^{-14}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2.111 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$v = 1.453 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$$

### Step 4 — Validity Check
Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$

$$v/c = \frac{1.453 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 0.484$$

Since $v/c < 0.5$, **non-relativistic mechanics is valid**. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> For voltages above **100 kV**, relativistic corrections become mandatory. Above **200 kV**, the electron approaches half the speed of light and $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}$ must be included.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Density at the Focal Spot

**Problem Context:** An EBW machine operates at $V = 80\ \text{kV}$ and $I_b = 50\ \text{mA}$ with a focal spot diameter $d = 0.4\ \text{mm}$. Calculate the power density.

### Step 1 — Calculate Beam Power

$$P = V \times I_b = 80{,}000 \times 0.050 = 4000\ \text{W} = 4\ \text{kW}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Focal Spot Area

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.4)^2}{4} = 0.1257\ \text{mm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Power Density

$$P_d = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{4000}{0.1257}$$

$$P_d = 31{,}825\ \text{W/mm}^2 \approx 3.18 \times 10^{4}\ \text{W/mm}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This value (**~3 × 10⁴ W/mm²**) is at the lower end of EBW. Industrial EBW machines typically achieve **10⁶ – 10⁹ W/mm²** with smaller focal spots (e.g., 0.1 mm) and higher beam currents (up to 1 A).

### Step 4 — Efficiency-Aware Power Density (Advanced)
If the energy conversion efficiency is $\eta = 0.75$ (i.e., 75% of beam power converts to heat), then:

$$P_{d,heat} = \eta \times P_d = 0.75 \times 3.18 \times 10^4 = 2.39 \times 10^4\ \text{W/mm}^2$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Photon Energy for a CO₂ Laser

**Problem Context:** A CO₂ laser emits infrared radiation at $\lambda = 10.6\ \mu\text{m}$. Calculate the energy of one photon.

### Step 1 — Apply Planck-Einstein Relation

$$E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$E_{photon} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^8)}{10.6 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$E_{photon} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.06 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$E_{photon} = 1.875 \times 10^{-20}\ \text{J}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Electron-Volts (Board-Exam Standard)

$$E_{photon}\ (\text{in eV}) = \frac{1.875 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.117\ \text{eV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each CO₂ laser photon carries only **0.117 eV**, but the beam contains **trillions of photons per second**, leading to massive cumulative energy delivery.

### Step 4 — Number of Photons per Second for a 1 kW Laser

$$N_{ph} = \frac{P_{laser}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{1000}{1.875 \times 10^{-20}} = 5.33 \times 10^{22}\ \text{photons/second}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Keyhole vs. Conduction Mode Welding

| Parameter | **Conduction Mode** | **Keyhole Mode** |
|---|---|---|
| Power density | $< 10^5\ \text{W/mm}^2$ | $> 10^6\ \text{W/mm}^2$ |
| Penetration shape | Hemispherical (bowl-shaped) | Deep, narrow (nail-head) |
| Depth-to-width ratio | $\leq 0.5$ | $\geq 5$ (up to 50 in EBW) |
| Vaporization | Minimal | Significant — keyhole cavity forms |
| Energy absorption | Surface only (one reflection) | Multiple internal reflections (Fresnel absorption) |
| Process examples | Low-power LBW, micro-TIG | EBW, high-power LBW |
| Applicable to | Thin sheets, precision | Thick sections, deep welds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight (Board Favorite):** In keyhole mode, **Fresnel absorption** traps the beam inside the vapor cavity, increasing the effective absorption from ~30% (conduction) to **>90%**. This is why EBW can weld **200 mm thick steel in a single pass**.

## 3.5 Key Performance Equations Summary

**Heat Input per unit weld length** (for moving beam process):

$$Q = \frac{\eta P}{v_w}$$

Where $v_w$ = welding speed (mm/s) and $\eta$ = process efficiency.

**Energy balance on the workpiece** (1D steady state):

$$\eta P = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T + Q_{loss}$$

Where $m = \rho A v_w \cdot \Delta t$ is the mass melted per unit time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
