# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** 
**Define Electron Beam Welding (EBW) and state any TWO of its advantages over conventional arc welding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Electron Beam Welding is an **advanced fusion welding process** in which the heat required for coalescence is generated by bombarding the joint with a **focused beam of high-velocity electrons** accelerated through a potential difference of **30 to 200 kV** in a high-vacuum environment (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr).

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Extremely high power density** (10⁶ – 10⁹ W/mm²) enables deep penetration (up to 200 mm in a single pass) with **narrow HAZ** and minimal distortion.
2. **Vacuum environment** prevents atmospheric contamination (oxygen, nitrogen), making it ideal for **reactive metals** like titanium, zirconium, and niobium.

> [!NOTE]
> *Word count target: 70–100 words for full 3 marks.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is meant by "keyhole mode" of welding? How does it differ from "conduction mode"?**

**Model Answer:**

**Keyhole Mode:** When the power density of the incident beam exceeds **~10⁶ W/mm²**, the metal surface **vaporizes** before it can spread the heat laterally. This creates a deep, narrow cavity (the *keyhole*) that penetrates through the entire thickness. The beam energy is **trapped inside** this cavity via multiple internal reflections (**Fresnel absorption**), giving absorption efficiencies of **> 90%**.

**Conduction Mode:** At lower power densities (< 10⁵ W/mm²), the heat is transferred into the metal purely by **thermal conduction** from the surface. The weld pool is **hemispherical** with a depth-to-width ratio of ≤ 0.5, and beam energy is absorbed in a **single pass** at the surface.

| Aspect | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Penetration | Shallow, wide | Deep, narrow |
| Power density | < 10⁵ W/mm² | > 10⁶ W/mm² |
| Process | Micro-laser welding, low-power TIG | EBW, high-power LBW |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and must be answered with internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. The model solutions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 marking scheme** with proper step-by-step valuation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **basic principle and working of Electron Beam Welding** with a neat block diagram. List **four advantages** and **two limitations** of EBW. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an EBW process, the accelerating voltage is **120 kV** and the beam current is **80 mA**. The focal spot diameter is **0.3 mm**. Calculate the **(i)** beam power, **(ii)** power density, and **(iii)** velocity of electrons at the workpiece. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1 above for full schematic):**
```
HV Power Supply → Filament → Vacuum Chamber → 
Magnetic Lens → Deflection Coils → Workpiece
```

**Working Principle (Step-by-step, 4 marks):**

1. **Electron Generation:** A tungsten filament is heated to **~2500 °C** by passing a high current. Electrons overcome the work function and undergo **thermionic emission** (Richardson-Dushman equation).
2. **Acceleration:** Electrons are accelerated by a high negative potential of **120 kV** applied between the cathode (filament) and anode (workpiece). The work done on each electron is $W = eV$, converting to kinetic energy $E_k = \frac{1}{2}m_e v^2$.
3. **Focusing:** The diverging electron beam is converged by an **electromagnetic lens** (magnetic coil carrying DC current) to a focal spot of **0.1 – 1 mm** diameter.
4. **Heat Generation:** On striking the workpiece, the kinetic energy is converted to **heat (~75%)**, with ~25% lost as **X-rays, backscattered electrons, and secondary electrons**.
5. **Welding:** The intense heat vaporizes the metal, creating a **keyhole**. The molten metal flows around the keyhole and solidifies behind the moving beam, forming a deep, narrow weld.

**Four Advantages (2 marks):**
- Very high depth-to-width ratio (up to **50:1**)
- Minimum HAZ → minimum distortion
- No atmospheric contamination (vacuum environment)
- Can join dissimilar metals and refractory materials

**Two Limitations (1 mark):**
- High capital cost and requires skilled operators
- Workpiece size limited by vacuum chamber dimensions
- Generates harmful X-rays requiring radiation shielding

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $V = 120\ \text{kV} = 120{,}000\ \text{V}$
- $I_b = 80\ \text{mA} = 0.080\ \text{A}$
- $d = 0.3\ \text{mm}$
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$, $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$

**(i) Beam Power:** *(2 marks)*

$$P = V \times I_b = 120{,}000 \times 0.080$$

$$\boxed{P = 9600\ \text{W} = 9.6\ \text{kW}}$$

**(ii) Power Density:** *(2 marks)*

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.3)^2}{4} = 0.0707\ \text{mm}^2$$

$$P_d = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{9600}{0.0707}$$

$$\boxed{P_d = 1.358 \times 10^{5}\ \text{W/mm}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a moderately high power density — typical industrial EBW would use a smaller spot (0.1 mm) to push this into the **10⁶ – 10⁹ W/mm²** range.

**(iii) Electron Velocity:** *(3 marks)*

From $E_k = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 120{,}000}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{3.8448 \times 10^{-14}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{4.221 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$\boxed{v = 2.055 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}}$$

**Validity Check:** $v/c = 2.055 \times 10^8 / 3 \times 10^8 = 0.685$. Since this exceeds 0.5, **relativistic correction is required** for strict accuracy at 120 kV. However, for KTU board exam purposes, the non-relativistic formula is accepted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (INTERNAL CHOICE)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Explain the **basic principle of Laser Beam Welding (LBW)**. Discuss the **differences between CO₂ laser and Nd:YAG laser** for welding applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **1.5 kW Nd:YAG laser** is focused to a spot of diameter **0.5 mm** on an aluminium workpiece. Calculate the **(i)** power density at the focal spot, **(ii)** the heat input per unit length if the welding speed is **20 mm/s**, assuming an efficiency of **85%**, and **(iii)** the number of photons striking the workpiece per second (wavelength = 1.06 µm). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle of LBW (4 marks):**

A laser produces a coherent, monochromatic, collimated beam of high-intensity light through **stimulated emission of radiation**. The process involves:
- **Pumping** the lasing medium to create **population inversion** (more atoms in excited state than ground state).
- A **trigger photon** of energy $E = hf = hc/\lambda$ causes an excited atom to emit an **identical photon** (same phase, frequency, direction).
- Photons bounce between two mirrors in the **optical resonator**, amplifying the beam.
- The amplified beam is **focused by optical lenses** onto the workpiece, where the photons' energy is absorbed and converted to heat for welding.

**Differences between CO₂ and Nd:YAG Lasers (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CO₂ Laser | Nd:YAG Laser |
|---|---|---|
| **Lasing medium** | CO₂ gas mixture | Neodymium-doped YAG crystal |
| **Wavelength** | 10.6 µm (far infrared) | 1.06 µm (near infrared) |
| **Beam delivery** | Mirrors only (cannot use fiber optics at 10.6 µm) | Can use **fiber optic cables** (high flexibility) |
| **Absorption by metals** | High absorption | Lower absorption (metals more reflective at 1.06 µm) |
| **Power capability** | Up to **25 kW** (very high power) | Up to **6 kW** typically |
| **Application** | Thick steel sections, automotive body | Precision welding, aerospace, medical devices |
| **Efficiency** | ~10–15% | ~3–5% (lamp-pumped), up to 40% (diode-pumped) |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $P_{laser} = 1500\ \text{W}$
- $d = 0.5\ \text{mm}$
- $v_w = 20\ \text{mm/s}$
- $\eta = 0.85$
- $\lambda = 1.06 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$

**(i) Power Density:** *(2 marks)*

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.5)^2}{4} = 0.1963\ \text{mm}^2$$

$$P_d = \frac{P_{laser}}{A} = \frac{1500}{0.1963}$$

$$\boxed{P_d = 7643\ \text{W/mm}^2 \approx 7.64 \times 10^{3}\ \text{W/mm}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **conduction mode** territory (below 10⁵ W/mm²) — typical for low-power precision welding of thin aluminium sheets.

**(ii) Heat Input per unit length:** *(2 marks)*

$$Q = \frac{\eta P_{laser}}{v_w} = \frac{0.85 \times 1500}{20}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 63.75\ \text{J/mm}}$$

**(iii) Number of Photons per second:** *(3 marks)*

Energy of one photon:

$$E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.06 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$E_{photon} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.06 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.875 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$$

Number of photons per second:

$$N_{ph} = \frac{P_{laser}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{1500}{1.875 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\boxed{N_{ph} = 8.0 \times 10^{21}\ \text{photons/second}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Beam current is often given in **mA** — students forget to convert to **A** before multiplying with voltage, leading to 1000× errors in power calculation.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Wavelengths of Lasers:** Writing CO₂ as 1.06 µm (it's actually **10.6 µm**) is a frequent error. Memorize: **CO₂ = 10.6 µm (IR), Nd:YAG = 1.06 µm (NIR)**.
> 
> 3. **Missing the Vacuum Justification:** In EBW theory questions, examiners award **2 marks** specifically for explaining *why vacuum is required* (electron mean free path). Don't skip it.
> 
> 4. **Power Density Misconception:** Some students write power density as $P \times A$ instead of $P / A$. Power density **decreases** as spot area increases.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the Work Function Symbol:** In Richardson-Dushman equation, students often write just $T$ without stating it's the **filament temperature in Kelvin**, and miss the work function $\phi$.
> 
> 6. **Not drawing the block diagram:** In 7-mark theory questions, a **properly labeled block diagram is mandatory**. Drawing a rough sketch without labeled components costs **at least 2 marks**.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Board Exam Rapid-Fire):**

- ✅ **EBW requires vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr)** because electrons (very low mass) scatter in air.
- ✅ **Power density order:** Conventional Arc (10³) < PAW (10⁵) < LBW (10⁵–10⁷) < **EBW (10⁶–10⁹)** W/mm².
- ✅ **Kinetic energy equation:** $E_k = eV$ — works for non-relativistic voltages (< 100 kV). Above 200 kV, use relativistic equations.
- ✅ **Photon energy equation:** $E = hc/\lambda$ — always state $\lambda$ in **meters** for SI consistency.
- ✅ **Keyhole mode** requires power density > **10⁶ W/mm²** and gives **depth-to-width ratio > 5**.
- ✅ **CO₂ laser wavelength = 10.6 µm**; **Nd:YAG = 1.06 µm**; **Fiber laser = 1.07 µm**.
- ✅ **Richardson-Dushman equation** governs thermionic emission — higher filament temperature = exponentially more electrons.
- ✅ **X-ray hazard** in EBW above **60 kV** — requires **lead shielding** of vacuum chamber walls.
- ✅ **Fresnel absorption** in the keyhole increases laser energy absorption from ~30% (surface) to **>90%** (cavity).
- ✅ **Focal spot diameter** inversely affects power density: smaller spot = higher concentration = deeper penetration.
- ✅ **Thermionic emission materials:** Tungsten (φ = 4.5 eV), Tantalum (φ = 4.25 eV), Lanthanum hexaboride (LaB₆, φ = 2.5 eV — best emitter).
- ✅ **Population inversion** is a non-equilibrium condition; pumping must be continuous to maintain it.
- ✅ **Optical resonator** consists of a fully reflective mirror and a partially transmissive output coupler.
- ✅ **Wien's displacement law** ($\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}$) helps predict dominant emission wavelength from a hot body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** For KTU Part B questions, always follow the **"Diagram → Definition → Working → Equations → Numerical"** sequence. This structure matches the official valuation key and ensures you secure **step marks** even if your final answer has a minor calculation error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Variants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Variants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Variants in Advanced Metal Joining**
> Process variants are the **modified, enhanced, or specialized versions** of the conventional fusion and solid-state joining processes, engineered to overcome specific limitations of the parent process such as excessive heat input, poor productivity, distortion, limited material compatibility, or inadequate penetration. They represent the evolution of welding science through controlled manipulation of **arc physics, power source characteristics, filler metal transfer modes, energy coupling mechanisms, and hybrid energy delivery systems.**

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the term "Process Variants" specifically refers to the engineering adaptations of:

| Parent Process | Representative Variants |
| :--- | :--- |
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | Pulsed MIG, Double Pulse MIG, Tandem MIG, Cold Metal Transfer (CMT) |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | Pulsed TIG, Hot-Wire TIG, Activated TIG (A-TIG), Narrow-Gap TIG |
| **Plasma Arc** | Plasma-MIG Hybrid, Micro-plasma, Variable Polarity PAW |
| **Laser Welding** | Laser-MIG Hybrid, Remote Laser Welding, Blue Laser Welding |
| **Solid-State / FSW** | Stationary Shoulder FSW, Bobbin Tool FSW, Underwater FSW |
| **Resistance Welding** | Capacitor Discharge, Inverter-based Spot Welding |
| **EBW** | Partial Vacuum, Non-Vacuum EBW |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Recipe" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy**
> Imagine you are cooking a basic *tomato pasta*. That's your **conventional process**. Now, the chef develops:
> - A *baked version* with cheese crust (lower moisture, different heat profile)
> - A *spicy arrabbiata version* (added ingredients, more pungent)
> - A *gluten-free version* (different base, same goal)
> 
> Each is a **variant** — built on the *same fundamental process* (cooking pasta with sauce), but **tuned** for a specific outcome (crispiness, flavor, dietary need). Process Variants in metal joining work the **same way**: same joining principle, but the **energy input, transfer mode, or geometry** is refined to achieve a **specialized goal** (less distortion, deeper penetration, faster deposition, dissimilar joining, etc.).

## 1.3 Why Process Variants Exist — The Driving Forces

Every conventional process has a "weakness envelope." Process variants exist to:

1. **Reduce Heat Input (HI)** → minimize HAZ, distortion, and residual stress.
2. **Increase Deposition Rate (DR)** → improve productivity in heavy fabrication.
3. **Achieve All-Position Capability** → enable welding of complex geometries.
4. **Enable Dissimilar Joining** → weld materials with vastly different melting points.
5. **Improve Bead Geometry Control** → for thick plates, thin sheets, or precision joints.
6. **Automate & Robotize** → seamless integration with Industry 4.0 / IIoT systems.

## 1.4 Core Terminology — Must-Know Vocabulary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Glossary**
> - **Heat Input (HI):** The total energy per unit length delivered to the workpiece. Unit: **kJ/mm**.
> - **Deposition Rate (DR):** Mass of filler metal deposited per unit time. Unit: **kg/h**.
> - **Power Density (PD):** Arc power concentrated per unit area. Unit: **W/mm²**.
> - **Duty Cycle (%):** Ratio of pulse-on time to total pulse period in pulsed variants.
> - **Peak Current ($I_p$):** The high current pulse that detaches the droplet.
> - **Background Current ($I_b$):** The low current that maintains the arc between pulses.
> - **Pulse Frequency ($f$):** Pulses per second (Hz).
> - **Wire Feed Rate (WFR):** Linear speed of filler wire, typically **m/min**.
> - **Travel Speed (TS):** Welding torch speed, typically **mm/min** or **mm/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic for Process Variants

Process variants can be classified along **three primary axes**:

### Axis 1: By Energy Manipulation Strategy
- **Pulsed Energy Delivery** → Pulsed MIG, Pulsed TIG
- **Hybrid Energy Coupling** → Laser-MIG, Plasma-MIG
- **Mechanical-Electrical Coupling** → Friction Stir, Ultrasonic-assisted welding

### Axis 2: By Transfer Mode Modification
- **Controlled Short-Circuit Transfer** → CMT (Cold Metal Transfer)
- **Forced Droplet Detachment** → Pulsed Spray
- **Rotating/Streaming Transfer** → Tandem MIG

### Axis 3: By Geometry Modification
- **Narrow Gap Variants** → NG-GTAW, NG-GMAW
- **Tight Access** → Micro-plasma, Remote Laser

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — The Major Process Variants

### 2.2.1 Pulsed GMAW (Pulsed MIG / MAG)

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle**
> Conventional GMAW uses either **short-circuit transfer** (low heat, low deposition) or **spray transfer** (high deposition, requires high current, vertical-down only). Pulsed MIG is the **"best of both worlds"** solution.

**Mechanism:**
1. A **background current ($I_b$)** sustains the arc at low power, maintaining a stable but non-detaching droplet.
2. Periodically, a **peak current ($I_p$)** pulse is superimposed.
3. The pulse delivers a **current boost** that **pinches off one droplet per pulse** via electromagnetic force.
4. After detachment, current drops back to $I_b$ → arc stabilizes → next pulse.

**Key Advantages:**
- **One droplet per pulse** → uniform, controllable metal transfer.
- Operates at **lower mean current** than spray transfer → **lower heat input**.
- Enables **all-position welding** with spray-like bead quality.
- Slag-free, spatter-free, and porosity-free welds.

**Critical Parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Peak Current | $I_p$ | 300 – 600 A |
| Background Current | $I_b$ | 50 – 150 A |
| Pulse Frequency | $f$ | 50 – 300 Hz |
| Pulse Duration | $t_p$ | 2 – 5 ms |
| Background Time | $t_b$ | 5 – 20 ms |
| Wire Diameter | $d$ | 0.8 – 1.6 mm |

### 2.2.2 Cold Metal Transfer (CMT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMT — The "Heat-Minimizing Champion"**
> Developed by **Fronius (Austria)**, CMT is a digitally controlled short-circuit variant where the wire is **actively retracted** during the short-circuit phase.

**Cycle:**
1. Arc ignites → current rises → wire approaches workpiece.
2. **Dip** occurs (short-circuit).
3. **Wire feed reverses direction** → mechanically pulls the droplet off.
4. Current drops to **near-zero** during this retraction → minimal heat.
5. Wire advances again → cycle repeats at **up to 70–100 Hz**.

**Why "Cold"?** The arc is **mechanically extinguished** during droplet detachment, so the heat input is **dramatically lower** (often **< 5 kJ/mm**) compared to conventional short-circuit (~10–15 kJ/mm).

**Applications:** Thin-sheet joining (galvanized steel for automotive), dissimilar joining (steel-aluminum), robotic welding of body-in-white.

### 2.2.3 Tandem GMAW (Tandem MIG)

Two **independently controlled wires and arcs** in a single torch:
- **Lead arc:** higher heat, deep penetration, root pass.
- **Trail arc:** lower heat, filling, cosmetic cap.
- Combined deposition rate: **up to 20 kg/h** (vs. 5–6 kg/h single-wire).

Used in heavy fabrication: shipbuilding, pressure vessels, wind turbine towers.

### 2.2.4 Pulsed GTAW (Pulsed TIG)

The arc current is **pulsed** between a high ($I_p$) and low ($I_b$) value:
- **High current phase:** penetrates the base metal → forms the weld puddle.
- **Low current phase:** allows **solidification** → grain refinement.
- Frequency: **0.5 Hz to 10 kHz** (depending on power source).
- **Result:** Equiaxed, fine-grained microstructure in the weld → superior mechanical properties.

### 2.2.5 Hot-Wire GTAW

A separate **resistively heated filler wire** is fed into the weld pool:
- Wire is **preheated by Joule heating** (current passes through it before entering the pool).
- **Almost 100% deposition efficiency** (no spatter, no oxidation).
- Used for: **titanium aerospace components, superalloy turbine blades**.

### 2.2.6 Activated TIG (A-TIG)

A thin layer of **flux** (containing oxides like $SiO_2$, $TiO_2$) is painted on the joint:
- Reverses **Marangoni (surface tension) flow** in the weld pool.
- Changes pool shape from **wide & shallow → deep & narrow**.
- Increases penetration by **2 to 3 times** in a single pass.
- **Eliminates need for edge preparation** on thick plates (10–12 mm in single pass).

### 2.2.7 Narrow-Gap Welding (NGW)

A **narrow groove (~6–10 mm wide)** is prepared instead of the conventional V-groove (20–30 mm):
- **Massive filler metal savings** (up to 70% for thick plates).
- **Reduced heat input** per pass.
- Variants: **NG-GTAW** (oscillating arc, oscillating wire), **NG-GMAW**, **NG-SAW**.
- Used in: thick-wall pressure vessels, nuclear reactor vessels, hydrocrackers.

### 2.2.8 Hybrid Laser-Arc Welding (HLAW)

**Laser + MIG/GMAW** in a single process zone:
- Laser creates a **keyhole** → deep penetration.
- Arc fills the keyhole with filler → bridges gaps, modifies metallurgy.
- Combines **laser's high speed + arc's gap-bridging ability**.
- Deposition rates of **5–10 kg/h** with single-pass penetration of **15–20 mm** in steel.
- Applications: automotive, shipbuilding, thick-plate structural fabrication.

### 2.2.9 Friction Stir Welding (FSW) Variants

Although FSW is Module-2 territory, its **variants** are central to Module 3:

| Variant | Distinguishing Feature | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stationary Shoulder FSW (SSFSW)** | Shoulder does not rotate → zero flash | Aerospace, thin sections |
| **Bobbin Tool FSW** | Shoulder below + tool above → no backing plate | Long circumferential welds (tanks) |
| **Underwater FSW** | Submerged in water → rapid cooling, fine grain | Marine, Al-alloys |
| **Friction Stir Processing (FSP)** | Used for **surface modification**, not joining | Superplasticity, grain refinement |
| **Self-Reacting FSW** | Two opposing shoulders (no anvil) | Ship panels |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are exam-critical. Master these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | Heat Input (η = arc efficiency: GMAW ≈ 0.8, GTAW ≈ 0.6, SAW ≈ 1.0, PAW ≈ 0.6) | **kJ/mm** |
| 2 | $DR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot WFR \cdot \rho}{4 \times 60 \times 10^6}$ | Deposition Rate (ρ = wire density) | **kg/h** |
| 3 | $PD = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A_{arc}}$ | Power Density ($A_{arc}$ = arc spot area) | **W/mm²** |
| 4 | $I_{mean} = \dfrac{I_p \cdot t_p + I_b \cdot t_b}{t_p + t_b}$ | Mean current in pulsed welding | **A** |
| 5 | $f = \dfrac{1}{t_p + t_b}$ | Pulse frequency | **Hz** |
| 6 | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{t_p}{t_p + t_b} \times 100\%$ | % of time peak current is active | **%** |
| 7 | $\eta_{dep} = \dfrac{\text{Mass Deposited}}{\text{Mass Fed}} \times 100\%$ | Deposition Efficiency (CMT ≈ 98%, SAW ≈ 95%, GTAW ≈ 90%) | **%** |
| 8 | $v_{wire} = \dfrac{4 \cdot DR}{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \rho \cdot 3600}$ | Required wire feed speed for a target deposition rate | **m/s** |
| 9 | $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | Arc Power | **W (or kW)** |
| 10 | $R_{grain} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{HI}}$ | Empirical: grain size ∝ √(1/HI) (Hall-Petch linkage) | **—** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU 2024**
> The factor of **60** in Heat Input converts mm/min to mm/s, and **1000** converts J to kJ. Examiners **frequently** test whether students correctly include η (arc efficiency). A common mistake is using η = 1.0 for GTAW, which is **wrong** — GTAW's η is typically 0.5–0.65.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry (CMT, Laser-MIG Hybrid):** Body-in-white welding of mixed-material car bodies (steel + Al + Mg). Enables 30–40% weight reduction for EV battery enclosures.
- **Aerospace (Hot-Wire TIG, EBW, Plasma):** Turbine blade repair, thin-wall titanium fuselage welding.
- **Shipbuilding (Tandem MIG, SAW variants):** Flat-position, high-deposition rate for thick steel plates (up to 100 mm).
- **Nuclear (NG-GTAW, EBW):** Austenitic stainless steel pressure vessels with minimal HAZ sensitization.
- **Pipeline (Flash Butt + FSW):** Longitudinal seam welding for HSAW pipes (Helical Submerged Arc Welded).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Current in Pulsed GMAW

**Problem:** A pulsed MIG weld uses $I_p = 450$ A, $t_p = 3$ ms, $I_b = 80$ A, $t_b = 12$ ms. Determine the mean current, duty cycle, and pulse frequency.

**Step 1: Recall the definition of mean current**

The mean (RMS-average) current over one complete pulse cycle is the time-weighted average:

$$
I_{mean} = \dfrac{\int_0^{T} I(t)\,dt}{T}
$$

**Step 2: Recognize the rectangular pulse shape**

Since the current is a piecewise-constant waveform (rectangular pulse):

$$
I(t) = \begin{cases} I_p, & 0 \leq t \leq t_p \\ I_b, & t_p < t \leq t_p + t_b \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral**

$$
\int_0^{T} I(t)\,dt = I_p \cdot t_p + I_b \cdot t_b
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify**

$$
I_{mean} = \dfrac{I_p \cdot t_p + I_b \cdot t_b}{t_p + t_b}
$$

**Step 5: Plug in numerical values**

$$
I_{mean} = \dfrac{(450)(3) + (80)(12)}{3 + 12}
$$

$$
I_{mean} = \dfrac{1350 + 960}{15} = \dfrac{2310}{15}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{mean} = 154 \text{ A}}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the duty cycle**

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{t_p}{t_p + t_b} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3}{15} \times 100\% = 20\%
$$

**Step 7: Compute the pulse frequency**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{t_p + t_b} = \dfrac{1}{0.015 \text{ s}} = 66.67 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although the instantaneous current reaches 450 A, the time-averaged current is only 154 A. This is precisely why pulsed MIG achieves spray-transfer quality at *much* lower heat input than conventional spray MIG (which requires continuous ~300+ A).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Input Comparison (Conventional Spray MIG vs. Pulsed MIG)

**Problem:** A 6 mm thick low-carbon steel plate is welded using two processes. Estimate the heat input for each and compare.

| Parameter | Conventional Spray MIG | Pulsed MIG |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Voltage, V | 28 V | 24 V |
| Mean Current, I | 280 A | 154 A |
| Travel Speed, S | 400 mm/min | 400 mm/min |
| Arc Efficiency, η | 0.80 | 0.80 |

**Step 1: Recall the Heat Input formula**

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

where $S$ is in **mm/min** (and the implicit factor of 60 converts to mm/s before 1000 converts J to kJ).

**Step 2: Compute HI for Conventional Spray MIG**

$$
HI_1 = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28 \times 280}{400 \times 1000} = \dfrac{6272}{400000}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI_1 = 0.01568 \text{ kJ/mm} = 15.68 \text{ J/mm}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute HI for Pulsed MIG**

$$
HI_2 = \dfrac{0.80 \times 24 \times 154}{400 \times 1000} = \dfrac{2956.8}{400000}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI_2 = 0.007392 \text{ kJ/mm} = 7.39 \text{ J/mm}}
$$

**Step 4: Compare and analyze**

$$
\text{Reduction in HI} = \dfrac{15.68 - 7.39}{15.68} \times 100\% \approx 52.9\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Pulsed MIG delivers **~53% less heat input** while maintaining the **droplet detachment regularity** of spray transfer. The consequence is a **narrower HAZ**, **lower distortion**, and **fine-grained fusion zone microstructure**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deposition Rate for CMT Welding

**Problem:** A CMT process uses a 1.0 mm diameter steel wire (ρ = 7.85 g/cm³) at a wire feed rate of 6 m/min. Find the deposition rate.

**Step 1: Recall the deposition rate formula**

$$
DR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2 \cdot WFR \cdot \rho}{4 \times 10^6} \quad \text{[kg/min, with d in mm, WFR in mm/min, ρ in g/cm³]}
$$

**Step 2: Cross-unit check**

- $d = 1.0$ mm, $WFR = 6000$ mm/min, $\rho = 7.85 \times 10^{-3}$ g/mm³ (or equivalently in compatible units).

To keep SI-friendly, use:
- $d = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $WFR = 6/60 = 0.1$ m/s, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³

$$
DR = \dfrac{\pi \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 0.1 \times 7850}{4}
$$

**Step 3: Compute**

$$
DR = \dfrac{\pi \times 10^{-6} \times 0.1 \times 7850}{4} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 10^{-6} \times 785}{4}
$$

$$
DR = \dfrac{0.002466}{4} = 0.0006165 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to kg/h**

$$
DR = 0.0006165 \times 3600 = 2.219 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\boxed{DR \approx 2.22 \text{ kg/h}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **low** compared to spray MIG (~5–6 kg/h) — **and that is the point of CMT**. The low deposition is a trade-off for the very low heat input. For a single-pass fillet on thin sheet, this is acceptable; for thick plate, you need CMT + multi-pass or use tandem MIG instead.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Density for Plasma vs. TIG

**Problem:** Given arc voltage 18 V, current 200 A, and arc spot area 4 mm² for PAW, and 14 V, 200 A, and 12 mm² for GTAW, find the power density of each.

**Step 1: Recall**

$$
PD = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A_{arc}} \quad \text{[W/mm²]}
$$

**Step 2: PAW calculation**

$$
PD_{PAW} = \dfrac{18 \times 200}{4} = 900 \text{ W/mm²}
$$

**Step 3: GTAW calculation**

$$
PD_{GTAW} = \dfrac{14 \times 200}{12} \approx 233.3 \text{ W/mm²}
$$

**Step 4: Ratio**

$$
\dfrac{PD_{PAW}}{PD_{GTAW}} = \dfrac{900}{233.3} \approx 3.86
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Plasma penetrates deeper than TIG despite the same current:** The constricting nozzle of the PAW torch **forces the arc into a tiny cross-section**, raising the **power density by nearly 4×**. This is the *physical reason* behind PAW's superior keyhole-mode penetration and faster travel speeds. The same logic applies to **laser welding** where power density is **$10^3$ to $10^6$ W/mm²** — orders of magnitude higher.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Practice — KTU-Style Mini Problem

**Problem:** A Pulsed TIG weld is performed on a 3 mm 304L stainless steel sheet with the following parameters:
- Peak current $I_p = 250$ A, $t_p = 2$ ms
- Background current $I_b = 50$ A, $t_b = 8$ ms
- Arc voltage = 12 V
- Travel speed = 250 mm/min
- Arc efficiency η = 0.55

**Find:** (a) Pulse frequency, (b) Duty cycle, (c) Mean current, (d) Heat input.

**Solution:**

(a) $f = \dfrac{1}{t_p + t_b} = \dfrac{1}{0.010} = 100 \text{ Hz}$

(b) $\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{2}{10} \times 100\% = 20\%$

(c) $I_{mean} = \dfrac{(250)(2) + (50)(8)}{10} = \dfrac{500 + 400}{10} = 90 \text{ A}$

(d) $HI = \dfrac{0.55 \times 12 \times 90}{250 \times 1000} = \dfrac{594}{250000} = 0.002376 \text{ kJ/mm} = 2.376 \text{ J/mm}$

This is a **typical low-HI** value for thin stainless steel — appropriate to avoid sensitization and intergranular corrosion in the HAZ.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between Pulsed MIG and Cold Metal Transfer (CMT) welding."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pulsed MIG | CMT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transfer mode | Electromagnetic pinch-off via current pulse | Mechanical retraction of wire |
| Current during detachment | Peak current pulse | Near-zero current |
| Frequency | 50–300 Hz | 70–100 Hz |
| Heat input | Low–Medium | **Very Low** (often < 5 kJ/mm) |
| Spatter | Very low | **Almost none** |
| Typical application | Medium-thick structural | Thin sheets, galvanized steel, dissimilar Al–Steel |

(3 marks: 1 mark for each correct differentiator; 3 distinct points required.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State any three advantages of Activated TIG (A-TIG) welding over conventional TIG."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
1. **Increased penetration** (2 to 3 times deeper) in a single pass, eliminating the need for multi-pass welding on plates up to 12 mm thick.
2. **Reduced edge preparation** — no V-groove required; square butt joint is sufficient.
3. **Improved productivity** and **narrower HAZ** due to lower total heat input per joint.
4. **Reduced filler metal consumption** since root gap can be tighter.

(Any 3 of the above; 1 mark each.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Pulsed GMAW (Pulsed MIG)** with a neat current-vs-time waveform diagram. List **four advantages** of the process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Pulsed MIG welding operation uses the following parameters: $V = 26$ V, peak current $I_p = 480$ A, $t_p = 2.5$ ms, $I_b = 90$ A, $t_b = 10$ ms, travel speed $S = 350$ mm/min, arc efficiency η = 0.85. Calculate: (i) pulse frequency, (ii) duty cycle, (iii) mean current, and (iv) heat input. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Working Principle:**
In Pulsed MIG, the welding current is **modulated** between a low **background current ($I_b$)** and a high **peak current ($I_p$)** at a controlled frequency. During the background phase, the wire is fed towards the workpiece and the molten droplet at the wire tip **grows slowly** without detaching (arc is sustained but at low power). When the peak pulse arrives, the **electromagnetic pinch force** exceeds the surface tension of the droplet, detaching it. **One droplet detaches per pulse**, giving extremely regular, controllable transfer.

**Current vs Time Waveform (described):**
A rectangular pulse train: baseline at $I_b$ for time $t_b$, then a sudden rise to $I_p$ for time $t_p$, repeating at frequency $f$.

**Four Advantages:**
1. Lower mean heat input compared to conventional spray MIG → narrow HAZ, minimal distortion.
2. All-position welding capability (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead) with spray-type bead quality.
3. Reduced spatter and porosity; excellent cosmetic bead appearance.
4. Wider parameter window → tolerant of stick-out variation; ideal for robotic automation.

(Marks distribution: Principle 2 marks, diagram 1 mark, advantages 2 marks, conclusion 2 marks.)

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Pulse frequency:**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{t_p + t_b} = \dfrac{1}{(2.5 + 10) \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1}{0.0125} = 80 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Duty cycle:**

$$
\text{DC} = \dfrac{t_p}{t_p + t_b} \times 100\% = \dfrac{2.5}{12.5} \times 100\% = 20\%
$$

**(iii) Mean current:**

$$
I_{mean} = \dfrac{I_p \cdot t_p + I_b \cdot t_b}{t_p + t_b} = \dfrac{(480)(2.5) + (90)(10)}{12.5} = \dfrac{1200 + 900}{12.5} = 168 \text{ A}
$$

**(iv) Heat input:**

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I_{mean}}{S \times 1000} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 26 \times 168}{350 \times 1000}
$$

$$
HI = \dfrac{3712.8}{350000} = 0.01061 \text{ kJ/mm} = 10.61 \text{ J/mm}
$$

(Each sub-part: 1.5 marks, with 0.5 for correct formula and 1.0 for calculation.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Cold Metal Transfer (CMT)** process. Explain how it differs from conventional short-circuit transfer welding. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Tandem MIG, Pulsed MIG, and CMT** in terms of (i) heat input, (ii) deposition rate, (iii) typical application, and (iv) limiting thickness. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**CMT Description:**
CMT is a **digitally controlled, dip-transfer (short-circuit) variant** of GMAW developed by Fronius. The unique feature is **active, mechanical wire retraction** during the short-circuit phase. The process cycle:

1. Arc ignites → current rises moderately.
2. Wire advances toward the workpiece.
3. Wire **dips into the pool** → short circuit detected.
4. **Wire feed reverses direction** (mechanically pulls the molten bridge apart).
5. **Current is reduced to near zero** during the retraction → no explosion, no spatter.
6. Wire advances again → cycle repeats at 70–100 Hz.

**Differences from Conventional Short-Circuit MIG:**

| Aspect | Conventional Short-Circuit | CMT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Droplet detachment | **Electromagnetic + surface tension** (causes spatter) | **Mechanical retraction** (no spatter) |
| Arc current during detachment | High (causes explosion of bridge) | Near-zero (heatless detachment) |
| Heat input | ~10–15 kJ/mm | **< 5 kJ/mm** |
| Spatter | Significant | **Negligible** |
| Suitable for galvanized steel | Poor (spatter, Zn burn-off) | **Excellent** |
| Process control | Open-loop or basic feedback | **Closed-loop digital, real-time** |

(Marks: 3 for description, 2 for sketch description, 2 for differences.)

---

**Model Solution (b):**

| Parameter | Tandem MIG | Pulsed MIG | CMT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Heat input** | High (1.5–3.0 kJ/mm) | Medium-Low (0.5–1.2 kJ/mm) | **Very Low** (0.2–0.5 kJ/mm) |
| **(ii) Deposition rate** | **Highest** (8–20 kg/h) | Medium (3–5 kg/h) | Low (1.5–3.0 kg/h) |
| **(iii) Typical application** | Thick-plate shipbuilding, wind towers | Structural fabrication, robotics | Automotive thin sheets, Al–Steel dissimilar |
| **(iv) Limiting thickness** | Up to 30+ mm single pass (with multi-arc) | Up to 8 mm single pass | Up to 4 mm (thin sheet specialist) |

(Each row: 1.75 marks ≈ 2 marks. 4 rows = 7 marks.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the arc efficiency factor (η) in Heat Input.** Many students write $HI = V \cdot I / S \cdot 1000$ and lose 1 mark. **Always state η explicitly** and use the correct value: GMAW ≈ 0.80, GTAW ≈ 0.55, SAW ≈ 1.0, PAW ≈ 0.60.
> 
> 2. **Unit confusion in WFR and DR.** Wire feed rate is in **m/min** in equipment displays but you need **m/s** in SI units. KTU examiners test this. Always show the unit conversion.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up Pulse Frequency and Wire Feed Rate.** Some students confuse the *electrical pulsing frequency* (Hz) with the *mechanical wire feed rate* (m/min). These are **two completely different parameters**.
> 
> 4. **Not drawing a proper waveform diagram in Pulsed MIG questions.** A **hand-drawn sketch** showing $I_p$ on top, $I_b$ at the base, with $t_p$ and $t_b$ labeled, fetches the **full 2 marks** in the KTU answer key. A textual description alone fetches only 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Stating "CMT is for stainless steel only."** CMT is **material-agnostic** — used for steel, stainless, Al, Mg, galvanized, and dissimilar combinations. Examiners deduct marks for over-restrictive applications.
> 
> 6. **Conflating "Power Density" with "Heat Input."** PD is **W/mm²** (intensive), HI is **J/mm** (extensive). KTU 2024 questions on this distinction carry 2–3 marks.
> 
> 7. **Ignoring internal choice instructions.** KTU ESE Module-wise questions allow you to skip an entire (a)-(b) block if you choose the alternative. **Read the question paper carefully** before answering.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Process Variants: Last-Minute Revision Points**
> 
> **Core Concepts:**
> - Process variants are **engineered refinements** of parent joining processes, not entirely new processes.
> - The three driving forces: **lower heat input, higher deposition, better joint metallurgy**.
> - All variants fall into 4 families: **Fusion, Solid-State, Hybrid, Geometry-modified**.
> 
> **Pulsed MIG — must remember:**
> - **One droplet per pulse** = regular, controllable transfer.
> - Formula: $I_{mean} = \dfrac{I_p t_p + I_b t_b}{t_p + t_b}$
> - Operates in **all positions** with **low heat input**.
> - Typical pulse frequency: **50–300 Hz**.
> 
> **CMT — must remember:**
> - **Mechanical wire retraction** during short-circuit.
> - **Heat input < 5 kJ/mm** — the lowest among arc processes.
> - **Zero spatter** — ideal for galvanized steel and dissimilar joining.
> - Developed by **Fronius (Austria)**.
> 
> **Tandem MIG — must remember:**
> - **Two independent arcs** in one torch (lead + trail).
> - **Deposition rates up to 20 kg/h** — productivity king.
> - **Flat/Horizontal only** (gravity-sensitive).
> - Used in **shipbuilding and wind-tower fabrication**.
> 
> **Pulsed TIG — must remember:**
> - Current pulsing → **grain refinement** in fusion zone.
> - For **thin stainless steel, titanium, superalloys**.
> - Duty cycle controls the **bead profile and depth-to-width ratio**.
> 
> **Hot-Wire TIG — must remember:**
> - Filler wire is **Joule-heated** before entering the pool.
> - **~100% deposition efficiency**, near-zero oxidation.
> - Used in **aerospace Ti-alloy welding**.
> 
> **A-TIG — must remember:**
> - Flux coating **reverses Marangoni flow** → deep, narrow penetration.
> - **2–3× penetration** of conventional TIG.
> - **Eliminates V-groove** for plates up to 10–12 mm.
> 
> **Hybrid Laser-Arc — must remember:**
> - Combines **laser (penetration) + MIG (gap-bridging)**.
> - **15–20 mm single-pass** in steel at high speed.
> - Deposition rates of **5–10 kg/h**.
> - Industry 4.0 ready.
> 
> **Formula Cheat-Sheet (one-liner recall):**
> - $HI = \eta V I / (S \times 1000)$ — always include η.
> - $I_{mean}$ = time-weighted average of $I_p$ and $I_b$.
> - $f = 1/(t_p + t_b)$.
> - $PD = VI/A_{arc}$ — high PD = deep penetration.
> - $DR \propto d^2 \times WFR \times \rho$.
> 
> **Examiner Traps to Avoid:**
> - Don't confuse **power density (W/mm²)** with **heat input (J/mm)**.
> - Don't claim CMT is for stainless steel only.
> - Don't forget to label $t_p$, $t_b$, $I_p$, $I_b$ in waveform diagrams.
> - Don't use η = 1.0 for GTAW (it's 0.5–0.65).
> 
> **Real-World Mapping (one-line memory hook):**
> - **Cars (body-in-white)** → CMT, Laser-MIG Hybrid.
> - **Ships (thick plate)** → Tandem MIG, SAW.
> - **Aerospace (Ti, superalloys)** → Hot-Wire TIG, EBW, Plasma.
> - **Nuclear (thick austenitic SS)** → Narrow-Gap TIG, EBW.
> - **Pipelines (HSAW)** → Flash Butt + FSW variants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Stages of Friction Welding
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Stages of Friction Welding

# Friction Welding — Stage-Wise Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction Welding (FW)** is classified by **ISO 857** as a *solid-state pressure welding* process in which coalescence is achieved by the heat generated from *mechanically-induced relative motion* between two workpieces held under an axial compressive force. No bulk melting occurs at the joint interface.

### The Three Canonical Stages of Rotary Friction Welding

The friction welding cycle is universally divided into **three sequential thermodynamic-mechanical stages** that govern the quality of the bond. Understanding these stages is a **high-frequency KTU module-3 topic**.

| Stage | Classical Name | Physical Condition |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Stage I** | Initial Friction Phase | Dry, asperity-level contact — solid phase |
| **Stage II** | Transition / Unsteady Phase | Onset of plastic deformation, partial sticking |
| **Stage III** | Forging (Upsetting) Phase | Axial pressure spike, expulsion of flash, consolidation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters (KTU 2024 Module-3 link):** Stage I controls *heat generation efficiency*, Stage II controls *bond-line temperature profile*, and Stage III controls *flash geometry and final grain refinement*. A poorly designed Stage II is the single most common cause of "cold weld" defects in KTU lab viva questions.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Phulka on a Tawa" Model

Imagine you are rolling a wooden cylinder (the *rotating workpiece*) against a flat hot-plate (the *stationary workpiece*) while pressing down firmly with your palm (**axial force**). Initially, only the high points of the rough cylinder touch the plate → you feel strong friction but little heat (Stage I). Within seconds, the contact points deform and begin to "stick and slip" → the plate becomes smoking hot and a thin molten film appears at the interface (Stage II). Finally, you press harder → a ring of softened metal is squeezed out as "flash," and the surfaces are metallurgically bonded (Stage III). This is exactly how friction welding behaves at the molecular level.

### Standard Process Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Interface Temperature Range:** 0.6 – 0.9 × T<sub>melting</sub> (K) — typically **900 °C to 1300 °C** for steels
- **Axial Shortening (Burn-off):** **2 – 5 mm** for a 20 mm diameter carbon-steel joint
- **Rotational Speed Range:** **200 – 4000 rpm**
- **Friction Pressure:** **20 – 100 MPa** (Stage I), rising to **150 – 300 MPa** in Stage III
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ):** 0.3 (dry) → 0 (fully plasticized) — dynamic value drops sharply in Stage II

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage I — The Initial Friction Phase (Solid-State Heating)

When the rotating member first contacts the stationary member, real contact occurs only at the summits of microscopic asperities (peaks of surface roughness, typically R<sub>a</sub> = 1.6 – 6.3 μm for as-machined surfaces).

- **Mechanism:** *Coulomb (dry) friction* dominates. The interface has not yet thermally softened.
- **Contact pressure** is very high at asperity tips → rapid adiabatic shear → local temperature spikes.
- **Heat generation is concentrated** in a thin cylindrical band (width ≈ 0.5 – 2 mm) at the interface.
- The frictional torque, T, rises sharply from zero to a peak value (called the **torque peak** or **friction peak**).
- Bulk temperature of workpieces remains low; only the interface heats up.
- **Duration:** Typically **0.5 – 3 seconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat-Generation Formula (Stage I):**
>
> $$Q_1 = \mu \cdot F_a \cdot v_r$$
>
> where v<sub>r</sub> = π·D·N is the rubbing velocity at the joint radius.

### 2.2 Stage II — Transition / Unsteady Phase (Viscoplastic Flow)

Once asperities soften, the friction regime shifts from *sliding* to a mixed regime of *partial sticking*.

- The dynamic coefficient of friction (μ) **drops continuously** because the interface becomes covered with a thin film of plasticized (visco-plastic) material.
- **Heat-generation rate begins to fall** even though axial pressure may still be rising — this is the hallmark "**friction-decay**" signature visible on a torque–time chart.
- Temperature at the interface climbs toward **0.8 – 0.9 T<sub>melting</sub>**, which is *below* the bulk melting point.
- A thin layer of **plasticized metal** (50 – 200 μm thick) is created.
- **Upset (axial shortening)** begins to accelerate — this is the *upset-onset* point.
- The microstructure of the bond-line shows a characteristic **deformed, recrystallized flow pattern**.
- **Duration:** Typically **1 – 6 seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Sticking-Sliding" Balance:** When μ → 0, the interface is in *full sticking*. The classic Vill-Sluhan model (1961) treats Stage II as a transition from sliding friction to sticking friction. This is the conceptual answer KTU examiners expect in 14-mark questions.

### 2.3 Stage III — Forging (Upsetting / Consolidation) Phase

The rotation is abruptly stopped (or allowed to decelerate under braking torque) and the axial force is **ramped up** to the forging pressure (often **1.5 – 2.5×** the friction pressure).

- **Mechanism:** Pure plastic upset; a large volume of super-plastic material is **expelled as flash** (a circumferential burr) → this physically removes the original contaminated interface.
- The axial shortening rate reaches a **second, larger peak** (called the *forging peak* or *upset peak*).
- Grain refinement occurs through dynamic recrystallization — a fine equiaxed grain zone forms at the weld line.
- The joint cools rapidly because plastic flow is now the only deformation mode and the heat source has been removed.
- **Duration:** Typically **0.2 – 1.0 second** of peak forging, followed by natural cooling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse "forging" in friction welding with conventional drop-forging. In FW, the *forging force* acts **on a hot, plasticized, but still-solid interface*. No melting has occurred in the bulk. The flash is solid (though it oxidizes immediately on exposure to air).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Stage |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Rubbing Velocity at radius r | $v_r = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | D = diameter (m), N = rotational speed (rev/s) | I, II |
| 2 | Heat Generation Rate | $\dot{Q} = \mu \cdot F_a \cdot v_r$ | F<sub>a</sub> = axial force (N), μ = friction coefficient | I |
| 3 | Frictional Power | $P = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot 2\pi N$ | T = torque (N·m), ω = angular velocity (rad/s) | I, II |
| 4 | Energy Input (over time t) | $E = \int_0^t \mu \cdot F_a \cdot v_r \, dt$ | J (joules) | I, II |
| 5 | Total Axial Shortening (Upset) | $\Delta L = \Delta L_I + \Delta L_{II} + \Delta L_{III}$ | mm | All |
| 6 | Interfacial Temperature (Carslaw–Jaeger) | $T_{int} = T_0 + \dfrac{q \cdot \sqrt{t}}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c}}$ | q = heat flux, k = thermal conductivity, ρ = density, c = specific heat | II |
| 7 | Flash Volume (conservation) | $V_{flash} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \left( D_0^2 - D_f^2 \right) \cdot \Delta L_{III}$ | D<sub>0</sub> = initial dia, D<sub>f</sub> = final dia | III |
| 8 | Upset Strain | $\varepsilon = \ln\left( \dfrac{L_0}{L_0 - \Delta L} \right)$ | L<sub>0</sub> = initial length | III |
| 9 | Bond-line Strain Rate (Stage II) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v_r}{d}$ | d = plasticized layer thickness | II |
| 10 | Hot-Flash Temperature (Mid-Stage) | $T_{flash} \approx 0.85 \cdot T_{melt}$ | K | III |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR STUDENTS:** All absolute-value bars in KTU answer scripts should be written as `\vert x \vert`, not `|x|`, to avoid the markdown table-corruption bug that students commonly report in digital submission portals.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Ti-6Al-4V blade-to-disc (BLISK) manufacture — Stage III forging is critical to suppress β-phase embrittlement.
- **Automotive Driveshafts:** SAE 1045 medium-carbon steel — Stage II duration must be ≥ 1.5 s to avoid "cold welds."
- **Bimetallic Valves:** Cu-to-steel or Al-to-steel transitions — used in cryogenic valves.
- **Hydraulic Cylinders:** Piston-rod-to-eye friction welding replaces flash-butt welding in high-end applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Energy Input Over a Friction-Welding Cycle

We derive the energy deposited into the workpieces as a function of time, explicitly showing the transition between Stage I (constant μ) and Stage II (μ → 0).

**Step 1 — Define instantaneous power** generated by friction at the interface ring of mean radius r and width w:

$$
P(t) = \mu(t) \cdot F_a(t) \cdot v_r(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the linear-velocity relation** for a rotary friction interface:

$$
v_r = \pi \cdot D \cdot N(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Combine** to get the power:

$$
P(t) = \mu(t) \cdot F_a(t) \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from t = 0 to t = t<sub>f</sub> (end of Stage II)** to obtain total friction-stage energy:

$$
E_{I+II} = \int_0^{t_f} \mu(t) \cdot F_a(t) \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot N(t)  dt
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the constant-pressure assumption** (F<sub>a</sub> = F<sub>friction</sub> = const) and constant rotational speed (N = const) typical of industrial practice:

$$
F_a(t) = F_f, \qquad N(t) = N
$$

This simplifies the integral:

$$
E_{I+II} = \pi \cdot D \cdot F_f \cdot N \int_0^{t_f} \mu(t)  dt
$$

**Step 6 — Model the friction-coefficient decay** as an exponential transition from μ<sub>0</sub> (Stage I) to μ<sub>∞</sub> (Stage II steady state):

$$
\mu(t) = \mu_\infty + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty) \cdot e^{-t / \tau}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute** and evaluate:

$$
E_{I+II} = \pi \cdot D \cdot F_f \cdot N \int_0^{t_f} \left[ \mu_\infty + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty) e^{-t/\tau} \right] dt
$$

**Step 8 — Integrate term by term:**

$$
E_{I+II} = \pi \cdot D \cdot F_f \cdot N \left[ \mu_\infty \cdot t_f + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty) \cdot \tau \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_f / \tau} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 9 — Examine the limiting cases:**

If the cycle ends in the *sliding* regime (t<sub>f</sub> ≪ τ), then $e^{-t_f / \tau} \approx 1 - t_f/\tau$ and:

$$
E_{I+II} \approx \pi \cdot D \cdot F_f \cdot N \cdot \mu_0 \cdot t_f \quad \text{(Stage I dominant)}
$$

If the cycle reaches *steady-state plasticization* (t<sub>f</sub> ≫ τ), then $e^{-t_f / \tau} \to 0$ and:

$$
E_{I+II} \approx \pi \cdot D \cdot F_f \cdot N \cdot \left( \mu_\infty \cdot t_f + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty) \cdot \tau \right) \quad \text{(Stage II dominant)}
$$

**Step 10 — Add the forging (Stage III) energy** as a *plastic-work* term:

$$
E_{III} = F_{upset} \cdot \Delta L_{III}
$$

**Step 11 — Total process energy:**

$$
E_{total} = \pi \cdot D \cdot F_f \cdot N \cdot \left[ \mu_\infty \cdot t_f + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty) \cdot \tau \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_f / \tau} \right) \right] + F_{upset} \cdot \Delta L_{III}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The first term is the *frictional heat* deposited mostly into the workpiece via conduction. The second term is the *mechanical work* of forging, of which 80–95 % is converted to additional heat. The remaining 5–20 % is stored as **residual elastic strain** at the weld — the KTU viva favourite!

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Stages from a Process Chart

The following matrix is what KTU examiners use to grade identification-type questions. **Memorize it.**

| Observable Signal | Stage I (Initial) | Stage II (Transition) | Stage III (Forging) |
|-------------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Torque (T)** | Rising sharply to **friction peak** | Falling exponentially | Near-zero (rotation stopped) |
| **Axial Force (F<sub>a</sub>)** | Constant (F<sub>1</sub>) | Constant or slightly rising | **Sudden spike to F<sub>3</sub> ≫ F<sub>1</sub>** |
| **Axial Shortening (ΔL)** | Negligible (< 0.5 mm) | Begins to rise smoothly | **Large, rapid jump** (final upset) |
| **Interface Temp.** | Rising fast (0.3 – 0.7 T<sub>m</sub>) | Plateauing at 0.8 – 0.9 T<sub>m</sub> | Drops rapidly (cooling) |
| **Friction Coefficient (μ)** | 0.3 – 0.5 (dry) | 0.05 – 0.15 (mixed) | ≈ 0 (sticking/plastic) |
| **Power (P)** | Rises to a peak | Slowly decays | → 0 |
| **Visual Flash** | None | Slight plastic extrusion | **Heavy, often spiral flash** |

### 3.3 Derivation: Bond-Line Temperature Using Heat-Conduction Model

The classical **Carslaw–Jaeger** solution for a continuous moving heat source on a semi-infinite solid is the basis for bond-line temperature prediction.

**Step 1 — Define the heat flux** at the interface, q, as a function of the friction power divided by the contact area:

$$
q = \dfrac{P(t)}{A_{contact}} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot F_a \cdot v_r}{A_{contact}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1-D transient heat-conduction solution** at depth z = 0:

$$
T_{int}(t) - T_0 = \dfrac{2 \cdot q}{\sqrt{\pi \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c}} \cdot \sqrt{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Re-arrange** to solve for time-to-reach-T<sub>int</sub>:

$$
t = \dfrac{\pi \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot (T_{int} - T_0)^2}{4 \cdot q^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in** typical steel values (k = 45 W/m·K, ρ = 7850 kg/m³, c = 490 J/kg·K, q = 50 MW/m²) to find Stage-II duration:

$$
t \approx \dfrac{\pi \cdot 45 \cdot 7850 \cdot 490 \cdot (1200 - 25)^2}{4 \cdot (50 \times 10^6)^2} \approx 3.6 \text{ s}
$$

This matches the **1 – 6 s** Stage-II range quoted in industrial handbooks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Process-Chart — The Friction-Welding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Clamp Stationary Workpiece] --> B[Bring Rotating Workpiece to Contact]
    B --> C{Stage I Initial Friction}
    C -->|Coulomb Friction Active| D[Dry Asperity Contact]
    D --> E[Torque Rises to Friction Peak]
    E --> F[Temperature Spikes at Interface 0.3 to 0.7 Tm]
    F --> G{Stage II Transition Phase}
    G -->|Mixed Sliding and Sticking| H[Plasticized Layer Forms]
    H --> I[Torque Decays Upset Begins]
    I --> J[Interface Reaches 0.85 Tm Plateau]
    J --> K{Stage III Forging Phase}
    K -->|Stop Rotation Apply Upset Force| L[Spike Axial Force to F3]
    L --> M[Flash Expulsion and Dynamic Recrystallization]
    M --> N[Hold and Cool Under Pressure]
    N --> O[Release and Unclamp Finished Weld]
    O --> P[END]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Force, Torque, Temperature Interrelationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        A1[Axial Force Fa] --> C1
        A2[Rotational Speed N] --> C1
        A3[Friction Coefficient mu] --> C1
    end
    C1[Friction Welding Controller] --> C2[Heat Generation Rate]
    C1 --> C3[Plastic Deformation Rate]
    C2 --> C4[Interface Temperature]
    C2 --> C5[Material Softening]
    C5 --> C6[Dynamic mu value]
    C6 --> C1
    C3 --> C7[Axial Shortening and Flash]
    C4 --> C8[Microstructural Evolution]
    C7 --> C9[Final Joint Strength]
    C8 --> C9
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three-Stage Functional Map (KTU Preferred Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Block1 [Stage I Initial Friction]
        B1A[Asperity Level Contact] --> B1B[Coulomb Friction Active]
        B1B --> B1C[Heat Generation Begins]
    end
    subgraph Block2 [Stage II Transition]
        B2A[Plasticized Layer Forms] --> B2B[Dynamic mu Decays]
        B2B --> B2C[Upset Starts]
    end
    subgraph Block3 [Stage III Forging]
        B3A[Rotation Halted] --> B3B[Axial Force Spikes]
        B3B --> B3C[Flash Expulsion]
        B3C --> B3D[Dynamic Recrystallization]
    end
    Block1 --> Block2
    Block2 --> Block3
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Plot — Qualitative Profiles of Process Variables vs Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageI [Stage I]
        S1A[Torque Rising] --> S1B[Force Constant]
        S1B --> S1C[Upset Near Zero]
        S1C --> S1D[Temperature Climbing]
    end
    subgraph StageII [Stage II]
        S2A[Torque Decaying] --> S2B[Force Slight Rise]
        S2B --> S2C[Upset Starts]
        S2C --> S2D[Temperature Plateau at 0.85 Tm]
    end
    subgraph StageIII [Stage III]
        S3A[Torque Near Zero] --> S3B[Force Spikes to Upset]
        S3B --> S3C[Upset Spike]
        S3C --> S3D[Temperature Falls]
    end
    StageI --> StageII
    StageII --> StageIII
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *List the three classical stages of rotary friction welding and state the dominant physical phenomenon in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Stage | Name | Dominant Phenomenon |
|-------|------|---------------------|
| I | Initial Friction | Asperity-level dry (Coulomb) friction, adiabatic heating of micro-contacts |
| II | Transition | Mixed sliding–sticking; formation of a thin plasticized layer |
| III | Forging | Plastic upset under elevated axial force; flash expulsion; dynamic recrystallization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "melting" in Stage II. There is **no bulk melting** in friction welding — the interface remains **sub-solidus** (≈ 0.85 T<sub>m</sub>). Writing "melting" costs 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Why does the frictional torque fall during Stage II even though the axial force is unchanged?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The interface heats up and develops a thin plasticized (visco-plastic) film (1 mark).
2. This film transitions the contact regime from *sliding* to *partial sticking*; the dynamic friction coefficient μ drops from ~0.4 to ~0.1 (1 mark).
3. Since torque $T = \dfrac{\mu \cdot F_a \cdot D}{2}$, with F<sub>a</sub> constant and μ falling, the measured torque decreases (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *With the help of a labeled process chart, describe the three stages of rotary friction welding. Emphasize the variation of torque, axial force, axial shortening, and interface temperature across the three stages. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

- **Diagram (2 marks):** A correctly drawn time–torque / time–force / time–upset chart with three clearly demarcated regions.
- **Stage I description (1.5 marks):** Asperity contact; Coulomb friction; friction-peak in torque; linear rise of interface temperature to ~0.7 T<sub>m</sub>; negligible axial shortening.
- **Stage II description (1.5 marks):** Plasticized layer formation; torque decay; axial force roughly constant; onset of axial upset; temperature plateau at 0.85 T<sub>m</sub>.
- **Stage III description (1.5 marks):** Rotation halted; axial force spike to forging pressure; large axial upset; flash expulsion; rapid temperature drop due to conduction away from interface and air-cooling of flash.
- **Linkage to microstructural outcome (0.5 mark):** Mention *dynamic recrystallization* producing a fine-grained weld zone.

**(b)** *Derive the expression for total heat input to a friction-welded joint, considering an exponentially decaying friction coefficient. Show the limiting cases for Stage-I-dominated and Stage-II-dominated cycles. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (7 marks):** The student is expected to reproduce the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Marks are awarded as follows:

- Correct power equation P(t) = μ·F·v<sub>r</sub> — *1 mark*
- Correct substitution of v<sub>r</sub> = π·D·N — *1 mark*
- Setup of integral with μ(t) = μ<sub>∞</sub> + (μ<sub>0</sub>−μ<sub>∞</sub>)e<sup>−t/τ</sup> — *2 marks*
- Correct evaluation of the integral — *1.5 marks*
- Derivation of both limiting cases (t ≪ τ and t ≫ τ) — *1 mark*
- Final boxed equation including forging term — *0.5 mark*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** *Explain the role of the "plasticized interfacial layer" in friction welding. How does its formation govern the transition from Stage I to Stage II, and what is its effect on the final weld microstructure? **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A thin (50 – 200 μm) layer of material at the interface where temperature has reached 0.8 – 0.9 T<sub>m</sub> and the material exhibits viscoplastic flow.
- **Role in transition (2 marks):** The layer changes the local friction regime from sliding to mixed/sticking, which is observable as a torque-decay on the process chart. It is the *physical marker* of the Stage-I → Stage-II transition.
- **Governing physics (2 marks):** Once the layer forms, friction is governed by the *shear strength* of the layer rather than by Coulomb friction; hence μ drops.
- **Microstructural effect (2 marks):** During Stage III, the layer is largely expelled as flash; the residual material undergoes *dynamic recrystallization*, giving a fine equiaxed grain zone at the weld line that improves ductility and toughness.

**(b)** *A 25 mm diameter mild-steel (AISI 1020) rod is to be joined to a similar rod by rotary friction welding. The friction pressure is 50 MPa, the rotational speed is 1500 rpm, the Stage-I friction coefficient is 0.4, and the Stage-II steady-state friction coefficient is 0.1. Stage I lasts 1.0 s and Stage II lasts 3.0 s. Calculate the total energy input, neglecting Stage III. **(7 marks)***

**Given:**
- D = 0.025 m
- p = 50 MPa → F<sub>a</sub> = p × A = 50 × 10⁶ × (π/4)(0.025)² = **24,544 N**
- N = 1500 rpm = 25 rev/s
- μ<sub>0</sub> = 0.4, μ<sub>∞</sub> = 0.1
- t<sub>1</sub> = 1.0 s, t<sub>2</sub> = 3.0 s

**Step 1 — Rubbing velocity** (at joint radius):

$$
v_r = \pi \cdot D \cdot N = \pi \cdot 0.025 \cdot 25 = 1.963 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Average power, Stage I:**

$$
P_I = \mu_0 \cdot F_a \cdot v_r = 0.4 \cdot 24{,}544 \cdot 1.963 = 19{,}277 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy, Stage I:**

$$
E_I = P_I \cdot t_1 = 19{,}277 \cdot 1.0 = 19{,}277 \text{ J} \approx 19.3 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Average power, Stage II** (use μ<sub>∞</sub> as approximate):

$$
P_{II} = \mu_\infty \cdot F_a \cdot v_r = 0.1 \cdot 24{,}544 \cdot 1.963 = 4{,}819 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy, Stage II:**

$$
E_{II} = P_{II} \cdot t_2 = 4{,}819 \cdot 3.0 = 14{,}457 \text{ J} \approx 14.5 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 6 — Total:**

$$
E_{total} = E_I + E_{II} = 19.3 + 14.5 = 33.8 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Answer: E<sub>total</sub> ≈ 33.8 kJ** (neglecting Stage III forging work)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Loss-of-Marks Pitfall:**
> 1. Mixing up the rubbing-velocity formula — students often write v = 2πrN and forget to multiply r by 2. **Always re-state v<sub>r</sub> = π·D·N in your answer.**
> 2. Using 1500 rpm directly in the formula without converting to rev/s (Hz). 1 rev/s = 60 rpm. *Subtract 1 mark* if this conversion is missing.
> 3. Using *force* (N) without computing it from pressure × area. The question gives **pressure**, not force — a 2-mark deduction if skipped.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision)

- **Three stages — Initial, Transition, Forging** (a.k.a. Friction, Heating, Forging) — in that order, *always*.
- **No bulk melting** in any stage; the interface is held at **0.6 – 0.9 T<sub>m</sub>**, which is *sub-solidus*.
- **Stage I hallmark:** Rising torque to a *friction peak* under constant axial force.
- **Stage II hallmark:** Decaying torque, *onset of axial upset*, plateau in interface temperature at ≈ 0.85 T<sub>m</sub>.
- **Stage III hallmark:** Rotation stopped, axial force *spikes* (typically 1.5 – 2.5× friction pressure), flash expulsion, dynamic recrystallization.
- **Coefficient of friction μ** is the *master variable* — it controls the transition between sliding and sticking.
- **Flash is solid** (oxidizes rapidly on exposure to air) — a circumferential burr whose width gives a quick quality indication in the KTU lab.
- **The single most-quoted formula in KTU 2024 module-3:** $Q = \mu \cdot F \cdot v_r = \mu \cdot F \cdot \pi D N$.
- **Exam tip:** Always draw a **torque–time chart** with three regions, label the *friction peak*, *torque decay*, and *upset peak* — this is a guaranteed 4–5 marks in 14-mark answers.
- **Real-world link to remember:** Aerospace BLISK manufacturing, automotive drive shafts, bimetallic valve trims, hydraulic piston rods.
- **Forging is NOT melting:** When asked "what is happening in Stage III?", write *plastic upset + dynamic recrystallization*, never "the metal is melted and resolidifies."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Process Parameters # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Process Parameters** in advanced metal joining are the *quantifiable independent input variables* that an engineer deliberately sets on the welding/joining machine to govern the thermal, mechanical, and metallurgical response of the workpiece and thereby produce a sound joint. They are the "control knobs" of the joining process — change them, and you change the microstructure, residual stress, distortion, defect content, and mechanical strength of the joint. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome CO3 of PEIET523), process parameters are classified into four functional families: | Family | Meaning | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Thermal parameters** | Control the heat input into the workpiece | Power (kW), current (mA), pulse duration (ms), focus position (mm) | | **Mechanical parameters** | Control the forces and motion between tool/workpiece | Rotational speed (rpm), traverse/welding speed (mm/min), axial force (kN), plunge depth (mm) | | **Geometric parameters** | Control the tool/shape that delivers energy | Tool shoulder diameter, pin profile, beam spot diameter, horn tip footprint | | **Atmospheric/Environmental parameters** | Control the medium surrounding the weld | Shielding gas flow rate, vacuum level (mbar), ambient humidity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Board examiner routinely tests the relationship between *one* process parameter (e.g., welding speed) and *one* output response (e.g., weld width, hardness, HAZ size). Always quote the directional trend in your answer: "**As rotational speed increases, peak temperature increases and grain size coarsens**". ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are cooking *appam* (a Kerala breakfast delicacy) on a pan. The **pan temperature**, the **amount of batter you pour**, the **time you let it cook**, and the **angle/tilt of the ladle** determine whether the appam is crispy at the edges and soft in the middle — or burnt and raw. In the same way, in Friction Stir Welding (FSW), if you **spin the tool too fast** (high rpm) and **move it too slowly** (low traverse speed), the metal "overcooks" — grains grow, the joint softens. If you **spin too slowly and move too fast**, the metal does not get enough heat — you get a "cold" joint full of voids and tunnel defects. So **process parameters = your recipe**, and **joint quality = the dish**. Master the recipe, master the weld. > [!NOTE] > A golden rule in advanced joining: **Heat Input (HI)** is the single most important derived quantity that links the primary parameters to the joint properties. $$\text{Heat Input} = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{v}$$ where $\eta$ is process efficiency, $P$ is the source power, and $v$ is the travel speed. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** for radiative heat loss: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ - **Typical FSW tool steel** upper service temperature: **~1000 °C** (below this, tool wear is acceptable). - **Typical LBW/EBW chamber vacuum** for EBW: **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ mbar**. - **Standard SI unit of power density**: **W/mm²** (used universally in LBW/EBW). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Process Parameters Matter — The "Why" Every advanced joining process can be thought of as a **cause → effect** chain: $$\underbrace{(\text{Process Parameters})}_{\text{CAUSE (inputs)}} \longrightarrow \underbrace{(\text{Thermal Cycle})}_{\text{TRANSIENT HEAT FLOW}} \longrightarrow \underbrace{(\text{Microstructure})}_{\text{Grain size, phases}} \longrightarrow \underbrace{(\text{Properties})}_{\text{Strength, fatigue, corrosion}}$$ The KTU examiner loves asking: *"How does parameter X affect property Y?"* You must answer in **directional** form: *increases, decreases, peaks at, or has a non-monotonic relationship with*. ## 2.2 Friction Stir Welding (FSW) — Process Parameters FSW uses a non-consumable rotating tool that softens (but does not melt) the material via frictional and plastic-deformation heating, then mechanically stirs the plasticized metal to forge a joint in solid state. **Primary parameters:** - **Rotational speed** $N$ (rpm) — typically **300 to 3000 rpm** for aluminium alloys. - **Welding/Traverse speed** $v$ (mm/min) — typically **20 to 500 mm/min**. - **Axial/Plunge force** $F_z$ (kN) — typically **5 to 50 kN**. - **Tool tilt angle** $\theta$ (°) — usually **2° to 3°** from vertical. - **Tool geometry** — shoulder diameter $D_s$, pin diameter $D_p$, pin length $L_p$, thread pitch. **Secondary (response) parameters:** - **Stir zone (SZ) temperature** $T_{peak}$ - **Heat input per unit length** $q$ (J/mm) - **Strain rate** $\dot{\varepsilon}$ in the deformation zone ## 2.3 Laser Beam Welding (LBW) — Process Parameters A focused coherent monochromatic photon beam melts and fuses the workpiece. Parameters: - **Laser power** $P$ (W or kW) — **0.5 kW to 25 kW** for industrial systems. - **Welding speed** $v$ (mm/s or m/min). - **Focal point position** (mm above/below the workpiece surface). - **Beam mode** (TEM$_{00}$, TEM$_{01}$*, multi-mode). - **Spot diameter** $d$ (mm). - **Pulse parameters** (for pulsed Nd:YAG): pulse energy $E_p$ (J), pulse duration $t_p$ (ms), pulse frequency $f$ (Hz), duty cycle. - **Shielding gas** (Ar, He, N₂) flow rate $Q$ (L/min). **Power density** is the defining metric: $$\text{Power Density} = \frac{P}{\pi (d/2)^2} \quad \left[\text{W/mm}^2\right]$$ - **< 10⁵ W/mm²** → conduction mode (shallow, wide weld). - **> 10⁶ W/mm²** → keyhole mode (deep, narrow weld with high depth-to-width ratio). ## 2.4 Electron Beam Welding (EBW) — Process Parameters A focused beam of high-velocity electrons (in vacuum) generates heat upon impact with the workpiece. - **Accelerating voltage** $V$ (kV) — typically **30 to 200 kV**. - **Beam current** $I_b$ (mA) — typically **10 to 1000 mA**. - **Beam power** $P_b = V \cdot I_b$ (W or kW). - **Welding speed** $v$ (mm/s). - **Focus current** $I_f$ (mA) — controls the focal spot size. - **Working distance** (mm) — distance from electron gun to workpiece. - **Vacuum level** — **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ mbar** (mandatory for hard vacuum EBW). **Depth-to-width ratio** in EBW can reach **30:1**, far exceeding arc welding's 1:1 to 2:1. ## 2.5 Ultrasonic Welding (USW) — Process Parameters High-frequency (typically **20 kHz, 35 kHz, or 40 kHz**) mechanical vibration combined with static pressure produces solid-state welds, predominantly for thin sheet and foil (e.g., battery tabs, aluminium foils). - **Vibration frequency** $f$ (kHz) — fixed by the transducer. - **Vibration amplitude** $a$ (µm) — typically **5 to 50 µm**. - **Clamping/static pressure** $F$ (N) — typically **50 to 3000 N**. - **Weld time** $t_w$ (s) — typically **0.1 to 2 s**. - **Weld energy** $E_w$ (J) — the controlled output in modern USW controllers. The **mechanical power input** is given by: $$P_{mech} = F \cdot v_{sonotrode} = F \cdot (2\pi f \cdot a) \cdot \cos\phi$$ where $\phi$ is the phase lag between force and velocity (ideally ≈ 0° in resonance). ## 2.6 Diffusion Bonding (DB) — Process Parameters A solid-state joining process where two clean, flat surfaces are held together at elevated temperature and pressure for a long time, allowing atoms to diffuse across the interface. - **Bonding temperature** $T$ (°C or K) — typically **0.5 to 0.8 $T_m$** (where $T_m$ is the melting point in K). - **Bonding pressure** $p$ (MPa) — typically **1 to 50 MPa**. - **Bonding time** $t$ (s or min) — typically **10 min to 4 hours**. - **Vacuum or inert atmosphere** — typically **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ mbar** or high-purity Ar. - **Surface roughness** (µm) — must be low (typically **Ra < 0.4 µm**) to promote intimate contact. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Process | Formula / Metric | Variables & Units | Validity / Note | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | All | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot P}{v}$ | $HI$ = J/mm; $\eta$ = efficiency; $P$ = W; $v$ = mm/s | Generic heat input; $\eta \approx 0.8$–$0.95$ for LBW/EBW, $\eta \approx 0.4$–$0.6$ for FSW | | 2 | FSW | $T_{peak} = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{N}{v}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{\mu P_{axial}}{\text{shoulder area}}$ | $T_{peak}$ in °C; $K,\alpha$ = empirical | Most influential group = **$N/v$ (pseudo-heat-index)** | | 3 | FSW | $q = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi \mu F_z \omega R_s$ | $q$ = W/mm; $\mu$ = friction coeff.; $F_z$ = N; $\omega$ = rad/s; $R_s$ = shoulder radius (m) | Per unit length heat flux | | 4 | LBW | $\text{PD} = \dfrac{P}{\pi (d/2)^2}$ | PD = W/mm²; $P$ = W; $d$ = spot diameter (mm) | Conduction mode if PD $< 10^5$; Keyhole if PD $> 10^6$ | | 5 | EBW | $P_b = V \cdot I_b$ | $V$ = kV; $I_b$ = mA; $P_b$ = kW | Beam power | | 6 | USW | $E_w = P_{mech} \cdot t_w$ | $E_w$ = J; $P_{mech}$ = W; $t_w$ = s | Modern USW uses energy-control mode | | 7 | DB | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | $D$ = m²/s; $Q_d$ = J/mol; $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K; $T$ = K | **Arrhenius diffusion law** | | 8 | DB | $x^2 \approx D \cdot t$ | $x$ = diffusion depth (m); $t$ = time (s) | One-dim. approximation of Fick's 2nd law | | 9 | All | $D/W \text{ ratio} = \dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{width}}$ | dimensionless | EBW: 10:1–30:1; LBW: 2:1–10:1; Arc: 1:1–2:1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's tip:** Never use the bare pipe symbol $\vert$ in tables. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ (e.g., $\eta \mid_{LBW} = 0.9$) to avoid breaking markdown syntax. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350)**: FSW is used for fuselage and wing skin panels — process parameters are tightly controlled (±2% on rpm) to certify fatigue life. - **Automotive (EV battery packs)**: USW is used to join copper/aluminium battery tabs — *weld energy* is the controlled parameter because it is a more reproducible output than time/pressure. - **Nuclear & Defence**: EBW is used for thick-section reactor components and missile casings because of the deep, narrow, low-distortion weld. - **Electronics**: LBW is used for hermetic sealing of medical devices and lithium-ion battery cells. - **Turbocharger & jet engine blades**: Diffusion bonding + superplastic forming (DB/SPF) produces hollow titanium blades — parameters must be precisely controlled to avoid Kirkendall porosity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input Formula in Friction Stir Welding (FSW) The KTU board regularly asks the derivation of heat input. We derive it rigorously. ### Statement Show that the heat generated per unit length of weld in FSW is given by $$q = \frac{2}{3}\,\pi\,\mu\,F_z\,\omega\,R_s$$ where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction at the tool–workpiece interface, $F_z$ is the axial plunge force, $\omega$ is the angular speed (rad/s), and $R_s$ is the shoulder radius. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Define the elemental friction force.** Consider an elemental annular ring on the shoulder at radius $r$ with width $dr$.
