The elemental normal force on this ring is the axial force distributed over the shoulder area: $$dF_z = \frac{F_z}{\pi R_s^2} \cdot 2\pi r\,dr = \frac{2 F_z}{R_s^2}\,r\,dr$$ **Step 2 — Define the elemental tangential (friction) force.** The friction force opposing rotation on this ring is: $$dF_t = \mu\,dF_z = \frac{2\mu F_z}{R_s^2}\,r\,dr$$ **Step 3 — Define the elemental torque.** The torque contribution from this ring is force × moment arm: $$dT = r\,dF_t = \frac{2\mu F_z}{R_s^2}\,r^2\,dr$$ **Step 4 — Integrate over the shoulder.** $$\begin{aligned} T &= \int_0^{R_s} \frac{2\mu F_z}{R_s^2}\,r^2\,dr \\ &= \frac{2\mu F_z}{R_s^2} \left[\frac{r^3}{3}\right]_0^{R_s} \\ &= \frac{2\mu F_z}{R_s^2} \cdot \frac{R_s^3}{3} \\ &= \frac{2}{3}\,\mu\,F_z\,R_s \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Convert torque to power.** The mechanical power dissipated as heat at the shoulder is: $$P = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2}{3}\,\mu\,F_z\,R_s\,\omega \quad [\text{W}]$$ **Step 6 — Convert to heat input per unit weld length.** The tool advances at traverse speed $v$ (in mm/s, converted to m/s as needed). Heat input per unit length is: $$\begin{aligned} q &= \frac{P}{v} \\ &= \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{\mu F_z R_s \omega}{v} \end{aligned}$$ Since $\omega$ is in rad/s, $R_s$ in m, $F_z$ in N, and $v$ in m/s, $q$ is in **W·s/m = J/m**. Dividing by 1000 gives **J/mm** — the unit used in KTU problems. > [!NOTE] > In KTU board valuations, you may safely quote this as the **standard FSW heat-input formula** and add the note: *"This formula neglects the heat generated at the pin (which adds a small correction of 5–10%)."* ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Diffusion Depth in Diffusion Bonding ### Statement Starting from Fick's first law of diffusion, derive the approximate diffusion depth $x$ in a diffusion bond held at temperature $T$ for time $t$. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Fick's first law in 1D.** $$J = -D\,\frac{\partial c}{\partial x}$$ where $J$ is flux (mol/m²·s), $c$ is concentration, and $D$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s). **Step 2 — Fick's second law (continuity).** $$\frac{\partial c}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{\partial^2 c}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 3 — For a thin interface of "infinite" source, the similarity solution** gives the characteristic diffusion length: $$x \approx \sqrt{D\,t}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the Arrhenius form of $D$:** $$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_d}{R\,T}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Combine:** $$\begin{aligned} x &\approx \sqrt{D_0\,t \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_d}{R\,T}\right)} \\ &= \sqrt{D_0\,t} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_d}{2R\,T}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Take the natural log for a sensitivity check:** $$\ln x = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(D_0\,t) - \frac{Q_d}{2R\,T}$$ This shows that **doubling the absolute temperature $T$ does NOT double the diffusion depth** — the dependence is exponential. This is why diffusion bonding temperatures are kept within the **0.5–0.8 $T_m$** window. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Welding Speed and Weld Width (LBW) A conduction-mode LBW pool on a thick plate can be approximated by a 2D semicircular cross-section. The heat balance gives: $$\text{Width } w \approx \sqrt{\frac{4 \alpha P}{\pi e \rho c_p v\, t_p}}$$ where $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s), $P$ = power (W), $e$ = base of natural log, $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K), $v$ = welding speed (m/s), $t_p$ = plate thickness (m). **Interpretation:** $w \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{v}}$ — doubling the speed reduces width by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** In an FSW operation on AA6061-T6 plates, the following parameters are set: rotational speed $N = 1200$ rpm, traverse speed $v = 60$ mm/min, axial force $F_z = 8$ kN, shoulder diameter $D_s = 18$ mm, and coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.45$. Compute the heat input per unit weld length in J/mm. Assume the pin contribution is negligible. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert units.** $$\omega = 2\pi N/60 = 2\pi \times 1200/60 = 40\pi \ \text{rad/s} \approx 125.66 \ \text{rad/s}$$ $$R_s = D_s/2 = 9 \ \text{mm} = 9 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}$$ $$F_z = 8000 \ \text{N}, \quad \mu = 0.45$$ $$v = 60 \ \text{mm/min} = 1 \ \text{mm/s} = 10^{-3} \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute torque.** $$T = \frac{2}{3}\mu F_z R_s = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.45 \times 8000 \times 9 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\begin{aligned} T &= \frac{2 \times 0.45 \times 8000 \times 9}{3} \times 10^{-3} \\ &= \frac{64800}{3} \times 10^{-3} \\ &= 21600 \times 10^{-3} \\ &= 21.6 \ \text{N·m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute power.** $$P = T \cdot \omega = 21.6 \times 125.66 \approx 2714.3 \ \text{W}$$ **Step 4 — Compute heat input per unit length.** $$q = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{2714.3 \ \text{W}}{1 \ \text{mm/s}} = 2714.3 \ \text{J/mm}$$ **Step 5 — Final answer.** $$\boxed{q \approx 2714 \ \text{J/mm} \ (\text{or } 2.71 \ \text{kJ/mm})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This value is realistic for FSW of aluminium — typical industrial FSW heat input is **0.5–5 kJ/mm**. A KTU student scoring full marks should quote the order of magnitude as a sanity check. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Parameter → Effect | Parameter ↑ (increases) | Heat input | Weld width | Penetration depth | Stir zone grain size | Joint strength (typical) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Rotational speed $N$ (FSW) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (coarsens) | Peaks, then ↓ | | Traverse speed $v$ (FSW) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | Peaks, then ↓ | | Axial force $F_z$ (FSW) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Peaks, then ↓ | | Power $P$ (LBW) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (keyhole) | — | Peaks, then ↓ | | Speed $v$ (LBW) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | — | Peaks, then ↓ | | Voltage $V$ (EBW) | ↑ | ↑ (slight) | ↑ (large) | — | Peaks, then ↓ | | Temperature $T$ (DB) | — | — | — | Coarsens substrate | ↑ (monotonic until grain growth) | | Pressure $p$ (DB) | — | — | — | Refines bond-line voids | ↑ until plastic flow limit | | Amplitude $a$ (USW) | ↑ (locally) | ↑ | — | — | Peaks, then ↓ | | Weld time $t_w$ (USW) | ↑ | ↑ | — | — | Peaks, then ↓ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How a Process Parameter Becomes a Joint Property ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Engineer sets process parameters
N, v, F, P, T, p, t] --> B[Energy Source delivers power
Mechanical / Thermal / Beam] B --> C[Workpiece responds
Plastic deformation / Melting / Diffusion] C --> D[Thermal cycle develops
T peak and t 8-5 cooling] D --> E[Microstructure evolves
Grain size, phases, HAZ] E --> F[Mechanical properties emerge
Strength, fatigue, hardness] F --> G{Joint quality
acceptable?} G -- Yes --> H[Certified joint
serial production] G -- No --> I[Defect analysis
porosity, tunnel, void] I --> A ``` ## 4.2 FSW Process Parameter Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Rotational speed N rpm] --> X1(Heat generation) P2[Traverse speed v mm/min] --> X1 P3[Axial force Fz kN] --> X1 P4[Tool tilt angle theta deg] --> X2(Material flow) P5[Pin geometry] --> X2 X1 --> Y1[Stir zone temperature T peak] X2 --> Y1 Y1 --> Z1[Microstructure: grain size, precipitates] Z1 --> Z2[Ultimate tensile strength] Z1 --> Z3[Fatigue life] Z1 --> Z4[Ductility elongation pct] ``` ## 4.3 Process Parameter Decision Tree — Defect Diagnosis in FSW ```mermaid flowchart TD S{Defect observed} --> S1[Tunnel defect
on advancing side] S --> S2[Flash / burr
excessive] S --> S3[Surface galling] S --> S4[Kissing bond] S1 --> A1[Increase N, decrease v, increase Fz] S2 --> A2[Decrease Fz, decrease tilt] S3 --> A3[Reduce N, polish tool] S4 --> A4[Increase T peak: higher N, lower v] ``` ## 4.4 USW Process Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USW[Ultrasonic Welding Stack] D1[AC supply 20-40 kHz] --> D2[Piezoelectric transducer] D2 --> D3[Booster / horn] D3 --> D4[Sonotrode tip] D4 --> D5[Workpiece sheets] D6[Anvil with knurl pattern] --> D5 end D7[Static pressure F] --> D5 D8[Weld time tw] --> D1 D1 --> D9[Controller monitors
weld energy E in J] D9 --> D10{Energy limit reached} D10 -- Yes --> D11[Weld complete] D10 -- No --> D1 ``` ## 4.5 LBW Beam Mode vs Power Density Diagram (Block) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Laser power P and spot d] --> B[Compute Power Density PD = P divided by spot area] B --> C{PD value} C -- PD less than 10^5 W/mm^2 --> D[Conduction mode
shallow wide weld
D/W less than 1] C -- PD between 10^5 and 10^6 --> E[Transition mode] C -- PD greater than 10^6 W/mm^2 --> F[Keyhole mode
deep narrow weld
D/W 5 to 10 plus] D --> G[Low penetration, low HAZ, low distortion] F --> H[High penetration, narrow HAZ, risk of porosity] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU viva:** A common viva question is *"If you increase the welding speed in LBW, will the depth-to-width ratio increase or decrease?"* Answer: **Increase** — because higher speed reduces conduction-mode widening, but if the beam is already in keyhole mode, the keyhole may collapse, leading to a sudden drop in penetration. So the relationship is **non-monotonic in the transition regime**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (model)]** *Define "process parameters" in advanced metal joining techniques. Give two examples each for FSW and LBW.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Process parameters are the *controllable independent input variables* that govern the thermal, mechanical, and metallurgical response of the workpiece during joining. They are deliberately set by the operator and include thermal, mechanical, geometric, and environmental variables. - **FSW examples:** (i) Rotational speed (rpm), (ii) Traverse speed (mm/min), (iii) Axial plunge force (kN). - **LBW examples:** (i) Laser power (W/kW), (ii) Pulse duration (ms), (iii) Focal position (mm), (iv) Shielding gas flow rate (L/min). *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for FSW examples, 1 mark for LBW examples.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (model)]** *What is the "N/v ratio" in FSW, and why is it important?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The N/v ratio is the **rotational speed to traverse speed** ratio, with units of **rotations per mm of weld length**. It is a key process index that correlates with: - The peak temperature in the stir zone (higher N/v → higher $T_{peak}$). - The strain rate and material flow around the pin. - The likelihood of defects: a low N/v gives cold welds with tunnel defects; a high N/v gives hot welds with flash and grain coarsening. - An optimum N/v exists for each alloy, beyond which strength falls. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for temperature effect, 1 mark for defect & optimum explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU Pattern Reminder:** In ESE, Module 3 questions carry 14 marks, with internal choice (either/or). You must answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B in full. ### Question A (14 Marks) — FSW-Dominant **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 (model)]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the FSW process. List the **primary process parameters** and classify them. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for **heat input per unit length** in FSW from first principles, starting from the elemental friction force on the tool shoulder. State two assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Process description and parameters (7 marks):** *Description (3 marks):* Friction Stir Welding is a **solid-state** joining process in which a non-consumable rotating tool, consisting of a shoulder and a profiled pin, is plunged into the joint line between two clamped plates. Frictional heat at the shoulder and plastic deformation around the pin soften the material to a plasticized state. As the tool traverses along the joint, the plasticized material is stirred from the leading edge to the trailing edge, forging a continuous solid-state bond **without melting**. (Schematic: tool with shoulder + threaded pin; arrows showing rotation and traverse; advancing side AS and retreating side RS.) *Parameters classified (4 marks):* (Diagrams are not in our text but the verbal sketch is acceptable.) - **Primary / machine-set parameters:** rotational speed $N$ (rpm), traverse speed $v$ (mm/min), axial plunge force $F_z$ (kN), tool tilt angle $\theta$ (°). - **Secondary / tool-geometry parameters:** shoulder diameter $D_s$, pin profile (cylindrical, tapered, threaded), pin length $L_p$, pin diameter $D_p$. - **Environmental parameters:** backing plate material, clamping method, shielding gas (used occasionally to suppress oxidation). **(b) Heat-input derivation (7 marks):** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Award marks as: - 1 mark — Writing the elemental normal force $dF_z$ and friction force $dF_t$. - 1 mark — Writing the elemental torque $dT = r\,dF_t$. - 1 mark — Setting up and evaluating the integral from 0 to $R_s$. - 1 mark — Final expression for torque: $T = \tfrac{2}{3}\mu F_z R_s$. - 1 mark — Power = $T \cdot \omega$. - 1 mark — Heat input per unit length $q = P/v$. - 1 mark — Stating the two assumptions: (i) uniform pressure distribution on shoulder, (ii) pin contribution is neglected (or that the entire axial load is borne by the shoulder). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often forget to **square the radius** in the integral and write $dF_z$ as $\tfrac{F_z}{\pi R_s^2}$ correctly. > - Some students write $P = T \cdot N$ (using rpm) instead of $P = T \cdot \omega$ (using rad/s). **Always convert** $N$ to $\omega$ explicitly to earn full marks. > - Omitting the two **assumptions** costs 1 mark. State them before/after the derivation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — LBW/EBW-Dominant **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 (model)]** **(a)** Compare **LBW and EBW** in terms of process parameters, depth-to-width ratio, and the role of vacuum. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pulsed Nd:YAG laser is used to weld a 1.2 mm thick stainless-steel lap joint. The pulse energy is 8 J, pulse duration is 4 ms, and pulse frequency is 10 Hz. The traverse speed is 5 mm/s and spot diameter is 0.8 mm. Calculate (i) the **peak power per pulse**, (ii) the **average power**, (iii) the **power density**, and (iv) the **heat input per unit length** in J/mm. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison (7 marks):** | Criterion | LBW | EBW | |---|---|---| | Energy source | Photons (coherent light) | Accelerated electrons | | Atmosphere | Shielding gas (Ar/He); atmospheric LBW possible | **Hard vacuum required**, $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ mbar | | Primary parameters | Power $P$, speed $v$, focal position, beam mode, pulse parameters | Voltage $V$, beam current $I_b$, focus current $I_f$, welding speed $v$ | | Power density | $10^5$–$10^7$ W/mm² | $10^6$–$10^8$ W/mm² | | Depth-to-width ratio | 2:1 to 10:1 | 10:1 to 30:1 (deepest of all fusion processes) | | HAZ / distortion | Very narrow HAZ, low distortion | Extremely narrow HAZ, lowest distortion | | Material suitability | Metals, non-metals, dissimilar | Mostly metals; vacuum limits size | | Vacuum role | Not essential (atmospheric LBW exists) | **Essential** — prevents electron scatter, avoids X-ray hazard, removes plasma interference | | Typical application | Auto body, battery sealing | Aero, nuclear, defence thick sections | *Mark split: 2 marks for parameters, 2 marks for D/W ratio, 2 marks for vacuum role, 1 mark for one valid application difference.* **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):** Given: $E_p = 8$ J, $t_p = 4$ ms $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $f = 10$ Hz, $v = 5$ mm/s, $d = 0.8$ mm. **(i) Peak power per pulse:** $$P_{peak} = \frac{E_p}{t_p} = \frac{8 \ \text{J}}{4 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}} = 2000 \ \text{W} = 2 \ \text{kW}$$ **(ii) Average power:** $$P_{avg} = E_p \cdot f = 8 \ \text{J} \times 10 \ \text{Hz} = 80 \ \text{W}$$ **(iii) Power density:** $$\text{Spot area} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.8)^2}{4} = 0.5027 \ \text{mm}^2$$ $$\text{PD} = \frac{P_{peak}}{\text{Area}} = \frac{2000}{0.5027} = 3978.9 \ \text{W/mm}^2 \approx 3.98 \times 10^3 \ \text{W/mm}^2$$ **(iv) Heat input per unit length:** $$HI = \frac{P_{avg}}{v} = \frac{80 \ \text{W}}{5 \ \text{mm/s}} = 16 \ \text{J/mm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout for Numerical:** > - Many students confuse **peak power** (per pulse) with **average power** (over many pulses). Use the correct numerator: $E_p/t_p$ for peak, $E_p \cdot f$ for average. > - **Power density** requires the **peak** power, not average. Using average power yields a 25× smaller (and wrong) PD. > - **Heat input** uses **average** power, not peak. Using peak would overestimate heat input. > - **Spot area** is $\pi d^2/4$, not $\pi d^2$ or $\pi d$. One mark lost per unit error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Process parameters = control inputs; process responses = outputs (T, microstructure, strength).** - **The single most important derived quantity** across all advanced joining processes is **heat input** $HI = \eta P/v$. - **FSW** is **solid-state**; it does **not** melt the parent metal — distinguish it from LBW/EBW which are fusion processes. - **FSW heat input** (per unit length): $q = \tfrac{2}{3}\mu F_z R_s \omega / v$. Pin contribution is small (5–10%) and often neglected. - **The N/v ratio** in FSW is the master index — it correlates with peak temperature, defect type, and optimum strength. - **FSW defects:** *low* N/v → tunnel/void defects (cold joint); *high* N/v → flash, thinning, grain coarsening. - **Tool tilt** of 2°–3° improves forging and consolidates material at the trailing edge. - **LBW modes:** Conduction (PD < $10^5$ W/mm², shallow) vs Keyhole (PD > $10^6$ W/mm², deep). - **EBW** delivers the highest **depth-to-width ratio** (up to 30:1) and operates in **vacuum** ($10^{-3}$–$10^{-6}$ mbar). - **EBW power**: $P_b = V \cdot I_b$ (voltage in kV × current in mA → kW). - **USW** uses **fixed frequency** (20/35/40 kHz) and **controls weld energy** in modern controllers. - **USW mechanical power**: $P = F \cdot 2\pi f a \cos\phi$ — amplitude $a$ in µm is the main "knob". - **Diffusion bonding** is governed by the **Arrhenius equation**: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q_d/RT)$, and diffusion depth $x \approx \sqrt{Dt}$. - **DB temperatures** are kept in the range **0.5–0.8 $T_m$** to avoid melting and excessive grain growth. - **DB pressure** (1–50 MPa) must be high enough to flatten asperities but low enough to avoid macro-plastic deformation. - **D/W ratio** is the metric KTU examiners use to compare fusion processes quickly: EBW > LBW > GTAW > SMAW. - **Power density** is the deciding metric for laser/electron beam processes — it determines conduction vs keyhole. - **Process efficiency $\eta$** is highest for EBW (≈ 0.9) and lowest for FSW shoulder friction (≈ 0.4–0.5). - **Always sanity-check** numerical answers against industrial order-of-magnitude values: FSW $q$ ≈ 0.5–5 kJ/mm; LBW $HI$ ≈ 5–100 J/mm; EBW $HI$ ≈ 50–500 J/mm. - **Non-monotonic** parameter–property curves are the rule, not the exception. Always state "**peaks at an optimum, then decreases**" rather than a simple "increases" or "decreases". - **Shielding gas** in LBW (Ar/He) prevents plasma plume and oxidation; in EBW, vacuum serves the same purpose more effectively. - **Common viva traps:** (i) confusing pulse energy with average energy, (ii) writing $P = T \cdot N$ instead of $P = T \cdot \omega$, (iii) forgetting to convert mm/min to mm/s in HI calculations. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute KTU 2024 Strategy:** For 14-mark FSW derivation questions, memorize the FSW heat-input derivation **line by line**. It appears almost every ESE cycle. Pair it with the **N/v vs defect** discussion for the descriptive 7-mark sub-part to maximize score.

topics.title: Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Advantages # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to "Advantages" in Advanced Metal Joining In the context of KTU's **PEIET523 – Advanced Metal Joining Techniques**, Module 3 typically encompasses high-energy-density and solid-state joining processes such as **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)**, **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)**, **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**, and **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**. The "Advantages" topic is a **comparative analytical study** of why these processes are preferred over conventional fusion welding methods (like SMAW, GMAW, GTAW) for specific industrial applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > *Advantages refer to the measurable technical, metallurgical, economic, and operational benefits offered by advanced joining processes — including superior weld quality, narrow heat-affected zones (HAZ), high depth-to-width ratios, minimal distortion, automation compatibility, and the ability to join dissimilar/refractory materials — that make them indispensable in aerospace, nuclear, automotive, and electronics industries.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Surgical Scalpel vs. The Butcher's Knife** Imagine you need to cut through a delicate cake without damaging the icing or the structure below: - A **conventional arc welding process** is like a **large kitchen knife** — it works, but it spreads heat over a wide area, damages surrounding regions (large HAZ), and may distort the cake (weld distortion). - An **advanced joining process** (EBW/LBW) is like a **precision surgical scalpel** — it focuses intense energy into a tiny, controlled spot, cutting cleanly with minimal collateral damage (narrow HAZ), no deformation, and a perfect finish. This **"focused energy"** is the single biggest advantage shared by most advanced joining techniques covered in Module 3. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Defining "Advantage" The key performance metrics (all **bold** for emphasis) used in KTU evaluations are: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Advanced Process Value | |---|---|---| | Power Density | $P_d$ | $10^6 - 10^9 \text{ W/mm}^2$ (for EBW/LBW) | | Depth-to-Width Ratio | $D/W$ | $10{:}1$ to $25{:}1$ (EBW); up to $12{:}1$ (LBW) | | HAZ Width | $W_{HAZ}$ | $< 0.1 \text{ mm}$ (EBW) | | Welding Speed | $v$ | $100 - 1000 \text{ mm/s}$ (LBW) | | Vacuum Level (EBW) | $P_v$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4} \text{ Pa}$ | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Advantages Categorized by Process ### 2.1.1 Electron Beam Welding (EBW) EBW uses a **focused beam of high-velocity electrons** accelerated through voltages of $30 - 200 \text{ kV}$ in a vacuum chamber. The kinetic energy of electrons is converted to **heat upon impact** with the workpiece. **Key Advantages:** 1. **Extremely High Power Density** ($10^6 - 10^9 \text{ W/mm}^2$) — causes deep penetration in a single pass. 2. **Vacuum Environment** — eliminates atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂), producing **chemically pure welds**. 3. **Narrow HAZ** — minimizes grain growth, residual stress, and distortion. 4. **High Depth-to-Width Ratio** — welds up to **200 mm thick** sections in a single pass. 5. **Joining Dissimilar & Refractory Metals** — W, Mo, Ti, Zr, and ceramics-to-metals. 6. **Reproducibility & Automation** — CNC-controlled beam deflection. > [!NOTE] > **Why does vacuum matter?** Without air, there's no oxidation, no nitrogen embrittlement, and no arc wandering — making EBW ideal for **nuclear reactor components** and **aerospace titanium alloys**. --- ### 2.1.2 Laser Beam Welding (LBW) LBW uses a **coherent, monochromatic, collimated photon beam** (typically CO₂ at 10.6 μm or Nd:YAG at 1.06 μm). The photons are absorbed by the workpiece, converting light energy directly into heat. **Key Advantages:** 1. **No Vacuum Required** — can be performed in air, inert gas, or underwater. 2. **High Travel Speeds** — up to **10 m/min** for thin sheets. 3. **Non-Contact Process** — no tool wear, no electrode consumption. 4. **Flexible Beam Delivery** — via fiber optics (Nd:YAG), enabling **robotic welding** of complex 3D geometries. 5. **Minimal Distortion** — low total heat input despite high power. 6. **Joins Dissimilar Metals** — Al-Cu, Ti-steel combinations in automotive body-in-white. --- ### 2.1.3 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) PAW is an evolution of **GTAW (TIG)** where the arc is constricted through a **copper nozzle** using a high-velocity inert gas (typically Argon), creating a "plasma jet" with temperatures reaching **20,000–30,000 °C**. **Key Advantages:** 1. **Higher Energy Density than TIG** — ~2× the arc energy concentration. 2. **Greater Penetration** — keyhole mode achieves **12 mm in a single pass** (vs. 3 mm in TIG). 3. **Stable Arc at Low Currents** — enables welding of thin sheets (< 1 mm) without burn-through. 4. **Two Gas Flow Control** — separate **plasma gas** and **shielding gas** allow metallurgical flexibility. 5. **Higher Travel Speeds** — improves productivity. --- ### 2.1.4 Friction Stir Welding (FSW) FSW is a **solid-state joining process** — the material never melts. A rotating non-consumable tool with a **shoulder** and **pin** is plunged into the joint line. Frictional heat softens the material, and the tool's stirring action forges the two pieces together. **Key Advantages:** 1. **No Melting → No Solidification Defects** — eliminates porosity, hot cracking, and liquation cracking. 2. **Excellent for Aluminum Alloys** — the de-facto process for **AA 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx** series (used in aerospace fuel tanks, ship decks). 3. **Low Distortion** — peak temperatures are only **0.6–0.9 × T_melting** (below melting point). 4. **Environmentally Clean** — no shielding gas, no fumes, no spatter, no UV radiation. 5. **Improved Mechanical Properties** — fine-grained, recrystallized microstructure in the stir zone. 6. **Can Join Dissimilar Alloys** — Al-Mg, Al-Cu, Al-steel (with appropriate tool). --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | Power Density | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Energy concentration per unit area | $\text{W/mm}^2$ | | Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Energy supplied per unit length | $\text{J/mm}$ | | Depth-to-Width Ratio | $R = \dfrac{D}{W}$ | Indicates penetration efficiency | dimensionless | | Penetration (EBW) | $D = k \cdot \sqrt{I \cdot V}$ | Empirical Keverian relation | $\text{mm}$ | | FSW Tool Rotational Speed | $\omega = \dfrac{v \cdot 2\pi R_p}{\Delta}$ | Stir pitch ratio control | $\text{rpm}$ | | HAZ Width | $W_{HAZ} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{H}{v}}$ | Narrower at higher speed | $\text{mm}$ | | Energy Coupling (LBW) | $\alpha = \dfrac{P_{abs}}{P_{inc}}$ | Fraction of laser absorbed | $0 < \alpha < 1$ | | Arc Pressure (PAW) | $P_{arc} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{8\pi^2 r^2}$ | Plasma constriction force | $\text{Pa}$ | | Peak Temp (FSW) | $T_{peak} \approx 0.8 \cdot T_m$ | Approximation for Al alloys | $\text{K}$ | | Travel Speed (LBW) | $v = \dfrac{P_{laser}}{H \cdot t}$ | Depends on sheet thickness | $\text{mm/s}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** In all numerical problems, always include units in the final answer. Most KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in a numerical solution. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Preferred Process | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Aerospace (Jet engines, rockets)** | EBW | Titanium & Inconel joining, vacuum purity | | **Automotive (Body-in-white)** | LBW | Speed, robotic flexibility, dissimilar joining | | **Shipbuilding (Aluminum decks)** | FSW | Long straight seams, no distortion | | **Nuclear Reactors** | EBW | Zirconium alloy fuel pin welding | | **Pressure Vessels (Cryogenic)** | PAW | Deep, defect-free single-pass welds | | **Railway (Aluminum carriages)** | FSW | Long seams, fatigue-critical joints | | **Electronics (Battery tabs)** | LBW | Micro-welding, precision | | **Medical Implants (Ti)** | EBW | Biocompatible, contamination-free joints | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Power Density and Process Selection ### Step 1: Definition of Power Density Power density is the most fundamental parameter that distinguishes conventional welding from advanced processes. $$P_d = \dfrac{P_{source}}{A_{spot}}$$ Where: - $P_{source}$ = Total power output of the heat source (W) - $A_{spot}$ = Area over which the power is focused (mm²) ### Step 2: Apply to Different Processes For a constant power $P = 10 \text{ kW}$, we compare spot areas: **For SMAW (Stick Electrode):** $$A_{spot} \approx 100 \text{ mm}^2 \Rightarrow P_d = \dfrac{10{,}000}{100} = 100 \text{ W/mm}^2$$ **For GTAW (TIG):** $$A_{spot} \approx 20 \text{ mm}^2 \Rightarrow P_d = \dfrac{10{,}000}{20} = 500 \text{ W/mm}^2$$ **For PAW (Plasma):** $$A_{spot} \approx 5 \text{ mm}^2 \Rightarrow P_d = \dfrac{10{,}000}{5} = 2{,}000 \text{ W/mm}^2$$ **For LBW (Laser):** $$A_{spot} \approx 0.1 \text{ mm}^2 \Rightarrow P_d = \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.1} = 10^{5} \text{ W/mm}^2$$ **For EBW (Electron Beam):** $$A_{spot} \approx 0.01 \text{ mm}^2 \Rightarrow P_d = \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.01} = 10^{6} \text{ W/mm}^2$$ ### Step 3: Interpretation The higher the power density, the smaller the time the heat has to diffuse into the surrounding base metal. This is the **fundamental reason** behind narrow HAZ and high $D/W$ ratio in advanced processes. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Depth of Penetration in EBW (Keverian Equation) ### Step 1: Empirical Foundation Keverian established that penetration depth in EBW follows a power-law dependence on beam current and accelerating voltage. $$D = k \cdot I^{a} \cdot V^{b}$$ ### Step 2: Apply Standard Constants For typical EBW on stainless steel, the empirical constants are: $$a = 0.65, \quad b = 0.33, \quad k = 0.165 \text{ (with units yielding mm)}$$ So: $$D = 0.165 \cdot I^{0.65} \cdot V^{0.33}$$ ### Step 3: Numerical Example For $I = 50 \text{ mA}$ and $V = 60 \text{ kV}$: **Step 3a: Compute $I^{0.65}$** $$\begin{aligned} I^{0.65} &= 50^{0.65} \\ &= e^{0.65 \cdot \ln(50)} \\ &= e^{0.65 \cdot 3.912} \\ &= e^{2.543} \\ &\approx 12.72 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3b: Compute $V^{0.33}$** $$\begin{aligned} V^{0.33} &= 60^{0.33} \\ &= e^{0.33 \cdot \ln(60)} \\ &= e^{0.33 \cdot 4.094} \\ &= e^{1.351} \\ &\approx 3.86 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3c: Final Multiplication** $$D = 0.165 \times 12.72 \times 3.86 \approx 8.10 \text{ mm}$$ This means at 50 mA / 60 kV, EBW penetrates ~8 mm of stainless steel in a single pass — illustrating the deep-penetration advantage. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Input Comparison Between PAW and GTAW ### Step 1: Heat Input Formula $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$ Where: - $\eta$ = Arc efficiency (0.6 for GTAW, 0.65 for PAW) - $V$ = Voltage (V) - $I$ = Current (A) - $v$ = Travel speed (mm/s) ### Step 2: Calculate for a 3 mm Stainless Steel Joint **Process 1: GTAW** - $V = 12 \text{ V}$, $I = 100 \text{ A}$, $v = 2 \text{ mm/s}$, $\eta = 0.6$ $$H_{GTAW} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 12 \times 100}{2 \times 1000} = \dfrac{720}{2000} = 0.36 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ **Process 2: PAW** - $V = 25 \text{ V}$, $I = 100 \text{ A}$, $v = 5 \text{ mm/s}$, $\eta = 0.65$ $$H_{PAW} = \dfrac{0.65 \times 25 \times 100}{5 \times 1000} = \dfrac{1625}{5000} = 0.325 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ ### Step 3: Interpretation PAW achieves the same penetration at a **lower heat input per unit length** because of higher travel speed. This translates to **narrower HAZ and less distortion** — a key advantage of PAW over GTAW for the same joint. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table) | Advantage Parameter | EBW | LBW | PAW | FSW | Conventional (GTAW) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Power Density (W/mm²) | $10^6 - 10^9$ | $10^5 - 10^7$ | $10^3 - 10^4$ | Solid-state (mechanical) | $10^2 - 10^3$ | | Vacuum Required? | **Yes** | No | No | No | No | | Maximum Penetration | **200 mm** | 25 mm | 12 mm | 50 mm | 6 mm | | HAZ Width | **Very narrow** | Narrow | Moderate | None (below T_m) | Wide | | Dissimilar Joining | Excellent | Excellent | Good | **Excellent** | Limited | | Refractory Metal Joining | **Yes** | Yes | Limited | Limited | No | | Distortion Level | **Very low** | Low | Low-Moderate | **Very low** | High | | Automation Readiness | High | **Very high** | Moderate | **Very high** | Low | | Capital Cost | **Very high** | High | Moderate | High | Low | | Operating Cost | High | Moderate | Low | **Low** | Low | | Operator Skill Needed | High | Moderate | High | **Moderate** | High | | Outdoor/Field Use | **No** (vacuum) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Fume/Spatter | None | None | Low | **None** | Moderate | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Comparative Block Architecture of Advanced Joining Processes ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([Module 3: Advanced Joining Processes]) Start --> EBW[EBW - Electron Beam Welding] Start --> LBW[LBW - Laser Beam Welding] Start --> PAW[PAW - Plasma Arc Welding] Start --> FSW[FSW - Friction Stir Welding] EBW --> EBW_A1[Advantage 1: Vacuum purity] EBW --> EBW_A2[Advantage 2: Ultra-high power density] EBW --> EBW_A3[Advantage 3: Deep penetration single pass] EBW --> EBW_A4[Advantage 4: Refractory and dissimilar metal joining] EBW --> EBW_L1[Limitation: Vacuum chamber required] EBW --> EBW_L2[Limitation: X-ray shielding needed] LBW --> LBW_A1[Advantage 1: No vacuum needed] LBW --> LBW_A2[Advantage 2: Fiber optic delivery for robotics] LBW --> LBW_A3[Advantage 3: High travel speeds] LBW --> LBW_A4[Advantage 4: Non-contact, no tool wear] LBW --> LBW_L1[Limitation: Reflective materials need coating] LBW --> LBW_L2[Limitation: Plasma plume absorption] PAW --> PAW_A1[Advantage 1: Deeper than TIG in single pass] PAW --> PAW_A2[Advantage 2: Stable arc at low currents] PAW --> PAW_A3[Advantage 3: Two-gas flexibility] PAW --> PAW_A4[Advantage 4: Versatile for thin and thick sections] PAW --> PAW_L1[Limitation: Nozzle wear and replacement] PAW --> PAW_L2[Limitation: More complex than GTAW] FSW --> FSW_A1[Advantage 1: Solid state, no melting] FSW --> FSW_A2[Advantage 2: No HAZ cracks, no porosity] FSW --> FSW_A3[Advantage 3: Excellent for Al alloys] FSW --> FSW_A4[Advantage 4: Environmentally clean process] FSW --> FSW_L1[Limitation: Requires backing anvil] FSW --> FSW_L2[Limitation: Exit hole at weld termination] EBW_A1 --> Use1[Aerospace jet engines, nuclear reactors] EBW_A3 --> Use1 EBW_A4 --> Use1 LBW_A1 --> Use2[Automotive body-in-white, shipbuilding] LBW_A2 --> Use2 LBW_A3 --> Use2 PAW_A1 --> Use3[Pressure vessels, aerospace thin tubes] PAW_A3 --> Use3 PAW_A4 --> Use3 FSW_A1 --> Use4[Aerospace fuel tanks, rail carriages, marine decks] FSW_A2 --> Use4 FSW_A3 --> Use4 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Logic for Selecting an Advanced Joining Process ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is the workpiece
highly reactive
or refractory?} Q1 -->|Yes, Ti/Zr/W| EBWpath[Use EBW in vacuum] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it an
aluminum alloy
long seam?} Q2 -->|Yes| FSWpath[Use FSW solid state] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need robotic
3D access?} Q3 -->|Yes| LBWpath[Use Nd:YAG LBW via fiber] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Moderate penetration
with high speed?} Q4 -->|Yes| PAWpath[Use PAW with keyhole mode] Q4 -->|No| Q5{Fallback: conventional
GTAW or GMAW} Q5 --> GTAWpath[Use GTAW with filler] EBWpath --> Industry1[Aerospace, Nuclear] FSWpath --> Industry2[Marine, Railway, Aerospace] LBWpath --> Industry3[Automotive, Electronics] PAWpath --> Industry4[Pressure vessels, Cryogenic] GTAWpath --> Industry5[General fabrication] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: HAZ and Power Density Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Low Power Density
10 to the power 2 W per mm squared] --> A1[Wide HAZ] A1 --> A2[Large grain growth] A2 --> A3[High distortion] B[Moderate Power Density
10 to the power 3 W per mm squared] --> B1[Moderate HAZ] B1 --> B2[Some grain refinement] B2 --> B3[Acceptable distortion] C[High Power Density
10 to the power 5 W per mm squared] --> C1[Narrow HAZ] C1 --> C2[Minimal grain growth] C2 --> C3[Low distortion] D[Ultra-High Power Density
10 to the power 7 W per mm squared] --> D1[Near-zero HAZ] D1 --> D2[Amorphous or fine nano structure] D2 --> D3[Negligible distortion] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: List any six advantages of Electron Beam Welding (EBW) over conventional arc welding processes.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The six key advantages of EBW are: 1. **Extremely high power density** ($10^6$ to $10^9$ W/mm²) enables deep penetration in a single pass (up to 200 mm). 2. **Vacuum environment** prevents atmospheric contamination (no O₂, N₂ pick-up) producing chemically pure welds. 3. **Very narrow Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** reduces grain growth, residual stress, and weld distortion. 4. **High depth-to-width ratio** (typically 20:1 to 25:1) gives strong, narrow welds with superior mechanical properties. 5. **Ability to join dissimilar and refractory metals** such as tungsten, molybdenum, titanium, zirconium, and ceramics-to-metals. 6. **Easily automated** with CNC-controlled beam deflection, ensuring high reproducibility and precision in production environments. > [!NOTE] > Each advantage carries 0.5 marks. The model answer should mention the technical reason behind each, not just the keyword. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Differentiate Friction Stir Welding (FSW) from conventional fusion welding processes. Mention any three advantages.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Friction Stir Welding (FSW) is a **solid-state joining process** where a rotating tool with a shoulder and pin generates frictional heat and mechanically stirs the materials together **without reaching the melting point**. In contrast, fusion welding melts and re-solidifies the base metal. **Three Advantages:** 1. **No solidification defects** — Since there is no melting, problems like porosity, hot cracking, and liquation cracking are completely eliminated. 2. **Excellent for Al-alloys** — FSW is the preferred process for age-hardenable aerospace aluminum alloys (AA 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx series) where fusion welding would cause strength loss in the HAZ. 3. **Low distortion and residual stress** — Peak temperatures reach only 60–90% of the melting point, so thermal contraction and warping are minimal. --- ## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.** --- ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]** **Q: Compare and contrast the advantages of Electron Beam Welding (EBW) and Laser Beam Welding (LBW). Discuss their specific industrial applications where each offers distinct advantages.** #### **(a) Discuss the operational principles and enumerate the advantages of EBW. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Operational Principle of EBW:** Electron Beam Welding uses a **high-velocity focused beam of electrons** generated by heating a tungsten filament cathode. These electrons are accelerated through a high voltage (30–200 kV) and focused by electromagnetic lenses onto the workpiece inside a vacuum chamber ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa). Upon impact, **99% of the kinetic energy** is converted into heat, vaporizing the metal locally and creating a deep, narrow keyhole. **Advantages of EBW:** 1. **Highest power density among all welding processes** — $10^6$ to $10^9$ W/mm², enabling penetration up to 200 mm in a single pass. 2. **Vacuum purity** — No atmospheric contamination, no oxide formation, no nitrogen embrittlement. 3. **Narrow HAZ and low distortion** — Heat input is highly localized; distortion is typically < 5% of GTAW values. 4. **High $D/W$ ratio** — A typical 20:1 ratio means strong welds with minimal filler metal. 5. **Joins dissimilar and refractory materials** — W, Mo, Ti, Zr, beryllium, and ceramic-metal combinations. 6. **Reproducibility via CNC** — Beam can be deflected, oscillated, or split for precise multi-spot welding. #### **(b) Discuss the operational principles and advantages of LBW. Compare both processes and list 3 industrial applications each. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Operational Principle of LBW:** Laser Beam Welding uses a **coherent, monochromatic, collimated beam of photons** from a CO₂ (10.6 μm) or Nd:YAG (1.06 μm) laser. The photons are absorbed by the workpiece surface, raising the local temperature above the vaporization point to form a **keyhole**. The hole is filled by molten metal flowing around it as the beam moves. **Advantages of LBW:** 1. **No vacuum required** — Can be performed in air, inert atmosphere, or even underwater. 2. **Fiber optic delivery** — Nd:YAG laser beams can be transmitted through fibers, enabling **robotic 3D welding**. 3. **Very high travel speeds** — Up to 10 m/min for thin sheets, drastically reducing cycle time. 4. **Non-contact, no electrode/tool wear** — Lower consumable cost. 5. **Minimal HAZ and distortion** — Comparable to EBW for thin sections. 6. **Versatile dissimilar joining** — Used for Al-Cu, Ti-steel, and coated steels (with appropriate surface prep). **Comparison Summary Table:** | Feature | EBW | LBW | |---|---|---| | Power Density | $10^6 - 10^9$ W/mm² | $10^5 - 10^7$ W/mm² | | Vacuum Needed | **Yes** | **No** | | Max. Penetration | 200 mm | 25 mm | | Beam Delivery | Direct in vacuum | Fiber optics (Nd:YAG) | | Capital Cost | Higher | High (but lower than EBW) | | Field Use | **Restricted** | **Possible** | **Industrial Applications:** - **EBW Specific:** Nuclear fuel pin welding (Zircaloy), jet engine Ti-alloy casings, rocket nozzle welding, vacuum tube manufacturing. - **LBW Specific:** Automotive tailored blank welding, battery tab welding in EVs, hermetic sealing of electronic packages, ship panel welding. --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** **Q: Explain the working principle of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) with a neat sketch. List and explain in detail at least six advantages of FSW over conventional fusion welding processes.** #### **(a) Working Principle of FSW with a labeled block diagram. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Principle of FSW:** Friction Stir Welding is a **solid-state joining process** invented at The Welding Institute (TWI), UK, in 1991. A **non-consumable rotating tool** consisting of a **shoulder** and a **protruding pin** is plunged into the joint line between two clamped plates. The tool rotation generates **frictional heat** at the contact surface, softening the material (but not melting it). As the tool traverses along the joint, the **shoulder provides forging pressure** and confines the softened material, while the **pin mechanically stirs** the plasticized material from the advancing side to the retreating side, forging a solid-state bond. **Key Regions in FSW:** - **Base Metal (BM):** Unaffected parent material. - **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):** Experiences thermal cycles but no plastic deformation. - **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ):** Undergoes both heat and deformation. - **Stir Zone (SZ) / Nugget:** The recrystallized, fine-grained welded region directly under the tool pin. **Block Flow of FSW Process:** ``` [Clamped Workpiece] | v [Rotating Tool with Pin + Shoulder] | v [Tool Plunges into Joint] | v [Frictional Heat Softens Material - Solid State] | v [Tool Traverses Along Joint Line] | v [Pin Stirs + Shoulder Forges Material] | v [Solid-State Bond Forms in Stir Zone] ``` #### **(b) List and explain six advantages of FSW. (7 Marks)** **Six Advantages of FSW:** 1. **No Melting → No Solidification Defects:** FSW operates below the melting point (peak temp ≈ 0.6–0.9 T_m). Therefore, common fusion defects — porosity, hot cracking, solidification cracking, and liquation cracking — are completely eliminated. 2. **Superior Mechanical Properties in the Weld Zone:** The stir zone undergoes **dynamic recrystallization**, producing a fine-grained equiaxed microstructure that is often **stronger than the parent metal** in Al-alloys. 3. **Excellent for Joining 'Unweldable' Al-Alloys:** Age-hardenable alloys (AA 2024, 6061, 7075) suffer HAZ softening in fusion welding. FSW preserves base metal strength, making it the de-facto process for aerospace Al-structures (e.g., Boeing Delta II, SpaceX Falcon 9 fuel tanks). 4. **Low Distortion and Residual Stress:** Lower peak temperature and absence of melting reduce thermal gradients, resulting in minimal angular distortion and warping — critical for long, thin aerospace panels. 5. **Environmentally Clean ('Green Welding'):** No shielding gas, no fumes, no spatter, no UV radiation, no consumable electrodes. FSW is energy-efficient and worker-friendly. 6. **Versatile Dissimilar Joining:** FSW can join dissimilar Al-alloys (e.g., AA 5083 to AA 6082), Al-Mg, Al-Cu, and even Al-to-steel (using bimetallic transition inserts) — applications where fusion welding fails. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "FSW is the best process" without comparing it. Always use comparative language ("FSW over GTAW" or "FSW over conventional fusion welding"). > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention the **solid-state** nature — this is the *defining* feature. Marks are lost if this isn't explicitly stated. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the **stir zone (nugget)** with the **HAZ**. FSW has both, but the stir zone is what gives FSW its superior properties. > - **Common Mistake 4:** For **EBW/LBW questions**, students often forget the **vacuum** aspect of EBW or the **fiber-optic delivery** of Nd:YAG LBW. Both are 1-mark keywords. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 – "Advantages"** - ✅ **Power density** is the master parameter: $P_d = P/A$. Higher $P_d$ → narrower HAZ → lower distortion. - ✅ **EBW advantages** = vacuum + ultra-high $P_d$ + refractory/dissimilar joining. **Limitation:** vacuum chamber makes it non-portable. - ✅ **LBW advantages** = no vacuum + fiber optic delivery + robotic flexibility. **Limitation:** plasma plume can absorb beam on some alloys. - ✅ **PAW advantages** = deeper than GTAW + stable low-current arc + two-gas flexibility. **Limitation:** nozzle wear. - ✅ **FSW advantages** = solid state → no solidification defects + excellent for Al-alloys + green process. **Limitation:** exit hole + needs rigid clamping. - ✅ **Depth-to-width ratio ($D/W$):** EBW (20:1) > LBW (5:1–12:1) > PAW (1:1 to 2:1) > GTAW (1:1). - ✅ **Peak temperature order:** EBW (vaporization) > LBW (vaporization) > PAW (molten) > FSW (0.8 T_m, solid-state). - ✅ **Industrial application mapping:** Aerospace = EBW/FSW, Automotive = LBW, Pressure vessels = PAW, Shipbuilding = FSW/LBW. - ✅ **Keverian equation** $D = 0.165 \cdot I^{0.65} \cdot V^{0.33}$ is the **must-remember** EBW penetration formula. - ✅ **Heat input formula** $H = \eta V I / v$ applies to all arc-based processes; FSW is **not** governed by this equation. - ✅ **Always state the metallurgical reason** for each advantage — KTU awards marks for technical depth, not keyword listing. - ✅ **Use comparative tables** in long answers — they earn **easy 2–3 marks** by providing structured, visible evidence of understanding.

topics.title: Limitations and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limitations and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Advanced Joining Processes (Module 3 Context)

Module 3 of **PEIET523 – Advanced Metal Joining Techniques** deals with high-energy-density beam welding processes, namely **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)**, **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)**, and **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**. Each of these processes has unique advantages, but every engineering process is bounded by inherent physical, metallurgical, and economic limitations. The study of limitations and applications forms the practical bridge between theoretical process knowledge and industrial decision-making.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, students must be able to evaluate the **applicability**, **suitability**, and **constraints** of each advanced joining process for a given engineering scenario. This topic is high-weightage and frequently tested in Parts A and B of the End Semester Examination.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Limitation** – A limitation is defined as an inherent physical, metallurgical, operational, or economic constraint that restricts the use of a welding process for a particular material, geometry, thickness, or production environment.

**Application** – An application is a specific industrial use case where a welding process is preferred or uniquely suited due to its operational characteristics such as heat input, weld profile, distortion control, and joint accessibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> The term *"high-energy-density beam"* is officially defined by KTU as any process delivering a power density greater than **10⁵ W/mm²** at the workpiece. EBW, LBW, and PAW all qualify under this definition.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you have three different "fire types" used to melt metal:

1. **Electron Beam** – A *super-precise surgical laser scalpel in a sealed vacuum chamber* – extremely concentrated, but cannot work in open air and needs a special room.
2. **Laser Beam** – A *targeted spotlight* – focused, fast, works in air, but struggles with highly reflective shiny metals like aluminum.
3. **Plasma Arc** – A *supercharged blowtorch* – powerful and works in air, but produces a wider heat-affected zone (HAZ) compared to beams.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of EBW, LBW, and PAW as three different "fountain pen tips" used to write on metal. The EBW is the finest tip (smallest spot, deepest penetration, needs a vacuum desk), the LBW is a versatile gel pen (fast, works on any paper, but expensive ink), and the PAW is a thick marker (works on rough surfaces, faster, but messy with thicker lines / wider HAZ).

### 1.3.1 Key Performance Metrics (to be remembered)

- **Power Density (W/mm²)** – Concentration of energy on the workpiece.
- **Depth-to-Width Ratio (D/W)** – Indicates penetration efficiency.
- **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** – The region adjacent to the weld that undergoes microstructural changes.
- **Vacuum Requirement (mbar)** – Pressure inside the EBW chamber.

---

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

The choice of process directly impacts:
- Aerospace component integrity (Ti, Inconel alloys)
- Automotive mass production (laser-welded tailored blanks)
- Nuclear pressure vessel fabrication (EBW thick sections)
- Micro-electronics and medical device manufacturing (precision laser micro-welding)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electron Beam Welding (EBW) – Limitations and Applications

### 2.1.1 Core Process Recap

EBW uses a focused beam of high-velocity electrons emitted from a heated tungsten cathode, accelerated through a high voltage (typically **30 kV to 200 kV**), and focused by electromagnetic lenses onto the workpiece inside a vacuum chamber (typically **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ mbar**). The kinetic energy of electrons converts to heat upon impact, producing welds with extremely high power density (**10⁶ to 10⁸ W/mm²**).

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Analysis – Why Each Limitation Exists

**Limitation 1: Vacuum Chamber Requirement**
- *Why:* Electrons are scattered by gas molecules. Without vacuum, the beam cannot travel from the gun to the workpiece without losing energy and focus.
- *Practical impact:* Maximum workpiece size is limited to the chamber volume (typically up to ~100 m³ for industrial EBW machines).
- *Cost impact:* Vacuum pumps, sealing systems, and cycle time to pump down (5–30 minutes) increase capital and operational cost.

**Limitation 2: Generation of X-rays**
- *Why:* When high-velocity electrons strike a metal surface, **bremsstrahlung (braking radiation)** X-rays are produced.
- *Safety requirement:* Lead-shielded chamber walls (≥ 25 mm lead equivalent) and operator safety protocols are mandatory.
- *Regulatory impact:* Complies with radiation safety norms (e.g., AERB in India, NRC in the US).

**Limitation 3: Spiking and Root Porosity**
- *Why:* Localized evaporation of alloying elements (especially in steels) creates vapor cavities that travel down the weld pool, forming spikes and root porosity.
- *Mitigation:* Beam oscillation, defocusing, and careful parameter selection.

**Limitation 4: Joint Fit-up Sensitivity**
- *Why:* The narrow, deep weld profile demands precise joint alignment (gap ≤ 0.1 mm typical for thick sections).
- *Practical impact:* Increases fixturing and pre-machining cost.

**Limitation 5: Material Limitations**
- *Why:* Materials with high vapor pressure (e.g., Mg, Zn, Cd) cannot be EB welded due to excessive evaporation.
- *Practical impact:* Excludes certain non-ferrous alloys.

### 2.1.3 Applications of EBW

- **Aerospace:** Rocket engine thrust chambers (Inconel, Ti alloys)
- **Nuclear:** Fuel element end-plug welds, reactor core components
- **Automotive:** Gearbox components, drive shafts
- **Electronics:** Vacuum-sealed device packaging
- **Heavy Industry:** Thick-section pressure vessel fabrication (up to 200 mm in a single pass)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Application:** The **Space Shuttle Main Engine (SSME)** used EBW extensively to join Inconel 718 tubes. The Apollo Lunar Module descent engine also relied on EBW for critical Ti-6Al-4V assemblies.

---

## 2.2 Laser Beam Welding (LBW) – Limitations and Applications

### 2.2.1 Core Process Recap

LBW uses a coherent, monochromatic, highly collimated photon beam (typically **CO₂ laser at 10.6 µm** or **Nd:YAG fiber laser at 1.06 µm**). The beam is focused via lenses (or mirrors for CO₂) onto the joint, generating power densities of **10⁵ to 10⁷ W/mm²**. Keyhole-mode welding is the dominant mechanism for deep penetration.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Analysis – Why Each Limitation Exists

**Limitation 1: High Capital and Operating Cost**
- *Why:* Laser sources (especially high-power fiber lasers > 4 kW) cost ₹50 lakh to ₹5 crore, and consumables (lenses, fibers, gas mixtures) add recurring cost.
- *Practical impact:* Justified only for high-value, high-volume production.

**Limitation 2: Reflectivity Problem**
- *Why:* Highly reflective metals (Al, Cu, Au) reflect the laser beam, reducing energy absorption.
- *Mitigation:* Use of higher-power lasers, surface texturing, anti-reflective coatings, or switching to green/blue diode lasers.

**Limitation 3: Plasma Plume Formation**
- *Why:* The metal vapor above the keyhole ionizes and forms a plasma cloud that absorbs and scatters the laser beam (**plasma shielding effect**).
- *Mitigation:* Use of **helium shielding gas** (high ionization potential ≈ 24.6 eV) and side-jet gas streams to disperse the plasma.

**Limitation 4: Joint Fit-up Tolerance**
- *Why:* The narrow focal spot (typically 0.2–0.6 mm) demands precise joint alignment.
- *Practical impact:* Gap tolerance is usually limited to ≤ 10% of sheet thickness.

**Limitation 5: HAZ Cracking in High-Carbon Steels**
- *Why:* Rapid cooling rates (10⁴ to 10⁶ °C/s) can form martensite and induce cold cracking.
- *Mitigation:* Preheating, post-weld heat treatment (PWHT), or use of lower-power defocused mode.

### 2.2.3 Applications of LBW

- **Automotive:** Tailored blank welding (different thickness/grade steel sheets joined for crash-resistant body panels)
- **Electronics:** Battery tab welding (Li-ion cells), hermetic sealing
- **Medical:** Pacemaker cases, stainless-steel stent welding
- **Shipbuilding:** High-speed linear welding of steel plates
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade repair, Ti-alloy skin-stringer joints
- **Jewelry and Watchmaking:** Precision micro-welding of precious metals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Note:** Modern automotive "hot-stamping" of boron steels (e.g., 22MnB5) for crash structures is almost exclusively done using **remote laser welding** with scanner optics, achieving cycle times < 2 seconds per joint.

---

## 2.3 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) – Limitations and Applications

### 2.3.1 Core Process Recap

PAW uses a constricted arc created by forcing the arc through a water-cooled copper nozzle with an orifice (typically **2.5–4.8 mm diameter**). A separate shielding gas (typically argon) and a plasma gas (also argon or argon + H₂) are used. The constriction increases arc temperature (up to **30,000 °C**), velocity (up to **Mach 1 at nozzle exit**), and energy density. Two operating modes exist:

- **Micro-plasma (PAW-M):** Current < 15 A, used for thin foils
- **Keyhole plasma (PAW-K):** Current 100–400 A, used for medium thicknesses

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis – Why Each Limitation Exists

**Limitation 1: Limited to Conductive Materials**
- *Why:* PAW requires the workpiece to complete the electrical circuit (non-transferred arc mode is possible but is rarely used for joining).
- *Practical impact:* Cannot be used for ceramics or composites directly.

**Limitation 2: Higher Heat Input than EBW/LBW**
- *Why:* Wider arc footprint and lower power density (10⁴ to 10⁵ W/mm²) result in larger HAZ.
- *Practical impact:* Not ideal for distortion-sensitive thin structures compared to laser welding.

**Limitation 3: Nozzle Wear and Maintenance**
- *Why:* The tungsten electrode and copper nozzle are exposed to extreme heat; thermal cycling causes wear, gas-turbulence distortion, and double-arcing.
- *Practical impact:* Regular nozzle replacement and parameter recalibration needed.

**Limitation 4: Complex Parameter Set**
- *Why:* Multiple independent variables (pilot arc current, main arc current, plasma gas flow, shielding gas flow, travel speed, standoff distance) must be controlled.
- *Practical impact:* Higher operator skill required.

**Limitation 5: Thickness Limitation**
- *Why:* Maximum practical single-pass thickness is ~10–12 mm in keyhole mode.
- *Practical impact:* For thicker sections, multi-pass or alternative processes (e.g., EBW) are preferred.

### 2.3.3 Applications of PAW

- **Aerospace:** Honeycomb structure welding, jet engine blade repair
- **Nuclear:** Stainless-steel tube-to-tubesheet welding
- **Chemical & Process Industry:** Stainless-steel tube and pipe welding
- **Electronics:** Hermetic sealing of relay cans and capacitors
- **Refrigeration:** Copper tube joining in heat exchangers
- **Medical Devices:** Surgical needle and catheter assembly

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Application:** The **Aero-Engine Combustion Liner** in many gas turbine engines is welded using **micro-plasma** or **keyhole plasma** to ensure tight, defect-free seams in thin Inconel/Hastelloy sheets.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Summary – Limitations vs Applications Matrix

| Parameter | EBW | LBW | PAW |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Density (W/mm²) | 10⁶ – 10⁸ | 10⁵ – 10⁷ | 10⁴ – 10⁵ |
| Operating Environment | Vacuum | Air / Shield gas | Air / Shield gas |
| Max Single-Pass Thickness | ~200 mm | ~25 mm (with filler) | ~12 mm |
| Typical D/W Ratio | 25 : 1 | 5 : 1 to 10 : 1 | 2 : 1 to 3 : 1 |
| Capital Cost | Very High | High | Moderate |
| Reflectivity Issue | No | Yes (Al, Cu) | No |
| Material Restriction | High (no Mg, Zn) | Moderate | Low |
| HAZ Width | Smallest | Small | Moderate |
| Joint Fit-up Tolerance | Very Tight | Tight | Moderate |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Density | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A}$ where $P$ = beam power, $A$ = focal spot area | W/mm² | Defines process class |
| 2 | Beam Spot Area (circular) | $A = \pi r^{2}$ | mm² | LBW / EBW focus |
| 3 | Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ where $\eta$ = efficiency, $S$ = travel speed | J/mm | All arc / beam processes |
| 4 | Penetration Depth (Fahrenheit / Swift-Hook approximation) | $d = k \cdot \dfrac{P}{S^{y}}$ where $k$ = material constant, $y \approx 0.7$ | mm | EBW / LBW |
| 5 | Depth-to-Width Ratio | $D/W = \dfrac{d}{w}$ | dimensionless | Weld profile quality |
| 6 | Energy per Unit Length | $E/L = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | J/mm | Beam welding |
| 7 | Beam Divergence (LBW) | $\theta_d = \dfrac{4 \lambda}{\pi D_0}$ | radians | Diffraction-limited spot |
| 8 | Electron Penetration Depth | $R = 0.043 \cdot E^{1.75}$ (for steel) | µm | EBW, where $E$ = keV |
| 9 | Rayleigh Length (focal depth) | $Z_R = \dfrac{\pi w_0^{2}}{\lambda}$ | mm | LBW focus |
| 10 | Cooling Rate Approx. | $T' = \dfrac{2\pi k \rho c (T_c - T_0)^{3}}{q^{2}}$ | °C/s | HAZ metallurgy |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** For Part B numerical questions, the most commonly tested formulas are **(3) Heat Input** and **(5) Depth-to-Width Ratio**. Always include the efficiency factor $\eta$ in heat input (≈ 0.7 for LBW, ≈ 0.85 for PAW, ≈ 0.9 for EBW).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Limitations–Applications analysis directly feeds into **process selection** decisions in industries such as:
- **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** development as per **ISO 15614** and **ASME Section IX**.
- **Failure analysis** in service (e.g., identifying LBW plasma shielding as the root cause of porosity).
- **Cost estimation** for tenders (e.g., evaluating whether EBW vacuum cycle time justifies the weld quality).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Penetration Depth in Beam Welding (Fahrenkrug–Swift-Hook Model)

The penetration depth $d$ in a high-energy-density process is empirically related to the beam power $P$ and travel speed $S$ by:

$$
d = k \cdot \frac{P}{S^{y}}
$$

Let us derive this from first principles by balancing the volumetric energy required to melt a unit length of weld with the energy supplied per unit length.

**Step 1:** Energy required to melt a unit length of weld bead of cross-section $A_m$:

$$
E_{m} = A_{m} \cdot \rho \cdot [c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L_{f}]
$$

where $\rho$ is density, $c$ is specific heat, $T_m$ is melting point, $T_0$ is ambient, $L_f$ is latent heat of fusion.

**Step 2:** Energy supplied per unit length by the beam:

$$
E_{s} = \eta \cdot \frac{P}{S}
$$

where $\eta$ is absorption/efficiency factor.

**Step 3:** Equating the energies and assuming bead width $w$ is roughly constant:

$$
\eta \cdot \frac{P}{S} = w \cdot d \cdot \rho \cdot [c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L_{f}]
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $d$:

$$
d = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{S \cdot w \cdot \rho \cdot [c (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L_{f}]}
$$

**Step 5:** In beam welding, bead width $w$ decreases with travel speed according to $w \propto S^{1-y}$, where empirical $y \approx 0.7$. Substituting and consolidating constants into $k$:

$$
d = k \cdot \frac{P}{S^{y}}
$$

This compact form is universally used in KTU numerical problems and industry handbook calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Power Density and Classification of Processes

The defining metric for advanced welding processes is power density $P_d$ at the focal spot:

$$
P_{d} = \frac{P}{A_{spot}} = \frac{P}{\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 1:** For a typical LBW with $P = 4 \text{ kW}$ and focal spot radius $r = 0.3 \text{ mm}$:

$$
P_{d} = \frac{4000}{\pi \cdot (0.3)^{2}} = \frac{4000}{0.2827} \approx 14147 \text{ W/mm}^{2} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** For EBW with $P = 10 \text{ kW}$ and $r = 0.1 \text{ mm}$:

$$
P_{d} = \frac{10000}{\pi \cdot (0.1)^{2}} = \frac{10000}{0.0314} \approx 318309 \text{ W/mm}^{2} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** This confirms EBW and LBW are high-energy-density processes, and explains the very narrow, deep weld profiles they produce.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Heat Input Comparison

Heat input $H$ is a critical parameter for predicting HAZ size and distortion:

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000} \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

where $V$ is voltage (V), $I$ is current (A), $S$ is travel speed (mm/min).

**Step 1:** Calculate for a typical PAW parameter set: $V = 25$ V, $I = 200$ A, $S = 200$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.85$:

$$
H = \frac{0.85 \cdot 25 \cdot 200}{200 \times 1000} = \frac{4250}{200000} = 0.02125 \text{ kJ/mm} = 21.25 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with LBW at $P = 4$ kW, $S = 2000$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.7$:

$$
H = \frac{0.7 \cdot 4000}{2000} = 1.4 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with EBW at $P = 10$ kW, $S = 1000$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.9$:

$$
H = \frac{0.9 \cdot 10000}{1000} = 9.0 \text{ J/mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion: Even though LBW has lower heat input per unit length, its power density is high, producing deep narrow welds with low distortion. PAW, while useful for thicker sections, produces more HAZ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher power density does NOT necessarily mean higher heat input** – these are two distinct metrics. KTU examiners test this distinction frequently.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Choosing the Right Process

The following decision matrix can be used to answer application-based KTU questions:

| Application Scenario | Best Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Welding Ti-6Al-4V rocket nozzle in vacuum | EBW | No contamination, deep penetration |
| Hermetic sealing of Li-ion battery cells | LBW | Non-contact, fast, precise |
| Stainless-steel thin-wall tube joining | PAW (micro-plasma) | Stable arc at low current |
| Welding Al alloy automotive body panels | LBW (fiber laser) | Low reflectivity with fiber laser + filler |
| Welding thick nuclear reactor core parts | EBW | Single-pass deep penetration, vacuum purity |
| Joining 0.1 mm thick Inconel honeycomb | PAW (micro-plasma) | Stable low-current arc |
| Joining Cu tubes in refrigerator | PAW | Conductive material, no reflectivity issue |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Numericals for Practice

**Numerical 1:** A Nd:YAG laser delivers **3 kW** at the workpiece. The focal spot diameter is **0.4 mm**. Calculate the power density and classify the process.

**Solution:**

Step 1 – Spot area:

$$
A = \pi r^{2} = \pi \cdot (0.2)^{2} = 0.1257 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Step 2 – Power density:

$$
P_{d} = \frac{3000}{0.1257} \approx 23873 \text{ W/mm}^{2} \approx 2.39 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/mm}^{2}
$$

Step 3 – Since $P_{d} > 10^{5}$ is the lower bound for high-energy-density; here it is $2.39 \times 10^{4}$ W/mm², which is borderline (deep-penetration mode only if keyhole forms). In practice, this falls into **conduction-mode LBW** for thin sheets.

---

**Numerical 2:** A PAW is performed at $V = 28$ V, $I = 150$ A, $S = 150$ mm/min, with $\eta = 0.80$. Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm.

**Solution:**

Step 1 – Apply the heat input formula:

$$
H = \frac{0.80 \cdot 28 \cdot 150}{150 \times 1000} = \frac{3360}{150000} = 0.0224 \text{ kJ/mm} = 22.4 \text{ J/mm}
$$

Step 2 – Interpret: This heat input is typical for keyhole PAW on 3–6 mm stainless steel sheets.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of EBW

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **process flow and major functional modules** of an Electron Beam Welding system, including its limitations handling.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Voltage Power Supply 30 to 200 kV] --> B[Electron Gun with Tungsten Cathode]
    B --> C[Beam Formation under Vacuum 10 to power negative 3 mbar]
    C --> D[Electromagnetic Focusing Lens]
    D --> E[Deflection Coils for Beam Steering]
    E --> F[Workpiece inside Vacuum Chamber]
    F --> G[Keyhole Formation and Melting]
    G --> H[Solidified Weld with High D over W Ratio]
    H --> I{Quality Verification}
    I --> J[Radiation Shielding Check]
    I --> K[Spiking and Porosity Inspection]
    I --> L[Acceptable Weld]
    subgraph LIMITATION_MODULE
        M1[Vacuum Limitation - Chamber Size] -.-> B
        M2[X-ray Radiation Hazard] -.-> J
        M3[High Vapor Pressure Materials Excluded] -.-> F
    end
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LBW

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Laser Source CO2 or NdYAG or Fiber] --> B[Beam Delivery System Mirrors or Fiber]
    B --> C[Focussing Lens or Collimator]
    C --> D[Beam Interaction Zone on Workpiece]
    D --> E[Keyhole Formation with Plasma Plume]
    E --> F[Shielding Gas Helium or Argon Jet]
    F --> G[Cooling and Solidification]
    G --> H[Welded Joint]
    H --> I{Defect Inspection}
    I --> J[Porosity Check]
    I --> K[Crack Detection]
    I --> L[Acceptance Decision]
    subgraph LIMITATION_MODULE
        L1[Reflectivity Issue for Al and Cu] -.-> D
        L2[Plasma Shielding Effect] -.-> F
        L3[High Capital Cost] -.-> A
        L4[Joint Fitup Tight Tolerance] -.-> D
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PAW

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source DC Constant Current] --> B[Torch with Tungsten Electrode]
    B --> C[Pilot Arc Initiation on Nozzle]
    C --> D[Plasma Gas Flow Argon or Argon plus H2]
    D --> E[Constriction in Copper Nozzle]
    E --> F[Plasma Jet at Mach Velocity]
    F --> G[Workpiece - Transferred Arc]
    G --> H[Keyhole or Meltin Mode Welding]
    H --> I[Shielding Gas Secondary Flow]
    I --> J[Welded Joint]
    J --> K{Quality Control}
    K --> L[Nozzle Wear Check]
    K --> M[HAZ Microstructure Analysis]
    K --> N[Acceptance]
    subgraph LIMITATION_MODULE
        N1[Only Conductive Materials] -.-> G
        N2[Higher Heat Input than EBW] -.-> H
        N3[Complex Multi Variable Control] -.-> A
        N4[Nozzle Wear and Maintenance] -.-> E
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Application Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Process Selection for Advanced Joining] --> Q1{Is workpiece large and thick > 50 mm?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> EBW[Choose EBW]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is high speed mass production required?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> LBW[Choose LBW especially fiber laser]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is material highly reflective Al or Cu?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> LBW2[Use Fiber Laser with Anti Reflective Prep]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is thickness 1 to 12 mm and conductive material?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> PAW[Choose PAW]
    Q4 -- No --> EBW2[Consider EBW if vacuum justified]
    EBW --> DONE[Document WPS and Validate]
    LBW --> DONE
    PAW --> DONE
    LBW2 --> DONE
    EBW2 --> DONE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
List any **three limitations** of Electron Beam Welding.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Vacuum chamber requirement** restricts the maximum size of the workpiece that can be welded, and adds cycle time for pumping down (5–30 minutes per batch).
2. **Generation of harmful X-rays** due to deceleration of high-energy electrons necessitates heavy lead shielding and radiation safety compliance.
3. **Spiking and root porosity** defects occur in certain alloys (e.g., low-alloy steels) due to localized vapor cavity formation.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Question]**
State any **three industrial applications** of Laser Beam Welding.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Automotive tailored blank welding** – joining dissimilar thickness steel sheets for crash-resistant body panels.
2. **Hermetic sealing of Li-ion battery cells** – precise, fast, non-contact welding of aluminum and copper tabs.
3. **Aerospace turbine blade repair** – precision welding of superalloy components with minimal HAZ.

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **confuse power density with heat input** – these are *different* metrics.
> 2. Many students **omit the efficiency factor $\eta$** when calculating heat input, leading to full-mark loss in numerical sub-parts.
> 3. **Listing limitations without justification** (i.e., explaining *why* the limitation exists) costs 1–2 marks per limitation.
> 4. For application questions, students must **name the specific industry or component** – generic answers like "used in industry" attract partial credit only.
> 5. Always draw a **labeled process schematic** (or a clean block diagram) for 5–7 mark questions on process description.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **limitations of Electron Beam Welding**, with a focus on vacuum requirement, X-ray generation, joint fit-up, and material restrictions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **applications of EBW, LBW, and PAW** in the aerospace industry, citing at least two specific components for each process. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**1. Vacuum Chamber Limitation (2 Marks)**
- EBW requires a vacuum of **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ mbar** to prevent electron scattering.
- Maximum workpiece size is limited by chamber volume (industrial chambers up to ~100 m³).
- Pump-down time of **5–30 minutes** per batch reduces productivity.
- *Why it exists:* Electrons have very small mass and are easily deflected by gas molecules.

**2. X-ray Radiation Hazard (2 Marks)**
- High-velocity electrons striking the metal produce **bremsstrahlung X-rays**.
- Lead shielding of **≥ 25 mm lead equivalent** is mandatory.
- *Safety:* Operators must follow AERB / NRC radiation protection guidelines.

**3. Joint Fit-up Sensitivity (1.5 Marks)**
- Gap tolerance ≤ 0.1 mm required for thick sections.
- Precise fixturing increases pre-weld cost.

**4. Material and Spiking Issues (1.5 Marks)**
- Materials with high vapor pressure (Mg, Zn, Cd) cannot be welded.
- Spiking and porosity in low-alloy steels require beam oscillation/defocusing.

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**Aerospace Applications Comparison (7 Marks)**

| Process | Aerospace Application 1 | Aerospace Application 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **EBW** | Rocket thrust chamber welding (Inconel 718) | Space Shuttle Main Engine nozzle assembly |
| **LBW** | Turbine blade crack repair (superalloys) | Ti-alloy skin-stringer joint in fuselage |
| **PAW** | Combustion liner seam welding (Hastelloy) | Honeycomb structure joining (thin Inconel) |

**Justification (1 Mark synthesis):**
- **EBW** is chosen for thick, mission-critical assemblies where vacuum purity and deep penetration outweigh cost.
- **LBW** is chosen for thin-section precision welds, especially where in-air operation is required.
- **PAW** is chosen for medium-thickness, moderate-volume production where the cost of a beam system is unjustified.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **limitations of Laser Beam Welding**, including reflectivity, plasma shielding, and HAZ cracking. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **principle and key applications of Plasma Arc Welding**. Discuss why PAW is preferred over TIG for thin-wall stainless steel tube welding. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**1. Reflectivity Issue (2 Marks)**
- Highly reflective metals (Al, Cu, Au) reflect much of the laser energy.
- For **Al alloys** at CO₂ 10.6 µm, initial absorption is only **3–5%**.
- *Mitigation:* Use fiber lasers (1.06 µm), surface texturing, or green diode lasers (532 nm).

**2. Plasma Plume Formation (2 Marks)**
- Metal vapor above the keyhole ionizes → **plasma shielding** absorbs/scatters laser beam.
- *Mitigation:* Use **helium shielding gas** (ionization potential 24.6 eV) and side-blowing techniques.

**3. HAZ Cracking in High-Carbon Steels (1.5 Marks)**
- Cooling rates of **10⁴ to 10⁶ °C/s** can produce brittle martensite → cold cracking.
- *Mitigation:* Preheating, post-weld heat treatment, or defocused mode operation.

**4. Cost and Fit-up (1.5 Marks)**
- Capital cost of **4 kW fiber laser ≈ ₹50 lakh to ₹1.5 crore**.
- Joint gap tolerance ≤ 10% of sheet thickness.

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**PAW Principle (3 Marks):**
- PAW uses a **constricted arc** formed by forcing the arc through a water-cooled copper nozzle orifice.
- **Plasma gas** (Ar or Ar + H₂) is ionized by a **pilot arc** between tungsten electrode and nozzle.
- The **transferred arc** is struck between electrode and workpiece, creating temperatures up to **30,000 °C** and jet velocities approaching **Mach 1**.
- Two operating modes: **Micro-plasma (I < 15 A)** for foils and **Keyhole PAW (I = 100–400 A)** for medium sections.

**Applications (2 Marks):**
- Stainless-steel tube-to-tubesheet welding in heat exchangers
- Combustion liner welding in gas turbines
- Refrigeration Cu tube joining
- Medical device (catheter, needle) assembly

**PAW vs TIG for Thin Stainless Steel Tubes (2 Marks):**
- PAW offers **higher energy concentration** (constricted arc) → better arc stability at low currents.
- PAW tolerates **wider parameter variation** while maintaining weld quality.
- PAW produces **narrower HAZ** and **less distortion** in thin tubes.
- PAW allows **higher travel speeds**, increasing productivity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Limitations and Applications" – Module 3**

- [x] **Three processes covered:** EBW, LBW, PAW – all are high-energy-density processes (P_d > 10⁵ W/mm²).
- [x] **EBW limitations:** vacuum, X-rays, spiking, fit-up, high-vapor-pressure materials excluded.
- [x] **EBW applications:** aerospace (Inconel, Ti), nuclear (reactor core), heavy industry (thick sections up to 200 mm).
- [x] **LBW limitations:** reflectivity (Al, Cu), plasma shielding, HAZ cracking, high cost, tight fit-up.
- [x] **LBW applications:** tailored blanks, Li-ion batteries, medical devices, turbine blade repair.
- [x] **PAW limitations:** only conductive materials, larger HAZ than beams, nozzle wear, complex parameters, thickness ≤ 12 mm.
- [x] **PAW applications:** honeycomb structures, tubesheet welding, refrigeration Cu tubes, surgical needles.
- [x] **Key formulas to memorize:** Power density $P_d = P/A$, Heat input $H = \eta V I / S$, Penetration $d = kP/S^{y}$.
- [x] **Distinguish clearly:** Power density ≠ Heat input. Power density is instantaneous concentration; heat input is total energy per unit length.
- [x] **Efficiency values to remember:** $\eta_{EBW} \approx 0.9$, $\eta_{LBW} \approx 0.7$, $\eta_{PAW} \approx 0.85$, $\eta_{TIG} \approx 0.7$.
- [x] **Always name the specific industry/component** in application questions – generic answers lose marks.
- [x] **Mention the *why*** of each limitation, not just the *what* – examiners award marks for justification.
- [x] **Use helium for LBW plasma shielding mitigation** – never argon (lower ionization potential ~15.8 eV).
- [x] **X-ray shielding requirement for EBW:** Lead ≥ 25 mm equivalent, AERB compliance in India.
- [x] **Plasma gas vs Shielding gas in PAW:** Plasma gas is constricted; shielding gas is the outer envelope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction Stir Welding - Process Variables and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Friction Stir Welding - Process Variables and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
