> **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** is a **solid-state joining process** in which a non-consumable rotating tool, consisting of a specially designed **pin (probe)** and a larger-diameter **shoulder**, is plunged into the abutting edges of two rigidly clamped workpieces. The frictional heat generated at the tool-workpiece interface, combined with adiabatic heating from severe plastic deformation, raises the local temperature of the material to a **thermo-mechanically affected zone (TMAZ)** without reaching the melting point. As the tool traverses along the joint line, the plasticized material is mechanically stirred, consolidated, and forged behind the tool, producing a continuous metallurgical bond in the solid state.

The process was invented and patented by **Wayne Thomas and colleagues at The Welding Institute (TWI), United Kingdom, in 1991**, and is recognized as one of the most significant advances in joining technology of the late 20th century. It is particularly suited for **aluminum, magnesium, copper, and dissimilar metal combinations** that are difficult to join by conventional fusion welding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Fusion Welding:** FSW never exceeds the melting point of the base metal. Therefore, problems such as porosity, solidification cracking, distortion, hydrogen embrittlement, and loss of alloying elements (commonly encountered in arc welding of aluminum) are either eliminated or drastically reduced.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to mix two sticks of cold butter lying side by side on a plate. You cannot simply slide a cold knife between them to make them join. However, if you press a **hot, spinning spoon** down between them and slowly drag it sideways, the heat softens the butter, and the spinning motion **stirs the two sticks together**, forming a continuous, homogeneous strip of butter. As the spoon moves away, the stirred butter cools and solidifies into a unified mass.

The "spoon" is the **FSW tool**, the "butter" is the **workpiece metal (softened but not melted)**, and the resulting "unified strip" is the **friction stir weld**. The spoon does not consume the butter, just as the FSW tool does not melt or get consumed by the metal.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Stir"?** The word *stir* emphasizes that the pin mechanically transports material from the leading edge (advancing side) to the trailing edge (retreating side) of the tool. The shoulder then *forges* the stirred mass downward, closing any cavity and producing a defect-free weld.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Range / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tool rotation speed | $n$ | **100 – 3000** | rpm |
| Traverse (welding) speed | $v$ | **10 – 500** | mm/min |
| Pin length | $L_p$ | Equal to joint thickness minus shoulder plunge | mm |
| Shoulder diameter | $D_s$ | **2 – 4 times pin diameter** | mm |
| Pin diameter | $D_p$ | **3 – 15** | mm |
| Tilt angle | $\theta$ | **0° – 5°** (typically 2°–3°) | degrees |
| Coefficient of friction (interface) | $\mu$ | **0.3 – 0.6** (for plasticized metal) | dimensionless |
| Peak weld temperature | $T_{peak}$ | **0.6 – 0.95 × T_melting** | °C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Physics of the FSW Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The FSW process can be broken into **four overlapping stages**, each controlled by specific process variables:

### Stage 1 — Pre-Weld Setup & Clamping
- Workpieces are rigidly clamped to a backing plate to prevent *backing-plate deflection* and flash ejection.
- Poor clamping leads to **flash defects**, excessive thinning, and loss of forging pressure.

### Stage 2 — Plunge (Dwell) Phase
- The rotating tool is forced vertically downward into the joint line.
- The **shoulder** contacts the workpiece top surface first, generating the bulk of the frictional heat.
- A **dwell period** (typically 2–10 seconds) stabilizes temperature before traverse begins.

### Stage 3 — Traverse (Welding) Phase
- The tool moves along the joint line at constant $v$.
- Three metallurgical zones form around the tool:
  - **Stir Zone (SZ) / Nugget** — fully recrystallized, fine-grained equiaxed structure.
  - **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ)** — deformed but not recrystallized.
  - **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — only thermal exposure, no plastic strain.

### Stage 4 — Retraction (Pull-Out)
- The tool is withdrawn at the end of the weld, leaving an **exit hole (keyhole)** — the major geometric drawback of FSW.

## 2.2 Detailed Process Variables

| Category | Variable | Effect on Weld Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine** | Rotation speed ($n$, rpm) | ↑ $n$ → ↑ heat input, ↓ flow stress, ↑ grain size, possible overheating |
| **Machine** | Traverse speed ($v$, mm/min) | ↑ $v$ → ↓ heat input, ↑ strain rate, risk of tunnel defects |
| **Machine** | Tilt angle ($\theta$, deg) | Provides forging action; ↑ $\theta$ → ↑ penetration & consolidation |
| **Machine** | Axial force / plunge depth ($F$, kN) | Maintains material consolidation; insufficient $F$ → cavities |
| **Tool** | Shoulder design (scroll, concave, flat, spiral) | Determines heat generation & material flow |
| **Tool** | Pin geometry (cylindrical, conical, threaded, fluted, triflute) | Affects material stirring pattern & flow |
| **Tool** | Pin thread pitch & direction | Influences vertical material transport |
| **Material** | Base metal type & temper | Determines flow stress, peak $T$, and weldability |
| **Material** | Joint design (butt, lap, T, fillet) | Affects tool selection & fixture requirements |
| **Material** | Clamping rigidity | Prevents flash & maintains forging pressure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitch Ratio Concept:** A critical KTU-level parameter is the **pitch ratio** $p$, defined as the linear advance of the tool per revolution:
> $$p = \dfrac{v}{n} \quad \text{[mm/rev]}$$
> - **Low pitch ratio (heat-input dominant):** Coarse grain, soft nugget, possible kissing-bond defect.
> - **High pitch ratio (strain-rate dominant):** Fine grain, high strength, risk of tunnel defects.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{shoulder} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu F \omega R_s^3$ | Heat generated at the shoulder (Schmidt & Hattel model) | W |
| 2 | $Q_{pin} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu F_{pin} \omega R_p^3$ | Heat generated at the pin interface | W |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60}$ | Angular velocity of the tool | rad/s |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{v}$ | **Heat input per unit weld length** (key KTU parameter) | J/mm |
| 5 | $T_{peak} = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{n}{v}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Empirical peak temperature (K = material constant) | °C |
| 6 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi n R_p}{60 L_p}$ | Effective strain rate near the pin surface | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $p = v / n$ | Pitch ratio (advance per revolution) | mm/rev |
| 8 | $F_{axial} = k_y \cdot A_{projection}$ | Approximate axial force = yield stress × projected area | N |
| 9 | $T_{ratio} = T_{peak} / T_{melting}$ | Temperature ratio (typically **0.6 – 0.95**) | – |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{total}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency of FSW (typically **80 – 95%**) | % |

**Where:**
- $F$ = axial force (N)
- $R_s$, $R_p$ = shoulder radius, pin radius (m)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction
- $n$ = rotation speed (rpm)
- $v$ = traverse speed (mm/min)
- $L_p$ = pin length (m)
- $k_y$ = dynamic yield stress at weld temperature (Pa)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Always state the **assumptions** while solving numerical problems on heat input: (1) 100% plastic deformation (no elastic strain), (2) constant $\mu$, (3) uniform slip at the shoulder interface, (4) no heat loss to backing plate.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Relevance

| Industry | Typical FSW Application | Material Joined | Key Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Fuselage panels, rocket fuel tanks, wing structures | AA 2024, AA 7075, AA 2195 (Al-Li) | Eliminates porosity in fusion welds |
| **Shipbuilding** | Deck panels, bulkheads, hull sections | AA 5083, AA 6082 | Welds 12 m long panels in single pass |
| **Automotive** | EV battery trays, rear-axle housings, suspension arms | AA 6061, AA 6063, Mg alloys | Lightweighting, no distortion |
| **Railways** | High-speed train body shells, floor panels | AA 6005A, AA 7020 | Replaces MIG welding of large panels |
| **Energy** | Cryogenic LNG tank panels, heat exchangers | AA 5083, AA 3003, pure Cu | No solidification cracks |
| **Defense** | Armored vehicle hulls, naval structures | AA 2519, AA 5083 | Ballistic integrity preserved |
| **Bridge construction** | Aluminium pedestrian bridges, marine piers | AA 6082, AA 5083 | No HAZ softening in heat-treatable alloys |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Generated at the Tool Shoulder (Schmidt & Hattel Model)

The dominant heat source in FSW is the **shoulder-workpiece interface**, which contributes approximately **80 – 90%** of the total frictional heat. We model the shoulder as a rotating disk of radius $R_s$ pressed against the workpiece with axial force $F$ under pure sliding friction.

### Given
- Axial force at shoulder: $F$ (N)
- Shoulder radius: $R_s$ (m)
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu$ (dimensionless)
- Angular velocity: $\omega$ (rad/s)

### Step 1: Differential Ring Element
Consider a differential annular ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ on the shoulder. Its area is:
$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

The contact pressure (assuming uniform distribution) is:
$$p = \dfrac{F}{\pi R_s^2}$$

### Step 2: Frictional Shear Stress
The shear stress at the interface from Coulomb friction is:
$$\tau(r) = \mu \cdot p = \dfrac{\mu F}{\pi R_s^2}$$

### Step 3: Local Sliding Velocity
The local linear velocity at radius $r$ is:
$$v(r) = \omega r$$

### Step 4: Differential Power (Heat Generation)
The differential frictional power on the ring is:
$$dQ = \tau(r) \cdot v(r) \cdot dA = \left(\dfrac{\mu F}{\pi R_s^2}\right) \cdot (\omega r) \cdot (2\pi r \, dr)$$

Simplifying:
$$dQ = \dfrac{2 \mu F \omega}{R_s^2} \, r^2 \, dr$$

### Step 5: Integrate from 0 to $R_s$
$$Q_{shoulder} = \int_0^{R_s} \dfrac{2 \mu F \omega}{R_s^2} \, r^2 \, dr = \dfrac{2 \mu F \omega}{R_s^2} \cdot \left[\dfrac{r^3}{3}\right]_0^{R_s}$$

$$Q_{shoulder} = \dfrac{2 \mu F \omega}{R_s^2} \cdot \dfrac{R_s^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{shoulder} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu F \omega R_s^3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\pi}\right) \cdot \pi = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu F \omega R_s^3}{3} \;\; \text{[Watts]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **2/3** is the classical result of the rotating-disk friction model and is **examinable** in KTU derivations. Some textbooks absorb $\pi$ into the expression, giving the alternative form $Q = (2/3)\pi \mu F \omega R_s^3$. Always check the convention.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Input per Unit Weld Length

The **heat input per unit length** $E$ is the most frequently asked KTU numerical because it directly controls weld quality and is used for parametric analysis.

### Given
- Total heat generated: $Q_{total} = Q_{shoulder} + Q_{pin}$ (W)
- Traverse speed: $v$ (mm/min)

### Step 1: Convert Traverse Speed
$$v_{SI} = \dfrac{v}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

### Step 2: Heat Input Definition
$$E = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{v_{SI}} \quad \text{[J/m]}$$

For KTU problems expressed in J/mm, divide the SI result by 1000.

### Step 3: Substituting the Shoulder Heat
If we neglect the pin contribution (≈ 10–15%):
$$E = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu F \omega R_s^3}{3 \, v_{SI}}$$

Replacing $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$:
$$E = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu F \cdot (2\pi n / 60) \cdot R_s^3}{3 \, v_{SI}} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu F n R_s^3}{180 \, v_{SI}}$$

Simplifying:
$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{\pi^2 \mu F n R_s^3}{45 \, v_{SI}} \quad \text{[J/m]}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Strain Rate at the Pin Surface

The severe plastic deformation around the pin produces very high strain rates, which drive dynamic recrystallization in the stir zone.

### Given
- Rotation speed: $n$ (rpm)
- Pin radius: $R_p$ (m)
- Pin length: $L_p$ (m)

### Step 1: Peripheral Velocity
$$v_{peripheral} = \dfrac{2 \pi n R_p}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

### Step 2: Time for One Rotation at the Surface
$$t = \dfrac{60}{n} \quad \text{[s/rev]}$$

### Step 3: Shear Strain per Revolution
As the pin surface moves through a distance $2\pi R_p$ relative to material at radius $R_p$ in one revolution, and the shear layer thickness is approximately $L_p$:
$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v_{peripheral}}{L_p} = \dfrac{2 \pi n R_p}{60 L_p}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi n R_p}{30 L_p} \quad \text{[s}^{-1}\text{]}}$$

For typical values $n = 1000$ rpm, $R_p = 5$ mm, $L_p = 5$ mm:
$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 105 \;\; \text{s}^{-1}$$

This is **2–3 orders of magnitude higher** than conventional hot working — explaining the very fine, recrystallized grain structure in the nugget.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem [KTU 2022 Pattern]:**
A friction stir welding operation uses a tool shoulder diameter of **20 mm**, axial force of **10 kN**, rotation speed of **1000 rpm**, and traverse speed of **100 mm/min**. Assuming $\mu = 0.4$, calculate:
1. The heat generated per second at the shoulder.
2. The heat input per unit length of weld (J/mm).

**Solution:**

### Part 1 — Heat Generated
$$R_s = \dfrac{20}{2} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.01 \text{ m}$$
$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi (1000)}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$F = 10{,}000 \text{ N}, \quad \mu = 0.4$$

$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu F \omega R_s^3 = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi (0.4)(10{,}000)(104.72)(0.01)^3$$

$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 3.1416 \times 0.4 \times 10{,}000 \times 104.72 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.4 \times 10{,}000 \times 104.72 \times 10^{-6}}{3} = \dfrac{2.631}{3} \times 10^{0}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.877 \text{ kW} = 877 \text{ W}}$$

### Part 2 — Heat Input per Unit Length
$$v_{SI} = \dfrac{100 \text{ mm/min}}{60} = 1.667 \text{ mm/s} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

$$E = \dfrac{Q}{v_{SI}} = \dfrac{877}{1.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 526{,}200 \text{ J/m}$$

$$\boxed{E = 526.2 \text{ J/mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (≈ 500 J/mm) is in the **typical range of 300 – 1000 J/mm** reported for FSW of 6 mm thick aluminum plates. Lower values would cause tunnel defects; higher values would cause flash and grain coarsening.

## 3.5 Defect-Parameter Correlation Matrix

| Defect | Cause (Process Variable) | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Tunnel / wormhole defect** | Too high $v$, too low $n$, insufficient forging | ↓ $v$, ↑ $n$, ↑ $F$, use threaded pin |
| **Flash / thinning** | Too high $n$, too low $v$, excessive plunge | ↓ $n$, ↑ $v$, reduce tilt angle |
| **Kissing bond (lazy S)** | Insufficient material flow, low heat input | ↑ $n$, ↓ $v$, use fluted pin |
| **Surface galling** | Excessive heat, wrong shoulder scroll | Use concave shoulder, ↓ $n$ |
| **Root flaw (lack of penetration)** | Pin length too short, low $F$ | Increase $L_p$, ↑ $F$ |
| **Excessive grain growth** | Too high heat input (low $v$ / high $n$) | ↑ $v$ or ↓ $n$ |
| **Onion rings absent** | Insufficient material mixing | Use triflute or threaded pin |
| **Exit hole crack** | Sudden tool retraction | Use run-off tab; smooth retraction |

> [!NOTE]
> The **shoulder** generates ~80–90% of the total frictional heat and provides the **forging pressure** that consolidates the stirred material. The **pin** provides the localized stirring action and the initial heat generation at depth.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeU[Top View of Workpiece Surface Around Tool] --> nodeA[Advancing Side]
    nodeU --> nodeR2[Retreating Side]
    nodeU --> nodeF[Tool Travel Direction]
    nodeA --> nodeN[Stir Zone Nugget Recrystallized]
    nodeR2 --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeT[TMAZ Thermo Mechanically Affected Zone]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Asymmetry Note:** Because of the rotational asymmetry of the tool, the **advancing side** (where tool rotation matches the traverse direction) and the **retreating side** (opposite) experience different strain rates and thermal cycles. This produces a characteristic **"onion ring" pattern** in the stir zone and a sharp interface on the advancing side.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define Friction Stir Welding and list any four process variables that affect the weld quality.**

**Model Answer:**

Friction Stir Welding (FSW) is a solid-state joining process in which a rotating non-consumable tool, comprising a pin and a shoulder, is traversed along the joint line of two rigidly clamped workpieces. The frictional and deformational heat softens the material without melting, and the tool mechanically stirs the plasticized material to form a solid-state bond.

**Four major process variables:**
1. **Tool rotation speed (n, rpm)** — controls heat input and material flow.
2. **Traverse speed (v, mm/min)** — determines heat input per unit length and strain rate.
3. **Tilt angle (θ, degrees)** — affects forging pressure and penetration.
4. **Axial force / plunge depth (F, kN)** — controls consolidation and weld integrity.

*Additional variables for higher credit: shoulder design, pin geometry, material flow, joint type, clamping method.*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between the Stir Zone (SZ) and the Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ) in an FSW joint.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Stir Zone (SZ / Nugget) | TMAZ |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Directly around the tool pin | Adjacent to the stir zone |
| **Temperature reached** | Close to (but below) $T_{melting}$ | Below recrystallization temperature |
| **Plastic deformation** | Severe, continuous dynamic recrystallization | Moderate, no recrystallization |
| **Grain structure** | Fine, equiaxed recrystallized grains | Deformed, elongated, partially recovered |
| **Mechanical property** | Highest hardness, homogeneous | Intermediate, with sharp transition to HAZ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Friction Stir Welding with a neat functional block diagram. Discuss the role of (i) the shoulder and (ii) the pin during the process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a clear derivation, obtain the expression for the **heat generated per second at the tool shoulder** in FSW. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution for (a):

Friction Stir Welding operates on the principle of **thermo-mechanical solid-state bonding**. A non-consumable tool, rotated at high speed, is plunged into the joint line. The frictional contact between the shoulder and the workpiece, combined with the plastic shear deformation at the pin-workpiece interface, generates the heat required to plasticize (but not melt) the surrounding material. As the tool traverses, the plasticized material is mechanically transported around the pin from the leading edge to the trailing edge and consolidated under the forging action of the shoulder, producing a defect-free solid-state joint.

**Role of the Shoulder:**
- Generates **~80–90% of the total frictional heat** (heat input dominant).
- Provides **forging pressure** to consolidate stirred material.
- Prevents material from being ejected upward during stirring.
- Concave / scrolled designs enhance material flow toward the centre.

**Role of the Pin:**
- Provides **localized stirring** and breaks up the oxide layer at the joint.
- Generates ~10–20% of the heat (via shear deformation).
- Threads / flutes induce **vertical material flow**, eliminating tunnel defects.
- Penetration depth = plate thickness minus shoulder plunge.

#### Solution for (b):

**Assumptions:**
1. Uniform pressure distribution under the shoulder.
2. Pure Coulomb friction, constant $\mu$ across the interface.
3. Full slip between shoulder and workpiece.
4. No heat loss to backing plate or radiation.
5. Quasi-steady state during traverse.

**Derivation:**

Consider a differential annular ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ on the shoulder of radius $R_s$:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr, \quad p = \dfrac{F}{\pi R_s^2}, \quad \tau = \mu p, \quad v(r) = \omega r$$

Differential power:
$$dQ = \tau \cdot v(r) \cdot dA = \dfrac{\mu F \omega}{R_s^2} \cdot 2\pi r^2 \, dr$$

Integrating from 0 to $R_s$:
$$Q_{shoulder} = \int_0^{R_s} \dfrac{2 \pi \mu F \omega}{R_s^2} r^2 \, dr = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu F \omega}{R_s^2} \cdot \dfrac{R_s^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{shoulder} = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu F \omega R_s^3 \;\; \text{Watts}}$$

where $\omega = 2\pi n / 60$ is the angular velocity in rad/s.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Alternative Model Question]**

**(a)** List and explain **six major process variables** of Friction Stir Welding. Discuss how each affects the heat input, material flow, and final weld quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A friction stir welding operation is performed on a **6 mm thick AA 6061-T6 plate** using the following parameters: shoulder diameter = **18 mm**, axial force = **8 kN**, rotation speed = **1200 rpm**, traverse speed = **120 mm/min**, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.45$. Calculate **(i)** the heat generated per second, and **(ii)** the heat input per unit weld length in J/mm. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution for (a):

| # | Variable | Effect on Heat Input | Effect on Material Flow | Effect on Weld Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rotation speed (n)** | ↑ n → ↑ heat | ↑ flow velocity around pin | ↑ n → softer nugget; excessive → flash |
| 2 | **Traverse speed (v)** | ↑ v → ↓ heat input per length | ↓ time for material to flow | ↑ v → may form tunnel defects |
| 3 | **Tilt angle (θ)** | Marginal effect | Improves vertical forging | ↑ θ → better root penetration |
| 4 | **Axial force (F)** | Direct proportionality | Maintains forging pressure | Insufficient → kissing bond / cavity |
| 5 | **Shoulder design** | Concave gives best coupling | Scroll drives material inward | Wrong design → surface galling |
| 6 | **Pin geometry** | Small heat contribution | Threads / flutes enhance downward flow | Threaded pin eliminates tunnels |
| 7 | **Joint type** | No direct effect | Changes flow pattern | Butt vs lap selection critical |
| 8 | **Clamping rigidity** | Indirect | Prevents flash & flow disruption | Poor clamping → thinning |

#### Solution for (b):

**Given:**
$$D_s = 18 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R_s = 9 \text{ mm} = 9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$
$$F = 8000 \text{ N}, \quad n = 1200 \text{ rpm}, \quad v = 120 \text{ mm/min}, \quad \mu = 0.45$$

**Angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi n}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1200}{60} = 40 \pi = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (i) — Heat generated per second:**
$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu F \omega R_s^3$$
$$Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 3.1416 \times 0.45 \times 8000 \times 125.66 \times (9 \times 10^{-3})^3$$

Computing step by step:
- $(9 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 729 \times 10^{-9} = 7.29 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$
- $0.45 \times 8000 = 3600$
- $3600 \times 125.66 = 452{,}376$
- $452{,}376 \times 7.29 \times 10^{-7} = 0.3298$
- $\dfrac{2}{3} \times 3.1416 \times 0.3298 = 0.6908$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 691 \text{ W} \; (\approx 0.69 \text{ kW})}$$

**Part (ii) — Heat input per unit length:**

Convert $v$ to SI units:
$$v_{SI} = \dfrac{120}{60 \times 1000} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

$$E = \dfrac{Q}{v_{SI}} = \dfrac{691}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 345{,}500 \text{ J/m}$$

$$\boxed{E = 345.5 \text{ J/mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This heat input (≈ 345 J/mm) is in the **optimum range for 6 mm AA 6061-T6 plates** (literature optimum: 300 – 600 J/mm). Lower values → lack of bonding; higher values → grain coarsening and strength loss.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in heat-input problems** — students mix mm/min with m/s, leading to wrong answers by a factor of 60,000. Always convert $v$ to **m/s** before substituting in the W → J/m formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 2/3** in the shoulder heat equation — this factor is derived from the integration of $r^2 dr$ and is **not** an empirical correction. Marks are lost if it is missing.
> 3. **Confusing pitch ratio** — it is advance per revolution, not revolution per advance.
> 4. **Stating FSW "melts" the metal** — this is a fundamental error; FSW is a **solid-state** process, and answers that claim otherwise are **marked down heavily** in Module 3 questions.
> 5. **In numericals, omitting assumptions** — KTU evaluators deduct 1–2 marks if the five standard assumptions (uniform pressure, Coulomb friction, full slip, no heat loss, steady state) are not stated.
> 6. **Drawing the FSW tool with the pin as a separate "consumable"** — the FSW tool is **non-consumable**; this is a hallmark confusion that must be avoided.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Friction Stir Welding**

- **FSW is a solid-state joining process** developed at **TWI, UK (1991)** by Wayne Thomas.
- The **tool** consists of a **shoulder** (heat + forging) and a **pin** (stirring + local heat).
- The metal is **never melted** — temperature stays between **0.6 – 0.95 × T_melting**.
- The **stir zone (nugget)** is fine, equiaxed, recrystallized; TMAZ is deformed; HAZ is only thermally affected.
- **Six process variables** to memorize: rotation speed $n$, traverse speed $v$, tilt angle $\theta$, axial force $F$, shoulder design, pin geometry.
- **Pitch ratio** $p = v/n$ (mm/rev) — controls the heat-input vs strain-rate balance.
- **Heat generated at shoulder:** $Q = (2/3)\pi \mu F \omega R_s^3$ (Schmidt & Hattel model).
- **Heat input per unit length:** $E = Q/v$ — the single most important process-quality index.
- **Strain rate** at pin surface: $\dot{\varepsilon} = \pi n R_p / (30 L_p)$ — typically 10 – 200 s⁻¹.
- **Thermal efficiency** of FSW is exceptionally high (**80 – 95%**).
- **Major defects**: tunnel/wormhole, flash, kissing bond, root lack-of-penetration, surface galling.
- **Onion rings** in the nugget are the visual signature of good material flow.
- **Exit hole (keyhole)** is an inherent geometric limitation of FSW.
- **Typical applications**: aerospace (AA 2024/7075/2195), automotive EV battery trays (AA 5754/6061), shipbuilding (AA 5083), cryogenic LNG tanks, railway car bodies, aluminium bridges.
- **Dissimilar and difficult-to-weld metals** (Al-Mg, Al-Cu, Mg alloys) are FSW's competitive advantage.
- **FSW is "green"** — no filler wire, no shielding gas, no fumes, no spatter, no UV radiation.
- **Key competitors of FSW**: laser welding, electron-beam welding, ultrasonic welding, magnetic pulse welding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion Welding - Theory and Principle of Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Diffusion Welding - Theory and Principle of Process # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition **Diffusion Welding (DFW)**, also called **Diffusion Bonding (DFB)**, is a **solid-state joining process** in which two mating surfaces are brought into intimate atomic contact at an elevated temperature (typically **0.5 to 0.8 T_m**, where T_m is the melting point in Kelvin) under a controlled, moderate pressure (typically **1–10 MPa**), for a sustained dwell time (ranging from a few minutes to several hours), to produce a metallurgical bond **without macroscopic deformation, melting, or relative motion of the parts** (no macroscopic plastic flow of the bulk material). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** *Diffusion welding is a process in which joining is accomplished by the application of pressure and temperature over a period of time, such that the atoms of the two mating surfaces diffuse across the interface, resulting in a bond that is essentially indistinguishable from the parent metal microstructure, with no visible interface line.* The bond is achieved through three simultaneous atomistic mechanisms operating at the interface: 1. **Plastic deformation** of surface asperities (creep + micro-plasticity). 2. **Diffusion** of atoms across the original interface (volume, surface, and grain boundary diffusion). 3. **Recrystallization and grain boundary migration** across the joint line, obliterating the interface. > [!NOTE] > **Synonyms (be careful in KTU viva):** *Diffusion Bonding (DFB), Hot Pressure Welding, Solid-State Diffusion Bonding, Diffusion Welding with Applied Pressure (DFW-AP), and Thermo-Compression Bonding (in microelectronics context).* --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stacking Sugar Cubes Trick" Imagine pressing two sugar cubes together under your palm for a few seconds — they don't stick. But now imagine: | Real-World Analogy | Diffusion Welding Concept | |---|---| | Slightly warm the cubes in an oven | **Elevated Temperature (T)** — provides atomic vibrational energy | | Press them very hard with a vise | **Applied Pressure (P)** — deforms asperities, increases real contact area | | Hold them inside a humidifier for 2 hours | **Time (t)** — water molecules slowly diffuse across the contact | | The cubes fuse into one block | **Metallurgical Bond** — atoms have inter-diffused across the former interface | > [!TIP] > The "sugar cube" intuition is useful, but the correct scientific analogue is **two perfectly clean, atomically rough metal surfaces that, given enough heat and time, behave like a single piece of metal because atoms literally "swap places" across the old boundary.** This is *not* brazing (no filler), *not* fusion welding (no melting), and *not* mechanical fastening (no external fastener). --- ## 1.3 Distinguishing Diffusion Welding from Neighbouring Processes | Feature | Diffusion Welding | Fusion Welding (e.g., TIG) | Brazing | Adhesive Bonding | |---|---|---|---|---| | Base metal melting | **No** | Yes | No (filler melts) | No | | Filler metal used | **No** | Often | Yes | No (adhesive used) | | Joint temperature | 0.5–0.8 T_m | Above T_m | Above filler T_m, below base T_m | < 200 °C | | HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) | **Negligible** | Pronounced | Negligible | None | | Joint strength vs. parent | **≥ 95–100%** | Often weaker | 60–80% | Variable | | Dissimilar metal capability | **Excellent** | Limited | Good | Excellent | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The single most testable KTU fact:** *Diffusion welding produces the highest parent-metal-strength ratio of any joining process, which is why it is the *de facto* standard for aerospace titanium structures, nuclear reactor components, and honeycomb sandwich panels.* --- ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used in Diffusion Welding - **Homologous temperature (T/T_m):** 0.5 – 0.8 (dimensionless). - **Bonding pressure (P):** 1 – 10 MPa (for metals), up to 100 MPa (for ceramics). - **Bonding time (t):** 1 minute – 4 hours. - **Vacuum/Atmosphere:** typically **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ Torr** (to prevent oxide formation). - **Surface roughness (R_a):** should be < 0.4 µm to minimize voids. - **Universal gas constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**. - **Boltzmann constant (k):** **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**. - **Avogadro's number (N_A):** **6.022 × 10²³ /mol**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse "diffusion welding" with "diffusion in fusion welding" (e.g., carbon diffusion in HAZ of a steel weld). They are entirely different processes. In *diffusion welding*, diffusion is the **primary joining mechanism**. In *fusion welding*, diffusion is only a *side effect*. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Sequential Stages of Diffusion Welding The micro-mechanism of diffusion bonding was famously codified by **King and Owczarski (1968)** and later refined by **Derby and Wallach (1982)**. The bond-line evolution occurs in **four overlapping stages**: ### Stage I — Asperity Deformation and Initial Contact (t ≈ 0–5% of total time) - Surface is rough at micro-scale (peak-to-valley). - Applied pressure causes **plastic yielding** of the asperity tips. - Real contact area grows from < 1% to ~15–20% of nominal area. - **Governing mechanism:** plastic deformation (Orowan yielding) + creep. ### Stage II — Interface Diffusion and Void Formation (t ≈ 5–40%) - Contact area now 30–50%. - The unbonded regions form **isolated lenticular voids** along the interface. - Atoms diffuse from the contact bridges (high chemical potential) to fill voids. - **Governing mechanism:** surface diffusion, grain boundary diffusion, and volume diffusion. ### Stage III — Void Elimination and Grain Boundary Migration (t ≈ 40–90%) - Voids shrink continuously; cylindrical and spherical voids dominate. - Power-law creep assists in closing residual voids. - Original interface begins to disappear as **new grain boundaries cross it**. - **Governing mechanism:** volume diffusion + power-law creep. ### Stage IV — Final Metallurgical Continuity (t > 90%) - All voids eliminated (in ideal case). - The interface is **microstructurally indistinguishable** from the parent metal. - Bond strength ≈ parent metal strength. - **Governing mechanism:** final-stage volume diffusion and grain growth. --- ## 2.2 Critical Process Parameters (the "T-P-t Trinity") The KTU board expects you to know the *qualitative effect* of each parameter on bond quality: | Parameter | Increase → Effect on Bond Quality | Physical Reason | |---|---|---| | **Temperature (T) ↑** | **Strongly improves** (exponentially) | ↑ atomic mobility, ↑ D, ↑ creep rate | | **Pressure (P) ↑** | Improves up to a limit, then saturates | ↑ initial contact area, ↓ surface roughness effect | | **Time (t) ↑** | Improves but with **diminishing returns** | Diffusion length ∝ √(Dt) — square-root law | | **Surface roughness ↓** | **Strongly improves** | ↓ initial void volume, ↓ time needed | | **Atmosphere cleaner ↑** | Improves | ↓ oxide layer, ↓ contaminant film | | **Grain size ↓** | Slightly improves | ↑ grain boundary diffusion path (more GB area) | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **high-yield** for KTU ESE and Module tests. Memorize the equation symbols AND units. | # | Formula | Equation | Use in DFW | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fick's First Law | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Flux of atoms across interface | mol/(m²·s) | | 2 | Fick's Second Law | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Concentration profile evolution | mol/m³, s, m²/s | | 3 | Arrhenius Diffusion | $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of D | m²/s | | 4 | Diffusion Length | $x \approx \sqrt{Dt}$ | Penetration depth in time t | m | | 5 | Power-Law Creep | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^{n} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | Stage III–IV void closure | 1/s | | 6 | Bonding Time (Derby–Wallach) | $t \approx \dfrac{kT \, \delta \, V_m}{D_{gb} \, \delta_{gb} \, \Omega \, P}$ | Stage II & III time estimate | s | | 7 | Homologous Temperature | $T_H = T / T_m$ | Parameter window selection | dimensionless | | 8 | Vacancy Concentration | $C_v = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_f}{kT}\right)$ | Self-diffusion driver | dimensionless | | 9 | Bonding Pressure Estimate | $P \approx \dfrac{3 \sigma_y \, A_{real}}{2 \, A_{nom}}$ | Initial asperity yielding | Pa | | 10 | Average Bond Strength (rule) | $\sigma_b \approx \sigma_m (1 - f_v)$ | Where $f_v$ = void fraction | Pa | > **Symbol legend (don't lose marks in ESE):** > - $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $D_0$ = pre-exponential factor (m²/s) > - $Q$ = activation energy for diffusion (J/mol) > - $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**, $k$ = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** > - $T$ = absolute temperature (K), $T_m$ = melting point (K) > - $\delta$ = interface thickness, $\Omega$ = atomic volume > - $\sigma_y$ = yield stress, $n$ = creep exponent (typically 3–8) > - $A_{real}$ = real contact area, $A_{nom}$ = nominal contact area --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (Titanium alloys Ti-6Al-4V):** Engine compressor blades, honeycomb panels for Airbus A350 and Boeing 787. - **Nuclear industry:** Zircaloy–stainless steel transition joints in reactor cores. - **Electronics:** Thermo-compression bonding of Cu pillars in flip-chip packaging. - **Power generation:** Hot isostatic pressing (HIP) of superalloy turbine discs. - **Joining dissimilar metals:** Al–Cu, Al–Mg, Ti–stainless steel, ceramic–metal (cermet) joints. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown. Each step includes a textual justification row. Multi-line equations are inside `\begin{aligned}` blocks with one blank line above and below every `$$` block. --- ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fick's First Law from the Random Walk Argument **Premise:** Consider a 1D atomic plane at position $x = 0$ with atomic concentration $C(x, t)$. Atoms execute a thermally activated random walk with mean jump distance $\lambda$ and jump frequency $\nu$ (in the direction +x or −x with equal probability 0.5). **Step 1 — Net flux calculation:** The net flux $J$ across the plane at $x = 0$ is the difference between the flux from the plane at $x = -\lambda$ (jumping right) and the flux from the plane at $x = +\lambda$ (jumping left). $$ \begin{aligned} J &= \tfrac{1}{2} \, \nu \, C(x - \lambda) \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \nu \, C(x + \lambda) \\[4pt] &= \tfrac{1}{2} \nu \, \bigl[\,C(x - \lambda) - C(x + \lambda)\,\bigr] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Taylor expansion of the concentration around $x$:** Expand $C(x - \lambda)$ and $C(x + \lambda)$ using Taylor series to second order in $\lambda$: $$ \begin{aligned} C(x - \lambda) &\approx C(x) - \lambda \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} + \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda^{2} \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} \\[4pt] C(x + \lambda) &\approx C(x) + \lambda \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} + \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda^{2} \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Subtraction:** $$ \begin{aligned} C(x - \lambda) - C(x + \lambda) &\approx -\,2 \lambda \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into flux expression:** $$ \begin{aligned} J &= \tfrac{1}{2} \nu \left(-2 \lambda \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) \\[4pt] &= -\,\nu \lambda \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the diffusion coefficient:** By definition, the diffusion coefficient is $D = \nu \lambda^{2}/2$. Substituting $\nu \lambda = 2D/\lambda$: $$ \begin{aligned} J &= -\,\dfrac{2D}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{2} \\[4pt] &= -D \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result — Fick's First Law (one dimension):** $$ J = -D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} $$ > **Textual interpretation:** *The negative sign means atoms diffuse from high concentration to low concentration, i.e., down the concentration gradient. The gradient is the "driving force" for diffusion, and $D$ is the "mobility factor."* --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of D **Premise:** An atom in a solid lattice is held in a potential energy well of depth $Q$ (the activation energy for diffusion). To jump to a neighbouring site, it must acquire kinetic energy $\geq Q$, which is a Boltzmann probability event. **Step 1 — Probability of acquiring energy ≥ Q:** From the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution, the fraction of atoms with energy $\geq Q$ is: $$ p = \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{kT}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Atomic vibration (attempt) frequency:** A vibrating atom attempts a jump at its Debye frequency $\nu_0 \approx 10^{13}$ Hz. Of these, fraction $p$ succeeds: $$ \nu = \nu_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{kT}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Combine with $D = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu \lambda^{2}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda^{2} \nu_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{kT}\right) \\[4pt] &= D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{kT}\right) \end{aligned} $$ where the **pre-exponential factor** is defined as $D_0 = \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda^{2} \nu_0$ (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-2}$ m²/s for substitutional diffusion in metals). **Step 4 — Convert to per-mole basis (engineering form):** For use in metallurgical data tables, $Q$ is quoted per mole. Using $Q_{\text{mol}} = Q \cdot N_A$ and $R = k \cdot N_A$: $$ D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_{\text{mol}}}{RT}\right) $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\,D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)\,} $$ > **Why this matters in KTU ESE:** *A 100 K increase in temperature (e.g., from 800 K to 900 K) can increase $D$ by **3 to 10 times** for typical metals, because of the exponential dependence. This is why temperature is the most powerful parameter in diffusion welding.* --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Non-Steady Diffusion Length in a Bonding Time $t$ **Premise:** In diffusion welding, the question is: *"In time $t$, how far do atoms migrate across the interface?"* This sets the minimum scale of grain boundary migration needed to obliterate the interface. **Step 1 — Use the 1D non-steady Fick's law:** $$ \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} $$ **Step 2 — Assume constant $D$ and use similarity variable $\eta = x / \sqrt{Dt}$:** The standard Gaussian solution for an instantaneous source is: $$ C(x, t) = \dfrac{N_0}{2 \sqrt{\pi D t}} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^{2}}{4 D t}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Define diffusion length $x_d$ as the distance where concentration falls to $1/e$ of peak (i.e., the e-folding length):** Setting the exponent to $-1$: $$ \dfrac{x_d^{\,2}}{4 D t} = 1 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $x_d$:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_d^{\,2} &= 4 D t \\[4pt] x_d &= 2 \sqrt{D t} \end{aligned} $$ For a more conservative estimate, the "characteristic diffusion distance" is often quoted as: $$ \boxed{\,x_d \approx \sqrt{D t}\,} $$ > **Textual interpretation:** *To obliterate a 1 µm interface line at $D = 10^{-14}$ m²/s, the time required is $t = x_d^{2}/D = (10^{-6})^{2}/10^{-14} = 100$ s. This is why diffusion welding times are typically 10–60 minutes — to ensure the diffusion length is several micrometres, large enough to encompass surface roughness and oxide remnants.* --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation) > **Problem (typical 7-mark ESE sub-part):** > Two plates of pure iron are to be diffusion welded. Given: > - $D_0 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s > - $Q = 240$ kJ/mol > - $T = 1183$ K (i.e., 910 °C, just above the α→γ transition) > - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) > > **Find:** (a) The diffusion coefficient $D$. (b) The diffusion length after 30 minutes. **Step 1 — Convert Q to J/mol:** $$ Q = 240 \text{ kJ/mol} = 240{,}000 \text{ J/mol} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the exponent:** $$ \begin{aligned} -\dfrac{Q}{RT} &= -\dfrac{240{,}000}{8.314 \times 1183} \\[4pt] &= -\dfrac{240{,}000}{9835.5} \\[4pt] &= -24.40 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $D$:** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= D_0 \, \exp(-24.40) \\[4pt] &= 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times (4.78 \times 10^{-11}) \\[4pt] &= 9.56 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}^{2}\!/\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ > **Note:** $\exp(-24.40) \approx 4.78 \times 10^{-11}$ (use calculator in ESE; do NOT approximate crudely). **Step 4 — Compute diffusion length for t = 30 min = 1800 s:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_d &= \sqrt{D t} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{9.56 \times 10^{-15} \times 1800} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{1.72 \times 10^{-11}} \\[4pt] &= 4.15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\[4pt] &= 4.15 \; \mu\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Engineering conclusion:** > A diffusion length of ~4 µm is **more than sufficient** to bridge surface asperities of typical machined surfaces (R_a ≈ 0.8 µm) and to eliminate oxide layers of 5–50 nm thickness. Therefore, at 910 °C and 30 min, the bond-line should be **microstructurally continuous**. --- ## 3.5 Summary of Key Derived Relationships $$ D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right) \quad ; \quad x_d = \sqrt{Dt} \quad ; \quad J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} $$ $$ \dot{\varepsilon}_{\text{creep}} = A \sigma^{n} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right) \quad ; \quad T_H = \dfrac{T}{T_m} \in [0.5,\; 0.8] $$ $$ t_{\text{bond}} \;\propto\; \frac{T}{D_{gb} \, P} \quad \text{(for grain-boundary-diffusion-controlled Stage II)} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Per the protocol, complex physical cross-sections (which Mermaid cannot render faithfully) are replaced by **block-level functional architecture flow diagrams** that map the physical and process interactions. --- ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Diffusion Welding Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Surface Preparation
Degrease, Chemical Etch, R_a less than 0.4 micrometer] --> A2[Stacking and Fixturing
Align mating surfaces, anti-slip fixture] A2 --> A3[Chamber Loading
Vacuum pump to 10 power negative 3 to 10 power negative 5 Torr] A3 --> A4[Heating Stage
Heat to 0.5 to 0.8 T_m at 10 to 30 K per minute] A4 --> A5[Pressure Application
Hydraulic ram 1 to 10 MPa ramp over 5 min] A5 --> A6[Dwell Stage
Hold T, P, t for 10 to 120 minutes] A6 --> A7{Process Monitoring
Thermocouples, LVDT, vacuum gauge} A7 --> A8[Controlled Cooling
Cool below 0.3 T_m before unloading] A8 --> A9[Inspection
Ultrasonic, Metallography, Tensile test] A7 -- Real time data --> A10[PLC Control Loop
PID feedback] A1 -.- A11[Input Material
Parent metal 1 and Parent metal 2] A9 -.- A12[Output Product
Monolithic joint] style A6 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px style A1 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#3d85c6,stroke-width:1px style A9 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#6aa84f,stroke-width:1px ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The yellow-highlighted node `A6 — Dwell Stage` is the *heart* of diffusion welding, where the four mechanism stages (Section 2.1) occur. Everything before is preparation; everything after is verification. --- ## 4.2 The Four-Stage Bond Formation Micro-Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR S0[Stage 0: Initial State
Two rough surfaces in loose contact] --> S1[Stage I: Asperity Deformation
Plastic yield at contact tips, real area 1 to 20 percent] S1 --> S2[Stage II: Diffusion Bridging
Surface and GB diffusion fills gaps, isolated voids form, area 30 to 50 percent] S2 --> S3[Stage III: Void Closure
Power law creep and volume diffusion shrink voids, area over 90 percent] S3 --> S4[Stage IV: Microstructural Continuity
Grain boundary migration erases interface, bond equals parent metal] S0 -.- M1[Driver: External pressure P] S1 -.- M2[Driver: Shear stress at asperity tips] S2 -.- M3[Driver: Chemical potential gradient, surface and GB diffusion] S3 -.- M4[Driver: Volume diffusion, vacancy flux, power law creep] S4 -.- M5[Driver: Grain boundary energy minimization, recrystallization] style S4 fill:#93c47d,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:3px style S2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The green node `S4 — Microstructural Continuity` is the *desired end state*. The yellow node `S2 — Diffusion Bridging` is the *rate-limiting stage* in most industrial DFW operations. --- ## 4.3 Process Parameter Interaction Matrix (Cause-Effect Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Temperature T] -->|Strongly exponential| Q1[Diffusion Coefficient D] P2[Pressure P] -->|Linear up to saturation| Q2[Real Contact Area and Creep Rate] P3[Time t] -->|Square root law| Q3[Diffusion Length x_d] P4[Surface Roughness R_a] -->|Inverse linear| Q4[Initial Void Volume] P5[Atmosphere / Vacuum] -->|Inverse exponential| Q5[Surface Oxide Thickness] Q1 --> Z[Joint Bond Quality] Q2 --> Z Q3 --> Z Q4 --> Z Q5 --> Z P1 --> P2 P1 --> P3 P2 --> P3 P4 --> P5 style Z fill:#6aa84f,stroke:#274e13,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The five process inputs (P1–P5) all converge on the single output `Z — Joint Bond Quality`. Note the cross-coupling arrows: T also affects P and t (interdependent selection of parameter window). --- ## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — DFW vs. Conventional Fusion Welding ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FW[FUSION WELDING] F1[Heat above T_m] --> F2[Local melting] F2 --> F3[Liquid pool] F3 --> F4[Solidification] F4 --> F5[HAZ, Residual stress, Distortion] end subgraph DW[DIFFUSION WELDING] D1[Heat to 0.5 to 0.8 T_m] --> D2[No melting, Solid state] D2 --> D3[Atomic diffusion across interface] D3 --> D4[Stage I to IV void closure] D4 --> D5[No HAZ, No distortion, Joint equals parent] end style F5 fill:#e06666,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff style D5 fill:#93c47d,stroke:#38761d,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define diffusion welding. State any four process parameters that affect the bond quality in diffusion welding.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for parameters):** > **Definition (1 m):** Diffusion welding is a *solid-state joining process* in which two cleaned mating surfaces are held together at an *elevated temperature* (0.5–0.8 T_m) and a *moderate pressure* for a *sufficient time* so that atoms diffuse across the interface and form a metallurgical bond without melting the base metals. > > **Four process parameters (½ m each = 2 m):** > 1. **Bonding temperature (T)** — typically 0.5–0.8 T_m; increases $D$ exponentially (Arrhenius). > 2. **Bonding pressure (P)** — typically 1–10 MPa; increases real area of contact. > 3. **Bonding time (t)** — typically 10 min – 2 h; square-root law with diffusion length. > 4. **Surface condition (R_a, cleanliness)** — R_a < 0.4 µm, degreased, oxide-free; minimizes voids and oxide interference. > > *(Optional 5th for extra credit: atmosphere — vacuum or inert gas to prevent oxidation.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of the Arrhenius equation in diffusion welding. Why is temperature considered the most dominant parameter?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Arrhenius equation (1.5 m):** The diffusion coefficient depends on temperature as $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$. This means a small increase in $T$ produces an *exponential* increase in $D$, and hence in the rate of atomic transport across the bond interface. > > **Why temperature dominates (1.5 m):** > 1. $D$ changes by an order of magnitude (10×) for every ~100 K rise in temperature (typical for metals). > 2. It also accelerates *creep* (Stage III void closure) by the same exponential factor. > 3. Increasing $T$ reduces the yield strength, so the same $P$ produces more asperity deformation. > 4. Pressure and time act only as linear/square-root modifiers; $T$ acts as an *exponential* multiplier — hence it dominates the kinetic response. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice between two questions (Q-A and Q-B). Both options are provided below with sub-parts and **full model solutions**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]** > **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **four sequential stages** of diffusion bond formation. Clearly indicate the dominant diffusion mechanism active in each stage. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A Ti-6Al-4V titanium alloy joint is to be diffusion welded. Given: > - $D_0 = 1.6 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, > - $Q = 169$ kJ/mol, > - $T = 1173$ K, > - Bonding pressure $P = 5$ MPa, > - Required diffusion length $x_d = 5 \; \mu\text{m}$. > > Calculate: (i) the diffusion coefficient $D$ at 1173 K, and (ii) the minimum bonding time required. Take $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Block diagram (3 marks) — text version:** ``` Stage I: [Rough Surfaces] → [Plastic Deformation] → [Real Contact ~ 15-20%] ↓ Stage II: [Interface Bridging] → [Surface + GB Diffusion] → [Isolated Voids Form] ↓ Stage III:[Void Shrinkage] → [Volume Diffusion + Power-Law Creep] → [Voids < 5%] ↓ Stage IV: [Grain Boundary Migration] → [Interface Obliterated] → [Bond = Parent] ``` **Description of each stage (1 mark each = 4 marks):** - **Stage I — Asperity Plastic Deformation:** Applied pressure P causes the surface asperity tips to plastically yield, increasing real contact area from < 1% to ~15–20%. The dominant mechanism is *plastic flow* (or *creep* at high T). Time: ~5% of total bonding time. - **Stage II — Interface Diffusion and Void Formation:** Material from contact bridges diffuses (by *surface diffusion* and *grain boundary diffusion*) into the unbonded regions, forming isolated lenticular voids. Real contact grows to 30–50%. Time: ~5–40% of total. - **Stage III — Void Closure:** Voids shrink continuously via *volume (lattice) diffusion* and *power-law creep*. This is the slowest stage and is the *rate-limiting step*. Time: ~40–90% of total. - **Stage IV — Microstructural Continuity:** New grain boundaries migrate across the original interface, and the bond becomes indistinguishable from the parent metal. The bond strength is essentially equal to the parent metal strength. Time: > 90% of total. > **Marker tip:** A 1-mark bonus is awarded if the student explicitly writes the *mechanism name* in *italics* as shown. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Given:** $D_0 = 1.6 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s; $Q = 169{,}000$ J/mol; $T = 1173$ K; $x_d = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m. **(i) Diffusion coefficient (3 marks):** Step 1 — Compute the exponent: $$ \begin{aligned} -\dfrac{Q}{RT} &= -\dfrac{169{,}000}{8.314 \times 1173} \\[4pt] &= -\dfrac{169{,}000}{9752.3} \\[4pt] &= -17.328 \end{aligned} $$ Step 2 — Compute the exponential: $$ \exp(-17.328) \approx 3.00 \times 10^{-8} $$ Step 3 — Compute $D$: $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 1.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.00 \times 10^{-8} \\[4pt] &= 4.80 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^{2}\!/\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ > **Answer (1 mark):** $D \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-14}$ m²/s. --- **(ii) Minimum bonding time (4 marks):** Step 1 — Use the diffusion length formula $x_d = \sqrt{Dt}$, solving for $t$: $$ t = \dfrac{x_d^{\,2}}{D} $$ Step 2 — Substitute values: $$ \begin{aligned} t &= \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{4.80 \times 10^{-14}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-12}}{4.80 \times 10^{-14}} \\[4pt] &= 520.8 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ Step 3 — Convert to minutes: $$ t = \dfrac{520.8}{60} \approx 8.68 \text{ min} $$ > **Answer (1 mark):** $t_{\min} \approx 8.7$ minutes. > > **Engineering interpretation (½ m for the explanation):** *In practice, designers use 2–3× this value (≈ 20–30 min) to ensure complete void elimination and grain boundary migration. Hence the recommended bonding time is 20–30 minutes at 1173 K and 5 MPa for Ti-6Al-4V.* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]** > **(a)** Differentiate between **diffusion welding, fusion welding, and brazing** with respect to: (i) bonding temperature relative to base metal melting point, (ii) use of filler metal, (iii) heat-affected zone, and (iv) typical joint efficiency. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Derive the **Arrhenius equation for diffusion** starting from the Boltzmann probability that an atom acquires energy $\geq Q$ to jump a lattice site. Hence, show that a 10% increase in bonding temperature (in Kelvin) at $T = 1000$ K with $Q = 200$ kJ/mol causes a **nearly threefold** increase in the diffusion coefficient. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Tabular comparison (each cell = ~½ m, total 4 m for table, 3 m for supporting explanation):** | Feature | Diffusion Welding | Fusion Welding | Brazing | |---|---|---|---| | **(i) Bonding temperature** | 0.5–0.8 T_m (below T_m) | Above T_m (with arc/laser) | Above filler T_m, below base T_m | | **(ii) Filler metal** | **None** | Often used (electrode/wire) | **Yes** (mandatory) | | **(iii) HAZ** | **Negligible / absent** | Pronounced (microstructural change) | Negligible but interface line visible | | **(iv) Joint efficiency** | **95–100%** of parent | 70–90% of parent (often weaker) | 60–80% of parent | **Supporting notes (3 marks):** - Diffusion welding is a *solid-state* process; the others involve *liquid phase* (fusion or filler). - Brazing uses a filler metal whose T_m is *below* the base metal's T_m; diffusion welding uses *no filler at all*. - Fusion welding creates a *cast dendritic structure* in the weld pool; diffusion welding creates a joint indistinguishable from wrought parent metal. - Joint efficiency is highest in DFW because there is *no fusion zone microstructural discontinuity*. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Boltzmann probability (1.5 m):** For an atom in a lattice potential well of depth $Q$, the probability of acquiring energy $\geq Q$ at temperature $T$ is given by the Boltzmann factor: $$ p = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{kT}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Atomic vibration frequency (1 m):** An atom vibrates at the Debye frequency $\nu_0 \approx 10^{13}$ Hz. Of these attempts, the fraction that succeeds in jumping is $p$. Hence the net jump frequency is: $$ \nu = \nu_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{kT}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Combine with $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda^{2}\nu$ (1.5 m):** $$ D = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda^{2}\nu_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{kT}\right) = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right) $$ where $D_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda^{2}\nu_0$. **Step 4 — Compute the 10% temperature rise effect (3 m):** At $T_1 = 1000$ K and $T_2 = 1.1 \times 1000 = 1100$ K, with $Q = 200{,}000$ J/mol and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K): $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} &= \dfrac{\exp(-Q / R T_2)}{\exp(-Q / R T_1)} = \exp\!\left[\,-\dfrac{Q}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)\,\right] \\[6pt] &= \exp\!\left[\,-\dfrac{200{,}000}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{1100}-\dfrac{1}{1000}\right)\,\right] \\[6pt] &= \exp\!\left[\,-24{,}057.0 \times \left(9.091 \times 10^{-4} - 1.000 \times 10^{-3}\right)\,\right] \\[6pt] &= \exp\!\left[\,-24{,}057.0 \times (-9.09 \times 10^{-5})\,\right] \\[6pt] &= \exp(2.187) \\[6pt] &\approx 8.91 \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion (½ m):** *A 10% increase in absolute temperature (from 1000 K to 1100 K) causes the diffusion coefficient to increase by a factor of **~8.9** (i.e., nearly an order of magnitude, much more than "threefold"). The "threefold" claim in the problem is conservative; the actual increase is closer to **9×**. This powerfully illustrates why temperature is the most dominant process parameter in diffusion welding.* > **Note for student:** In the ESE, do not blindly accept a problem's numerical claim. Always work it out and correct it if needed — you may earn a *bonus mark* for spotting the misprint and stating the true value. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in Diffusion Welding questions:** > 1. **Conflating "diffusion welding" with "diffusion in fusion welding"** (–2 marks). Always clarify that diffusion is the *primary mechanism* in DFW and a *side effect* in fusion welding. > 2. **Forgetting to convert Q to J/mol** (–1 mark). If $Q$ is given in kJ/mol, multiply by 1000 before substitution. > 3. **Writing $T$ in °C instead of K** (–1 mark). Arrhenius equation *requires* absolute temperature. > 4. **Using $|x|$ inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in $\LaTeX$ instead. > 5. **Not mentioning the four stages by name** in long answers (–2 marks). Examiners look for "Stage I, II, III, IV" labels explicitly. > 6. **Omitting the "no melting, solid-state" qualifier** in the definition (–1 mark). This is the *single most important* feature. > 7. **Stating "diffusion welding is fast"** (–1 mark). It is the *slowest* of all welding processes; do not confuse with friction stir welding. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Use this checklist for the last 10 minutes before the exam.** Tick each item mentally. - [ ] **Definition:** Diffusion welding = *solid-state*, no melting, no filler, 0.5–0.8 T_m, 1–10 MPa, 10 min–2 h. - [ ] **Four Stages:** *I — asperity deformation; II — interface diffusion + void formation; III — void closure; IV — grain boundary migration.* - [ ] **Dominant mechanism in each stage:** *plastic flow → surface/GB diffusion → volume diffusion + creep → recrystallization.* - [ ] **Most dominant parameter:** *Temperature (T)* — affects D exponentially (Arrhenius). - [ ] **Arrhenius equation:** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ — must use *Kelvin* and *J/mol*. - [ ] **Diffusion length:** $x_d = \sqrt{Dt}$ — square-root dependence on time. - [ ] **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \, \partial C / \partial x$ — net flux is *down* the gradient. - [ ] **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); Boltzmann constant $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. - [ ] **Surface prep requirement:** $R_a < 0.4 \; \mu\text{m}$, degreased, oxide-free. - [ ] **Atmosphere:** vacuum ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ Torr) or high-purity inert gas (Ar). - [ ] **Advantages:** *No HAZ, no distortion, no melting, joins dissimilar metals, near-parent strength.* - [ ] **Disadvantages:** *Slow, expensive tooling, requires precise surface finish, limited to small/simple geometries (or Hot Isostatic Pressing for complex shapes).* - [ ] **Industrial applications:** *Aerospace Ti-alloy honeycomb, nuclear Zr-steel transition joints, superalloy turbine discs (HIP), Cu pillar flip-chip bonding.* - [ ] **Common confusion to avoid:** *Diffusion welding ≠ diffusion in fusion welding HAZ. It is NOT brazing (no filler). It is NOT forge welding (uses much less deformation).* - [ ] **Typical values to memorize:** $D_0 \sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-2}$ m²/s; $Q \sim 100$ to $400$ kJ/mol for substitutional diffusion in metals; $T \sim 0.5$–$0.8 \, T_m$. - [ ] **Examiner's favourite sub-question:** *"Why is temperature the most dominant parameter?"* — answer with Arrhenius exponential dependence.

topics.title: Key Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Key Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of "Key Variables" in Metal Joining

In the context of **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques** (PEIET523 – Module 3), **Key Variables** are defined as the independently controllable process parameters, material parameters, and geometric parameters whose magnitudes directly govern the **heat flow, metallurgical transformations, mechanical properties, distortion, and overall quality** of a welded joint. They are the primary "knobs" an engineer adjusts to obtain a sound weld meeting the specified service requirements.

KTU 2024 Scheme broadly classifies these variables into four families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification of Key Welding Variables**
> 1. **Primary Process Variables (Electrical/Thermal)** – current, voltage, travel speed, heat input.
> 2. **Mechanical Variables** – electrode force, torch angle, traverse speed, plunge depth, axial load.
> 3. **Material/Metallurgical Variables** – base metal, filler metal, shielding gas, flux composition.
> 4. **Geometric/Design Variables** – joint type, root gap, groove angle, plate thickness, fit-up tolerance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Cooking a Perfect Dosa"**
> Think of welding like cooking a dosa on a tawa.
> - **Heat input** is the flame setting on the stove.
> - **Travel speed** is how fast you spread the batter.
> - **Welding current** is the size of the ladle pouring the batter.
> - **Joint fit-up** is how clean and flat the tawa surface is.
> - **Shielding gas** is the protective cover you put over the dosa so dust does not fall.
>
> If any one variable is wrong (too much heat, too slow a spread, dirty tawa), the dosa (weld) becomes either burnt, under-cooked, or full of impurities (porosity, cracks, lack of fusion). The "recipe" for a perfect weld is the **correct combination of all key variables**, not just one.

## 1.3 Why Are Key Variables So Important?

Every advanced joining process — from **GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding)** and **GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding)** to **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)**, **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**, **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** and **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** — is fundamentally a **heat-and-force-driven** operation. The mechanical and metallurgical integrity of the resulting joint is a deterministic function of these variables. KTU examiners test this topic because:

1. It is the **foundation for design of experiments (DOE)** in welding.
2. It underpins **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** as per **ISO 15614 / ASME Section IX**.
3. It directly maps to the course outcomes: **CO3** (select suitable joining process) and **CO4** (optimize process parameters for desired weld quality).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Memory:** **"P-M-M-G"** → **P**rocess, **M**echanical, **M**aterial, **G**eometric variables. Use this in any KTU 3-mark definition answer to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Key Variables — Detailed Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Primary Process Variables (Electrical & Thermal)

These are the variables that **directly control the heat generated at the welding zone**.

**(a) Welding Current (I), measured in Amperes (A)**
- Governs the **rate of metal deposition** and **penetration depth**.
- Higher current → deeper penetration, wider HAZ (Heat Affected Zone), higher deposition rate.
- Too high → burn-through, undercut, excessive spatter, distortion.
- Too low → lack of fusion, lack of penetration, porosity.

**(b) Arc Voltage (V), measured in Volts (V)**
- Governs the **arc length** and **bead width**.
- Higher voltage → wider, flatter bead, more dilution with base metal.
- Too high → porosity, increased spatter, wide HAZ.
- Too low → narrow bead, insufficient shielding, stubbing of electrode.

**(c) Travel Speed (S), measured in mm/min or mm/s**
- Governs the **heat input per unit length** of weld.
- Faster travel → less heat input → narrower bead and HAZ.
- Too slow → excessive heat input → wide HAZ, distortion, burn-through.
- Too fast → underfill, lack of fusion, porosity.

**(d) Heat Input (HI), measured in kJ/mm or J/mm**
- This is the **derived parameter** that combines I, V, and S.
- The single most quoted KTU variable — it correlates with HAZ size, mechanical properties, and distortion.

**(e) Duty Cycle / Arc Time**
- For pulsed processes like **Pulsed GMAW** and **Pulsed GTAW**, the **peak current, background current, pulse frequency, and pulse-on time** are the key variables that decouple penetration from deposition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Welding Variables (frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions)**
> - **Peak Current (Ip)** – controls penetration.
> - **Background Current (Ib)** – maintains arc without excessive heat.
> - **Pulse Frequency (f)** – typically 50–500 Hz.
> - **Pulse-on Time (tp)** – fraction of cycle where Ip is active.
> - **Mean Current (Im)** = Ip × (tp/T) + Ib × (1 − tp/T).

### Pillar 2: Mechanical / Kinematic Variables

**(a) Electrode Force (F), in kN or N**
- Critical for **resistance welding** (spot, projection, seam, flash).
- Too high → excessive indentation, thin weld nugget.
- Too low → spatter, porosity, undersized nugget.

**(b) Weld Time / Squeeze Time / Hold Time / Off Time**
- Governs the **thermal cycle** of resistance welds.
- Standard cycle: **Squeeze → Weld → Hold → Off**.

**(c) Tool Rotation Speed (ω), in rpm — for FSW**
- Critical for **frictional heat generation** and **material flow**.

**(d) Traverse Speed (v), in mm/min — for FSW**
- Affects **heat input per unit length** and **stir zone microstructure**.

**(e) Tilt Angle / Plunge Depth — for FSW**
- Affects **stirring efficiency** and **defect formation (wormhole, tunnel defect)**.

### Pillar 3: Material & Metallurgical Variables

- **Base metal composition** (C, Cr, Ni, Mo content) — affects hardenability and cracking tendency.
- **Filler metal selection** — must match or exceed base metal strength; affects dilution.
- **Shielding gas composition** — Ar, He, CO₂, O₂, H₂, N₂ mixtures (e.g., 98% Ar + 2% O₂ for GMAW of mild steel).
- **Shielding gas flow rate** — typically 10–20 L/min; too low → porosity; too high → turbulence, gas entrapment.
- **Flux composition** (in SAW, SMAW) — provides deoxidation, slag formation, arc stabilization.

### Pillar 4: Geometric & Design Variables

- **Joint type** — butt, lap, T-joint, corner, edge.
- **Groove angle** — V, U, J, double-V.
- **Root gap and root face** — affects penetration and dilution.
- **Plate thickness** — drives selection of current, number of passes, and preheat.
- **Welding position** — 1G/2G/3G/4G/1F/2F (flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Variable / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (HI)** | $HI = \eta \cdot \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $\eta$ = process efficiency (GMAW ≈ 0.8, GTAW ≈ 0.6, SMAW ≈ 0.8, SAW ≈ 0.9, LBW/EBW ≈ 0.4) |
| 2 | Heat Input (SI form) | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ | J/mm | Use when S is in mm/s |
| 3 | **Deposition Rate (DR)** | $DR = k \cdot I$ | kg/h | k depends on electrode diameter and process |
| 4 | **Joule's Heating (Resistance Welding)** | $Q = I^{2} R t$ | Joules | R is dynamic resistance at interface |
| 5 | **Heat Generation Rate (RSW)** | $q = \dfrac{I^{2} R}{J}$ | W or J/s | J = 4.18 J/cal mechanical equivalent |
| 6 | **Nugget Diameter (RSW)** | $d = f(\sqrt{Q}, F)$ | mm | Empirical; increases with Q, decreases with F |
| 7 | **Pulse Mean Current (GTAW/GMAW)** | $I_{m} = I_{p} \cdot \dfrac{t_{p}}{T} + I_{b} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{t_{p}}{T}\right)$ | A | $T$ = pulse period = $1/f$ |
| 8 | **FSW Pseudo Heat Input** | $HI_{FSW} = \dfrac{\omega \cdot T_{torque}}{v}$ | J/mm | $\omega$ in rad/s, $T_{torque}$ in N·m, $v$ in mm/s |
| 9 | **Cooling Rate (t8/5)** | Empirical from heat input, plate thickness, joint type | °C/s | Governs HAZ microstructure |
| 10 | **Penetration Depth (P)** | $P \propto \dfrac{I^{2}}{S \cdot d}$ | mm | d = electrode/workpiece diameter |
| 11 | **Bead Width (W)** | $W \propto V \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{S}}$ | mm | Empirical relation |
| 12 | **Reynolds-like Stir Number (FSW)** | $St = \dfrac{\omega \cdot r^{2}}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | r = tool shoulder radius, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity |
| 13 | **Voltage–Arc Length** | $V_{arc} \approx 20 + 0.04 \cdot I$ (approx.) | V | Linear approximation for steel |
| 14 | **Dilution (%D)** | $D\% = \dfrac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}} \times 100$ | % | A = cross-sectional area melted |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the heat input formula, students frequently forget to convert S from **mm/min to mm/s** (or vice versa) and lose 2 marks. Always write the **units next to your substitution** in the answer sheet.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Process | Critical Variable Emphasized |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive body-in-white | RSW | Electrode force, weld time, current |
| Shipbuilding (thick plates) | SAW / GMAW | Heat input, interpass temperature, number of passes |
| Aerospace (Ti alloys) | GTAW / LBW | Pulse parameters, shielding gas purity, travel speed |
| Railway & bridges | SMAW / FCAW | Heat input control to avoid HAZ softening |
| Spacecraft & nuclear | EBW | Vacuum level, beam current, focus current |
| Aluminum ship decks | FSW | Tool rotation, traverse speed, tilt angle |
| Pipeline (API 1104) | GMAW (STT/CMT) | Arc length correction, voltage, wire feed speed |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world link:** The **Lincoln Procedure Handbook** and **AWS D1.1** code both use the heat input formula to limit maximum HI to avoid HAZ cracking in C-Mn and low-alloy steels. For example, AWS D1.1 limits HI to **3.0 kJ/mm** for quenched & tempered steels to preserve their strength.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Input Formula

### Step 1 — Start from Electrical Power

The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the arc is given by the standard electrical engineering relation:

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

where $P$ is in Watts, $V$ is the arc voltage in Volts, and $I$ is the welding current in Amperes.

> This equation means: for every second the arc burns, $V \cdot I$ Joules of electrical energy are supplied.

### Step 2 — Account for Process Efficiency (η)

In practice, not all electrical energy is converted into useful heat at the workpiece. A fraction is lost to the electrode, the surrounding shielding gas, radiation, and convection. Hence we introduce a **thermal efficiency factor** $\eta$:

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

> For GMAW, $\eta \approx 0.80$; for GTAW, $\eta \approx 0.60$ (more heat lost to the non-consumable tungsten electrode).

### Step 3 — Compute Energy Per Unit Length of Weld

If the arc travels a length $L$ in time $t$, then the travel speed is $S = L/t$. The energy deposited in length $L$ is:

$$
E = P_{useful} \cdot t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

Dividing by length $L$:

$$
\frac{E}{L} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{t}{L} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Units for KTU Standard Practice

KTU board examinations use **kJ/mm**. Convert using:

- $V$ in Volts, $I$ in Amps, $S$ in **mm/min** → multiply numerator by 60 to convert seconds to minutes.
- Convert Joules to kiloJoules → divide by 1000.

$$
\boxed{HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** In a GMAW process, voltage = 28 V, current = 250 A, travel speed = 5 mm/s, efficiency = 0.85. Find the heat input in kJ/mm.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert travel speed to mm/min:
$$
S = 5 \text{ mm/s} \times 60 = 300 \text{ mm/min}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute into formula:
$$
HI = \frac{0.85 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}
$$

Step 3 — Compute numerator:
$$
0.85 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60 = 0.85 \times 420{,}000 = 357{,}000
$$

Step 4 — Compute denominator:
$$
300 \times 1000 = 300{,}000
$$

Step 5 — Final answer:
$$
HI = \frac{357{,}000}{300{,}000} = 1.19 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer interpretation:** A heat input of **1.19 kJ/mm** is moderate — typical for thin-section mild steel GMAW. If this value exceeded **3.0 kJ/mm**, AWS D1.1 would flag it as potentially damaging for quenched & tempered steels.

## 3.2 Derivation of Pulse Mean Current

In pulsed arc welding, the current alternates between a **peak level $I_p$** (during time $t_p$) and a **background level $I_b$** (during the rest of the period $T - t_p$).

### Step 1 — Total Charge Per Cycle

Charge is the integral of current over time:
$$
Q_{cycle} = I_p \cdot t_p + I_b \cdot (T - t_p)
$$

### Step 2 — Mean Current = Charge Per Unit Time

$$
I_m = \frac{Q_{cycle}}{T} = \frac{I_p \cdot t_p + I_b \cdot (T - t_p)}{T}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$
I_m = I_p \cdot \frac{t_p}{T} + I_b \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t_p}{T}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

Given $I_p = 400$ A, $I_b = 100$ A, $t_p = 2$ ms, $T = 10$ ms:

$$
I_m = 400 \times \frac{2}{10} + 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{2}{10}\right)
$$

$$
I_m = 400 \times 0.2 + 100 \times 0.8 = 80 + 80 = 160 \text{ A}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Joule's Law for Resistance Welding

For resistance spot welding, the heat generated at the interface is:

### Step 1 — Resistance Definition

Ohm's law: $V = I R$, so $R = V/I$.

### Step 2 — Power Dissipated

$$
P = V \cdot I = I^2 R
$$

### Step 3 — Total Heat in Time t

$$
\boxed{Q = I^2 R t \;\; \text{(Joules)}}
$$

> This is the **First Law of Resistance Welding** — heat generated is proportional to the **square** of current, hence current is the most sensitive variable.

### Step 4 — Heat Generation Rate (Power Density Form)

$$
q = \frac{I^2 R}{J}
$$

where $J$ = 4.186 J/cal (mechanical equivalent of heat). This converts the heat to calories, which is the traditional unit used in welding heat calculations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Increasing Each Variable

| Variable Increased | Penetration | Bead Width | HAZ Width | Distortion | Spatter | Porosity Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current (I) | ↑↑ Strong | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Voltage (V) | ↓ Slight | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| Travel Speed (S) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Heat Input (HI) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ (if excessive) |
| Electrode Force (F) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↓ |
| Weld Time (RSW) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (if long) |
| FSW Tool Speed (ω) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | — | — (tunnel defect if too fast) |
| FSW Traverse Speed (v) | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | — | ↑ (tunnel defect if too fast) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Principle:** No single variable can be maximized or minimized — every parameter has a "sweet spot" window. KTU 14-mark questions often ask you to **discuss the interdependence** of two variables (e.g., current vs travel speed).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Interaction of Key Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Base Metal + Filler + Joint Geometry] --> B[Process Selection]
    B --> C{Process Family}
    C -->|Arc Welding| D1[GMAW / GTAW / SMAW / SAW]
    C -->|Resistance Welding| D2[RSW / Projection / Seam]
    C -->|Solid State| D3[FSW / USW / Forge / Roll]
    C -->|High Energy Beam| D4[LBW / EBW / PAW]

    D1 --> E1[Primary Variables: I, V, S]
    D2 --> E2[Primary Variables: I, t, F]
    D3 --> E3[Primary Variables: omega, v, F, Tilt]
    D4 --> E4[Primary Variables: Power, Speed, Focus]

    E1 --> F1[Heat Input HI eta V I 60 / S 1000]
    E2 --> F2[Joule Heat Q = I squared R t]
    E3 --> F3[Frictional HI = mu omega F / v]
    E4 --> F4[Beam Power Density P / A beam]

    F1 --> G[Weld Pool Dynamics]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G

    G --> H[Output: Weld Quality]
    H --> H1[Penetration and Bead Geometry]
    H --> H2[HAZ Microstructure and Hardness]
    H --> H3[Residual Stress and Distortion]
    H --> H4[Defects Porosity, Cracks, Lack of Fusion]

    H1 --> I[Quality Verification: NDT, Mechanical Testing]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I

    I --> J{Meets WPS Spec?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accept Weld]
    J -->|No| L[Adjust Variables and Iterate]
    L --> E1
    L --> E2
    L --> E3
    L --> E4
```

## 4.2 Heat Input Variable Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current I Amperes] -->|Proportional| B[Heat Input HI]
    C[Voltage V Volts] -->|Proportional| B
    D[Travel Speed S mm/min] -->|Inversely Proportional| B
    E[Process Efficiency eta] -->|Proportional| B

    B --> F[HAZ Size]
    B --> G[Penetration Depth]
    B --> H[Cooling Time t8/5]
    B --> I[Residual Stress and Distortion]
    B --> J[Mechanical Properties: UTS, Hardness, Toughness]

    F --> K[Final Weld Quality]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L{Weld Acceptable per Code}
    L -->|Yes| M[Approved WPS]
    L -->|No| N[Re-optimize via DOE]
    N --> A
    N --> C
    N --> D
```

## 4.3 Resistance Spot Welding Cycle Diagram (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Squeeze Time 0.1 to 0.4 s] --> B[Weld Time 0.1 to 1.0 s]
    B --> C[Hold Time 0.1 to 0.5 s]
    C --> D[Off Time]
```
