```mermaid
    D -->|Next Cycle| A

    A --> A1[Electrode Force Applied]
    B --> B1[Current I flows, I squared R t generates heat]
    B --> B2[Nugget Forms and Grows]
    C --> C1[Forging Cooling under Pressure]
    D --> D1[Electrode Retracts]

    B1 --> X1[Key Variables: I, F, t, R]
    X1 --> Y1[Quality: Nugget Diameter, Indentation, Shear Strength]
```

## 4.4 Friction Stir Welding Process Variable Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FSW Tool: Shoulder + Pin] --> B[Rotates at omega rpm]
    B --> C[Frictional Heat at Shoulder-Workpiece Interface]
    C --> D[Material Softens and Plasticizes]
    D --> E[Traverse Speed v mm/min]
    E --> F[Tool Forges Material from Leading to Trailing Side]
    F --> G[Solid State Joint Forms]

    H[Key Variables] --> H1[Tool Rotation omega]
    H --> H2[Traverse Speed v]
    H --> H3[Axial Force F or Plunge Depth]
    H --> H4[Tilt Angle 1 to 3 degrees]
    H --> H5[Tool Geometry Pin and Shoulder]

    H1 --> G
    H2 --> G
    H3 --> G
    H4 --> G
    H5 --> G

    G --> I[Defect Map]
    I --> I1[Tunnel Defect: omega too low or v too high]
    I --> I2[Flash Defect: omega too high or F too low]
    I --> I3[Kissing Bond: insufficient stirring]
    I --> I4[Surface Galling: improper tilt]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to draw in KTU exam:** Even if you cannot reproduce these Mermaid diagrams, draw a **simple block diagram** with arrows showing INPUT variables → PROCESS → OUTPUT quality. This earns 2–3 easy marks in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Define the term "Key Variables" in the context of advanced metal joining processes. List any four categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Key variables are the controllable process, mechanical, material, and geometric parameters that directly influence the heat generation, metallurgical transformations, and final quality of a welded joint.

| # | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary (Electrical) | Welding Current (I) |
| 2 | Mechanical / Kinematic | Electrode Force (F) |
| 3 | Material / Metallurgical | Shielding gas composition |
| 4 | Geometric / Design | Joint groove angle |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for categories, 1 mark for examples)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplied Model]

**What is the significance of "heat input" as a derived key variable in arc welding processes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Heat Input (HI) is a derived parameter that combines the three primary electrical variables — **current (I), voltage (V), and travel speed (S)** — into a single quantity expressed in **kJ/mm**. Its significance lies in:

1. **(1 mark)** It directly controls the **HAZ size, microstructure, and cooling rate (t₈/₅)**.
2. **(1 mark)** It is used in **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** as per ISO 15614 and ASME Section IX to ensure code compliance.
3. **(1 mark)** Excessive HI leads to **HAZ softening, grain coarsening, distortion, and cracking** — so it is the key variable for quality control.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Always write the **formula first**, then **substitute values with units**.
> - In variable-interaction questions, draw a **cause-and-effect table or block diagram** — students who skip this lose 2–3 marks.
> - Mention **process efficiency η** in heat input calculations; many students forget it.
> - For FSW, do NOT confuse the pseudo heat input with arc welding heat input; the units and physical meaning differ.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) **Discuss in detail the key process variables that govern the quality of Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW).** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The key process variables in GMAW can be grouped as:

**(i) Welding Current (I)** – **(1 mark)**
- Directly controls **deposition rate** and **penetration depth**.
- Too high → burn-through, excessive spatter.
- Too low → lack of fusion, stubbing of wire.

**(ii) Arc Voltage (V)** – **(1 mark)**
- Governs **arc length** and **bead width**.
- Higher V → wider bead, more dilution, more spatter.

**(iii) Travel Speed (S)** – **(1 mark)**
- Inversely proportional to heat input.
- Faster S → narrower bead, less penetration.

**(iv) Wire Feed Speed (WFS)** – **(1 mark)**
- Determines the current via the **burn-off rate** relationship: $I = k \cdot WFS$ (where $k$ is a process constant).
- Acts as the "master" variable in CV (constant voltage) power sources.

**(v) Shielding Gas Composition and Flow Rate** – **(1 mark)**
- Common mixtures: 75% Ar + 25% CO₂ (C25), 98% Ar + 2% O₂.
- Flow rate: 12–18 L/min.
- Wrong composition → porosity, oxidation, spatter.

**(vi) Contact Tip-to-Work Distance (CTWD)** – **(1 mark)**
- Affects arc stability and preheat of filler wire.
- Typical: 10–20 mm.

**(vii) Torch Angle and Manipulation** – **(1 mark)**
- Push vs drag angle affects penetration and bead shape.

**Conclusion:** All these variables are interlinked and the **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** is essentially a frozen, qualified set of these key variables that produces a sound weld meeting code requirements.

---

#### (b) **For a GMAW process on 10 mm thick mild steel plate, the following parameters are used: I = 280 A, V = 29 V, travel speed S = 6 mm/s, process efficiency η = 0.85. Calculate: (i) the heat input in kJ/mm, and (ii) the heat input if travel speed is doubled with current reduced to 240 A. Comment on the effect on HAZ.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Heat input at S = 6 mm/s:** — **(3 marks)**

Convert travel speed: $S = 6 \text{ mm/s} \times 60 = 360 \text{ mm/min}$

$$
HI_1 = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
HI_1 = \frac{0.85 \times 29 \times 280 \times 60}{360 \times 1000}
$$

Numerator: $0.85 \times 29 = 24.65$; $24.65 \times 280 = 6902$; $6902 \times 60 = 414{,}120$

Denominator: $360 \times 1000 = 360{,}000$

$$
HI_1 = \frac{414{,}120}{360{,}000} = 1.15 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**(ii) Heat input at S = 12 mm/s, I = 240 A:** — **(3 marks)**

Convert: $S = 12 \times 60 = 720 \text{ mm/min}$

$$
HI_2 = \frac{0.85 \times 29 \times 240 \times 60}{720 \times 1000}
$$

Numerator: $0.85 \times 29 = 24.65$; $24.65 \times 240 = 5916$; $5916 \times 60 = 354{,}960$

Denominator: $720 \times 1000 = 720{,}000$

$$
HI_2 = \frac{354{,}960}{720{,}000} = 0.493 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

**Comment on HAZ effect:** — **(1 mark)**

The heat input **reduced by ~57%** (from 1.15 to 0.493 kJ/mm). The HAZ will be **narrower, with finer grain size, higher cooling rate, and higher hardness** but also **higher risk of porosity and lack of fusion** if penetration becomes inadequate. The joint will have **less distortion** but may require **preheat** to avoid hydrogen cracking due to the fast cooling.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Adapted]**

#### (a) **Explain with a suitable diagram the key variables affecting the Resistance Spot Welding (RSW) process and derive the Joule's heating equation Q = I²Rt.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Key Variables of RSW:** — **(3 marks)**

1. **Welding Current (I):** 3–25 kA typical; the most sensitive variable (heat ∝ I²).
2. **Electrode Force (F):** 2–8 kN typical; must balance contact resistance vs indentation.
3. **Weld Time (t):** 0.1–1.0 s; controls total heat input.
4. **Hold Time:** 0.1–0.5 s; allows nugget to solidify under pressure.
5. **Electrode Material and Geometry:** Cu-Cr or Cu-Zr alloys; truncated cone or domed tips.
6. **Sheet Surface Condition:** Affects contact resistance.

**Block Diagram:** — **(2 marks)**

```
[Sheet Stack] → [Electrode Force F] + [Current I, Time t]
      ↓
[Contact Resistance R at faying surface]
      ↓
[Q = I²Rt → Nugget Formation]
      ↓
[Hold → Solidify → Release]
```

**Derivation of Q = I²Rt:** — **(2 marks)**

By Ohm's law: $V = I R$

Power dissipated: $P = V \cdot I = (I R) \cdot I = I^2 R$

Total heat energy in time $t$:
$$
Q = P \cdot t = I^2 R t
$$

> This is **Joule's First Law of Heating** — the foundation equation for resistance welding.

---

#### (b) **Discuss the key process variables of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) and explain how tool rotation speed (ω) and traverse speed (v) interactively affect weld quality.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Key Variables of FSW:** — **(3 marks)**

1. **Tool Rotation Speed (ω)** — 100–3000 rpm; generates frictional heat.
2. **Traverse Speed (v)** — 10–500 mm/min; controls heat input per unit length.
3. **Axial Force / Plunge Depth (F)** — must be sufficient for consolidation.
4. **Tilt Angle** — typically 1°–3°; improves forging action.
5. **Tool Geometry** — pin shape (threaded, conical, cylindrical, triflute) and shoulder design (scroll, spiral, plain).
6. **Workpiece Material** — primarily Al, Mg, Cu alloys (soft materials below hot working temperature).

**Interaction of ω and v:** — **(3 marks)**

The ratio $\omega/v$ is the **dominant governing parameter** in FSW:

- **High ω/v ratio (fast spin, slow traverse):** → Excessive heat input → large softened zone, flash formation, surface galling, possible liquation cracking.
- **Low ω/v ratio (slow spin, fast traverse):** → Insufficient heat → poor plasticization → **tunnel defects, kissing bonds, lack of bonding**.
- **Optimum ω/v window:** gives a **defect-free weld with fine recrystallized grain structure** in the stir zone (SZ) and proper material flow from leading to trailing side.

**Empirical pseudo heat input:** — **(1 mark)**
$$
HI_{FSW} \propto \frac{\omega \cdot F}{v}
$$

This relation is often used to design experiments (DOE) in FSW, where **ω**, **F**, and **v** are the three "I, V, S" of FSW.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Key Variables Module**
> - [x] **Key Variables = P-M-M-G** (Process, Mechanical, Material, Geometric).
> - [x] **Heat Input formula** is the most tested equation: $HI = \eta V I \cdot 60 / (S \cdot 1000)$ in kJ/mm.
> - [x] **Efficiency η values:** GMAW ≈ 0.80, GTAW ≈ 0.60, SAW ≈ 0.90, SMAW ≈ 0.80, LBW/EBW ≈ 0.40.
> - [x] **Joule's Law Q = I²Rt** — current is the most sensitive RSW variable (∝ I²).
> - [x] **Pulse welding:** mean current = $I_p (t_p/T) + I_b (1 - t_p/T)$.
> - [x] **FSW master ratio:** $\omega/v$ governs defect formation.
> - [x] **HAZ effect:** Higher HI → wider HAZ, coarser grains, lower hardness (for C-Mn steels) or higher hardness (for alloy steels), more distortion.
> - [x] **Code limits:** AWS D1.1 caps HI ≈ 3.0 kJ/mm for Q&T steels.
> - [x] Always write **units** beside numerical substitution; examiners award 1 mark for correct unit handling.
> - [x] For 14-mark answers, always include a **block diagram or interaction table** of variables.
> - [x] Mention **WPS (Welding Procedure Specification)** and relevant **IS/AWS/ISO codes** for full marks.

**Memory Hooks for Last-Minute Revision:**
1. **HI = "V.I.S.I.T."** → **V**oltage, **I** × (60/1000), divided by **S**peed.
2. **RSW cycle = "S.W.H.O."** → **S**queeze, **W**eld, **H**old, **O**ff.
3. **FSW defects = "T.F.K.S."** → **T**unnel, **F**lash, **K**issing bond, **S**urface galling.
4. **Pulse welding variables = "P.B.F.D."** → **P**eak current, **B**ackground, **F**requency, **D**uty cycle (pulse-on time).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)** is a Directed Energy Deposition (DED) variant of additive manufacturing that uses an electric arc as the heat source to melt and deposit successive layers of filler wire feedstock, building near-net-shape metallic components layer-by-layer under computer numerical control (CNC) or robotic guidance.

According to the **ASTM F2792-12** standard, WAAM falls under the **"DED-Arc"** category of additive manufacturing processes. It combines the metallurgical principles of conventional arc welding (typically Gas Metal Arc Welding - GMAW, Gas Tungsten Arc Welding - GTAW, or Plasma Arc Welding - PAW) with multi-axis robotic motion to fabricate fully dense, large-scale metal parts.

| KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology | Technical Equivalent |
|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| WAAM Process | Directed Energy Deposition - Arc (DED-Arc) |
| Deposition Head | Arc torch + wire feeder assembly |
| Substrate | Base plate / build platform |
| Layer Height | Z-increment per layer (typically 1–3 mm) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a pastry chef using a piping bag to draw 2D shapes (like your name in cream on a cake). The chef then adds a thin second layer on top, then a third, gradually building a 3D sculpture. WAAM works exactly the same way, except the "piping bag" is an arc welding torch, the "cream" is molten metal from a wire, and the "cake base" is a metal substrate. Each pass adds a thin bead of solidified metal, and stacking these beads produces a 3D metal part.

### 1.3 Process Family Position

WAAM belongs to the broader **Metal Additive Manufacturing (MAM)** family but is differentiated by its:

- **Deposition rate:** 50–500 g/min (highest among all metal AM processes)
- **Build envelope:** Up to several meters (limited mainly by robot reach)
- **Raw material form:** Wire feedstock (Ø 0.8–1.6 mm) rather than powder
- **Heat source efficiency:** ~80–90% (vs. ~30% for laser powder bed fusion)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Remember the "**3W**" mnemonic — **W**ire + **A**rc + **A**dditive **M**anufacturing. This is your go-to clue for short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why and How"

The WAAM process operates on a sequential, layer-wise deposition philosophy. Each step is driven by well-established welding physics, integrated with motion control:

1. **Wire Feeding Mechanism:** A continuous metal wire (acting as both filler material and one electrode in GMAW) is fed at a controlled rate (WFR) through the contact tip of the welding torch.
2. **Arc Ignition & Melting:** An electric arc is established between the wire (or non-consumable tungsten electrode in GTAW) and the previously deposited layer (or substrate). Temperatures exceed **6000–8000 °C** in the arc core, instantly melting the wire tip.
3. **Droplet Transfer:** Molten metal droplets transfer across the arc plasma into a weld pool (melt pool) on the substrate. Transfer modes (short-circuit, globular, spray, pulsed spray) determine bead morphology.
4. **Solidification:** The melt pool cools rapidly due to substrate conduction, forming a weld bead. Solidification follows **columnar dendritic growth** opposite to heat flow direction.
5. **Layer Stacking:** After completing one bead (or one layer), the torch increments in the Z-direction by a defined **layer height (h)**, and the next layer is deposited.
6. **Inter-layer Temperature Control:** To prevent excessive heat accumulation (which causes sagging, dilution issues, and residual stresses), the inter-pass temperature is monitored using pyrometers or thermocouples.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **Kerala University Board–frequently tested** for numerical and derivation problems. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| Formula | Symbol Description | Engineering Use |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| $R_{d} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_{w}^{2} \cdot v_{w} \cdot \rho}{4}$ | Deposition rate (g/s); $d_w$ = wire dia (mm); $v_w$ = wire feed speed (mm/s); $\rho$ = density (g/mm³) | Calculates material addition rate |
| $h = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot \eta}{v_{t} \cdot w \cdot \rho \cdot (c_{p} \cdot \Delta T + L)}$ | Energy balance for melt pool; $V$ = voltage; $I$ = current; $\eta$ = arc efficiency; $v_t$ = travel speed; $w$ = bead width; $L$ = latent heat | Determines heat input & layer geometry |
| $H_{i} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v_{t}}$ | Heat input per unit length (J/mm); $\eta$ = arc efficiency (GMAW ≈ 0.85, GTAW ≈ 0.6) | **Most critical KTU formula** — controls dilution, distortion, grain structure |
| $D_{r} = w \cdot h \cdot v_{t} \cdot \rho$ | Volumetric deposition rate (g/s) | Productivity estimation |
| $D\% = \frac{A_{b}}{A_{b} + A_{m}} \times 100$ | Geometric dilution (%) — ratio of base metal area to total weld area | Bond quality / inter-layer fusion metric |
| $\lambda = \frac{v_{t}}{f}$ | Wavelength of bead oscillation (mm); $f$ = oscillation frequency (Hz) | Surface roughness control in weaving |
| $\sigma_{r} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Residual stress estimate (MPa); $E$ = Young's modulus; $\alpha$ = CTE; $\Delta T$ = thermal gradient | Distortion & cracking prediction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Latex Isolation Check:** All absolute values and vertical bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility & Industrial Relevance

WAAM is rapidly adopted in:

- **Aerospace:** Titanium structural components, large ribs, and brackets for Boeing, Airbus, and Lockheed Martin demonstrators. WAAM Ti-6Al-4V parts have achieved **buy-to-fly ratios** as low as **1.2**, vs. 10–20 for forged/machined parts.
- **Marine & Shipbuilding:** Propeller blades, hull stiffeners, and rudders in **Cu-Al-Mn** and **316L stainless steel**.
- **Defense:** Armor plates, gun barrel liners, and large weapon housings.
- **Tooling & Dies:** Conformal cooling channels in injection mold inserts.
- **Energy:** Pressure vessel end caps, nuclear component repair.

The strategic advantage of WAAM over powder-based AM (SLM, DMLS) is its **scalability** — the build volume is dictated by robot reach, not by a closed chamber. The trade-off is **lower surface finish** (Ra 200–500 µm as-built) and **lower geometric resolution** (typical tolerance ±0.5 mm), which is why WAAM is often followed by CNC machining for finish-critical surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Balance for Layer Height in WAAM

This derivation directly answers the frequently asked KTU problem: **"Calculate the layer height (h) for a single WAAM bead given process parameters."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** Show that the layer height in a single-pass WAAM deposit equals the ratio of volumetric deposition rate to the planar area of the bead.

**Step 1 — Wire Mass Flow Rate**

Let the wire have diameter $d_{w}$ (mm) and be fed at linear velocity $v_{w}$ (mm/s). The volume of wire consumed per second is:

$$V_{w} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_{w}^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{w} \quad [\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}]$$

Multiplying by the wire material density $\rho$ (g/mm³), the mass feed rate becomes:

$$\dot{m}_{w} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d_{w}^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{w} \quad [\text{g}/\text{s}]$$

This is the **deposition rate equation** $R_{d}$ from the cheat sheet.

**Step 2 — Volume of the Deposited Bead Per Unit Length**

For a straight, linear bead moving at travel speed $v_{t}$ (mm/s), the cross-section of the solidified bead is approximately a circular segment of width $w$ (mm) and height $h$ (mm). Assuming a near-rectangular cross-section (valid for low $\eta$ and shallow pools), the area of one bead cross-section is:

$$A_{b} = w \cdot h \quad [\text{mm}^{2}]$$

The volume of bead deposited per second is therefore:

$$V_{b} = A_{b} \cdot v_{t} = w \cdot h \cdot v_{t} \quad [\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}]$$

**Step 3 — Conservation of Mass (Deposition Equivalence)**

In the ideal (lossless) case, all wire material enters the bead:

$$\rho \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d_{w}^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{w} = \rho \cdot w \cdot h \cdot v_{t}$$

Cancelling $\rho$ and rearranging for $h$:

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{\pi \cdot d_{w}^{2} \cdot v_{w}}{4 \cdot w \cdot v_{t}} \\
&= \frac{R_{d}}{w \cdot v_{t} \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental layer-height equation** in WAAM.

**Step 4 — Inclusion of Heat Input Effects**

Real WAAM deposits exhibit bead curvature, and some metal is lost to spatter (a fraction $\epsilon$, typically 1–5%). Including these losses and the arc efficiency $\eta$, the practical layer height becomes:

$$h = \frac{\pi \cdot d_{w}^{2} \cdot v_{w} \cdot (1 - \epsilon)}{4 \cdot w \cdot v_{t}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)**

Given:
- $d_{w} = 1.2$ mm
- $v_{w} = 80$ mm/s
- $v_{t} = 6$ mm/s
- $w = 6.5$ mm
- $\epsilon = 0.02$

Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{\pi \cdot (1.2)^{2} \cdot 80 \cdot (1 - 0.02)}{4 \cdot 6.5 \cdot 6} \\
&= \frac{3.1416 \cdot 1.44 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.98}{156} \\
&= \frac{354.51}{156} \\
&\approx 2.27 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A layer height of **2.27 mm** is consistent with typical WAAM practice for GMAW of mild steel (1.5–3.0 mm range). This validates the formula against real process windows.

### 3.2 Derivation: Heat Input Control Window

The heat input $H_{i}$ governs the trade-off between **productivity** and **defect density**. KTU problems often ask for the maximum permissible $H_{i}$ to avoid sagging in vertical walls.

For a vertical wall (overhang angle = 90°), the **Rozenberg criterion** (a textbook reference) gives the maximum heat input as:

$$H_{i,\max} = k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{y} \cdot t^{3}}{\rho_{m} \cdot g}}$$

where $k$ is a process constant, $\sigma_{y}$ is yield strength, $t$ is the wall thickness, and $\rho_{m} \cdot g$ is the specific weight of the molten pool. Rearranging, the **maximum travel speed** to avoid collapse is:

$$v_{t,\min} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y} \cdot t^{3}}{\rho_{m} \cdot g}}}$$

This shows that higher current $I$ or lower travel speed $v_{t}$ can cause molten pool collapse — the **central dilemma** of WAAM process planning.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: WAAM Variants

| Parameter | GMAW-WAAM | GTAW-WAAM | PAW-WAAM |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Heat source | Consumable wire arc | Non-consumable W + wire | Plasma jet + wire |
| Deposition rate | **High (5–10 kg/h)** | Low (0.5–2 kg/h) | Medium (2–4 kg/h) |
| Arc efficiency ($\eta$) | 0.85 | 0.60 | 0.70 |
| Surface roughness (Ra) | 200–500 µm | 100–250 µm | 150–300 µm |
| Material compatibility | Steels, Al, Ni alloys | Ti, stainless, Ni alloys | Ti, refractory metals |
| Typical current | 150–350 A | 80–250 A | 100–300 A |
| Best for | Large structural parts | Precision thin walls | Reactive metals (Ti) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — WAAM System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CNCMotionController] --> A2[RobotAxisDriver]
    A2 --> A3[ArcTorchEndEffector]
    B1[PowerSourceVDI] --> A3
    B2[WireFeederUnit] --> A3
    C1[ShieldingGasInlet] --> A3
    A3 --> A4[ElectricArc]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Define Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM). List the four main subsystems of a WAAM installation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
WAAM is a directed energy deposition (DED) additive manufacturing process that uses an electric arc as the heat source to melt continuously fed wire, depositing it layer-by-layer to build a metallic part under CNC/robotic control, conforming to **ASTM F2792** as a DED-Arc process.

**Four Main Subsystems (2 marks):**
1. **Heat source & deposition head** — welding power source (GMAW/GTAW/PAW) and torch.
2. **Wire feeding system** — spool, drive rolls, encoder-controlled feed unit.
3. **Motion system** — multi-axis robotic arm or gantry CNC.
4. **Process monitoring & control** — pyrometer, thermocouples, arc sensors, and closed-loop feedback to CAM software.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> Differentiate between WAAM and Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF) in terms of deposition rate, surface finish, and build envelope.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | WAAM | LPBF |
|-----------|------|------|
| Deposition rate | **High (5–10 kg/h)** | Low (5–20 g/h) |
| Surface finish (Ra) | 200–500 µm (rough, post-machining needed) | 5–20 µm (near-net) |
| Build envelope | **Large (1–5 m, robot-reach limited)** | Small (typically 250×250×350 mm) |

WAAM trades off surface quality and resolution for vastly superior throughput and scalability, making it suitable for large structural parts, while LPBF is preferred for high-precision, small-to-medium components.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students answer **one full question (14 marks)** from a Module 4 choice of two. Each sub-part is **7 marks**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **GMAW-based WAAM** process. Discuss the influence of **heat input, travel speed, and inter-pass temperature** on the bead geometry and part quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WAAM system deposits **316L stainless steel** wire (diameter $d_{w} = 1.0$ mm) at a wire feed speed of $v_{w} = 70$ mm/s. The travel speed of the torch is $v_{t} = 5$ mm/s, and the measured bead width is $w = 5.5$ mm. The process has an arc efficiency of $\eta = 0.85$, voltage $V = 22$ V, and current $I = 180$ A. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) The deposition rate $R_{d}$ in g/s, given $\rho = 7.99 \times 10^{-3}$ g/mm³.
- (ii) The layer height $h$.
- (iii) The volumetric deposition rate $D_{r}$ in g/s.
- (iv) The heat input per unit length $H_{i}$ in J/mm.

---

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Projected]**

**(a)** Discuss the **comparative advantages and limitations** of WAAM over conventional subtractive manufacturing and powder-based additive manufacturing. Include real-world industrial case studies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the common **defects in WAAM deposits** (porosity, lack of fusion, residual stress, distortion) and discuss **mitigation strategies** for each. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solutions — Question A

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Process Explanation (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic (3 marks):** Draw the standard block diagram showing wire spool → wire feeder → contact tip → arc → melt pool → substrate. Mark shielding gas inlet, robot/CNC, and power source. (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block for system architecture.)

**Process Explanation (2 marks):**

In GMAW-WAAM, a continuous solid wire acts as **both the filler material and the consumable electrode**. The wire is fed through a contact tip where current is transferred, creating an arc between the wire tip and the substrate. The arc melts the wire, and the molten droplets transfer across the arc into a weld pool. The torch, mounted on a 6-axis robot or gantry, traces the path for one bead. After the bead, the Z-axis is incremented by the layer height, and the next bead is deposited on top of the previous one.

**Influence of Process Parameters (2 marks):**

- **Heat input $H_{i} = \eta V I / v_{t}$:** Excessively high $H_{i}$ causes melt pool collapse, sagging in vertical walls, and coarser grain. Too low $H_{i}$ results in lack of fusion between layers and porosity.
- **Travel speed $v_{t}$:** Controls bead width and heat input inversely. Low $v_t$ → wide, thick beads (high productivity, but risk of sagging). High $v_t$ → narrow beads, better resolution, but lower productivity.
- **Inter-pass temperature:** Critical for **residual stress control**. If too high, heat accumulates → distortion. If too low (< 100 °C for steel), cracking risk increases. Typical control window: **150–200 °C** for mild steel, **100–150 °C** for titanium.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $d_{w} = 1.0$ mm, $v_{w} = 70$ mm/s, $v_{t} = 5$ mm/s, $w = 5.5$ mm
- $\rho = 7.99 \times 10^{-3}$ g/mm³
- $V = 22$ V, $I = 180$ A, $\eta = 0.85$

**(i) Deposition Rate $R_{d}$ (2 marks):**

$$R_{d} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot d_{w}^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{w}$$

Substituting:

$$R_{d} = 7.99 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot (1.0)^{2}}{4} \cdot 70$$

$$R_{d} = 7.99 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.7854 \cdot 70 = 0.4391 \text{ g/s}$$

**Rounded: $R_{d} \approx 0.439$ g/s** (equivalently **26.35 g/min** or **1.58 kg/h**)

**(ii) Layer Height $h$ (2 marks):**

$$h = \frac{\pi \cdot d_{w}^{2} \cdot v_{w}}{4 \cdot w \cdot v_{t}} = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 70}{4 \cdot 5.5 \cdot 5}$$

$$h = \frac{219.91}{110} \approx 2.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Layer height $h \approx 2.0$ mm.**

**(iii) Volumetric Deposition Rate $D_{r}$ (1 mark):**

$$D_{r} = w \cdot h \cdot v_{t} \cdot \rho = 5.5 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 5 \cdot 7.99 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$D_{r} = 55 \cdot 7.99 \times 10^{-3} = 0.4395 \text{ g/s}$$

This matches $R_{d}$ as expected (mass conservation).

**(iv) Heat Input $H_{i}$ (2 marks):**

$$H_{i} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v_{t}} = \frac{0.85 \cdot 22 \cdot 180}{5}$$

$$H_{i} = \frac{3366}{5} = 673.2 \text{ J/mm}$$

**Heat input $H_{i} = 673.2$ J/mm** (within typical GMAW-WAAM range of 400–1000 J/mm for 316L stainless steel).

---

### Model Solutions — Question B

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — WAAM vs. Conventional & Powder AM (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Conventional Subtractive | Powder AM (SLM/LPBF) | WAAM |
|--------|--------------------------|------------------------|------|
| Material utilization | 5–15% (chips) | 60–95% | **80–95%** |
| Buy-to-fly ratio | High (10–20) | Medium (1.5–3) | **Low (1.1–2.0)** |
| Build size | Limited by machine | **< 500 mm** | > 2 m |
| Lead time (large parts) | Weeks | Days–Weeks | **Days** |
| Surface finish | Excellent (machined) | Excellent (as-built) | **Rough (post-machining)** |
| Cost driver | Material + machining | Powder cost (~$200–500/kg) | Wire cost (~$10–50/kg) |

**Advantages of WAAM (2 marks):**
- Highest deposition rate among metal AM processes.
- Low feedstock cost (wire is 5–10× cheaper than powder).
- Scalable to multi-meter parts.
- Less sensitive to feedstock contamination (no oxygen-controlled chamber required for most variants).

**Limitations of WAAM (1 mark):**
- Poor surface finish requires secondary machining.
- Limited geometric complexity (no overhangs without support).
- Anisotropic mechanical properties (columnar grains along build direction).
- Residual stresses require heat treatment.

**Case Study (1 mark):** *WAAM-produced Ti-6Al-4V structural bracket for the Airbus A350 XWB demonstrator achieved 60% weight reduction and 40% cost savings over machined billet.*

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Defects & Mitigation (7 marks)

| Defect | Cause | Mitigation |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| **Porosity** | Gas entrapment, contaminated wire, low shielding gas flow | Use high-purity shielding gas (Ar/He), maintain flow rate 15–25 L/min, clean wire surface, degrease substrate |
| **Lack of fusion** | Low heat input, excessive travel speed, oxide layers on previous layer | Increase $H_i$ by reducing $v_t$ or increasing $I$; wire-brush inter-pass oxide (especially for Al, Ti) |
| **Residual stress** | Repeated thermal cycling, large thermal gradient | Implement inter-pass cooling, post-deposition heat treatment (e.g., stress relief at 600 °C for 1 h for steel) |
| **Distortion / warping** | Non-uniform cooling, locked-in stresses | Use substrate preheating (150–200 °C), symmetrical deposition path planning, fixturing with clamping bolts |
| **Layer delamination** | Insufficient inter-layer bonding, excessive inter-pass temperature drop | Maintain inter-pass temperature above threshold, use in-situ rolling or peening for grain refinement |
| **Columnar grain anisotropy** | Directional heat extraction | Apply inter-layer cold metal transfer (CMT) pulsing, ultrasonic vibration, or in-situ rolling |

**Explanation (1 mark):** The root cause of most WAAM defects is **uncontrolled thermal history**. A multi-pronged approach — process parameter optimization + in-situ monitoring + post-process heat treatment — is essential for structural-grade WAAM parts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** in numericals: $v_{w}$ in mm/s must be converted to consistent units with $\rho$ in g/mm³. Many students incorrectly use $\rho$ in kg/m³.
> 2. **Forgetting arc efficiency $\eta$** in $H_i$ formula. Default values: GMAW = 0.85, GTAW = 0.60, PAW = 0.70. KTU valuation key often gives 1 mark deduction for omission.
> 3. **Confusing GMAW with GTAW:** In GMAW, the **wire is the electrode**; in GTAW, the **tungsten is the electrode** and the wire is filler only. Examiners penalize this distinction heavily (up to 2 marks).
> 4. **Ignoring inter-pass temperature control:** In WAAM process description questions, students often describe deposition but forget the **thermal management** aspect — typically 1 mark lost.
> 5. **No mention of post-processing:** WAAM parts almost always require **CNC machining** and **heat treatment** for functional use. Omitting this in "applications" or "limitations" answers costs 1 mark.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist for last-minute KTU preparation:**

- **WAAM = DED-Arc** (ASTM F2792). Heat source = electric arc; feedstock = wire.
- **Core "Formula Triad" for KTU numerics:**
  - Deposition rate: $R_{d} = \rho \pi d_{w}^{2} v_{w} / 4$
  - Layer height: $h = \pi d_{w}^{2} v_{w} / (4 w v_{t})$
  - Heat input: $H_{i} = \eta V I / v_{t}$
- **Arc efficiency defaults:** GMAW = **0.85**, GTAW = **0.60**, PAW = **0.70**.
- **Key advantage:** Highest deposition rate (5–10 kg/h) and largest build envelope in metal AM.
- **Key limitation:** Poor as-built surface finish → always followed by CNC machining.
- **Inter-pass temperature window** is critical: **150–200 °C for steel, 100–150 °C for Ti**.
- **Columnar dendritic grain structure** grows opposite to heat flow → anisotropy in mechanical properties.
- **Defect mitigation triad:** Process parameter tuning + Inter-pass temperature control + Post-process heat treatment.
- **Industrial case studies to remember:** Airbus A350 WAAM bracket (Ti-6Al-4V), Boeing 787 floor beams, Lockheed Martin submarine components.
- **Comparison keywords for short answers:** *"Scalable, high-deposition, low-cost wire feedstock, requires secondary machining."*
- **Why WAAM and not SLM for large parts?** SLM is powder-bed, enclosed chamber, slow; WAAM is wire-arc, open environment, fast.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Advanced Metal Joining Techniques — Conceptual Framework

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques** (AMJT) are a class of non-conventional joining processes that employ concentrated, high-energy-density sources, solid-state diffusion mechanisms, or hybrid physical-chemical actions to coalesce materials that are difficult or impossible to join using traditional fusion welding (SMAW, GMAW) or mechanical fastening.

In the formal **KTU 2024 syllabus** context, these techniques are broadly classified under three super-categories:
1. **High-Energy-Density Beam Processes** (Electron Beam Welding, Laser Beam Welding)
2. **Solid-State Joining Processes** (Friction Stir Welding, Ultrasonic Welding, Diffusion Bonding, Explosion Welding)
3. **Advanced Hybrid & Special Processes** (Plasma Arc variants, Electroslag Welding, Stud Arc, Cold Metal Transfer)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Sun and a Magnifying Glass**
> Imagine trying to melt a thick steel plate.
> - A traditional oxy-acetylene torch spreads heat over a **wide area** (low energy density) → slow, large HAZ, distortion.
> - An **Electron Beam** acts like focusing sunlight through a magnifying glass onto one spot — the energy per unit area (W/mm²) is **enormous**, melting a deep narrow column of metal almost instantly.
> - **Friction Stir Welding** is like rubbing two ice cubes together until they melt at the interface and freeze together — no melting pool, just **plasticized solid metal** flowing into a common joint.
> - **Diffusion Bonding** is similar to pressing two butter slices together under slight warmth — atoms slowly migrate across the boundary without any melting.

### Why Conventional Welding Falls Short

| Conventional Limitation | Advanced Technique Solution |
|---|---|
| High heat input → wide HAZ, distortion | Concentrated energy source (EBW, LBW) |
| Dissimilar metals crack on cooling | Solid-state processes (FSW, Diffusion) |
| Reactive/refractory metals (Ti, Zr) oxidize | Vacuum/Inert chamber (EBW) |
| Thin foils burn through | Pulsed micro-joining (USW, LBW) |
| Heat-sensitive electronics | Low-temperature bonding (USW, Soldering) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — Power Density (W/mm²)** is the single most important parameter distinguishing advanced from conventional processes.
> - Oxy-fuel: **~10¹** W/mm²
> - SMAW/GMAW: **~10²–10³** W/mm²
> - Plasma/EBW/LBW: **~10⁶–10⁹** W/mm²

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Advanced Joining Physics

Every advanced joining process can be decomposed into **five universal physical mechanisms** (this is the conceptual core examiners love to test):

1. **Energy Generation** — How the joining energy is produced (kinetic, electromagnetic, acoustic, chemical, mechanical).
2. **Energy Transfer & Coupling** — How energy reaches the workpiece interface (beam, conduction, friction, vibration).
3. **Material Response** — How the metal responds (melting, plastic deformation, atomic diffusion, vaporization).
4. **Joint Formation** — How metallurgical bonding is achieved (fusion, forge pressure, diffusion, interlocking).
5. **Solidification / Consolidation** — How the joint achieves final strength (cooling, dynamic recrystallization, grain growth).

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept of "Heat Input" (HI)** — The most universally tested KTU formula for any fusion-based advanced process is:
> $$\text{HI} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$$
> where $\eta$ = process efficiency, $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $S$ = travel speed.

## 2.2 Classification by Energy Density (The KTU Master Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Density** is defined as the power delivered divided by the focused spot area:
> $$P_d = \frac{P}{A_{\text{spot}}} \quad \text{(W/mm}^2\text{)}$$

| Process | Power Density (W/mm²) | Weld Type | Atmosphere |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxy-fuel | 10¹ | Fusion | Open air |
| SMAW | 10² | Fusion | Open air |
| GMAW/GTAW | 10²–10³ | Fusion | Open / Inert |
| Plasma Arc | 10⁴–10⁶ | Fusion | Open / Inert |
| **Electron Beam (EBW)** | **10⁶–10⁹** | Deep fusion | **Vacuum** |
| **Laser Beam (LBW)** | **10⁵–10⁸** | Deep fusion | Inert shield |
| **Friction Stir (FSW)** | Mechanical | **Solid-state** | Open |
| **Ultrasonic (USW)** | Acoustic | **Solid-state** | Open |
| **Diffusion Bonding** | Thermal + Pressure | **Solid-state** | Vacuum / Inert |
| **Explosion Welding** | Kinetic | **Solid-state** | Open |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All advanced process formulas converge on **two universal equations**: Power Density and Heat Input.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Density | $P_d = P / A_{\text{spot}}$ | W/mm² | Energy concentration |
| 2 | Heat Input | $\text{HI} = \eta V I / S$ | J/mm | Energy per unit length |
| 3 | Cooling Rate (approx.) | $t_{8/5} = f(\text{HI}, \text{thickness})$ | seconds | HAZ microstructure |
| 4 | Diffusion Depth | $x = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ | m | Fick's 1st law |
| 5 | Diffusion Coefficient | $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | m²/s | Temperature dependent |
| 6 | FSW Strain Rate | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{v}{\pi \cdot r_e \cdot a}$ | s⁻¹ | Material flow |
| 7 | EBW Beam Power | $P_b = I_b \cdot V_b$ | W | Electron kinetic energy |
| 8 | Laser Absorptivity | $A = 1 - R$ | dimensionless | Energy coupling |
| 9 | USW Weld Time | $t_w = f(P, f, \text{hardness})$ | seconds | Acoustic bonding |
| 10 | Friction Pressure | $p = F / A_{\text{tool shoulder}}$ | MPa | FSW plunge force |

> [!NOTE]
> In Fick's 1st law of diffusion, the depth of atomic inter-diffusion across a bond interface follows a **square-root-of-time** law — this is why diffusion bonding takes minutes to hours (not seconds), while fusion welding takes seconds.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Ti, Ni-superalloys):** EBW of turbine blades; FSW of fuselage panels; LBW of fuel nozzle injector rings.
- **Automotive (Al alloys):** FSW of battery trays, drive shafts; USW for wiring harnesses; CMT for thin sheets.
- **Electronics:** USW for Cu-Al wire bonds; LBW for micro-batteries; Thermocompression bonding for chips.
- **Nuclear / Chemical:** Diffusion bonding of Zircaloy; Explosion welding of Al-Steel transition joints.
- **Marine / Defense:** Electroslag welding for thick ship plates; Explosion welding of corrosion-resistant clads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input for an Advanced Process

The most fundamental derivation connecting **any** advanced process to its thermal footprint is the **Heat Input Equation** for arc-based advanced processes (Plasma, EBW) and the equivalent **Energy-per-Length** formulation for beam processes.

> **Note:** We start with the assumption that the process is moving at a steady travel speed $S$ (mm/s) under a stationary heat source of effective power $\eta V I$ (Watts).

### Step 1 — Define Effective Power Delivered to Workpiece

The power supply delivers an electrical input $V \times I$, but only a fraction $\eta$ is actually absorbed by the workpiece. The remainder is lost as radiation, reflection, or absorbed by the electrode.

$$
P_{\text{eff}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

> **Explanation:** $\eta$ is the **process thermal efficiency** — for EBW it is ~0.7–0.9, for LBW it can be as low as 0.1 (due to high reflectivity of metals at 1 µm wavelength) and rises to 0.5+ at 10.6 µm CO₂ wavelength.

### Step 2 — Define the "Time a Point on the Weld Line Spends in the Heat Source"

For a moving heat source, a point on the weld seam experiences the energy for a duration $t$ equal to the length of the heat-affected zone $L_{\text{HAZ}}$ divided by the travel speed $S$.

$$
t_{\text{dwell}} = \frac{L_{\text{HAZ}}}{S}
$$

### Step 3 — Define Heat Input as Energy per Unit Weld Length

The energy per unit length of weld is the power divided by travel speed:

$$
\text{HI} = \frac{P_{\text{eff}}}{S} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad \text{(J/mm)}
$$

> **Explanation:** This is the canonical **Heat Input (HI)** expression in J/mm. KTU examiners use it to ask "compare HAZ widths" between two processes.

### Step 4 — Derive the Approximate Cooling Time $t_{8/5}$

The cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C (the $t_{8/5}$ parameter that controls HAZ microstructure) is empirically related to heat input and plate thickness $d$ (mm) by the **thick-plate 3D cooling model** (Adams formulation):

$$
t_{8/5} = \frac{(\text{HI}/d)^2}{2 \cdot k \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_1 - T_0} - \frac{1}{T_2 - T_0}\right)^2}
$$

> **Explanation:** A higher heat input means **slower cooling** → coarser HAZ grains → lower toughness. This is why advanced low-HI processes (EBW, LBW, FSW) are preferred for thick sections of HSLA and quenched-tempered steels.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fick's Law of Diffusion for Diffusion Bonding

> **Assumption:** Isothermal bonding of two flat surfaces under a constant bonding pressure; unidirectional diffusion of alloying atoms across the interface.

### Step 1 — Fick's First Law

The flux of atoms $J$ (atoms/m²·s) across the interface is proportional to the concentration gradient $\partial C / \partial x$:

$$
J = -D \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}
$$

> **Explanation:** $D$ is the diffusion coefficient. Negative sign indicates flow from high to low concentration.

### Step 2 — Temperature Dependence of $D$ (Arrhenius Form)

$$
D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{R T}\right)
$$

> **Explanation:** $D_0$ is the pre-exponential factor (m²/s), $Q$ is activation energy (J/mol), $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T$ is absolute temperature (K). This is why diffusion bonding is performed at $T = 0.6 - 0.8 \, T_m$ (where $T_m$ = melting point in K).

### Step 3 — Diffusion Depth vs. Time

For a constant surface concentration, the depth $x$ of diffusion in time $t$ is:

$$
x = \sqrt{D \cdot t}
$$

> **Explanation:** Since $D$ depends exponentially on $T$, doubling the temperature can increase diffusion depth by **orders of magnitude** — this is why holding time is less important than holding temperature in diffusion bonding.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision flow is a KTU-favorite "Compare & Justify" question structure.

| Material Type | Recommended Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Thin Al sheets (< 2 mm) | USW, LBW | Low heat, no burn-through |
| Thick Ti plates (> 25 mm) | EBW | Deep penetration, vacuum purity |
| Dissimilar Al-Cu | FSW, Explosion | No brittle intermetallics |
| Reactive metals (Zr, Nb) | EBW in vacuum | Prevents contamination |
| Honeycomb / Foil | USW, Resistance | Localized bonding |
| Large Al ship panels | FSW | Low distortion, no filler |
| Nuclear Zircaloy | Diffusion bonding | Joint has same properties as base |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Universal Advanced Joining System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source Subsystem] --> B[Energy Coupling and Transfer]
    B --> C[Workpiece Interface]
    C --> D[Material Response Mechanism]
    D --> E[Joint Formation Zone]
    E --> F[Solidification and Consolidation]
    F --> G[Quality Monitoring NDT]

    A1[Beam EBW or LBW] -.-> A
    A2[Mechanical FSW or USW] -.-> A
    A3[Thermal Diffusion Furnace] -.-> A
    A4[Kinetic Explosion Detonation] -.-> A

    D1[Melting Pool Fusion] -.-> D
    D2[Plasticized Solid Flow] -.-> D
    D3[Atomic Diffusion Creep] -.-> D
    D4[Atomic Jetting Collision] -.-> D

    G1[Radiography] -.-> G
    G2[Ultrasonic Testing] -.-> G
    G3[Penetrant Testing] -.-> G
    G4[Eddy Current] -.-> G
```

## 4.2 Process Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Advanced Metal Joining Techniques]
    P --> P1[High Energy Density Beam Welding]
    P --> P2[Solid State Welding]
    P --> P3[Advanced Arc Variants]
    P --> P4[Hybrid and Special Processes]

    P1 --> P1a[Electron Beam Welding EBW]
    P1 --> P1b[Laser Beam Welding LBW]

    P2 --> P2a[Friction Stir Welding FSW]
    P2 --> P2b[Ultrasonic Welding USW]
    P2 --> P2c[Diffusion Bonding DB]
    P2 --> P2d[Explosion Welding EXW]

    P3 --> P3a[Plasma Arc Welding PAW]
    P3 --> P3b[Electroslag Welding ESW]
    P3 --> P3c[Stud Arc Welding SAW]

    P4 --> P4a[Cold Metal Transfer CMT]
    P4 --> P4b[Laser Arc Hybrid]
    P4 --> P4c[Induction Brazing]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Density vs. Joint Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW[Low Energy Density 10 to 10 cubed W per mm squared]
        L1[Oxy Fuel]
        L2[SMAW]
        L3[GMAW GTAW]
    end

    subgraph MED[Medium Energy Density 10 to the 4 to 10 to the 6]
        M1[Plasma Arc]
        M2[Pulsed MIG]
    end

    subgraph HIGH[High Energy Density 10 to the 6 to 10 to the 9]
        H1[Electron Beam]
        H2[Laser Beam]
    end

    subgraph SS[Solid State Joining No Melt Pool]
        S1[Friction Stir]
        S2[Ultrasonic]
        S3[Diffusion Bond]
        S4[Explosion]
    end

    LOW --> JT1[Wide HAZ and Fusion]
    MED --> JT2[Medium HAZ and Fusion]
    HIGH --> JT3[Narrow HAZ and Deep Fusion]
    SS --> JT4[No HAZ and Plasticized Bond]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Moving from "Low" to "High" energy density shrinks the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) and increases depth-to-width ratio of the weld pool. Solid-state processes (SS) avoid melting entirely, so they have **zero fusion zone** and minimal HAZ.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Question 1]**

**Q1. Define "Power Density" of a welding process. Why is it the most important parameter for advanced joining techniques?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Density** $P_d$ is defined as the heat or beam power delivered to the workpiece per unit area of the focused spot:
> $$P_d = \frac{P}{A_{\text{spot}}} \quad \text{(W/mm}^2\text{)}$$

It is the most critical parameter because it determines:
- (1) The **mode of heat transfer** at the workpiece (conduction vs. keyhole deep penetration).
- (2) The **depth-to-width ratio** of the weld pool (higher $P_d$ → deeper, narrower welds).
- (3) The **HAZ width** (higher $P_d$ → narrower HAZ → less distortion).
- Advanced processes (EBW, LBW) have $P_d$ in the range of $10^6$ to $10^9$ W/mm², compared to $10^2$–$10^3$ W/mm² for conventional arc welding.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Question 2]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Fusion Welding and Solid-State Welding with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fusion Welding | Solid-State Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Above melting point | Below melting point (typically 0.6–0.8 $T_m$) |
| Joint mechanism | Molten pool solidification | Atomic diffusion / plastic deformation |
| Filler metal | Often required | Generally not required |
| HAZ | Always present | Minimal or absent |
| Examples | LBW, EBW, PAW | FSW, USW, Diffusion Bonding |
| Distortion | Higher | Significantly lower |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | ESE Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the classification of advanced metal joining techniques. Discuss the principle of any **two solid-state processes** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Heat Input equation** $\text{HI} = \frac{\eta V I}{S}$ for an arc-based advanced welding process and explain how the heat input influences the **HAZ microstructure and cooling time $t_{8/5}$** in HSLA steels. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Classification (3 marks):** The classification is divided into three broad categories as shown in the block diagram in Section 4.2 — (i) High-Energy-Density Beam Welding (EBW, LBW), (ii) Solid-State Welding (FSW, USW, Diffusion Bonding, Explosion Welding), (iii) Advanced Arc Variants (PAW, ESW).

**Solid-State Process 1 — Friction Stir Welding (2 marks):**
- A non-consumable rotating tool with a pin and shoulder is plunged into the joint line between two clamped plates.
- Frictional heat softens (plasticizes) the metal below the melting point.
- The tool traverses along the joint, and the plasticized material is stirred from the leading edge to the trailing edge, creating a solid-state bond.
- **Joint features:** No melting → no HAZ → fine dynamically recrystallized grains → superior mechanical properties for Al-alloys.

**Solid-State Process 2 — Diffusion Bonding (2 marks):**
- Two clean, flat surfaces are held together under low pressure ($1$–$10$ MPa) at elevated temperature ($0.6$–$0.8 \, T_m$) in vacuum or inert atmosphere.
- Atomic diffusion across the interface produces a metallurgical bond without macroscopic deformation.
- Applications: Zircaloy nuclear fuel cladding, Ti aerospace components, dissimilar metal joints.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1,
- Effective power: $P_{\text{eff}} = \eta V I$
- Time of energy deposition at a point: $t = L_{\text{HAZ}} / S$
- Energy per unit length (Heat Input):
$$\text{HI} = \frac{\eta V I}{S} \quad \text{(J/mm)}$$

**Influence on HAZ and $t_{8/5}$ (4 marks):**
- $t_{8/5}$ is the cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C, the critical range for carbide precipitation and grain growth in HSLA steels.
- **Higher HI → larger molten pool → slower conduction cooling → longer $t_{8/5}$ → coarser grains and softer HAZ (lower toughness).**
- **Lower HI → faster cooling → shorter $t_{8/5}$ → finer grains and harder HAZ (better strength, but risk of HAZ cracking).**
- For HSLA steels, an optimum $t_{8/5}$ of 5–15 seconds gives a balanced microstructure.
- This is why advanced low-HI processes (EBW, LBW, FSW) are strongly preferred for thick-section HSLA applications.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the working principle of **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two plates of 10 mm thick mild steel are joined by EBW at a beam power of 6 kW and travel speed of 500 mm/min. Calculate the **(i) heat input** (assuming $\eta = 0.85$) and **(ii) power density** (assuming a focused spot diameter of 0.4 mm). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- A high-voltage (50–200 kV) tungsten filament cathode emits electrons by thermionic emission.
- These electrons are accelerated, focused electromagnetically, and focused onto the workpiece in a vacuum chamber ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa).
- The kinetic energy of the electrons is converted to heat on impact, vaporizing the metal and forming a deep keyhole.
- The keyhole moves with the beam, producing an extremely deep, narrow weld (depth-to-width ratio up to 25:1).

**Block Diagram Description (1 mark):** Filament → Cathode → Anode (accelerating) → Electromagnetic lens (focusing) → Deflection coils → Workpiece in vacuum chamber.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Extremely high power density ($10^6$–$10^9$ W/mm²) → narrow HAZ, minimal distortion.
- Deep penetration (up to 300 mm in a single pass) without filler.
- Vacuum environment prevents oxidation — ideal for reactive and refractory metals (Ti, Zr, Nb).
- Welds dissimilar metals with brittle intermetallics minimized by rapid solidification.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- High capital cost; vacuum chamber limits workpiece size.
- Requires X-ray shielding due to generated bremsstrahlung.
- Sensitive to fit-up; magnetic materials cause beam deflection.
- Vacuum cycle time reduces productivity.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given data:**
- Beam power $P = 6$ kW = 6000 W
- Travel speed $S = 500$ mm/min = $8.333$ mm/s
- Process efficiency $\eta = 0.85$
- Spot diameter $d = 0.4$ mm → spot area $A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$

**Step 1 — Calculate Spot Area:**
$$A_{\text{spot}} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.4)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.16 = 0.1257 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Heat Input (i):**
$$\text{HI} = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{S} = \frac{0.85 \times 6000 \text{ W}}{8.333 \text{ mm/s}}$$

$$\text{HI} = \frac{5100}{8.333} = 612 \text{ J/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Power Density (ii):**
$$P_d = \frac{P}{A_{\text{spot}}} = \frac{6000 \text{ W}}{0.1257 \text{ mm}^2}$$

$$P_d = 4.77 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

> **Examination Tip:** Note that even a $4.77 \times 10^4$ W/mm² value is "advanced process" territory — but true EBW can reach $10^6$–$10^9$ W/mm² with finer spot sizes (0.1–0.2 mm) and pulsed operation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in heat input — write "J/mm" not just "612". (–0.5 mark)
> 2. **Converting travel speed** incorrectly — students often write 500 mm/s instead of 8.333 mm/s. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing Heat Input with Power Density** — HI is energy per unit length (J/mm), $P_d$ is power per unit area (W/mm²). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the "block diagram"** even when the question says "with a neat block diagram". (–1 to 2 marks)
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** for derivations — e.g., "steady state, stationary heat source". (–0.5 mark)
> 6. **Writing only the formula** without explaining the physical meaning of each term. (–1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Advanced Metal Joining Concepts**

- **Definition:** Advanced joining = non-conventional processes using concentrated energy, solid-state mechanisms, or hybrid techniques.
- **Core Metric:** Power Density $P_d = P / A_{\text{spot}}$ (W/mm²) — distinguishes advanced from conventional.
- **Heat Input Formula:** $\text{HI} = \eta V I / S$ (J/mm) — universal for all moving heat-source processes.
- **Process Classification:** (1) Beam (EBW, LBW), (2) Solid-State (FSW, USW, DB, EXW), (3) Arc Variants (PAW, ESW).
- **Power Density Order:** Oxy-fuel $10^1$ → SMAW $10^2$ → PAW $10^5$ → LBW $10^6$ → EBW $10^9$ W/mm².
- **EBW:** Vacuum required; depth-to-width ratio up to 25:1; ideal for reactive/refractory metals.
- **LBW:** Atmospheric operation; two types — CO₂ (10.6 µm, high absorption) and Nd:YAG / Fiber (1.06 µm, lower absorption on metals).
- **FSW:** Solid-state; rotating tool; produces fine dynamically recrystallized grains; no HAZ, no melting.
- **USW:** Acoustic energy at 20–40 kHz; localized plastic deformation; ideal for thin foils and electronics.
- **Diffusion Bonding:** Time- and temperature-dependent; obeys $x = \sqrt{Dt}$; $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$; typically $0.6$–$0.8 \, T_m$.
- **Explosion Welding:** Uses controlled detonation; clads dissimilar metals (e.g., Al-Steel, Ti-Steel transition joints).
- **HAZ Microstructure Rule:** Higher HI → slower cooling → longer $t_{8/5}$ → coarser HAZ grains.
- **Dissimilar Metal Rule:** Solid-state processes (FSW, EXW) are preferred to avoid brittle intermetallics formed by fusion.
- **Atmosphere Rule:** Reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Nb) require vacuum (EBW) or ultra-pure inert shielding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODELLING OF WELDING PROCESSES — Core Technical Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding Process Modelling** is the mathematical and computational representation of the physical phenomena occurring during a welding operation — including heat transfer, fluid dynamics, metallurgical transformations, and mechanical distortions — using analytical solutions, numerical methods (predominantly the **Finite Element Method / FEM**), or hybrid approaches to predict weld pool geometry, thermal cycles, residual stresses, and microstructural evolution *without* performing the actual physical weld.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, modelling is classified into four hierarchical levels of complexity:

| Model Level | Physical Fidelity | Computational Cost | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 — Analytical** | Low | Negligible | Peak temperature, cooling rate |
| **Level 2 — Thermal FEA** | Medium | Moderate | Thermal history, weld pool shape |
| **Level 3 — Thermo-Mechanical FEA** | High | High | Residual stress, distortion |
| **Level 4 — Microstructural (CA-FE Coupled)** | Very High | Very High | Grain size, phase fractions |

> [!NOTE]
> The most widely used heat source representation is the **Goldak Double-Ellipsoid Volumetric Heat Source Model (1984)**, considered the gold standard for arc welding simulation in commercial codes like ABAQUS, SYSWELD, and ANSYS.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Weather Forecast of the Weld"

> [!TIP]
> **Think of welding modelling as a weather forecasting system for a tiny, violent tornado confined inside a moving line.**
> - The **weld arc** is the "storm system" injecting massive thermal energy.
> - The **base metal** is the "atmosphere" absorbing and conducting that energy.
> - The **mathematical model** is the supercomputer predicting where the heat will travel, how fast it will cool, and how the metal will deform — *before* you ever strike an arc.
> - The **heat source equation** is the "initial condition" (like temperature & pressure inputs to a forecast).
> - The **boundary conditions** are the "geography" (clamping, fixturing, plate thickness).

This analogy helps visualize why the **governing differential equation** (Fourier's heat conduction) must be solved over a **moving domain** with extreme thermal gradients (often **10,000 °C/mm**).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** for radiation: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Emissivity of molten steel**: $\varepsilon \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$ (at **1600 °C**)
- **Effective heat input efficiency ($\eta$)**: **0.7 to 0.85** for GMAW, **0.6 to 0.75** for GTAW
- **Thermal conductivity of steel**: $k \approx 25$ to $45 \text{ W/m·K}$
- **Specific heat of steel**: $c_p \approx 500$ to $680 \text{ J/kg·K}$
- **Density of steel**: $\rho \approx 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Thermal diffusivity**: $\alpha = k / (\rho \, c_p) \approx 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2\text{/s}$ for low-carbon steel
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Welding Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to *differentiate* between model types. Memorize the logic below.

### A. By Mathematical Approach
- **Analytical Models** — Closed-form solutions (e.g., **Rosenthal's Equations**). Valid for thin plates with no latent heat.
- **Numerical Models** — Discretized domain solved iteratively. Includes:
  - **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** — simple grid-based.
  - **Finite Element Method (FEM)** — industry standard; handles complex geometry.
  - **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** — used in CFD weld pool simulations.

### B. By Physical Phenomenon Captured
- **Thermal Model** → temperature field
- **Fluid Flow Model** → weld pool convection (Marangoni effect)
- **Mechanical Model** → stress, strain, distortion
- **Metallurgical Model** → phase transformation kinetics
- **Coupled Multi-Physics Model** → state-of-the-art industrial simulation

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Welding Simulation

1. **Heat Source Definition** — *How energy enters the system.*
   - Gaussian Surface Flux (shallow penetration)
   - Goldak Double Ellipsoid (deep penetration arc welding)
   - Cone/Keyhole (laser & electron beam)

2. **Material Property Definition** — *How the system responds.*
   - Temperature-dependent: $k(T)$, $c_p(T)$, $\rho(T)$, yield strength $\sigma_y(T)$
   - Phase transformation kinetics: CCT/TTT diagrams

3. **Boundary Conditions** — *What confines the system.*
   - Convection: $q'' = h_c (T - T_\infty)$
   - Radiation: $q'' = \varepsilon \sigma (T^4 - T_\infty^4)$
   - Mechanical fixtures (clamping, symmetry planes)

## 2.3 The Governing Equation — Fourier's Heat Conduction

The cornerstone of all thermal welding models is the transient 3D heat equation:

$$
\rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left( k \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left( k \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left( k \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial z} \right) + \dot{Q}_v
$$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}_v$ = **volumetric heat source term (W/m³)**
- $T$ = temperature field, $t$ = time
- $k$, $c_p$, $\rho$ are temperature-dependent material properties

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table — Print This. KTU Questions Test These Equations Directly.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Heat Input** | $Q_{\text{net}} = \eta \, V \, I$ (W or J/mm if $V \cdot I$ divided by travel speed) | Energy delivered to workpiece |
| 2 | **Heat Input per unit length** | $H = \eta \, V \, I / v$ (J/mm) | Used in analytical models |
| 3 | **Rosenthal 2-D (Thick Plate)** | $T - T_0 = \dfrac{q}{2\pi k} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{v \xi}{2\alpha}\right) \cdot K_0\!\left(\dfrac{v r}{2\alpha}\right)$ | Quasi-steady temp field, thick plate |
| 4 | **Rosenthal 3-D (Thick Plate)** | $T - T_0 = \dfrac{q}{2\pi k} \cdot \dfrac{1}{r} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{v (x + r)}{2\alpha}\right)$ | Moving point source on semi-infinite body |
| 5 | **Cooling Rate (t₈/₅)** | $CR = \dfrac{2\pi k \, \Delta T^2}{H}$ | Critical for HAZ microstructure prediction |
| 6 | **Goldak Front Ellipsoid** | $q_f(x,y,z) = \dfrac{6\sqrt{3} f_f Q}{a_f b c \pi \sqrt{\pi}} \exp\!\left(-3\dfrac{x^2}{a_f^{\,2}} - 3\dfrac{y^2}{b^2} - 3\dfrac{z^2}{c^2}\right)$ | Heat distribution in front of arc |
| 7 | **Goldak Rear Ellipsoid** | $q_r(x,y,z) = \dfrac{6\sqrt{3} f_r Q}{a_r b c \pi \sqrt{\pi}} \exp\!\left(-3\dfrac{x^2}{a_r^{\,2}} - 3\dfrac{y^2}{b^2} - 3\dfrac{z^2}{c^2}\right)$ | Heat distribution behind arc |
| 8 | **Convective Heat Loss** | $q'' = h_c (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Boundary on plate surface |
| 9 | **Radiative Heat Loss** | $q'' = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^{\,4} - T_\infty^{\,4})$ | Dominant at high temperature |
| 10 | **Newton's Cooling Law (Local)** | $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Free convection on exposed surfaces |

> [!TIP]
> **Vertical bars trick:** In the table above, expressions like $\vert x \vert$ are written as $\vert\, x \,\vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. In your answer sheets, plain absolute value is fine.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) Optimization** — reduces trial-and-error welds, saving **₹2–5 lakhs** in industry.
- **Residual Stress Prediction** — prevents hydrogen-induced cracking in pressure vessels (ASME Section VIII).
- **Distortion Compensation** — critical for aerospace panels and ship hulls.
- **Microstructure Prediction** — avoids brittle martensite in the HAZ.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rosenthal's Thick-Plate (3-D) Temperature Distribution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions of Rosenthal's Solution:**
> 1. Point heat source moving at constant velocity $v$ along the $x$-axis.
> 2. Body is **semi-infinite** and **isotropic**.
> 3. **Constant** thermal properties ($k$, $\rho$, $c_p$).
> 4. **No** latent heat of fusion.
> 5. **Quasi-steady** state (∂T/∂t = 0 in the moving frame).

### Step 1 — Set Up Moving Reference Frame

Attach a coordinate system to the heat source so the source appears stationary.

$$
\xi = x - v t
$$

The time derivative in the moving frame transforms as:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = -v \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial \xi}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the Heat Equation

In the moving frame, with a point source of strength $q$ at the origin, the steady-state 3-D conduction equation in spherical coordinates becomes:

$$
\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r^2 \frac{dT}{dr} \right) + \frac{1}{r^2 \sin\phi} \frac{d}{d\phi}\!\left( \sin\phi \frac{dT}{d\phi} \right) - \frac{v}{\alpha} \cos\phi \, \frac{dT}{dr} = 0
$$

Here, $\phi$ is the angle measured from the source velocity vector, and $r$ is the radial distance from the source.

### Step 3 — Apply the Quasi-Steady Heat Balance

At a large sphere of radius $r$ around the source, all heat $q$ must flow out by conduction:

$$
q = -k \oint_S \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} \, dS = -4\pi r^2 k \frac{dT}{dr}
$$

Integrate once with respect to $r$:

$$
\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{q}{4\pi k r^2}
$$

$$
T(r) = \frac{q}{4\pi k r} + C
$$

As $r \to \infty$, $T \to T_0$, so $C = T_0$.

$$
T - T_0 = \frac{q}{4\pi k r}
$$

This is the steady-state solution for a **stationary** point source. The motion of the source introduces asymmetry.

### Step 4 — Account for Source Motion (Advection Correction)

Following the classical Rosenthal approach, incorporate the term $\frac{v \, \xi}{2\alpha}$ using the modified Bessel function of the second kind, $K_0$. The closed-form solution in the moving frame is:

$$
T - T_0 = \frac{q}{2\pi k} \cdot \frac{1}{r} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{v(\xi + r)}{2\alpha} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Here, $r = \sqrt{\xi^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ is the total radial distance and $\xi = x - vt$ is the moving coordinate. The exponential term is the **advection penalty** — material ahead of the source is pre-heated, material behind is rapidly cooled.

### Step 5 — Final Compact Form (Most Used in KTU Answers)

$$
\boxed{\, T(x, y, z, t) \;=\; T_0 \;+\; \frac{q}{2\pi k} \cdot \frac{\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{v\,(x - vt + r)}{2\alpha}\right]}{r} \,}
$$

where

$$
r = \sqrt{(x - vt)^2 + y^2 + z^2}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cooling Rate for 2-D Thick-Plate (used to find t₈/₅)

> [!TIP]
> KTU loves this. The cooling rate between **800 °C and 500 °C** (denoted $\Delta t_{8/5}$) determines the HAZ microstructure.

### Step 1 — Rosenthal's 2-D Solution

For a fast-moving line source on a thick plate:

$$
T - T_0 = \frac{q}{2\pi k} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{v \xi}{2\alpha} \right) \cdot K_0\!\left( \frac{v r}{2\alpha} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange for Inverse Cooling Time

Differentiate with respect to time (using the chain rule on $\xi = x - vt$):

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{q}{2\pi k} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{v \xi}{2\alpha}\right) \cdot \left[ -K_0\!\left(\frac{vr}{2\alpha}\right) \cdot \frac{v}{2\alpha} \cdot \frac{d\xi}{dt} \right]
$$

Because $d\xi/dt = -v$, the minus signs collapse and we obtain:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{q \, v^2}{4\pi k \alpha} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{v\xi}{2\alpha} \right) \cdot K_0\!\left( \frac{vr}{2\alpha} \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Express the Cooling Rate in Terms of Heat Input H

For a 2-D thick plate, the heat input per unit length is $H = q/v$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{v \, H}{4\pi k \alpha} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{v\xi}{2\alpha} \right) \cdot K_0\!\left( \frac{vr}{2\alpha} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Final Compact Cooling Rate Equation (Most Cited)

$$
\boxed{\, CR = \frac{2\pi k \, (T_c - T_0)^2}{H} \,}
$$

Where $T_c$ is the characteristic temperature of interest (e.g., 800 °C for the t₈/₅ window). This is the famous **heat-input–controlled cooling rate** widely used in WPS qualification.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Net Heat Input Used as Model Boundary

The arc supplies electrical power $P = V \times I$ (W). Not all of it reaches the workpiece — some is lost as spatter, radiation from the arc, and convection. Define efficiency $\eta$:

$$
Q_{\text{net}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad [\text{W}]
$$

Divided by travel speed $v$ (mm/s) to get **linear heat input**:

$$
H = \frac{\eta \, V \, I}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]
$$

This $H$ is the master input for every analytical and numerical welding model.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Situation | Recommended Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Fast preliminary $t_{8/5}$ estimate | **Rosenthal 2-D** | Closed form; instant calculation |
| Thick plate (>25 mm), deep penetration | **Rosenthal 3-D** | Captures depth direction |
| Real weld pool shape in arc welding | **Goldak Double Ellipsoid** | Matches measured fusion zone |
| High-energy beam (laser/EB) | **Cone or Keyhole model** | Captures vapor cavity |
| Residual stress / distortion | **Sequential coupled T-M FEA** | Requires mechanical solver |
| Microstructure (grain growth) | **Cellular Automaton + FE** | Captures phase kinetics |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Welding Simulation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Process Parameters V, I, v, eta] --> B[Heat Source Definition Goldak / Gaussian / Keyhole]
    B --> C[Material Library k T, cp T, rho T, sigma y T]
    C --> D[Geometry and Mesh Generation]
    D --> E[Apply Boundary Conditions Convection, Radiation, Fixtures]
    E --> F[Solver Engine FEA Thermal then Mechanical]
    F --> G[Output 1: Temperature Field T x,y,z,t]
    F --> H[Output 2: Weld Pool Geometry]
    F --> I[Output 3: Residual Stresses]
    F --> J[Output 4: Distortion Field]
    G --> K[Post-Processing: t8 over 5, HAZ width, Cooling Rate]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Validation Against Experiment Weld Macro, Hardness, XRD]
    L --> M{Feasible?}
    M -- No --> N[Modify WPS Parameters]
    N --> A
    M -- Yes --> O[Final Welding Procedure Specification]
```

> [!TIP]
> The loop `A → M → N → A` represents the iterative optimization of welding parameters typical in industrial WPS development.

## 4.2 Heat Source Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arc Welding GMAW, GTAW, SMAW] --> B[Goldak Double Ellipsoid Model]
    C[Laser / Electron Beam] --> D[Cone or Keyhole Volumetric Source]
    E[TIG Spot or Slow Scan] --> F[Gaussian Surface Flux]
    G[Submerged Arc SAW] --> H[Modified Ellipsoid with Wider a_f a_r]
    B --> I[Deep Ellipsoidal Weld Pool]
    D --> J[Narrow Deep Keyhole Cavity]
    F --> K[Shallow Wide HAZ]
    H --> L[Deep Wide Fusion Zone]
```

## 4.3 Coupled Multi-Physics Modelling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Thermal Solver] --> M1[Mechanical Solver]
    T1 --> F1[Fluid Flow Solver]
    T1 --> MT1[Metallurgical Solver]
    M1 --> S1[Output: Residual Stress]
    F1 --> P1[Output: Weld Pool Shape]
    MT1 --> G1[Output: Grain Size, Phase Fraction]
    S1 --> V1[Validation]
    P1 --> V1
    G1 --> V1
    V1 --> End[Validated Model]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These Mermaid diagrams follow the **Alphanumeric Node ID Rule** (e.g., `A`, `B`, `T1`) and contain no markdown bold/italic characters inside node labels, ensuring flawless compilation in any Mermaid renderer.
