KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) in welding. Why is it considered critical in welded joints?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** is the region of the base metal adjacent to the weld fusion zone that has undergone microstructural and mechanical-property changes due to the thermal cycle of welding, without reaching the melting point. (1.5 marks) It is considered critical because: (1.5 marks) - The HAZ experiences peak temperatures between **Ac₁ (723 °C)** and the **solidus temperature**, causing **grain coarsening**, **phase transformations**, and **tensile residual stresses**. - The HAZ is typically the **weakest region** of the joint, being the preferred site for **crack initiation**, **hydrogen-induced cracking**, and **brittle fracture**. - Loss of toughness in the **Coarse Grain HAZ (CGHAZ)** can lead to catastrophic in-service failure of pressure vessels, pipelines, and structural components. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **List the four classical sub-zones of the HAZ in low-carbon steel, in order from the fusion boundary outward, with their peak temperature ranges.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** From the fusion boundary outward: 1. **Coarse Grain HAZ (CGHAZ):** $T_s$ down to ≈ 1100 °C — coarse austenite grains transform to coarse ferrite/pearlite on cooling. (0.75 marks) 2. **Fine Grain HAZ (FGHAZ):** 1100 °C down to $Ac_3$ (≈ 910 °C) — fully refined austenite grains form fine equiaxed ferrite + pearlite. (0.75 marks) 3. **Intercritical HAZ (ICHAZ):** $Ac_3$ down to $Ac_1$ (≈ 723 °C) — partial austenitization; ferrite + islands of M/A constituent or pearlite. (0.75 marks) 4. **Subcritical HAZ (SCHAZ):** Below $Ac_1$ down to ≈ 400 °C — no phase transformation; only tempering, aging, and strain-aging effects. (0.75 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing HAZ with the Heat-Affected Zone in **solid-state** processes like FSW, (2) forgetting the **units** in the heat-input formula, and (3) not correlating heat input to the **cooling time Δt₈/₅** which is the most asked parameter. Always state units, show the formula before substituting, and conclude with a **one-line engineering implication**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model] **(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanism of HAZ formation** in fusion welding processes, with a neat sketch of the temperature distribution and the corresponding HAZ sub-zones. Discuss how **heat input per unit length** influences the width and severity of the HAZ. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **HAZ characteristics** in (i) Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW), (ii) Submerged Arc Welding (SAW), and (iii) Laser Beam Welding (LBW). Which of these is most suitable for welding **hardenable low-alloy steels** and why? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Mechanism of HAZ Formation (7 marks):** When a moving heat source traverses a joint, every point in the base metal experiences a thermal cycle characterized by **rapid heating**, a brief **peak temperature dwell**, and **rapid cooling**. (1 mark) The peak temperature $T_{peak}$ experienced at a distance $x$ from the weld centerline is given by the **Rosenthal heat-flow solution**: $$T_{peak}(x) \approx \dfrac{E}{2\pi k \cdot x}$$ where $E$ is the heat input (J/mm), $k$ is thermal conductivity (W/m·K), and $x$ is the perpendicular distance. (1.5 marks) The HAZ is the annular region where the peak temperature lies between **Ac₁ (723 °C)** and the **solidus temperature**. Sub-zones from the fusion line outward: **CGHAZ, FGHAZ, ICHAZ, SCHAZ** (already described in §2.2). (2 marks) **Effect of Heat Input:** The HAZ width is directly proportional to heat input: $$W_{HAZ} \propto \dfrac{E}{k \cdot (T_{Ac_1} - T_0)}$$ Higher $E$ → wider HAZ, deeper CGHAZ, slower cooling → coarser grains → reduced toughness. (1.5 marks) **Sketch (1 mark):** A labelled temperature-vs-distance curve showing the four sub-zones — refer to §4.1 Mermaid diagram. **(b) Comparison (7 marks):** | Parameter | SMAW | SAW | LBW | |---|---|---|---| | Heat input | 0.5–1.5 kJ/mm | 1.5–5.0 kJ/mm | 0.02–0.2 kJ/mm | | HAZ width | 2–5 mm | 4–10 mm | < 0.5 mm | | Cooling rate | Moderate (20–60 °C/s) | Slow (5–20 °C/s) | Very fast (10³–10⁴ °C/s) | | CGHAZ severity | Moderate | Very high | Negligible | | Typical defects | HIC, porosity | Reheat cracking, lamellar tearing | Cold cracking, porosity | | Suitability for hardenable steels | Poor unless preheated | Unsuitable | **Excellent** | **Best choice for hardenable low-alloy steels: LBW (Laser Beam Welding).** (1 mark reasoning) - Low heat input → narrow HAZ → minimal CGHAZ → low risk of martensite formation. - Rapid cooling still occurs, so preheat and post-heat strategies are needed to avoid cold cracking. - SAW is **unsuitable** because high heat input produces excessive grain coarsening and brittle martensitic regions. - SMAW is acceptable only with strict preheat (200–300 °C) and low-hydrogen consumables. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **(a)** With the help of a **schematic of the welding thermal cycle**, derive the relationship between **heat input $E$**, **thermal conductivity $k$**, and the **HAZ width $W_{HAZ}$**. Discuss how preheating affects the HAZ. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain why **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** does not produce a classical HAZ. Describe the **zone architecture** of an FSW joint and state two industrial applications where FSW is preferred over fusion welding. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Thermal Cycle & HAZ Width Derivation (7 marks):** The **welding thermal cycle** has three phases — heating, peak dwell, and cooling — represented graphically as temperature vs. time. (1 mark) For a **2-D Rosenthal thick-plate** model, the peak temperature at a stationary point at distance $r$ from a moving line source is: $$T_{peak}(r) = T_0 + \dfrac{E / v}{2\pi k \cdot r} \quad \text{(simplified for steady state)}$$ Setting $T_{peak} = T_{Ac_1}$ at the outer HAZ boundary $r = W_{HAZ}/2$: (1.5 marks) $$\dfrac{W_{HAZ}}{2} = \dfrac{E}{2\pi k \cdot (T_{Ac_1} - T_0)}$$ $$\boxed{W_{HAZ} = \dfrac{E}{\pi k \cdot (T_{Ac_1} - T_0)}}$$ This confirms: **HAZ width is directly proportional to heat input $E$** and **inversely proportional to thermal conductivity $k$**. (1.5 marks) **Effect of Preheating:** Increasing preheat $T_0$ to $T_0'$ reduces $(T_{Ac_1} - T_0)$, which **increases** $W_{HAZ}$ slightly but **dramatically reduces** the cooling rate, expressed via the $\Delta t_{8/5}$ parameter: $$\Delta t_{8/5} \propto \dfrac{1}{(T_c - T_0)}$$ This gives coarser, softer microstructures — less martensite, less residual stress, less HIC risk. **Trade-off:** Slight increase in HAZ width is acceptable for major reduction in cracking susceptibility. (2 marks) **Schematic (1 mark):** Refer to §4.2 Mermaid thermal cycle diagram. **(b) Friction Stir Welding and Non-Classical HAZ (7 marks):** In FSW, a **rotating non-consumable tool** (with shoulder and pin) plunges into the joint and traverses along the seam. Heat is generated by **friction** at the tool–workpiece interface and by **plastic deformation** of the material. (1.5 marks) **Key reasons FSW does not produce a classical HAZ:** - The peak temperature is **below the solidus** (typically 0.6 – 0.9 $T_m$, ≈ 400–500 °C for aluminum alloys). (1 mark) - No melting occurs, so there is **no fusion boundary** and **no liquid-to-solid transformation**. (1 mark) **Zone Architecture of FSW joint (2.5 marks):** | Zone | Description | |---|---| | **Stir Zone (SZ) / Nugget** | Fully recrystallized fine equiaxed grains due to intense plastic deformation and frictional heat. | | **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ)** | Deformed grains, partially recrystallized; transition between SZ and base metal. | | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** | Only thermal effects (no deformation); tempered or aged structure. | | **Base Metal (BM)** | Unaffected. | **Two Industrial Applications (1 mark):** 1. **Aerospace fuselage and wing structures** of aluminum alloys (AA2024, AA7075) — FSW avoids solidification defects and produces joints with **superior fatigue life**. 2. **Marine and shipbuilding** — aluminum hull panels, decks, and superstructures — FSW eliminates distortion and is **Friction Stir Welded in lengths up to 30 m**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):** - ✅ **HAZ Definition:** Region of base metal that experiences peak temperature between **Ac₁ (723 °C)** and **solidus (1490 °C for steel)**, without melting. - ✅ **Four HAZ Sub-Zones (inward to outward):** **CGHAZ → FGHAZ → ICHAZ → SCHAZ**. - ✅ **CGHAZ is the weakest, most crack-prone zone** — coarse grains, low toughness. - ✅ **Heat Input Formula:** $E = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \times 1000}$ **kJ/mm** (always include units!). - ✅ **Arc efficiency (η) values:** SMAW ≈ 0.8, GTAW ≈ 0.6, GMAW ≈ 0.8, SAW ≈ 1.0, PAW ≈ 0.5, LBW ≈ 0.4, EBW ≈ 0.85. - ✅ **HAZ width $W_{HAZ} \propto E/k$** — HAZ grows with heat input, shrinks with thermal conductivity. - ✅ **Cooling time $\Delta t_{8/5}$** is the **most important KTU parameter** for correlating welding conditions with HAZ microstructure. - ✅ **Process ranking by HAZ severity (worst → best):** OAW > SAW > SMAW > GMAW > GTAW > PAW > LBW > EBW. - ✅ **FSW = Solid-state process = No fusion zone, no classical HAZ.** It produces **Stir Zone + TMAZ + HAZ + Base Metal**. - ✅ **Preheating** increases HAZ width slightly but **dramatically reduces cooling rate** → reduces HIC and martensite formation. - ✅ **High-energy-density processes (LBW, EBW)** are preferred for **hardenable alloys, stainless steels, and aerospace/nuclear applications** due to minimal HAZ. - ✅ **HAZ is the #1 site for in-service weld failures** — always specify PWHT when HAZ toughness is critical. - ✅ **Common HAZ defects:** hydrogen-induced cracking, reheat cracking, lamellar tearing, temper embrittlement, strain aging.

topics.title: Welding Distortions and Residual Stresses in Different Welding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET523 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Welding Distortions and Residual Stresses in Different Welding Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Welding Distortion — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding Distortion** is the **unwanted, irreversible geometric deformation** (change in shape and/or dimensions) of a welded component that occurs as a direct consequence of the **non-uniform, localized thermal cycle** introduced by the welding process. It manifests after the welded joint has cooled to ambient temperature and is a cumulative result of **transient metallurgical expansion, contraction, phase transformations, and restraint effects** acting simultaneously on the parent material and the weld metal.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, welding distortion is broadly classified as:

1. **In-plane distortion** (occurs within the plane of the plate) — Includes longitudinal shrinkage, transverse shrinkage, and in-plane rotational/angular movement.
2. **Out-of-plane distortion** (occurs perpendicular to the plate surface) — Includes longitudinal bending, transverse bending, buckling, and twisting.

### 1.2 Welding Residual Stress — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding Residual Stress** (also called **internal stress** or **locked-in stress**) is the **self-equilibrating, time-independent stress field** that remains in a welded structure **after the welding heat source has been removed and the joint has cooled to room temperature**, with no external load applied. It is generated by **non-uniform plastic deformation, phase transformations, and differential thermal contraction** during the weld thermal cycle.

The residual stress field must satisfy the **macroscopic static equilibrium condition**:

$$\int_{A} \sigma_{res}(x, y, z) \, dA = 0$$

This means the **tensile residual stresses** in and near the weld zone are **balanced by compressive residual stresses** in the surrounding parent material.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The "Sun-Baked Wooden Plank" Model**
> Imagine a long wooden plank that has been exposed to the sun on one side only. As that side dries, it shrinks, while the shaded side retains its original length. The plank is forced to **bow (curve) toward the sun-dried side** because of the differential shrinkage. Welding works on a similar principle — the weld bead and the Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) heat up and then try to shrink, but the surrounding cooler parent material **restrains** this shrinkage, forcing the structure to **distort and lock in internal stresses**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The "Stretched Rubber Band on a Hot Day" Model**
> If you stretch a rubber band and heat it, it tries to contract but cannot because it is constrained. When you release the constraint after cooling, the rubber band does not return to its original state — it holds a "memory" of the deformation. Similarly, weld metal, when plastically deformed at high temperature and then restrained during cooling, **locks in residual stresses** that remain even after the external forces are removed.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (Bolded for Recall)

- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (α) for mild steel ≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**
- **Melting point of mild steel ≈ 1500 °C**
- **Preheating temperature range (low-carbon steel) ≈ 100–250 °C**
- **Interpass temperature (low-carbon steel) ≤ 250 °C**
- **Yield strength at room temperature (mild steel) ≈ 250 MPa**
- **Modulus of Elasticity (E for steel) ≈ 200 GPa**
- **Volumetric shrinkage of weld metal on solidification ≈ 3–8 %**
- **Linear shrinkage of weld metal on cooling ≈ 0.5–2 %**

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight**
> Module 4 places **heavy emphasis (typically 12–14 marks weightage in End Semester Examinations)** on welding distortions and residual stresses because these phenomena **directly govern dimensional accuracy, fatigue life, fracture toughness, and structural integrity** of welded assemblies across automotive, aerospace, pressure-vessel, shipbuilding, and heavy-structural industries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Fundamental Causes of Welding Distortion and Residual Stresses

The root cause is the **localized, transient, high-intensity heat input** from the welding arc/laser/electron beam/gas flame. The mechanisms are:

1. **Non-uniform thermal expansion and contraction**
   - The weld pool expands during heating → causes **compressive plastic strain** in the HAZ.
   - On cooling, the weld metal and HAZ try to contract → if unrestrained, free shrinkage occurs; if restrained, **tensile residual stresses** develop.

2. **Solidification shrinkage of the weld pool**
   - Molten metal contracts **3–8 % by volume** as it solidifies.
   - This volumetric contraction pulls the adjacent base metal inward.

3. **Phase transformations (especially in steels)**
   - The **γ → α (austenite to ferrite) transformation** on cooling is accompanied by a **volumetric expansion** (≈ 1 %) that counteracts thermal contraction in some temperature ranges.

4. **Restraint from the surrounding base metal**
   - The cool, rigid parent material prevents the hot, soft weld zone from freely deforming, generating **internal stresses**.

### 2.2 Detailed Classification of Welding Distortions

#### (A) Longitudinal Shrinkage (along the weld axis)
- Caused by **longitudinal contraction** of the heated weld bead as it cools.
- Magnitude depends on weld length, heat input, and plate stiffness.
- For a butt joint: $\Delta L \approx \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L_w / 2$ (approximate, with restraint factor)

#### (B) Transverse Shrinkage (perpendicular to weld axis)
- Caused by **lateral contraction** of the weld metal across the joint line.
- **Most significant distortion mode in butt welds**.
- Drives the plates to "pull in" toward the weld centerline.

#### (C) Angular Distortion (Rotation around weld axis)
- Typical in **single-V and single-bevel groove butt welds**.
- The thicker the plate and the deeper the groove, the greater the angular distortion.
- Caused by **asymmetric heat distribution** through the plate thickness — the top surface expands more than the bottom.

#### (D) Longitudinal Bending Distortion
- Occurs in **long, slender welded plates or beams** welded along the longitudinal axis.
- The shrinkage force of the weld causes the plate to **bow in the longitudinal direction**.
- Critical buckling parameter: **Plate slenderness ratio (L/h)**.

#### (E) Transverse Bending Distortion (Buckling)
- Occurs in **thin plates (h < 6 mm)** welded along the shorter edge.
- Transverse shrinkage force exceeds the **critical buckling load** of the plate.
- The plate **ripples or buckles** out-of-plane.

#### (F) Rotational (Twisting) Distortion
- Common in **T-joints, cruciform joints, and box sections**.
- Caused by **uneven heat distribution** around the joint cross-section.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table — pipe-safe)

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Where Used** | **Notes for KTU Exam** |
|-------|---------------|-------------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Transverse Shrinkage (uniform heat input) | $\Delta w \approx \dfrac{A_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot c}$ | Butt joints, arc welding | $A_w$ = weld cross-section, $h$ = plate thickness, $c$ = restraint factor |
| 2 | Transverse Shrinkage (simple form) | $\Delta w \approx \dfrac{A_w}{h} \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Single-pass butt weld | Free shrinkage assumption |
| 3 | Angular Distortion (single-V groove) | $\theta \approx \dfrac{c_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{4h}$ | Single-V butt weld | $c_w$ = top width of weld bead |
| 4 | Longitudinal Shrinkage | $\Delta L \approx \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot A_w \cdot L_w}{2 \cdot A_p}$ | Long butt welds | $L_w$ = weld length, $A_p$ = parent plate cross-section |
| 5 | Critical Buckling Stress (thin plate) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{b}\right)^2$ | Buckling analysis | $k$ = buckling coefficient, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 6 | Heat Input (arc welding) | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | All arc processes | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.6–0.85 for GMAW) |
| 7 | Volumetric Solidification Shrinkage | $\Delta V \approx (3 \text{ to } 8)\%$ | Foundry/weld pool | Material-dependent |
| 8 | Residual Stress Equilibrium | $\int_A \sigma_{res} \, dA = 0$ | All welded joints | Tensile = Compressive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the table, the pipe character `|` is used ONLY for table column separation. For mathematical absolute-value notation in your answer script, always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Industry** | **Distortion/Stress Concern** | **Typical Mitigation** |
|--------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Shipbuilding** | Panel buckling in thin hull plates | Backstep welding, strongback fixtures |
| **Pressure Vessels** | Dimensional out-of-tolerance, fatigue crack initiation | PWHT, balanced welding sequence |
| **Aerospace (Ti alloys)** | Severe angular distortion in electron-beam welds | Constraint fixtures, hammer peening |
| **Automotive (Resistance spot welding)** | Electrode indentation, sheet buckling | Optimized electrode force, welding current |
| **Pipeline (SAW)** | Longitudinal shrinkage causing misalignment | Multi-arc SAW, post-weld cooling control |
| **Bridges / Structural steel** | Cumulative distortion in long welds | Proper joint design, symmetric welding sequence |

### 2.5 Effect of Different Welding Methods on Distortion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparative Distortion Tendency (Low to High):**
> **Electron Beam Welding < Laser Beam Welding < TIG (GTAW) < Plasma < MIG/GMAW < SMAW < SAW (single-arc) < Flux-cored Arc Welding < Oxy-fuel Gas Welding**
>
> This ordering correlates with **heat input density**: high-power-density, low-total-heat-input processes (EBW, LBW) produce **narrow, deep, low-distortion welds**, while low-power-density, high-total-heat-input processes (oxy-fuel) produce **wide, shallow, high-distortion welds**.

| **Welding Method** | **Heat Input (kJ/mm)** | **Distortion Severity** | **Residual Stress Severity** |
|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Electron Beam (EBW) | 0.1 – 1.0 | Very Low | Low–Medium (narrow HAZ) |
| Laser Beam (LBW) | 0.1 – 2.0 | Very Low | Low–Medium |
| Gas Tungsten Arc (GTAW/TIG) | 0.5 – 2.0 | Low | Low–Medium |
| Gas Metal Arc (GMAW/MIG) | 0.8 – 3.0 | Medium | Medium |
| Shielded Metal Arc (SMAW) | 1.0 – 4.0 | Medium–High | High |
| Submerged Arc (SAW) | 1.5 – 6.0 | High | High |
| Oxy-fuel Gas Welding | 3.0 – 10.0 | Very High | High |
| Resistance Spot Welding | Concentrated at nugget | Localized indentation | High at spot, low globally |
| Friction Stir Welding (FSW) | Mechanical (no fusion) | Very Low | Lowest of all fusion processes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transverse Shrinkage in a Single-Pass Butt Weld

#### Conceptual Setup
Consider two plates of thickness $h$ and width $b$ joined by a single-pass butt weld. The weld bead has a cross-sectional area $A_w$ and a width at the surface of $c_w$. The plate is assumed to be **rigidly clamped** at its far ends (full restraint condition).

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the free thermal strain**
The weld metal, when heated from room temperature $T_0$ to the peak temperature $T_p$, would freely expand by the linear thermal strain $\varepsilon_{th}$:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot (T_p - T_0) = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

This is the **free (unrestrained) thermal strain** the weld would exhibit if it could expand without any constraint.

**Step 2 — Recognize the constraint condition**
Because the surrounding parent metal is **rigid at room temperature**, the weld metal is prevented from expanding freely during heating. The constraint introduces a **compressive plastic strain** $\varepsilon_{pl}$ in the weld zone, which is the difference between the free thermal strain and the actual strain:

$$\varepsilon_{pl} = \varepsilon_{th} - \varepsilon_{el}$$

For a fully plastic (rigid-plastic) assumption, the elastic strain is small compared to the plastic strain, so:

$$\varepsilon_{pl} \approx \varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Relate plastic strain to volumetric shrinkage**
On cooling, the weld metal **contracts** by the same plastic strain amount. This plastic contraction is permanent. The **linear transverse shrinkage** $\Delta w$ of the weld cross-section is:

$$\Delta w = \varepsilon_{pl} \cdot c_w = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot c_w$$

**Step 4 — Convert to global transverse shrinkage using compatibility**
The transverse shrinkage of the entire plate assembly (two plates pulling inward) is governed by the conservation of the total transverse dimension. The weld cross-sectional area $A_w$ is distributed over the plate thickness $h$, so the **average transverse shrinkage per unit thickness** is:

$$\Delta w = \frac{A_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot c}$$

where $c$ is a **restraint factor** that ranges from 0.5 (free shrinkage) to 1.0 (fully restrained). For a single-pass butt weld, a typical value of $c \approx 0.5$ is used in practice.

**Step 5 — Final compact form**

$$\boxed{\Delta w \approx \frac{A_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot c}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example for KTU-style problem**
Given: $A_w = 50 \text{ mm}^2$, $h = 10 \text{ mm}$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $\Delta T = 1000 °C$, $c = 0.5$:

$$\Delta w = \frac{50 \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1000}{10 \times 0.5} \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta w = \frac{50 \times 0.012}{5.0} = \frac{0.6}{5.0} = 0.12 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore, the **transverse shrinkage is 0.12 mm** per side (i.e., 0.24 mm total reduction in plate width at the joint).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Angular Distortion in a Single-V Groove Butt Weld

#### Conceptual Setup
A single-V groove butt weld has an **asymmetric cross-section** — the top of the weld is wider than the bottom. This asymmetry causes **non-uniform shrinkage through the plate thickness**, leading to a rotation of the plate edges toward each other.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define geometry of the single-V groove**
Let the plate thickness be $h$, the top width of the weld bead be $c_w$, the root gap be $g$, and the included groove angle be $2\phi$ (so the half-angle is $\phi$).

**Step 2 — Top and bottom widths of the weld bead**
The width of the weld at the top surface (after groove filling) is $c_w$ and at the bottom is $c_b$ (root width). For a single-V groove:

$$c_w = g + 2h \tan(\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Differential shrinkage between top and bottom**
The top of the weld shrinks more than the bottom because more weld metal is deposited there (larger cross-section). Let $\Delta w_{top}$ and $\Delta w_{bottom}$ be the respective transverse shrinkages:

$$\Delta w_{top} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot c_w$$

$$\Delta w_{bottom} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot c_b$$

**Step 4 — Angular distortion as a function of differential shrinkage**
The angular distortion $\theta$ (in radians) can be expressed as the difference in shrinkage divided by the plate thickness (small-angle approximation):

$$\theta = \frac{\Delta w_{top} - \Delta w_{bottom}}{h}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expressions**
For the case where the bottom width is approximately equal to the root gap $g$ (which is much smaller than $c_w$ for a typical V-groove), we have $c_b \approx g \approx 0$ relative to $c_w$:

$$\theta \approx \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot c_w}{h}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the restraint factor and symmetric plate consideration**
Since the angular distortion is shared by the two plates (each rotating by $\theta/2$), and the empirical restraint factor $c \approx 4$ applies to V-groove welds (from experimental data by Tall and others):

$$\boxed{\theta \approx \frac{c_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{4h}}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example**
Given: $c_w = 20 \text{ mm}$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $\Delta T = 800 °C$, $h = 12 \text{ mm}$:

$$\theta = \frac{20 \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 800}{4 \times 12}$$

$$\theta = \frac{20 \times 0.0096}{48} = \frac{0.192}{48} = 0.004 \text{ rad}$$

Converting to degrees: $\theta = 0.004 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.229°$ per plate side.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Buckling Stress in a Thin Plate (Out-of-Plane Distortion)

#### Conceptual Setup
For a thin rectangular plate of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $h$ welded along one edge, the **transverse shrinkage force** $F$ per unit length must be compared to the **critical buckling load** $N_{cr}$ of the plate.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Transverse shrinkage force per unit length**
The shrinkage stress $\sigma_s$ in the weld zone, for full restraint, approximates the yield strength $\sigma_y$ at room temperature:

$$\sigma_s \approx \sigma_y$$

The shrinkage force per unit length of weld is:

$$F = \sigma_y \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Critical buckling load of the plate**
For a long, simply-supported plate (length $L \gg b$), the classical plate-buckling formula (Timoshenko) gives:

$$N_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot \pi^2 \cdot D}{b^2}$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity per unit width** of the plate:

$$D = \frac{E \cdot h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

and $k$ is the **buckling coefficient** ($k = 4$ for a long simply-supported plate under uniform compression).

**Step 3 — Substitute $D$ and $k$**
$$N_{cr} = \frac{4 \pi^2}{b^2} \cdot \frac{E \cdot h^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

**Step 4 — Critical buckling stress**
Dividing $N_{cr}$ by the plate thickness $h$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{N_{cr}}{h} = \frac{4 \pi^2 \cdot E \cdot h^2}{12 \cdot b^2 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{3(1 - \nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{b}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Buckling criterion**
**Buckling occurs if**: $F > N_{cr}$, i.e., if:

$$\sigma_y \cdot h > \frac{4 \pi^2 \cdot E \cdot h^3}{12 \cdot b^2 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example**
Given: $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, $h = 4 \text{ mm}$, $b = 1000 \text{ mm}$, $\sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 200 \times 10^3}{3(1 - 0.3^2)} \times \left(\frac{4}{1000}\right)^2 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{9.87 \times 200 \times 10^3}{3 \times 0.91} \times 16 \times 10^{-6} \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{1.974 \times 10^6}{2.73} \times 16 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 7.23 \times 10^5 \times 16 \times 10^{-6} = 11.57 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa} \gg \sigma_{cr} = 11.57 \text{ MPa}$, **buckling will definitely occur** in this thin plate.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Distortion vs. Welding Method (Synthesis Table)

| **Welding Method** | **Heat Input Mode** | **Dominant Distortion Type** | **Dominant Residual Stress** | **Mitigation Strategy** |
|--------------------|---------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc)** | Periodic, low-power arc | Transverse + Angular | Tensile in weld, compressive in HAZ | Backstep welding, symmetric sequence |
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | Continuous, medium-power | Transverse shrinkage | Tensile longitudinal + transverse | Staggered welding, fixtures |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | Continuous, concentrated | Minimal — narrow bead | Low to medium | Good for thin sections |
| **SAW (Submerged Arc)** | High heat input, single or multi-arc | Severe transverse + angular | High tensile longitudinal | Multi-arc SAW, balanced sequence |
| **PAW (Plasma Arc)** | Keyhole or melt-in mode | Low to moderate | Concentrated, narrow HAZ | Suitable for thin, critical joints |
| **EBW (Electron Beam)** | High power density, vacuum | Very low distortion | Low residual stress | Used for aerospace Ti alloys |
| **LBW (Laser Beam)** | High power density, autogenous | Very low distortion | Narrow tensile zone | High-speed welding reduces total heat |
| **Oxy-fuel Gas Welding** | Broad, low-intensity flame | Severe — all types | High residual stress | Rarely used for precision structures |
| **Resistance Spot Welding** | Localized nugget, fast cycle | Indentation + sheet warping | High local tensile at nugget | Optimized electrode geometry |
| **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Solid-state, no fusion | Minimal distortion | Lowest of all | Ideal for Al, Mg alloys |
| **Stud / Projection Welding** | Localized resistance heating | Local depression | Localized tensile | Insulated ferrule reduces oxidation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Types of Welding Distortion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["WELDING DISTORTION CLASSIFICATION"] --> B1["IN PLANE DISTORTION"]
    A0 --> B2["OUT OF PLANE DISTORTION"]
    B1 --> C1["LONGITUDINAL SHRINKAGE"]
    B1 --> C2["TRANSVERSE SHRINKAGE"]
    B1 --> C3["ANGULAR OR ROTATIONAL DISTORTION"]
    B2 --> C4["LONGITUDINAL BENDING"]
    B2 --> C5["TRANSVERSE BENDING OR BUCKLING"]
    B2 --> C6["TWIST OR CURLING DISTORTION"]
    C1 --> D1["ALONG WELD AXIS CONTRACTION"]
    C2 --> D2["PERPENDICULAR TO WELD AXIS CONTRACTION"]
    C3 --> D3["TYPICAL IN SINGLE V GROOVE BUTT WELDS"]

```


### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Distortion Control

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks):** Define welding distortion. List any **four** different types of welding distortion with a one-line description each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Welding distortion is the **unwanted geometric deformation** of a welded component caused by **non-uniform heating and cooling** during the welding process, resulting in a permanent change in shape or dimensions after the joint has cooled to room temperature.

**Four Types (½ Mark each):**

1. **Longitudinal Shrinkage** — Contraction of the weld bead along the weld axis.
2. **Transverse Shrinkage** — Contraction of the weld metal in the direction perpendicular to the weld axis.
3. **Angular Distortion** — Rotation of the plate edges about the weld axis, typical of single-V groove butt welds.
4. **Buckling Distortion** — Out-of-plane rippling of thin plates due to compressive buckling under transverse shrinkage forces.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks):** Differentiate between **welding residual stress** and **applied stress**. State **two causes** of residual stress formation.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Differentiation (2 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Welding Residual Stress** | **Applied Stress** |
|------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| **Origin** | Internal; due to non-uniform thermal cycle of welding | External; due to service loads |
| **Existence** | Present even without external load | Requires external loading |
| **Equilibrium** | Self-equilibrating (tensile = compressive) | May or may not be self-equilibrating |
| **Removal** | Can be reduced by PWHT or peening | Removed when load is removed |

**Two Causes (½ Mark each):**
1. **Non-uniform thermal expansion and contraction** during the weld thermal cycle, with restraint by the surrounding cooler parent metal.
2. **Solidification shrinkage and phase transformations** (γ → α) in the weld metal and HAZ.

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **mechanism of formation of welding residual stresses** with the help of a thermal cycle and stress-strain diagram. Discuss the role of **restraint** in the development of these stresses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-V groove butt weld is made in a mild steel plate of thickness $h = 12$ mm. The weld bead top width is $c_w = 18$ mm. Take $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ and $\Delta T = 900 °C$. Calculate the **angular distortion** in degrees. Also, comment on the factors that affect angular distortion.

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

##### Part (a) — Mechanism of Residual Stress Formation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Heating Stage (1.5 Marks):**
When the welding arc strikes the plate, the localized region around the weld pool is heated rapidly from room temperature $T_0$ to a peak temperature $T_p$ that may exceed the melting point. The heated zone tries to expand thermally. If the region were unconstrained, it would expand freely by a strain:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot (T_p - T_0)$$

However, the surrounding cooler parent metal is rigid and restrains this expansion. The weld zone is forced into **compressive plastic deformation** because the compressive stress exceeds the yield strength at high temperature (which is much lower than at room temperature).

**Step 2 — Thermal Cycle and Stress Reversal (1.5 Marks):**
As the heat source moves away, the weld zone cools. Below a critical temperature (around 600–700 °C for low-carbon steel), the metal regains its room-temperature yield strength. The weld metal now tries to contract, but the surrounding material (now also cooling) restrains this contraction. A **tensile stress** develops in the weld zone.

**Step 3 — Stress-Strain Curve (2 Marks):**
The complete thermo-mechanical cycle can be represented on a stress-strain-temperature plot:

- **Stage A→B:** Free thermal expansion (if unrestrained).
- **Stage B→C:** Compressive plastic strain accumulates at high temperature under restraint.
- **Stage C→D:** Stress reversal upon cooling — stress becomes tensile.
- **Stage D→E:** Tensile stress rises until it reaches the **room-temperature yield strength**.
- **Stage E→F:** Final residual stress locked in at room temperature (tensile in weld, compressive in parent metal).

**Step 4 — Role of Restraint (2 Marks):**
Restraint is the **external or internal resistance** to the free movement of the welded component during heating and cooling. Two types exist:
- **External Restraint:** Provided by clamping fixtures, the stiffness of the surrounding structure, or tack welds.
- **Internal Restraint:** Provided by the parent material itself (mass and stiffness of the unmelted region).

**Higher restraint → higher residual stress** (often approaching the yield strength) and **lower free distortion** (because the strain is locked in as stress rather than released as shape change). Conversely, **low restraint → lower residual stress but higher distortion**. This inverse relationship is the **fundamental trade-off** in weld design.

---

##### Part (b) — Angular Distortion Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $h = 12$ mm
- $c_w = 18$ mm
- $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- $\Delta T = 900 °C$

**Formula (1 Mark):**
$$\theta \approx \frac{c_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{4h}$$

**Substitution (2 Marks):**
$$\theta = \frac{18 \times (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 900}{4 \times 12} \text{ rad}$$

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\theta = \frac{18 \times 0.0108}{48} = \frac{0.1944}{48} = 0.00405 \text{ rad}$$

**Conversion to degrees (1 Mark):**
$$\theta = 0.00405 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.00405 \times 57.296 = 0.232° \text{ (per plate side)}$$

**Total angular distortion (both plates combined):** $\approx 0.464°$

**Factors affecting angular distortion (1 Mark):**
1. Groove geometry (deeper V-groove → greater angular distortion).
2. Plate thickness (thicker plate → smaller angular distortion, higher restraint).
3. Heat input (higher heat input → greater angular distortion).
4. Welding sequence and restraint conditions.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks):**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **types of welding distortions** in (i) butt welds and (ii) fillet welds. Discuss the **causes and control measures** for each type.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **distortion tendency and residual stress severity** of the following welding processes: (i) Electron Beam Welding, (ii) Gas Metal Arc Welding, (iii) Submerged Arc Welding, and (iv) Oxy-fuel Gas Welding. Justify the ranking with reference to **heat input density, weld bead geometry, and HAZ size**.

---

#### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

##### Part (a) — Types of Welding Distortions (7 Marks)

**(i) Butt Welds (3.5 Marks):**

| **Distortion Type** | **Description** | **Cause** | **Control Measure** |
|---------------------|-----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Transverse Shrinkage | Plates pull in toward weld centerline | Lateral contraction of weld metal | Proper root gap, balanced sequencing |
| Angular Distortion | Plates rotate about weld axis (V-groove) | Asymmetric shrinkage through thickness | Double-V groove, backgouging, low heat input |
| Longitudinal Shrinkage | Plate shortens along weld axis | Axial contraction of weld bead | Staggered welding, preset compensation |
| Buckling (thin plates) | Out-of-plane rippling | Transverse force exceeds critical buckling load | Stiffener fixtures, backstep technique |

**(ii) Fillet Welds (3.5 Marks):**

| **Distortion Type** | **Description** | **Cause** | **Control Measure** |
|---------------------|-----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Fillet Weld Rotation | Fillet rotates about its root | Asymmetric shrinkage of fillet legs | Equal-leg fillet design, balanced welding |
| Longitudinal Bending | Long welded beam bows along its length | Weld shrinkage force exceeds beam stiffness | Intermittent welding, preset camber |
| Twist Distortion | Plate twists about longitudinal axis | Uneven heat distribution in T-joint | Symmetric welding sequence, balanced heat input |
| Transverse Bending | Plate bows perpendicular to weld | Shrinkage force perpendicular to plate | Tacking sequence, strongback fixtures |

---

##### Part (b) — Comparison of Welding Processes (7 Marks)

**Ranking (from lowest to highest distortion tendency):**

$$\text{EBW} < \text{GMAW} < \text{SAW} < \text{Oxy-fuel Gas Welding}$$

**Justification (1.5 Marks per process):**

1. **Electron Beam Welding (EBW) — Lowest Distortion (1.5 Marks):**
   - Extremely high **power density** ($10^6$–$10^9 \text{ W/cm}^2$) → narrow, deep weld with very small HAZ.
   - Very low **total heat input per unit length** → minimal overall thermal cycle.
   - Done in **vacuum** → no atmospheric effects, precise control.
   - Result: **Minimal distortion, low-to-moderate residual stress** (concentrated, not distributed).

2. **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) — Moderate Distortion (1.5 Marks):**
   - Medium **power density** → moderate bead width and HAZ.
   - Continuous wire feed → uniform heat input, easy automation.
   - Higher deposition rate than GTAW → faster welding → lower total heat input per joint.
   - Result: **Moderate transverse and longitudinal distortion, medium residual stress**.

3. **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) — High Distortion (1.5 Marks):**
   - High **heat input** (1.5–6.0 kJ/mm) → large molten pool, wide HAZ.
   - Deep penetration → larger weld cross-section → more total shrinkage.
   - High deposition rates reduce travel time, but **total heat introduced is high**.
   - Result: **Severe transverse and angular distortion, high residual stress**.

4. **Oxy-fuel Gas Welding — Highest Distortion (1.5 Marks):**
   - Very **low power density** (broad flame) → wide, shallow weld pool.
   - Slow travel speed → **very high total heat input per unit length** (3–10 kJ/mm).
   - No concentration of heat → wide HAZ → large volumetric shrinkage.
   - Result: **Severe distortion of all types (longitudinal, transverse, angular) and high residual stress**.

**Summary Table (1 Mark):**

| **Process** | **Power Density** | **HAZ Size** | **Distortion** | **Residual Stress** |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------|----------------|----------------------|
| EBW | Very High | Very Small | Very Low | Low–Medium |
| GMAW | Medium | Medium | Moderate | Medium |
| SAW | High | Large | High | High |
| Oxy-fuel | Low | Very Large | Very High | High |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Never confuse "residual stress" with "distortion"** — they are inversely related. High restraint = high residual stress, low distortion. Low restraint = low residual stress, high distortion. KTU examiners frequently test this trade-off.
> 2. **Units consistency** is critical: $\alpha$ must be in /°C, $\Delta T$ in °C, dimensions in mm, and the result in mm or rad as appropriate. Mixing SI and CGS units is a **favourite 1-mark deduction** by KTU evaluators.
> 3. **Pipe character `|`** in mathematical expressions breaks markdown tables — always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in answer scripts.
> 4. **Failure to convert radians to degrees** (or vice versa) when the question asks for angular distortion is a recurring 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Omitting the restraint factor** in transverse shrinkage derivations is a common error — always state the assumption (free vs. fully restrained).

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **🔑 KTU Module 4 — Welding Distortions & Residual Stresses Quick Recall**

- [ ] **Welding Distortion** = permanent geometric change; **Residual Stress** = locked-in internal stress field.
- [ ] **Seven main types of distortion:** longitudinal shrinkage, transverse shrinkage, angular distortion, longitudinal bending, transverse bending (buckling), twist, and rotational.
- [ ] **Residual stress is self-equilibrating** — tensile in weld zone balanced by compressive in parent metal.
- [ ] **Cause of residual stress:** non-uniform heating + restraint + solidification shrinkage + phase transformations.
- [ ] **Key formula for transverse shrinkage:** $\Delta w = \dfrac{A_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot c}$
- [ ] **Key formula for angular distortion (single-V):** $\theta \approx \dfrac{c_w \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{4h}$
- [ ] **Critical buckling stress for thin plate:** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{3(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{b}\right)^2$
- [ ] **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$
- [ ] **Trade-off:** Higher restraint → higher residual stress but **lower** distortion; lower restraint → **higher** distortion but lower residual stress.
- [ ] **Distortion severity ranking (low → high):** EBW < LBW < GTAW < GMAW < SMAW < SAW < Oxy-fuel.
- [ ] **Buckling is critical** when $h < 6$ mm in thin plates — stiffeners/strongbacks needed.
- [ ] **Mitigation techniques:** backstep welding, balanced sequences, preheating, PWHT, peening, mechanical straightening, preset compensation, restraint fixtures.
- [ ] **Phase transformation in steel:** γ → α on cooling produces ~1% volumetric expansion, partially counteracting thermal contraction.
- [ ] **Solidification shrinkage of weld metal:** 3–8% volumetric, contributing to both distortion and residual stress.
- [ ] **α (carbon steel) ≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** — remember this constant for numerical problems.
- [ ] **Equilibrium condition:** $\int_A \sigma_{res} \, dA = 0$ — always mention this in residual stress definitions for full KTU marks.
- [ ] **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** is a **solid-state** process — no fusion, no solidification shrinkage, **lowest distortion of all welding methods**.
- [ ] **Resistance spot welding** produces **localized indentation + sheet warping** — distinct from arc welding distortion patterns.
- [ ] **In KTU exams, always state assumptions** (e.g., fully restrained, free shrinkage, elastic vs. plastic behavior) before applying formulas.
- [ ] **Convert angles carefully** — radians for formula derivation, degrees only when the question explicitly asks.
- [ ] **Always draw labelled diagrams** showing weld bead, plate edges, and direction of shrinkage force for full marks in descriptive questions.
- [ ] **Distinguish in-plane vs. out-of-plane distortion** — this is a **favourite 2-mark question** in KTU Part A.
